# FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention вҖ” Dao et al. 2022
# ArXiv: 2205.14135
# Fuente: arXiv (acceso abierto)

## Abstract


Transformers are slow and memory-hungry on long sequences, since the time and memory complexity of self-attention are quadratic in sequence length.
Approximate attention methods have attempted to address this problem by
trading off model quality to reduce the compute complexity, but often do not achieve wall-clock speedup.
We argue that a missing principle is making attention algorithms IO-awareвҖ”accounting for reads and writes between levels of GPU memory.
We propose FlashAttention, an IO-aware exact attention algorithm that uses tiling to reduce the number of memory reads/writes between GPU high bandwidth memory (HBM) and GPU on-chip SRAM.
We analyze the IO complexity of FlashAttention, showing that it requires fewer HBM accesses than standard attention, and is optimal for a range of SRAM sizes.
We also extend FlashAttention to block-sparse attention, yielding an approximate attention algorithm that is faster than any existing approximate attention method.
FlashAttention trains Transformers faster than existing baselines: 15% end-to-end wall-clock speedup on BERT-large (seq.В length 512) compared to the MLPerf 1.1 training speed record, 3Г—\times speedup on GPT-2 (seq.В length 1K), and 2.4Г—\times speedup on long-range arena (seq.В length 1K-4K).
FlashAttention and block-sparse FlashAttention enable longer context in Transformers, yielding higher quality models (0.7 better perplexity on GPT-2 and 6.4 points of lift on long-document classification) and entirely new capabilities: the first Transformers to achieve better-than-chance performance on the Path-X challenge (seq.В length 16K, 61.4% accuracy) and Path-256 (seq.В length 64K, 63.1% accuracy).


## 
1 Introduction


Transformer modelsВ [82] have emerged as the most widely used architecture in applications such as natural language processing and image classification.
Transformers have grown largerВ [5] and deeperВ [83], but equipping them with longer context remains difficultВ [80], since the self-attention module at their heart has time and memory complexity quadratic in sequence length.
An important question is whether making attention faster and more memory-efficient can help Transformer models address their runtime and memory challenges for long sequences.


Many approximate attention methods have aimed to reduce the compute and memory requirements of attention.
These methods range from sparse-approximationВ [51, 74] to low-rank approximationВ [84, 50, 12], and their combinationsВ [3, 92, 9].
Although these methods reduce the compute requirements to linear or near-linear in sequence length, many of them do not display wall-clock speedup against standard attention and have not gained wide adoption. One main reason is that they focus on FLOP reduction (which may not correlate with wall-clock speed) and tend to ignore overheads from memory access (IO).


Figure 1: 
Left: FlashAttention uses tiling to prevent materialization of the large NГ—Nрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒN\times N attention matrix (dotted box) on (relatively) slow GPU HBM. In the outer loop (red arrows), FlashAttention loops through blocks of the рқҗҠрқҗҠ\mathbf{K} and рқҗ•рқҗ•\mathbf{V} matrices and loads them to fast on-chip SRAM.
In each block, FlashAttention loops over blocks of рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q} matrix (blue arrows), loading them to SRAM, and writing the output of the attention computation back to HBM.
Right: Speedup over the PyTorch implementation of attention on GPT-2.
FlashAttention does not read and write the large NГ—Nрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒN\times N attention matrix to HBM, resulting in an 7.6Г—\times speedup on the attention computation.


In this paper, we argue that a missing principle is making attention algorithms IO-awareВ [1]вҖ”that is, carefully accounting for reads and writes to different levels of fast and slow memory (e.g., between fast GPU on-chip SRAM and relatively slow GPU high bandwidth memory, or HBMВ [45], FigureВ 1 left).
On modern GPUs, compute speed has out-paced memory speedВ [61, 62, 63], and most operations in Transformers are bottlenecked by memory accessesВ [43].
IO-aware algorithms have been critical for similar memory-bound operations, when reading and writing data can account for a large portion of the runtimeвҖ”such as database joinsВ [71], image processingВ [70], numerical linear algebraВ [4], and moreВ [85, 40].
However, common Python interfaces to deep learning such as PyTorch and Tensorflow do not allow fine-grained control of memory access.


We propose FlashAttention, a new attention algorithm that computes exact attention with far fewer memory accesses.
Our main goal is to avoid reading and writing the attention matrix to and from HBM.
This requires (i) computing the softmax reduction without access to the whole input (ii) not storing the large intermediate attention matrix for the backward pass.
We apply two well-established techniques to address these challenges.
(i) We restructure the attention computation to split the input into blocks and make several passes over input blocks, thus incrementally performing the softmax reduction (also known as tiling). (ii) We store the softmax normalization factor from the forward pass to quickly recompute attention on-chip in the backward pass, which is faster than the standard approach of reading the intermediate attention matrix from HBM.
We implement FlashAttention in CUDA to achieve fine-grained control over memory access and fuse all the attention operations into one GPU kernel.
Even with the increased FLOPs due to recomputation, our algorithm both runs faster (up to 7.6x on GPT-2В [67], FigureВ 1 right) and uses less memoryвҖ”linear in sequence lengthвҖ”than standard attention, thanks to the massively reduced amount of HBM access.


We analyze the IO complexityВ [1] of FlashAttention, proving that it requires OвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1O(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}) HBM accesses where dрқ‘‘d is the head dimension and Mрқ‘ҖM is the size of SRAM, as compared to О©вҖӢ(NвҖӢd+N2)О©
рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Ғ2\Omega(Nd+N^{2}) of standard attention.
For typical values of dрқ‘‘d and Mрқ‘ҖM, FlashAttention requires many times fewer HBM accesses compared to standard attention (up to 9Г—\times fewer, as shown inВ Fig.В 2).
Moreover, we provide a lower bound, showing that no exact attention algorithm can asymptotically improve on the number of HBM accesses over all SRAM sizes.


We also show that FlashAttention can serve as a
useful primitive for realizing the potential of approximate attention algorithms by overcoming their issues with memory access overhead.
As a proof of concept, we implement block-sparse FlashAttention, a sparse attention algorithm that is 2-4Г—\times faster than even FlashAttention, scaling up to sequence length of 64k.
We prove that block-sparse FlashAttention has better IO complexity than FlashAttention by a factor proportional to the sparsity ratio.
We discuss further extensions to other operations (attention on multi-GPU, kernel regression, block-sparse matrix multiply) inВ SectionВ 5.
We open-source FlashAttention to make it easier to build on this primitive.111FlashAttention code is available at https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-attention


We empirically validate that FlashAttention speeds up model training and improves model quality by modeling longer context. We also benchmark the runtime and memory footprint of FlashAttention and block-sparse FlashAttention compared to prior attention implementations.


- 
вҖў 


Faster Model Training. FlashAttention trains Transformer models faster in wall-clock time. We train BERT-large (seq.В length 512) 15% faster than the training speed record in MLPerf 1.1В [58], GPT2 (seq.В length 1K) 3Г—\times faster than baseline implementations from HuggingFaceВ [87] and Megatron-LMВ [77], and long-range arena (seq.В length 1K-4K) 2.4Г—\times faster than baselines.


- 
вҖў 


Higher Quality Models. FlashAttention scales Transformers to longer sequences, which improves their quality and enables new capabilities.
We observe a 0.7 improvement in perplexity on GPT-2 and 6.4 points of lift from modeling longer sequences on long-document classificationВ [13].
FlashAttention enables the first Transformer that can achieve better-than-chance performance on the Path-XВ [80] challenge, solely from using a longer sequence length (16K).
Block-sparse FlashAttention enables a Transformer to scale to even longer sequences (64K), resulting in the first model that can achieve better-than-chance performance on Path-256.


- 
вҖў 


Benchmarking Attention. FlashAttention is up to 3Г—\times faster than the standard attention implementation across common sequence lengths from 128 to 2K and scales up to 64K.
Up to sequence length of 512, FlashAttention is both faster and more memory-efficient than any existing attention method, whereas for sequence length beyond 1K, some approximate attention methods (e.g., Linformer) start to become faster.
On the other hand, block-sparse FlashAttention is faster than all existing approximate attention methods that we know of.


## 
2 Background


We provide some background on the performance characteristics of common deep
learning operations on modern hardware (GPUs).
We also describe the standard implementation of attention.


### 
2.1 Hardware Performance


We focus here on GPUs.
Performance on other hardware accelerators are similarВ [48, 46].


GPU Memory Hierarchy.
The GPU memory hierarchy (Fig.В 1 left) comprises multiple forms of memory of different
sizes and speeds, with smaller memory being faster.
As an example, the A100 GPU has 40-80GB of high bandwidth memory (HBM) with
bandwidth 1.5-2.0TB/s and 192KB of on-chip SRAM per each of 108 streaming
multiprocessors with
bandwidth estimated around 19TB/sВ [45, 44].
The on-chip SRAM is an order of magnitude faster than HBM but many orders of
magnitude smaller in size.
As compute has gotten faster relative to memory speedВ [61, 62, 63], operations
are increasingly bottlenecked by memory (HBM) accesses.
Thus exploiting fast SRAM becomes more important.


Execution Model.
GPUs have a massive number of threads to execute an operation
(called a kernel).
Each kernel loads inputs from HBM to registers and SRAM, computes, then writes outputs to HBM.


Performance characteristics. Depending on the balance of computation and memory accesses, operations can be
classified as either compute-bound or memory-bound.
This is commonly measured by the arithmetic intensityВ [85],
which is the number of arithmetic operations per byte of memory access.


- 
1. 


Compute-bound: the time taken by the operation is determined by how many
arithmetic operations there are, while time accessing HBM
is much smaller. Typical examples are matrix multiply with large inner
dimension, and convolution with large number of channels.


- 
2. 


Memory-bound: the time taken by the operation is determined by the
number of memory accesses, while time spent in computation is much smaller.
Examples include most other operations:
elementwise (e.g., activation, dropout), and reduction (e.g., sum,
softmax, batch norm, layer norm).


Kernel fusion.
The most common approach to accelerate memory-bound operations is
kernel fusion: if there are multiple operations applied to the same input,
the input can be loaded once from HBM, instead of multiple times for each operation.
Compilers can automatically fuse many elementwise operationsВ [53, 65, 75].
However, in the context of model training, the intermediate values still need
to be written to HBM to save for the backward pass, reducing the
effectiveness of naive kernel fusion.


### 
2.2 Standard Attention Implementation


Given input sequences рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} where Nрқ‘ҒN is the sequence length and
dрқ‘‘d is the head dimension, we want to compute the attention output рқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—dрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}:


 | 
 | рқҗ’=рқҗҗрқҗҠвҠӨвҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗҸ=softmaxвҖӢ(рқҗ’)вҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗҺ=рқҗҸрқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d,formulae-sequenceрқҗ’superscriptрқҗҗрқҗҠtopsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒрқҗҸsoftmaxрқҗ’superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒрқҗҺрқҗҸрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S})\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d},
 | 


where softmaxsoftmax\mathrm{softmax} is applied row-wise.


Standard attention implementations materialize the matrices рқҗ’рқҗ’\mathbf{S} and рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P} to HBM, which takes OвҖӢ(N2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2O(N^{2}) memory.
Often Nвү«dmuch-greater-thanрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘N\gg d (e.g., for GPT2, N=1024рқ‘Ғ1024N=1024 and d=64рқ‘‘64d=64).
We describe the standard attention implementation inВ AlgorithmВ .
As some or most of the operations are memory-bound (e.g., softmax), the large number of
memory accesses translates to slow wall-clock time.


This problem is exacerbated by other elementwise operations applied
to the attention matrix, such as masking applied to рқҗ’рқҗ’\mathbf{S} or dropout applied to рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P}.
As a result, there have been many attempts to fuse several elementwise
operations, such as fusing masking with softmaxВ [77].


In SectionВ 3.2, we will show that the standard attention implementation
performs HBM accesses quadratic in the sequence length Nрқ‘ҒN.
We also compare the number of FLOPs and number of HBM accesses of standard
attention and of our method (FlashAttention).


Algorithm 0 Standard Attention Implementation


0:В В Matrices рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} in HBM.


1:В В  Load рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ
- рқҗҗрқҗҠ\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K} by blocks from HBM, compute
рқҗ’=рқҗҗрқҗҠвҠӨрқҗ’superscriptрқҗҗрқҗҠtop\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}, write рқҗ’рқҗ’\mathbf{S} to HBM.


2:В В  Read рқҗ’рқҗ’\mathbf{S} from HBM, compute рқҗҸ=softmaxвҖӢ(рқҗ’)рқҗҸsoftmaxрқҗ’\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S}), write рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P} to
HBM.


3:В В  Load рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P} and рқҗ•рқҗ•\mathbf{V} by blocks from HBM, compute рқҗҺ=рқҗҸрқҗ•рқҗҺрқҗҸрқҗ•\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}\mathbf{V}, write рқҗҺрқҗҺ\mathbf{O} to
HBM.


4:В В Return рқҗҺрқҗҺ\mathbf{O}.


## 
3 FlashAttention: Algorithm, Analysis, and Extensions


We show how to compute exact attention with fewer HBM reads/writes and without storing large intermediate matrices for the backward pass.
This yields an attention algorithm that is both memory efficient and faster
in wall-clock time.
We analyze its IO complexity, showing that our method requires
much fewer HBM accesses compared to standard attention.
We further show that FlashAttention can serve as a useful primitive by extending it to handle block-sparse attention.


We focus here on the forward pass for ease of exposition; AppendixВ B contains details
for the backward.


### 
3.1 An Efficient Attention Algorithm With Tiling and Recomputation


Given the inputs рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} in HBM, we aim to compute the attention output рқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—dрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} and write it to HBM.
Our goal is to reduce the amount of HBM accesses (to sub-quadratic in Nрқ‘ҒN).


We apply two established techniques (tiling, recomputation) to overcome the
technical challenge of computing exact attention in sub-quadratic HBM accesses.
We describe this inВ AlgorithmВ 1.
The main idea is that we split the inputs рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V} into blocks,
load them from slow HBM to fast SRAM, then compute the attention output with
respect to those blocks.
By scaling the output of each block by the right normalization factor before
adding them up, we get the correct result at the end.


Tiling.
We compute attention by blocks.
Softmax couples columns of рқҗҠрқҗҠ\mathbf{K},
so we decompose the large softmax
with scalingВ [60, 51, 66].

For numerical stability, the softmax of vector xвҲҲв„қBрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptв„қрқҗөx\in\mathbb{R}^{B} is computed as:


 | 
 | mвҖӢ(x):=maxixi,fвҖӢ(x):=[ex1вҲ’mвҖӢ(x)вҖҰexBвҲ’mвҖӢ(x)],в„“вҖӢ(x):=вҲ‘ifвҖӢ(x)i,softmaxвҖӢ(x):=fвҖӢ(x)в„“вҖӢ(x).formulae-sequenceassignрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙ
- subscriptрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–formulae-sequenceassignрқ‘“рқ‘Ҙmatrixsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ҡрқ‘ҘвҖҰsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗөрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙformulae-sequenceassignв„“рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘–рқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–assignsoftmaxрқ‘Ҙрқ‘“рқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ҙm(x):=\max_{i}\ \ x_{i},\quad f(x):=\begin{bmatrix}e^{x_{1}-m(x)}&\ldots&e^{x_{B}-m(x)}\end{bmatrix},\quad\ell(x):=\sum_{i}f(x)_{i},\quad\mathrm{softmax}(x):=\frac{f(x)}{\ell(x)}.
 | 


For vectors x(1),x(2)вҲҲв„қB
- superscriptрқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2superscriptв„қрқҗөx^{(1)},x^{(2)}\in\mathbb{R}^{B}, we can decompose the softmax of the concatenated x=[x(1)вҖӢx(2)]вҲҲв„қ2вҖӢBрқ‘Ҙmatrixsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2superscriptв„қ2рқҗөx=\begin{bmatrix}x^{(1)}\ x^{(2)}\end{bmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^{2B} as:


 | 
 | mвҖӢ(x)=mвҖӢ([x(1)вҖӢx(2)])=maxвҒЎ(mвҖӢ(x(1)),mвҖӢ(x(2))),fвҖӢ(x)=[emвҖӢ(x(1))вҲ’mвҖӢ(x)вҖӢfвҖӢ(x(1))emвҖӢ(x(2))вҲ’mвҖӢ(x)вҖӢfвҖӢ(x(2))],formulae-sequenceрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҡmatrixsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2рқ‘ҡsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ҡsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ2рқ‘“рқ‘Ҙmatrixsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘ҡsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙрқ‘“superscriptрқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘ҡsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ2рқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙрқ‘“superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2\displaystyle m(x)=m(\begin{bmatrix}x^{(1)}\ x^{(2)}\end{bmatrix})=\max(m(x^{(1)}),m(x^{(2)})),\quad f(x)=\begin{bmatrix}e^{m(x^{(1)})-m(x)}f(x^{(1)})&e^{m(x^{(2)})-m(x)}f(x^{(2)})\end{bmatrix},
 | 


 | 
 | в„“вҖӢ(x)=в„“вҖӢ([x(1)вҖӢx(2)])=emвҖӢ(x(1))вҲ’mвҖӢ(x)вҖӢв„“вҖӢ(x(1))+emвҖӢ(x(2))вҲ’mвҖӢ(x)вҖӢв„“вҖӢ(x(2)),softmaxвҖӢ(x)=fвҖӢ(x)в„“вҖӢ(x).formulae-sequenceв„“рқ‘Ҙв„“matrixsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2
superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘ҡsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙв„“superscriptрқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘ҡsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ2рқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙв„“superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2softmaxрқ‘Ҙрқ‘“рқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle\ell(x)=\ell(\begin{bmatrix}x^{(1)}\ x^{(2)}\end{bmatrix})=e^{m(x^{(1)})-m(x)}\ell(x^{(1)})+e^{m(x^{(2)})-m(x)}\ell(x^{(2)}),\quad\mathrm{softmax}(x)=\frac{f(x)}{\ell(x)}.
 | 


Therefore if we keep track of some extra statistics (mвҖӢ(x),в„“вҖӢ(x)
- рқ‘ҡрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ҙm(x),\ell(x)), we can compute softmax one block at a time.222This style of aggregation is called algebraic aggregationВ [33].

We thus split the inputs рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V} into blocks (AlgorithmВ 1 line 3), compute the softmax values along with extra statistics (AlgorithmВ 1 line 10), and combine the results (AlgorithmВ 1 line 12).


Recomputation.
One of our goals is to not store OвҖӢ(N2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2O(N^{2}) intermediate values for the backward
pass.
The backward pass typically requires the matrices
рқҗ’,рқҗҸвҲҲв„қNГ—N
- рқҗ’рқҗҸsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{S},\mathbf{P}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} to compute the gradients with respect to рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}.
However, by storing the output рқҗҺрқҗҺ\mathbf{O} and the softmax normalization statistics (m,в„“)рқ‘ҡв„“(m,\ell), we can
recompute the attention matrix рқҗ’рқҗ’\mathbf{S} and рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P} easily in the backward pass from blocks of рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V} in SRAM.
This can be seen as a form of selective gradient checkpointingВ [34, 10].
While gradient checkpointing has been suggested to reduce the maximum amount of memory requiredВ [66], all implementations (that we know off) have to trade speed for memory.
In contrast, even with more FLOPs, our recomputation speeds up the backward pass due to reduced HBM accesses (Fig.В 2).
The full backward pass description is inВ AppendixВ B.


Implementation details: Kernel fusion.
Tiling enables us to implement our algorithm in one CUDA kernel, loading input from HBM,
performing all the computation steps (matrix multiply, softmax, optionally
masking and dropout, matrix multiply), then write the result back to HBM (masking and dropout inВ AppendixВ B).
This avoids repeatedly reading and writing of inputs and outputs from and to HBM.


Algorithm 1 FlashAttention


0:В В Matrices рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} in HBM, on-chip SRAM of
size Mрқ‘ҖM.


1:В В Set block sizes Bc=вҢҲM4вҖӢdвҢү,Br=minвҒЎ(вҢҲM4вҖӢdвҢү,d)formulae-sequencesubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘Җ4рқ‘‘subscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘Җ4рқ‘‘рқ‘‘B_{c}=\left\lceil\frac{M}{4d}\right\rceil,B_{r}=\min\left(\left\lceil\frac{M}{4d}\right\rceil,d\right).

and divide рқҗҠ,рқҗ•

to HBM.

We show FlashAttentionвҖҷs correctness, runtime, and memory requirement (proof inВ AppendixВ C).


###### 
Theorem 1.


AlgorithmВ 1 returns рқҗҺ=softmaxвҖӢ(рқҗҗрқҗҠвҠӨ)вҖӢрқҗ•рқҗҺsoftmaxsuperscriptрқҗҗрқҗҠtopрқҗ•\mathbf{O}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top})\mathbf{V} with OвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2рқ‘‘O(N^{2}d) FLOPs and
requires OвҖӢ(N)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒO(N) additional memory beyond inputs and output.


### 
3.2 Analysis: IO Complexity of FlashAttention


We analyze the IO complexity of FlashAttention, showing
significant reduction in HBM accesses compared to standard attention.
We also provide a lower bound, proving that no exact attention algorithm can asymptotically improve on HBM accesses over all
SRAM sizes.
Proofs are in AppendixВ C.


###### 
Theorem 2.


Let Nрқ‘ҒN be the sequence length, dрқ‘‘d be the head dimension, and Mрқ‘ҖM be size of
SRAM with dвүӨMвүӨNвҖӢdрқ‘‘рқ‘Җрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘d\leq M\leq Nd.
Standard attention (AlgorithmВ ) requires ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢd+N2)Оҳ
рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Ғ2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) HBM
accesses, while FlashAttention (AlgorithmВ 1) requires
ОҳвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1)Оҳsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1\Theta(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}) HBM accesses.


For typical values of dрқ‘‘d (64-128) and Mрқ‘ҖM (around 100KB), d2superscriptрқ‘‘2d^{2} is many
times smaller than Mрқ‘ҖM, and thus FlashAttention requires many times fewer
HBM accesses than standard implementation.
This leads to both faster execution and lower memory footprint, which we
validate inВ SectionВ 4.3.


The main idea of the proof is that given the SRAM size of Mрқ‘ҖM, we
can load blocks of рқҗҠ,рқҗ•
- рқҗҠрқҗ•\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V} of size ОҳвҖӢ(M)Оҳрқ‘Җ\Theta(M) each (AlgorithmВ 1 line 6).
For each block of рқҗҠрқҗҠ\mathbf{K} and рқҗ•рқҗ•\mathbf{V}, we iterate over all blocks of рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q}
(AlgorithmВ 1 line 8) to compute the
intermediate values, resulting in ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢdвҖӢMвҲ’1)Оҳрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Җ1\Theta(NdM^{-1}) passes over рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q}.
Each pass loads ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢd)Оҳрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\Theta(Nd) elements, which amounts to ОҳвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1)Оҳsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1\Theta(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}) HBM accesses.
We similarly prove that the backward pass of standard attention requires
ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢd+N2)Оҳ
рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Ғ2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) HBM accesses while the backward pass of FlashAttention requires
ОҳвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1)Оҳsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1\Theta(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}) HBM accesses (AppendixВ B).


We prove a lower-bound: one cannot asymptotically improve on the number of HBM
accesses for all values of Mрқ‘ҖM (the SRAM size) when computing exact attention.


###### 
Proposition 3.


Let Nрқ‘ҒN be the sequence length, dрқ‘‘d be the head dimension, and Mрқ‘ҖM be size of
SRAM with dвүӨMвүӨNвҖӢdрқ‘‘рқ‘Җрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘d\leq M\leq Nd.
There does not exist an algorithm to compute exact attention with
oвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1)рқ‘ңsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1o(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}) HBM accesses for all Mрқ‘ҖM in the range
[d,NвҖӢd]рқ‘‘рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘[d,Nd].


The proof relies on the fact that for M=ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢd)рқ‘ҖОҳрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘M=\Theta(Nd) any algorithm must perform
О©вҖӢ(N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1)=О©вҖӢ(NвҖӢd)О©superscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1О©рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\Omega(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1})=\Omega(Nd) HBM accesses.
This type of lower bound over a subrange of Mрқ‘ҖM is common in the streaming
algorithms literatureВ [88].
We leave proving parameterized complexityВ [27] lower
bounds in terms of Mрқ‘ҖM as exciting future work.


We validate that the number of HBM accesses is the main determining factor of
attention run-time.
InВ Fig.В 2 (left), we see that even though FlashAttention has
higher FLOP count compared to standard attention (due to recomputation in the
backward pass), it has much fewer HBM accesses, resulting in much faster
runtime.
InВ Fig.В 2 (middle), we vary the block size Bcsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җB_{c} of FlashAttention, which results in different amounts of HBM accesses, and measure the
runtime of the forward pass.
As block size increases, the number of HBM accesses decreases (as we make fewer
passes over the input), and runtime decreases.
For large enough block size (beyond 256), the runtime is then bottlenecked by
other factors (e.g., arithmetic operations).
Moreover, larger block size will not fit into the small SRAM size.


 | 


 | Attention
 | Standard
 | FlashAttention


 | GFLOPs
 | 66.6
 | 75.2


 | HBM R/W (GB)
 | 40.3
 | 4.4


 | Runtime (ms)
 | 41.7
 | 7.3


Figure 2: 
Left: Forward + backward runtime of
standard attention and FlashAttention for GPT-2 medium
(seq.В length 1024, head dim.В 64, 16 heads, batch size 64) on
A100 GPU.
HBM access is the primary factor affecting runtime.
Middle: Forward runtime of FlashAttention (seq.В length 1024, head
dim.В 64, 16 heads, batch size 64)
on A100 GPU. Fewer HBM accesses result in faster runtime, up to a point.
Right: The runtime (for seq.В length 4K) of
block-sparse FlashAttention is faster than FlashAttention by a factor proportional
to the sparsity.


### 
3.3 Extension: Block-Sparse FlashAttention


We extend FlashAttention to approximate attention:
we propose block-sparse FlashAttention, whose IO
complexity is smaller than FlashAttention by a factor proportional to the
sparsity.


Given inputs рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} and a mask matrix
рқҗҢ~вҲҲ{0,1}NГ—N~рқҗҢsuperscript01рқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\tilde{\mathbf{M}}\in\{0,1\}^{N\times N}, we want to compute:


 | 
 | рқҗ’=рқҗҗрқҗҠвҠӨвҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗҸ=softmaxвҖӢ(рқҗ’вҠҷ\vmathbbвҖӢ1рқҗҢ~)вҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗҺ=рқҗҸрқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d,formulae-sequenceрқҗ’superscriptрқҗҗрқҗҠtopsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒрқҗҸsoftmaxdirect-productрқҗ’\vmathbbsubscript1~рқҗҢsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒрқҗҺрқҗҸрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S}\odot\vmathbb{1}_{\tilde{\mathbf{M}}})\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d},
 | 


where (рқҗ’вҠҷ\vmathbbвҖӢ1рқҗҢ~)kвҖӢl=рқҗ’kвҖӢlsubscriptdirect-productрқҗ’\vmathbbsubscript1~рқҗҢрқ‘ҳрқ‘ҷsubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘ҳрқ‘ҷ(\mathbf{S}\odot\vmathbb{1}_{\tilde{\mathbf{M}}})_{kl}=\mathbf{S}_{kl} if
рқҗҢ~kвҖӢl=1subscript~рқҗҢрқ‘ҳрқ‘ҷ1\tilde{\mathbf{M}}_{kl}=1 and вҲ’вҲһ-\infty if рқҗҢkвҖӢl=0subscriptрқҗҢрқ‘ҳрқ‘ҷ0\mathbf{M}_{kl}=0.
We require рқҗҢ~~рқҗҢ\tilde{\mathbf{M}} to have block form: for some block sizes Br,Bc
- subscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җB_{r},B_{c},
for all k,l
- рқ‘ҳрқ‘ҷk,l, рқҗҢ~k,l=рқҗҢiвҖӢjsubscript~рқҗҢ
- рқ‘ҳрқ‘ҷsubscriptрқҗҢрқ‘–рқ‘—\tilde{\mathbf{M}}_{k,l}=\mathbf{M}_{ij} with
i=вҢҠk/BrвҢӢ,j=вҢҠl/BcвҢӢformulae-sequenceрқ‘–рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘—рқ‘ҷsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җi=\lfloor k/B_{r}\rfloor,j=\lfloor l/B_{c}\rfloor for some рқҗҢвҲҲ{0,1}N/BrГ—N/BcрқҗҢsuperscript01рқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{M}\in\{0,1\}^{N/B_{r}\times N/B_{c}}.


Given a predefined block sparsity mask рқҗҢвҲҲ{0,1}N/BrГ—N/BcрқҗҢsuperscript01рқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{M}\in\{0,1\}^{N/B_{r}\times N/B_{c}} we can
easily adaptВ AlgorithmВ 1 to only compute the nonzero blocks of the
attention matrix.
The algorithm is identical toВ AlgorithmВ 1, except we skip zero
blocks.
We reproduce the algorithm description inВ AlgorithmВ 5 in
AppendixВ B.


We also analyze the IO complexity of block-sparse FlashAttention.


###### 
Proposition 4.


Let Nрқ‘ҒN be the sequence length, dрқ‘‘d be the head dimension, and Mрқ‘ҖM be size of
SRAM with dвүӨMвүӨNвҖӢdрқ‘‘рқ‘Җрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘d\leq M\leq Nd.
Block-sparse FlashAttention (AlgorithmВ 5) requires
ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢd+N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1вҖӢs)Оҳ
рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1рқ‘ \Theta(Nd+N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}s) HBM accesses where sрқ‘ s is the
fraction of nonzero blocks in the block-sparsity mask.


We see that applying block-sparsity yields a direct improvement by the sparsity to the larger term in the IO
complexity.
For large sequence lengths Nрқ‘ҒN, sрқ‘ s is often set to
NвҲ’1/2superscriptрқ‘Ғ12N^{-1/2}В [11] or
NвҲ’1вҖӢlogвҒЎNsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ1рқ‘ҒN^{-1}\log NВ [92, 3, 17], resulting in ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢN)Оҳрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\Theta(N\sqrt{N}) or
ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢlogвҒЎN)Оҳрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\Theta(N\log N) IO complexity.
For downstream experiments, we use the fixed butterfly sparsity
patternВ [17], which has been shown to be able to approximate
arbitrary sparsityВ [16].


InВ Fig.В 2 (right), we validate that as the sparsity increases, the
runtime of block-sparse FlashAttention improves proportionally.
On the LRA benchmark, block-sparse FlashAttention achieves 2.8Г—\times
speedup, while performing on par with standard attention (SectionВ 4).


## 
4 Experiments


We evaluate the impact of using FlashAttention to train Transformer models.
We validate two claims about training time and model accuracy, and report attention runtime and memory benchmarks.


- 
вҖў 


Training Speed. FlashAttention outperforms the MLPerf 1.1В [58] speed record for BERT by 15%, and speeds up GPT-2 up to 3Г—\times over HuggingFaceВ [87] and 1.8Г—1.8\times over MegatronВ [77] over standard Transformers.
FlashAttention speeds up the long-range
arena (LRA) benchmark 2.4Г—\times.


- 
вҖў 


Quality. FlashAttention scales Transformers to longer sequences, yielding higher quality. FlashAttention trains GPT-2 with context length 4K faster
than Megatron trains GPT-2 with context length 1K, while
achieving 0.7 better perplexity.
Modeling longer sequences yields 6.4 points of lift on two long-document classification tasks.
Finally, FlashAttention yields the first Transformer that can achieve
better-than-random performance on the challenging Path-X task (sequence
length 16K), and block-sparse FlashAttention yields the first sequence model that we know of that can achieve better-than-random performance on Path-256 (sequence length 64K).


- 
вҖў 


Benchmarking Attention. We measure the runtime and memory performance of FlashAttention and block-sparse FlashAttention based on sequence length.
We confirm that the memory footprint of FlashAttention scales linearly with seq.В length and is up to 3Г—\times faster than standard attention for common seq.В lengths (up to 2K).
We confirm that runtime of block-sparse FlashAttention scales linearly in seq.В length and is faster than all existing approximate attention baselines.


Additional experiment details are inВ AppendixВ E.


### 
4.1 Faster Models with FlashAttention


##### BERT.


FlashAttention yields the fastest single-node BERT training speed that we know of.
We train a BERT-largeВ [22] model
with FlashAttention on Wikipedia.
TableВ 1 compares our training time to the implementation from Nvidia that set the
training speed record for MLPerf 1.1В [58].
Our implementation is 15% faster.

to reach the

##### GPT-2.


FlashAttention yields faster training times for GPT-2В [67] on the large OpenWebtext datasetВ [32] than the widely used HuggingFaceВ [87] and Megatron-LMВ [77] implementations.
TableВ 2 shows up to 3Г—\times end-to-end speedup compared to Huggingface
and 1.7Г—\times speedup compared to Megatron-LM.
FlashAttention achieves the same perplexity as the other two
implementations, as we do not change the model definition.
AppendixВ E includes plots of the validation perplexity throughout training,
confirming that FlashAttention is as numerically stable as the baselines
and produces the same training / validation curves.

### 
4.2 Better Models with Longer Sequences


##### Language Modeling with Long Context.


The runtime and memory-efficiency of FlashAttention allow us to increase the context length of
GPT-2 by 4Г—\times while still running faster than the optimized
implementation from Megatron-LM.
TableВ 4 shows that that GPT-2 with FlashAttention and
context length 4K is still 30% faster than GPT-2 from Megatron with context
length 1K, while achieving 0.7 better perplexity.

##### Long Document Classification.


Training Transformers with longer sequences with FlashAttention improves performance on the MIMIC-IIIВ [47] and ECtHRВ [6, 7] datasets.
MIMIC-III contains intensive care unit patient discharge summaries, each annotated with multiple labels.
ECtHR contains legal cases from the European Court of Human Rights, each of which is mapped to articles of the Convention of Human Rights that were allegedly violaged.
Both of these datasets contain very long text documents; the average number of tokens in MIMIC is 2,395 tokens, and the longest document contains 14,562 tokens, while the average and longest numbers in ECtHR are 2,197 and 49,392, respectively.
We evaluate lift from increasing the sequence length of a pretrained RoBERTa modelВ [56] (we repeat the positional embeddings, as inВ Beltagy etВ al. [3]).


TableВ 6 shows that sequence length 16K outperforms length 512 by 4.3 points on MIMIC, and that length 8K outperforms length 512 by 8.5 points on ECtHR.
The discrepancies may be due to subtle distribution shifts: MIMIC-III contains specialized medical text and thus may be more susceptible to a distribution shift in the document length, whereas ECtHR contains general language.

##### Path-X and Path-256.


The Path-X and Path-256 benchmarks are challenging tasks from the long-range arena benchmark designed to test long context.
The task is to classify whether two points in a black and white 128Г—\times128 (or 256Г—\times256) image have a path connecting them, and the images are fed to the transformer one pixel at a time.
In prior work, all transformer models have either run out of memory, or only
achieved random performanceВ [80].
There has been a search for alternative architectures that can model such long contextВ [37].
We present here the first result of Transformer models being able to solve
Path-X and Path-256 (TableВ 6).
We pretrain a transformer on Path-64, and then transfer to Path-X by spatially interpolating the positional embeddings.
FlashAttention achieves 61.4 accuracy on Path-X.
Additionally, block-sparse FlashAttention enables the Transformers to scale to sequence length 64K, achieving 63.1 accuracy444Path-256 requires longer sequences but has relatively shorter paths than Path-X, so it is easier to obtain a higher accuracy. on Path-256.


### 
4.3 Benchmarking Attention


Figure 3: Left: runtime of forward pass + backward pass. Right: attention memory usage.


We vary sequence length and measure runtime and memory usage of FlashAttention and block-sparse FlashAttention against various attention baselines on one A100 GPU with 40 GB HBM, with dropout and a padding mask.
We compare against reference implementations for exact attention, approximate attention, and sparse attention.
We report a subset of baselines in the main body; AppendixВ E contains more baselines and full details.


##### Runtime.


FigureВ 3 (left) reports the runtime in milliseconds of the forward + backward pass of FlashAttention and block-sparse FlashAttention compared to the baselines in exact, approximate, and sparse attention (exact numbers in AppendixВ E).
Runtime grows quadratically with sequence length, but FlashAttention runs significantly faster than exact attention baselines, up to 3Г—\times faster than the PyTorch implementation.
The runtimes of many approximate/sparse attention mechanisms grow linearly with sequence length, but FlashAttention still runs faster than approximate and sparse attention for short sequences due to fewer memory accesses.
The approximate attention runtimes begin to cross over with FlashAttention at sequences between 512 and 1024.
On the other hand, block-sparse FlashAttention is faster than all implementations of exact, sparse, and approximate attention that we know of, across all sequence lengths.


##### Memory Footprint.


FigureВ 3 (right) shows the memory footprint of FlashAttention and block-sparse FlashAttention compared to various exact, approximate, and sparse attention baselines.
FlashAttention and block-sparse FlashAttention have the same memory footprint, which grows linearly with sequence length.
FlashAttention is up to 20Г—\times more memory efficient than exact attention baselines, and is more memory-efficient than the approximate attention baselines.
All other algorithms except for Linformer run out of memory on an A100 GPU before 64K, and FlashAttention is still 2Г—\times more efficient than Linformer.


## 
5 Limitations and Future Directions


We discuss limitations of our approach and future directions. Related work is given inВ AppendixВ A.


Compiling to CUDA. Our current approach to building IO-aware implementations of attention requires writing a new CUDA kernel for each new attention implementation.
This requires writing the attention algorithm in a considerably lower-level language than PyTorch, and requires significant engineering effort.
Implementations may also not be transferrable across GPU architectures.
These limitations suggest the need for a method that supports writing attention
algorithms in a high-level language (e.g., PyTorch), and compiling to IO-aware implementations in CUDAвҖ”similar to efforts such as Halide in image processingВ [70].


IO-Aware Deep Learning.
We believe that the IO-aware approach can extend beyond attention.
Attention is the most memory-intensive computation in Transformers, but every layer in a deep network touches GPU HBM.
We hope our work inspires IO-aware implementations of additional modules.
We discuss these potential extensions inВ AppendixВ D.


Multi-GPU IO-Aware Methods.
Our IO-aware implementation of attention is optimal within constants for computing attention on a single GPU.
However, the attention computation may be parallelizable across multiple GPUsВ [72].
Using multiple GPUs adds an additional layer to IO analysisвҖ”accounting for data transfer between GPUs.
We hope our work inspires future work in this direction.

Our implementation uses ApexвҖҷs FMHA code (https://github.com/NVIDIA/apex/tree/master/apex/contrib/csrc/fmha) as a starting point.
We thank Young-Jun Ko for the in-depth explanation of his FMHA implementation and for his thoughtful answers to our questions about CUDA.

## 
Appendix A Related Work


IO-Aware Runtime Optimization.
The broad concept of optimizing for reading and writing to fast/slow memory has a long history in computer science and has been known by many names.
We draw the most direct connection to the literature of analyzing I/O complexity in this workВ [1], but concepts of memory hierarchies are fundamental and has appeared in many forms, from the working set modelВ [21], to data localityВ [86], to the Roofline model of arithmetic intensityВ [85], to analyses of scalabilityВ [59], to standard textbook treatments of computer architectureВ [40].
We hope that this work encourages the community to adopt these ideas in more parts of the deep learning stack.


Efficient ML Models with Structured Matrices.
Matrix multiply is the core computational bottleneck of most machine learning
models.
To reduce the computational complexity, there have been numerous approaches to
learn over a more efficient set of matrices.
These matrices are called structured matrices, which have subquadratic
(oвҖӢ(n2)рқ‘ңsuperscriptрқ‘ӣ2o(n^{2}) for dimension nГ—nрқ‘ӣрқ‘ӣn\times n) number of parameters and runtime.
Most common examples of structured matrices are sparse and low-rank matrices,
along with fast transforms commonly encountered in signal processing (Fourier,
Chebyshev, sine/cosine, orthogonal polynomials).
There have been several more general classes of structured matrices proposed in
machine learning: Toeplitz-likeВ [78],
low-displacement rankВ [49],
quasi-separableВ [25]).
The butterfly pattern we use for our block-sparse attention is motivated by the
fact that butterfly matricesВ [64, 15] and their
products have been shown to be able to express any structured matrices with
almost optimal runtime and number of
parametersВ [20, 16].
However, even though structured matrices are efficient in theory, they have not
seen wide adoption since it is hard to translate their efficiency to wall-clock
speedup since dense unconstrained matrix multiply has very optimize
implementation, a phenomenon known as the hardware
lotteryВ [41].
Extensions of butterfly matricesВ [17, 18] aimed
to make butterfly matrices more hardware-friendly.


Sparse Training.
Our block-sparse FlashAttention can be seen as a step towards making sparse model
training more efficient.
Sparse models have seen success in compressing models for inference (pruning) by
sparsifying the weight
matricesВ [39, 38, 76, 55, 23].
For model training, the lottery
tickets hypothesisВ [28, 29, 30]
suggests that there are a set of small sub-networks derived from a larger dense
network that performs as well as the original dense network.
Out block-sparse FlashAttention can also be seen as a fixed lottery ticket in the
context of attention: we fix the sparsity pattern to be the butterfly pattern
through training, and observe that it performs almost as well as the (dense)
FlashAttention on the Long-range Arena tasks.


Efficient Transformer.
Transformer-based models have become the most widely-used architecture in
natural language processingВ [22] and computer
visionВ [24, 91].
However, one of their computational bottlenecks is that their time and memory
scales quadratic in the sequence length.
There are numerous approaches to overcome this bottleneck, including
approximation with hashing (i.e., sparse) such as
ReformerВ [51] and SmyrfВ [19] and with
low-rank approximation such as
PerformerВ [12, 54].
One can even combine sparse and low-rank approximation for better accuracy
(e.g., LongformerВ [3],
BigBirdВ [92], ScatterbrainВ [9],
Long-short transformerВ [94], CombinerВ [73]).
Other approaches include compressing along the sequence dimension to attend to
multiple tokens at
onceВ [89, 79, 52, 57].
One can also attend over the states from previous sequences to help lengthen the
context (e.g., Transformer-XLВ [14] and Compressive
TransformerВ [69]).
We recommend the surveyВ [81] for more details.


There are several lines of work on developing other modules instead of attention
to model longer context. HiPPOВ [35] and its extensions, most
notably S4В [36, 37, 31] projects the
history on a polynomial basis, allowing accurate reconstruction of the history
through state-space models.
They combine the strengths of CNNs (efficient training), RNNs (efficient
inference), and continuous models (robust to change in sampling rates).
LambdaNetworksВ [2], AFTВ [93]
and FLASHВ [42] are other attempts at replacing attention
in the context of image classification and language modeling.


## 
Appendix B Algorithm Details


We first derive the forward and backward passes of attention and show that
they can be computed in a memory-efficient manner (requiring extra memory linear
instead of quadratic in the sequence length).
Though they reduce the amount of extra memory required, naively they still incur
quadratic HBM accesses, resulting in slower execution speed.
We describe the FlashAttention algorithm to implement both the forward and the
backward passes on GPUs that reduces HBM accesses, leading to both faster
runtime and smaller memory footprint.


### 
B.1 Memory-efficient forward pass


The main challenge in making attention memory-efficient is the softmax that
couples the columns of рқҗҠрқҗҠ\mathbf{K} (and columns of рқҗ•рқҗ•\mathbf{V}).
Our approach is to compute the softmax normalization constant separately to
decouple the columns.
This techniqueВ [60] has been used in the
literatureВ [51, 66] to show that attention
computation does not need quadratic extra memory (though the number of
HBM accesses is still quadratic, resulting in slow run-time).


For simplicity, we omit here the max-shifting step during softmax.
The full algorithm inВ SectionВ B.3 contains all the steps.


Recall that given input sequences рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, we want to
compute the attention output рқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—dрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}:


 | 
 | рқҗ’=рқҗҗрқҗҠвҠӨвҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗҸ=softmaxвҖӢ(рқҗ’)вҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗҺ=рқҗҸрқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d.formulae-sequenceрқҗ’superscriptрқҗҗрқҗҠtopsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒрқҗҸsoftmaxрқҗ’superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒрқҗҺрқҗҸрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S})\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}.
 | 


We have that SiвҖӢj=qiTвҖӢkjsubscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—S_{ij}=q_{i}^{T}k_{j} where qisubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–q_{i} and kjsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—k_{j} are the iрқ‘–i-th and
jрқ‘—j-th columns of рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q} and рқҗҠрқҗҠ\mathbf{K} respectively.
Define the normalization constants of softmax:


 | 
 | Li=вҲ‘jeqiTвҖӢkj.subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘—superscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—L_{i}=\sum_{j}e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}.
 | 
 | (1)


Let vjsubscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—v_{j} be the jрқ‘—j-th column of рқҗ•рқҗ•\mathbf{V}, then the iрқ‘–i-th columns of the output is


 | 
 | oi=Pi:вҖӢрқҗ•=вҲ‘jPiвҖӢjвҖӢvj=вҲ‘jeqiTвҖӢkjLiвҖӢvj.subscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentрқҗ•subscriptрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘—superscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—o_{i}=P_{i:}\mathbf{V}=\sum_{j}P_{ij}v_{j}=\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}v_{j}.
 | 
 | (2)


We see that once Lisubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–L_{i} is computed, we can compute oisubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–o_{i} without extra memory
by repeatedly summing eqiTвҖӢkjLiвҖӢvjsuperscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}v_{j}.
Therefore the forward pass can be computed with OвҖӢ(n)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ӣO(n) extra memory:


- 
1. 


Compute Lisubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–L_{i} for all iрқ‘–i according to Eq.В 1, which takes OвҖӢ(n)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ӣO(n)
extra memory.


- 
2. 


Compute oisubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–o_{i} for all iрқ‘–i according to Eq.В 2, which takes
OвҖӢ(d)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘‘O(d) extra memory.


### 
B.2 Memory-efficient backward pass


We derive the backward pass of attention and show that it can also be computed
with linear memory.
Rabe and Staats [66] suggests that the backward pass can be done without
quadratic extra memory by applying gradient checkpointing to the
memory-efficient forward pass.
We instead derive the backward pass explicitly and show how it can be computed
in a memory-efficient manner.


Suppose that there is a scalar loss function П•italic-П•\phi, and let the output gradient
be рқҗқрқҗҺвҲҲв„қnГ—dрқҗқрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘\mathbf{dO}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d} (where рқҗқрқҗҺрқҗқрқҗҺ\mathbf{dO} denotes
вҲӮП•вҲӮрқҗҺ
italic-П•
рқҗҺ\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{O}}).
We want to compute the input gradients рқҗқрқҗҗ,рқҗқрқҗҠ,рқҗқрқҗ•вҲҲв„қnГ—d
- рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}
(where рқҗқрқҗҗ,рқҗқрқҗҠ,рқҗқрқҗ•
- рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗ•\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV} denote
вҲӮП•вҲӮрқҗҗ,вҲӮП•вҲӮрқҗҠ,вҲӮП•вҲӮрқҗ•
- 
italic-П•
рқҗҗ
italic-П•
рқҗҠ
italic-П•
рқҗ•\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{Q}},\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{K}},\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial\mathbf{V}}
respectively).


The gradient рқҗқрқҗ•рқҗқрқҗ•\mathbf{dV} is easy to see.
Applying reverse-mode autodiff by hand (aka the chain rule), we obtain (in
matrix notation) рқҗқрқҗ•=рқҗҸTвҖӢрқҗқрқҗҺрқҗқрқҗ•superscriptрқҗҸрқ‘ҮрқҗқрқҗҺ\mathbf{dV}=\mathbf{P}^{T}\mathbf{dO}.
Thus:


 | 
 | dвҖӢvj=вҲ‘iPiвҖӢjвҖӢdвҖӢoi=вҲ‘ieqiTвҖӢkjLiвҖӢdвҖӢoi.рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘–superscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–dv_{j}=\sum_{i}P_{ij}do_{i}=\sum_{i}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}do_{i}.
 | 
 | (3)


Since we already computed Lisubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–L_{i}, dвҖӢvjрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—dv_{j} can be computed without extra memory by
repeated summing.


The gradients рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҗ\mathbf{dQ} and рқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗҠ\mathbf{dK} are a little more complicated.
We go through the gradients рқҗқрқҗҸрқҗқрқҗҸ\mathbf{dP} and рқҗқрқҗ’рқҗқрқҗ’\mathbf{dS} first.
From Eq.В 2, we have that рқҗқрқҗҸ=рқҗқрқҗҺрқҗ•TрқҗқрқҗҸsuperscriptрқҗқрқҗҺрқҗ•рқ‘Ү\mathbf{dP}=\mathbf{dO}\mathbf{V}^{T}, and so:


 | 
 | dвҖӢPiвҖӢj=dвҖӢoiTвҖӢvj.рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘‘superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—dP_{ij}=do_{i}^{T}v_{j}.
 | 


Recall that Pi:=softmaxвҖӢ(Si:)subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsoftmaxsubscriptрқ‘Ҷ:рқ‘–absentP_{i:}=\mathrm{softmax}(S_{i:}).
Using the fact that the Jacobian of y=softmaxвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ұsoftmaxрқ‘Ҙy=\mathrm{softmax}(x) is diagвҖӢ(y)вҲ’yвҖӢyTdiagрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ү\mathrm{diag}(y)-yy^{T}, we
have that


 | 
 | dвҖӢSi:=(diagвҖӢ(Pi:)вҲ’Pi:вҖӢPi:T)вҖӢdвҖӢPi:=Pi:вҲҳdвҖӢPi:вҲ’(Pi:TвҖӢdвҖӢPi:)вҖӢPi:,рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ҷ:рқ‘–absentdiagsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentрқ‘Үрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentрқ‘Үрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentdS_{i:}=(\mathrm{diag}(P_{i:})-P_{i:}P_{i:}^{T})dP_{i:}=P_{i:}\circ dP_{i:}-(P_{i:}^{T}dP_{i:})P_{i:},
 | 


where вҲҳ\circ denotes pointwise multiplication.


Define


 | 
 | Di=Pi:TвҖӢdвҖӢPi:=вҲ‘jeqiTвҖӢkjLiвҖӢdвҖӢoiTвҖӢvj=dвҖӢoiTвҖӢвҲ‘jeqiвҠӨвҖӢkjLiвҖӢvj=dвҖӢoiTвҖӢoi,subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentрқ‘Үрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsubscriptрқ‘—superscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–рқ‘‘superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—рқ‘‘superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘—superscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–topsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—рқ‘‘superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–D_{i}=P_{i:}^{T}dP_{i:}=\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}do_{i}^{T}v_{j}=do_{i}^{T}\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{\top}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}v_{j}=do_{i}^{T}o_{i},
 | 
 | (4)


then


 | 
 | dвҖӢSi:=Pi:вҲҳdвҖӢPi:вҲ’DiвҖӢPi:.рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ҷ:рқ‘–absentsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentsubscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentdS_{i:}=P_{i:}\circ dP_{i:}-D_{i}P_{i:}.
 | 


Hence


 | 
 | dвҖӢSiвҖӢj=PiвҖӢjвҖӢdвҖӢPiвҖӢjвҲ’DiвҖӢPiвҖӢj=PiвҖӢjвҖӢ(dвҖӢPiвҖӢjвҲ’Di).рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–dS_{ij}=P_{ij}dP_{ij}-D_{i}P_{ij}=P_{ij}(dP_{ij}-D_{i}).
 | 


Now we can get the gradients рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҗ\mathbf{dQ} and рқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗҠ\mathbf{dK}.
Recall that SiвҖӢj=qiTвҖӢkjsubscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—S_{ij}=q_{i}^{T}k_{j}, so


 | 
 | dвҖӢqi=вҲ‘jdвҖӢSiвҖӢjвҖӢkj=вҲ‘jPiвҖӢjвҖӢ(dвҖӢPiвҖӢjвҲ’Di)вҖӢkj=вҲ‘jeqiTвҖӢkjLiвҖӢ(dвҖӢoiTвҖӢvjвҲ’Di)вҖӢkj.рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘—рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘—superscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–рқ‘‘superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—dq_{i}=\sum_{j}dS_{ij}k_{j}=\sum_{j}P_{ij}(dP_{ij}-D_{i})k_{j}=\sum_{j}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}(do_{i}^{T}v_{j}-D_{i})k_{j}.
 | 
 | (5)


Similarly,


 | 
 | dвҖӢkj=вҲ‘idвҖӢSiвҖӢjвҖӢqi=вҲ‘iPiвҖӢjвҖӢ(dвҖӢPiвҖӢjвҲ’Di)вҖӢqi=вҲ‘ieqiTвҖӢkjLiвҖӢ(dвҖӢoiTвҖӢvjвҲ’Di)вҖӢqi.рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘–рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘–superscriptрқ‘’superscriptsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–рқ‘‘superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–рқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–dk_{j}=\sum_{i}dS_{ij}q_{i}=\sum_{i}P_{ij}(dP_{ij}-D_{i})q_{i}=\sum_{i}\frac{e^{q_{i}^{T}k_{j}}}{L_{i}}(do_{i}^{T}v_{j}-D_{i})q_{i}.
 | 
 | (6)


Therefore the backward pass can also be computed with OвҖӢ(n)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ӣO(n) extra memory:


- 
1. 


Compute dвҖӢvjрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—dv_{j} for all jрқ‘—j according to Eq.В 3, which takes
OвҖӢ(d)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘‘O(d) extra memory.


- 
2. 


Compute Disubscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–D_{i} for all iрқ‘–i according to Eq.В 4, which takes OвҖӢ(n)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ӣO(n)
extra memory.


- 
3. 


Compute dвҖӢqiрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–dq_{i} for all iрқ‘–i according to Eq.В 5, which takes
OвҖӢ(d)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘‘O(d) extra memory.


- 
4. 


Compute dвҖӢkjрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘—dk_{j} for all jрқ‘—j according to Eq.В 6, which takes
OвҖӢ(d)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘‘O(d) extra memory.


### 
B.3 FlashAttention: Forward Pass


We describe the full details of FlashAttention forward pass.
Given input sequences рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, we want to
compute the attention output рқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—dрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}:


 | 
 | рқҗ’=П„вҖӢрқҗҗрқҗҠвҠӨвҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗ’masked=maskвҖӢ(S)вҲҲв„қNГ—N,рқҗҸ=softmaxвҖӢ(рқҗ’masked)вҲҲв„қNГ—N,formulae-sequenceрқҗ’рқңҸsuperscriptрқҗҗрқҗҠtopsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғsuperscriptрқҗ’maskedmaskрқ‘Ҷsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒрқҗҸsoftmaxsuperscriptрқҗ’maskedsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\displaystyle\mathbf{S}=\tau\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{S}^{\mathrm{masked}}=\textsc{mask}(S)\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},\quad\mathbf{P}=\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{S}^{\mathrm{masked}})\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N},
 | 


 | 
 | рқҗҸdropped=dropoutвҖӢ(рқҗҸ,pdrop),рқҗҺ=рқҗҸdroppedвҖӢрқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d,formulae-sequencesuperscriptрқҗҸdroppeddropoutрқҗҸsubscriptрқ‘қdropрқҗҺsuperscriptрқҗҸdroppedрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\displaystyle\mathbf{P}^{\mathrm{dropped}}=\mathrm{dropout}(\mathbf{P},p_{\mathrm{drop}}),\quad\mathbf{O}=\mathbf{P}^{\mathrm{dropped}}\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d},
 | 


where П„вҲҲв„қрқңҸв„қ\tau\in\mathbb{R} is some softmax scaling (typically 1d1рқ‘‘\frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}),
mask is some masking function that sets some entries of the input to
вҲ’вҲһ-\infty and keep other entries the same (e.g., key padding mask when sequences
in the batch donвҖҷt have the same lengths and are padded), and
dropoutвҖӢ(x,p)dropoutрқ‘Ҙрқ‘қ\mathrm{dropout}(x,p) applies dropout to xрқ‘Ҙx elementwise (i.e., output x1вҲ’pрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘қ\frac{x}{1-p}
with probability 1вҲ’p1рқ‘қ1-p and output 0 with probability pрқ‘қp for each element xрқ‘Ҙx).


The full algorithm is inВ AlgorithmВ 2.
We save the output рқҗҺрқҗҺ\mathbf{O}, the softmax statistics в„“в„“\ell and mрқ‘ҡm, and the pseudo-random
number generator state в„ӣв„ӣ{\cal R} for the backward pass.


Algorithm 2 FlashAttention Forward Pass


0:В В Matrices рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} in HBM, on-chip SRAM of
size Mрқ‘ҖM, softmax scaling constant П„вҲҲв„қрқңҸв„қ\tau\in\mathbb{R}, masking function
mask, dropout probability pdropsubscriptрқ‘қdropp_{\mathrm{drop}}.


1:В В Initialize the pseudo-random number generator state в„ӣв„ӣ{\cal R} and save to HBM.


2:В В Set block sizes Bc=вҢҲM4вҖӢdвҢү,Br=minвҒЎ(вҢҲM4вҖӢdвҢү,d)formulae-sequencesubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘Җ4рқ‘‘subscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘Җ4рқ‘‘рқ‘‘B_{c}=\left\lceil\frac{M}{4d}\right\rceil,B_{r}=\min\left(\left\lceil\frac{M}{4d}\right\rceil,d\right).


3:В В Initialize рқҗҺ=(0)NГ—dвҲҲв„қNГ—d,в„“=(0)NвҲҲв„қN,m=(вҲ’вҲһ)NвҲҲв„қNformulae-sequenceрқҗҺsubscript0рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘в„“subscript0рқ‘Ғsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҡsubscriptрқ‘Ғsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{O}=(0)_{N\times d}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d},\ell=(0)_{N}\in\mathbb{R}^{N},m=(-\infty)_{N}\in\mathbb{R}^{N} in HBM.


4:В В Divide рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q} into Tr=вҢҲNBrвҢүsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹT_{r}=\left\lceil\frac{N}{B_{r}}\right\rceil blocks рқҗҗ1,вҖҰ,рқҗҗTr
- subscriptрқҗҗ1вҖҰsubscriptрқҗҗsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ\mathbf{Q}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{Q}_{T_{r}} of size BrГ—dsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘‘B_{r}\times d each,
and divide рқҗҠ,рқҗ•
- рқҗҠрқҗ•\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V} in to Tc=вҢҲNBcвҢүsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җT_{c}=\left\lceil\frac{N}{B_{c}}\right\rceil blocks рқҗҠ1,вҖҰ,рқҗҠTc
- subscriptрқҗҠ1вҖҰsubscriptрқҗҠsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җ\mathbf{K}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{K}_{T_{c}} and
рқҗ•1,вҖҰ,рқҗ•Tc
- subscriptрқҗ•1вҖҰsubscriptрқҗ•subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җ\mathbf{V}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{V}_{T_{c}}, of size BcГ—dsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘B_{c}\times d each.


5:В В Divide рқҗҺрқҗҺ\mathbf{O} into Trsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹT_{r} blocks рқҗҺi,вҖҰ,рқҗҺTr
- subscriptрқҗҺрқ‘–вҖҰsubscriptрқҗҺsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ\mathbf{O}_{i},\dots,\mathbf{O}_{T_{r}} of size
BrГ—dsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘‘B_{r}\times d each, divide в„“в„“\ell into Trsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹT_{r} blocks в„“i,вҖҰ,в„“Tr
- subscriptв„“рқ‘–вҖҰsubscriptв„“subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ\ell_{i},\dots,\ell_{T_{r}} of size
Brsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹB_{r} each, divide mрқ‘ҡm into Trsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹT_{r} blocks m1,вҖҰ,mTr
- subscriptрқ‘ҡ1вҖҰsubscriptрқ‘ҡsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹm_{1},\dots,m_{T_{r}} of size Brsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹB_{r} each.


6:В В forВ 1вүӨjвүӨTc1рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җ1\leq j\leq T_{c}В do


7:В В В В В Load рқҗҠj,рқҗ•j
- subscriptрқҗҠрқ‘—subscriptрқҗ•рқ‘—\mathbf{K}_{j},\mathbf{V}_{j} from HBM to on-chip SRAM.


8:В В В В В forВ 1вүӨiвүӨTr1рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ1\leq i\leq T_{r}В do


9:В В В В В В В В Load рқҗҗi,рқҗҺi,в„“i,mi
- subscriptрқҗҗрқ‘–subscriptрқҗҺрқ‘–subscriptв„“рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–\mathbf{Q}_{i},\mathbf{O}_{i},\ell_{i},m_{i} from HBM to on-chip SRAM.


10:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute рқҗ’iвҖӢj=П„вҖӢрқҗҗiвҖӢрқҗҠjTвҲҲв„қBrГ—Bcsubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—рқңҸsubscriptрқҗҗрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқҗҠрқ‘—рқ‘Үsuperscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{S}_{ij}=\tau\mathbf{Q}_{i}\mathbf{K}_{j}^{T}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}}.


11:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute рқҗ’iвҖӢjmasked=maskвҖӢ(рқҗ’iвҖӢj)superscriptsubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—maskedmasksubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—\mathbf{S}_{ij}^{\mathrm{masked}}=\textsc{mask}(\mathbf{S}_{ij}).


12:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute m~iвҖӢj=rowmaxвҖӢ(рқҗ’iвҖӢjmasked)вҲҲв„қBrsubscript~рқ‘ҡрқ‘–рқ‘—rowmaxsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—maskedsuperscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹ\tilde{m}_{ij}=\mathrm{rowmax}(\mathbf{S}_{ij}^{\mathrm{masked}})\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}}, рқҗҸ~iвҖӢj=expвҒЎ(рқҗ’iвҖӢjmaskedвҲ’m~iвҖӢj)вҲҲв„қBrГ—Bcsubscript~рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptsubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—maskedsubscript~рқ‘ҡрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij}=\exp(\mathbf{S}_{ij}^{\mathrm{masked}}-\tilde{m}_{ij})\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}} (pointwise),
в„“~iвҖӢj=rowsumвҖӢ(рқҗҸ~iвҖӢj)вҲҲв„қBrsubscript~в„“рқ‘–рқ‘—rowsumsubscript~рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹ\tilde{\ell}_{ij}=\mathrm{rowsum}(\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij})\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}}.


13:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute minew=maxвҒЎ(mi,m~iвҖӢj)вҲҲв„қBrsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–newsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–subscript~рқ‘ҡрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹm_{i}^{\mathrm{new}}=\max(m_{i},\tilde{m}_{ij})\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}}, в„“inew=emiвҲ’minewвҖӢв„“i+em~iвҖӢjвҲ’minewвҖӢв„“~iвҖӢjвҲҲв„қBrsuperscriptsubscriptв„“рқ‘–new
superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–newsubscriptв„“рқ‘–superscriptрқ‘’subscript~рқ‘ҡрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–newsubscript~в„“рқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹ\ell_{i}^{\mathrm{new}}=e^{m_{i}-m_{i}^{\mathrm{new}}}\ell_{i}+e^{\tilde{m}_{ij}-m_{i}^{\mathrm{new}}}\tilde{\ell}_{ij}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}}.


14:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute рқҗҸ~iвҖӢjdropped=dropoutвҖӢ(рқҗҸ~iвҖӢj,pdrop)superscriptsubscript~рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—droppeddropoutsubscript~рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘қdrop\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij}^{\mathrm{dropped}}=\mathrm{dropout}(\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij},p_{\mathrm{drop}}).


15:В В В В В В В В Write рқҗҺiвҶҗdiagвҖӢ(в„“inew)вҲ’1вҖӢ(diagвҖӢ(в„“i)вҖӢemiвҲ’minewвҖӢрқҗҺi+em~iвҖӢjвҲ’minewвҖӢрқҗҸ~iвҖӢjdroppedвҖӢрқҗ•j)вҶҗsubscriptрқҗҺрқ‘–diagsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptв„“рқ‘–new1
diagsubscriptв„“рқ‘–superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–newsubscriptрқҗҺрқ‘–superscriptрқ‘’subscript~рқ‘ҡрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–newsuperscriptsubscript~рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—droppedsubscriptрқҗ•рқ‘—\mathbf{O}_{i}\leftarrow\mathrm{diag}(\ell_{i}^{\mathrm{new}})^{-1}(\mathrm{diag}(\ell_{i})e^{m_{i}-m_{i}^{\mathrm{new}}}\mathbf{O}_{i}+e^{\tilde{m}_{ij}-m_{i}^{\mathrm{new}}}\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij}^{\mathrm{dropped}}\mathbf{V}_{j})
to HBM.


16:В В В В В В В В Write в„“iвҶҗв„“inewвҶҗsubscriptв„“рқ‘–superscriptsubscriptв„“рқ‘–new\ell_{i}\leftarrow\ell_{i}^{\mathrm{new}}, miвҶҗminewвҶҗsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–newm_{i}\leftarrow m_{i}^{\mathrm{new}} to HBM.


17:В В В В В endВ for


18:В В endВ for


19:В В Return рқҗҺ,в„“,m,в„ӣ
- рқҗҺв„“рқ‘ҡв„ӣ\mathbf{O},\ell,m,{\cal R}.


### 
B.4 FlashAttention: Backward Pass


We describe the full details of FlashAttention backward pass.
Given input sequences рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, the output рқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—dрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d},
and the output gradient рқҗқрқҗҺрқҗқрқҗҺ\mathbf{dO}, we want to
compute the input gradients рқҗқрқҗҗ,рқҗқрқҗҠ,рқҗқрқҗ•вҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}.


We first describe the standard attention backward pass inВ AlgorithmВ 3 for completeness.


Algorithm 3 Standard Attention Backward Pass


0:В В Matrices рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•,рқҗқрқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•рқҗқрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V},\mathbf{dO}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, рқҗҸвҲҲв„қNГ—NрқҗҸsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{P}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} in HBM.


1:В В Load рқҗҸ,рқҗқрқҗҺ
- рқҗҸрқҗқрқҗҺ\mathbf{P},\mathbf{dO} by blocks from HBM, compute
рқҗқрқҗ•=рқҗҸвҠӨвҖӢрқҗқрқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—dрқҗқрқҗ•superscriptрқҗҸtopрқҗқрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{dV}=\mathbf{P}^{\top}\mathbf{dO}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d}, write рқҗқрқҗ•рқҗқрқҗ•\mathbf{dV} to HBM.


2:В В Load рқҗқрқҗҺ,рқҗ•
- рқҗқрқҗҺрқҗ•\mathbf{dO},\mathbf{V} by blocks from HBM, compute
рқҗқрқҗҸ=рқҗқрқҗҺрқҗ•вҠӨвҲҲв„қNГ—NрқҗқрқҗҸsuperscriptрқҗқрқҗҺрқҗ•topsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{dP}=\mathbf{dO}\mathbf{V}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, write рқҗқрқҗҸрқҗқрқҗҸ\mathbf{dP} to HBM.


3:В В Read рқҗҸ,рқҗқрқҗҸ
- рқҗҸрқҗқрқҗҸ\mathbf{P},\mathbf{dP} from HBM, compute рқҗқрқҗ’вҲҲв„қNГ—Nрқҗқрқҗ’superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{dS}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} where
dвҖӢSiвҖӢj=PiвҖӢjвҖӢ(dвҖӢPiвҖӢjвҲ’вҲ‘lPiвҖӢlвҖӢdвҖӢPiвҖӢl)рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘ҷsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘ҷdS_{ij}=P_{ij}(dP_{ij}-\sum_{l}P_{il}dP_{il}), write рқҗқрқҗ’рқҗқрқҗ’\mathbf{dS} to
HBM.


4:В В Load рқҗқрқҗ’рқҗқрқҗ’\mathbf{dS} and рқҗҠрқҗҠ\mathbf{K} by blocks from HBM, compute рқҗқрқҗҗ=рқҗқрқҗ’рқҗҠрқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗ’рқҗҠ\mathbf{dQ}=\mathbf{dS}\mathbf{K},
write рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҗ\mathbf{dQ} to HBM.


5:В В Load рқҗқрқҗ’рқҗқрқҗ’\mathbf{dS} and рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q} by blocks from HBM, compute рқҗқрқҗҠ=рқҗқрқҗ’вҠӨвҖӢрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҠsuperscriptрқҗқрқҗ’topрқҗҗ\mathbf{dK}=\mathbf{dS}^{\top}\mathbf{Q}, write рқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗҠ\mathbf{dK} to
HBM.


6:В В Return рқҗқрқҗҗ,рқҗқрқҗҠ,рқҗқрқҗ•
- рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗ•\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV}.


We now make two observations about FlashAttention backward pass:


- 
1. 


We do not need to store the dropout mask of size OвҖӢ(N2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2O(N^{2}) from the
forward pass.
Instead, we can save the pseudo-random number generator states from
the forward pass and re-generate the dropout mask in the backward pass.
This allows us to only use OвҖӢ(N)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒO(N) extra memory.


- 
2. 


When computing the softmax gradient, we useВ Eq.В 4 to compute
Di=Pi:вҠӨвҖӢdвҖӢPi:subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absenttopрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentD_{i}=P_{i:}^{\top}dP_{i:} without reducing over Pi:subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentP_{i:} and dвҖӢPi:рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘ғ:рқ‘–absentdP_{i:} of size
Nрқ‘ҒN (they might not fit into SRAM).
Instead we can rewrite Di=dвҖӢoiвҠӨвҖӢoisubscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘‘superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–topsubscriptрқ‘ңрқ‘–D_{i}=do_{i}^{\top}o_{i} and compute the dot product between
vectors of size dрқ‘‘d.


The full FlashAttention backward pass algorithm is inВ AlgorithmВ 4.
Conceptually it is just a block version of the derivation
inВ SectionВ B.2.


Algorithm 4 FlashAttention Backward Pass


0:В В Matrices рқҗҗ,рқҗҠ,рқҗ•,рқҗҺ,рқҗқрқҗҺвҲҲв„қNГ—d
- рқҗҗрқҗҠрқҗ•рқҗҺрқҗқрқҗҺsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V},\mathbf{O},\mathbf{dO}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} in HBM,
vectors в„“,mвҲҲв„қN
- в„“рқ‘ҡsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\ell,m\in\mathbb{R}^{N} in HBM, on-chip SRAM of
size Mрқ‘ҖM, softmax scaling constant П„вҲҲв„қрқңҸв„қ\tau\in\mathbb{R}, masking function
mask, dropout probability pdropsubscriptрқ‘қdropp_{\mathrm{drop}}, pseudo-random number
generator state в„ӣв„ӣ{\cal R} from the forward pass.


1:В В Set the pseudo-random number generator state to в„ӣв„ӣ{\cal R}.

5:В В Initialize рқҗқрқҗҗ=(0)NГ—dрқҗқрқҗҗsubscript0рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{dQ}=(0)_{N\times d} in HBM and divide it into Trsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹT_{r} blocks рқҗқрқҗҗ1,вҖҰ,рқҗқрқҗҗTr
- subscriptрқҗқрқҗҗ1вҖҰsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҗsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹ\mathbf{dQ}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{dQ}_{T_{r}} of size BrГ—dsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘‘B_{r}\times d each.
Initialize рқҗқрқҗҠ=(0)NГ—d,рқҗқрқҗ•=(0)NГ—dformulae-sequenceрқҗқрқҗҠsubscript0рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘рқҗқрқҗ•subscript0рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘\mathbf{dK}=(0)_{N\times d},\mathbf{dV}=(0)_{N\times d} in HBM and divide рқҗқрқҗҠ,рқҗқрқҗ•
- рқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗ•\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV} in to Tcsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җT_{c} blocks рқҗқрқҗҠ1,вҖҰ,рқҗқрқҗҠTc
- subscriptрқҗқрқҗҠ1вҖҰsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҠsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җ\mathbf{dK}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{dK}_{T_{c}} and
рқҗқрқҗ•1,вҖҰ,рқҗқрқҗ•Tc
- subscriptрқҗқрқҗ•1вҖҰsubscriptрқҗқрқҗ•subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җ\mathbf{dV}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{dV}_{T_{c}}, of size BcГ—dsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘B_{c}\times d each.


6:В В forВ 1вүӨjвүӨTc1рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җ1\leq j\leq T_{c}В do

12:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute рқҗ’iвҖӢjmasked=maskвҖӢ(рқҗ’iвҖӢj)superscriptsubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—maskedmasksubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—\mathbf{S}_{ij}^{\mathrm{masked}}=\textsc{mask}(\mathbf{S}_{ij}).


13:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute рқҗҸiвҖӢj=diagвҖӢ(li)вҲ’1вҖӢexpвҒЎ(рқҗ’iвҖӢjmaskedвҲ’mi)вҲҲв„қBrГ—BcsubscriptрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—diagsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘–1superscriptsubscriptрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—maskedsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘–superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{P}_{ij}=\mathrm{diag}(l_{i})^{-1}\exp(\mathbf{S}_{ij}^{\mathrm{masked}}-m_{i})\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}}.


14:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute dropout mask рқҗҷiвҖӢjвҲҲв„қBrГ—Bcsubscriptрқҗҷрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{Z}_{ij}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}} where
each entry has value 11вҲ’pdrop11subscriptрқ‘қdrop\frac{1}{1-p_{\mathrm{drop}}} with probability
1вҲ’pdrop1subscriptрқ‘қdrop1-p_{\mathrm{drop}} and value 0 with probability pdropsubscriptрқ‘қdropp_{\mathrm{drop}}.


15:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute
рқҗҸiвҖӢjdropped=рқҗҸiвҖӢjвҲҳрқҗҷiвҖӢjsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—droppedsubscriptрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗҷрқ‘–рқ‘—\mathbf{P}_{ij}^{\mathrm{dropped}}=\mathbf{P}_{ij}\circ\mathbf{Z}_{ij} (pointwise multiply).


16:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute
рқҗқрқҗ•j~вҶҗрқҗқрқҗ•j~+(рқҗҸiвҖӢjdropped)вҠӨвҖӢрқҗқрқҗҺiвҲҲв„қBcГ—dвҶҗ~subscriptрқҗқрқҗ•рқ‘—
~subscriptрқҗқрқҗ•рқ‘—superscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—droppedtopsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҺрқ‘–superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘\tilde{\mathbf{dV}_{j}}\leftarrow\tilde{\mathbf{dV}_{j}}+(\mathbf{P}_{ij}^{\mathrm{dropped}})^{\top}\mathbf{dO}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{c}\times d}.


17:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute
рқҗқрқҗҸiвҖӢjdropped=рқҗқрқҗҺiвҖӢрқҗ•jвҠӨвҲҲв„қBrГ—BcsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—droppedsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҺрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқҗ•рқ‘—topsuperscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{dP}_{ij}^{\mathrm{dropped}}=\mathbf{dO}_{i}\mathbf{V}_{j}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}}.


18:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute
рқҗқрқҗҸiвҖӢj=рқҗқрқҗҸiвҖӢjdroppedвҲҳрқҗҷiвҖӢjsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—droppedsubscriptрқҗҷрқ‘–рқ‘—\mathbf{dP}_{ij}=\mathbf{dP}_{ij}^{\mathrm{dropped}}\circ\mathbf{Z}_{ij} (pointwise multiply).


19:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute Di=rowsumвҖӢ(рқҗқрқҗҺiвҲҳрқҗҺi)вҲҲв„қBrsubscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–rowsumsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҺрқ‘–subscriptрқҗҺрқ‘–superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹD_{i}=\mathrm{rowsum}(\mathbf{dO}_{i}\circ\mathbf{O}_{i})\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}}.


20:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute рқҗқрқҗ’iвҖӢj=рқҗҸiвҖӢjвҲҳ(рқҗқрқҗҸiвҖӢjвҲ’Di)вҲҲв„қBrГ—Bcsubscriptрқҗқрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗқрқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘–superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{dS}_{ij}=\mathbf{P}_{ij}\circ(\mathbf{dP}_{ij}-D_{i})\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}}.


21:В В В В В В В В Write
рқҗқрқҗҗiвҶҗрқҗқрқҗҗi+П„вҖӢрқҗқрқҗ’iвҖӢjвҖӢрқҗҠjвҲҲв„қBrГ—dвҶҗsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҗрқ‘–
subscriptрқҗқрқҗҗрқ‘–рқңҸsubscriptрқҗқрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗҠрқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘‘\mathbf{dQ}_{i}\leftarrow\mathbf{dQ}_{i}+\tau\mathbf{dS}_{ij}\mathbf{K}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times d} to HBM.


22:В В В В В В В В On chip, compute рқҗқрқҗҠ~jвҶҗрқҗқрқҗҠ~j+П„вҖӢрқҗқрқҗ’iвҖӢjвҠӨвҖӢрқҗҗiвҲҲв„қBcГ—dвҶҗsubscript~рқҗқрқҗҠрқ‘—
subscript~рқҗқрқҗҠрқ‘—рқңҸsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗқрқҗ’рқ‘–рқ‘—topsubscriptрқҗҗрқ‘–superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘\tilde{\mathbf{dK}}_{j}\leftarrow\tilde{\mathbf{dK}}_{j}+\tau\mathbf{dS}_{ij}^{\top}\mathbf{Q}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{c}\times d}.


23:В В В В В endВ for


24:В В В В В Write рқҗқрқҗҠjвҶҗрқҗқрқҗҠj~,рқҗқрқҗ•jвҶҗрқҗқрқҗ•j~formulae-sequenceвҶҗsubscriptрқҗқрқҗҠрқ‘—~subscriptрқҗқрқҗҠрқ‘—вҶҗsubscriptрқҗқрқҗ•рқ‘—~subscriptрқҗқрқҗ•рқ‘—\mathbf{dK}_{j}\leftarrow\tilde{\mathbf{dK}_{j}},\mathbf{dV}_{j}\leftarrow\tilde{\mathbf{dV}_{j}} to HBM.


25:В В endВ for


26:В В Return рқҗқрқҗҗ,рқҗқрқҗҠ,рқҗқрқҗ•
- рқҗқрқҗҗрқҗқрқҗҠрқҗқрқҗ•\mathbf{dQ},\mathbf{dK},\mathbf{dV}.


We see that similar to the forward pass, the backward pass performs OвҖӢ(N2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2O(N^{2})
FLOPs and only requires OвҖӢ(N)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒO(N) extra memory beyond inputs, output, output
gradient, and input gradients.


We analyze the IO-complexity of the backward pass, similar to the forward pass (TheoremВ 2).


###### 
Theorem 5.


Let Nрқ‘ҒN be the sequence length, dрқ‘‘d be the head dimension, and Mрқ‘ҖM be size of
SRAM with dвүӨMвүӨNвҖӢdрқ‘‘рқ‘Җрқ‘Ғрқ‘‘d\leq M\leq Nd.
Standard attention (AlgorithmВ ) backward pass requires ОҳвҖӢ(NвҖӢd+N2)Оҳ
рқ‘Ғрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Ғ2\Theta(Nd+N^{2}) HBM
accesses, while FlashAttention backward pass (AlgorithmВ 4) requires
ОҳвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd2вҖӢMвҲ’1)Оҳsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘‘2superscriptрқ‘Җ1\Theta(N^{2}d^{2}M^{-1}) HBM accesses.


The proof is inВ AppendixВ C.


### 
B.5 Comparison with Rabe and Staats [66]


We describe here some similarities and differences between our FlashAttention algorithm and the algorithm of Rabe and Staats [66].


Conceptually, both FlashAttention and Rabe and Staats [66] operate on blocks of the
attention matrix using the well-established technique of tiling (or softmax
scaling)В [60, 51].
To reduce the memory footprint, both methods avoid storing the large attention
matrix in the forward pass and recompute it in the backward pass.


The first major difference is that Rabe and Staats [66] focuses on the reducing
the total memory footprint (maximum amount of GPU memory required) while
FlashAttention focuses on reducing memory accesses (the number of memory
reads/writes).
As mentioned inВ SectionВ 2, the amount of memory access is the
primary determining factor of runtime.
Reducing memory accesses also necessarily reduces the total amount of memory
required (e.g., if an operation incurs AрқҗҙA memory accesses, then its total
memory requirement is at most AрқҗҙA).
As a result, FlashAttention is faster than standard attention (2-4Г—\times) while
Rabe and Staats [66] is around the same speed or slightly slower than standard
attention.
In terms of total memory required, both methods offer substantial memory saving.


The second difference between the two methods is the way information is summarized
from each block to pass to the next block.
Rabe and Staats [66] summarizes each block with its temporary output along with the
softmax normalization statistics.
At the end of the forward pass, the temporary outputs of all the blocks are combined using
the statistics to produce the final output.
FlashAttention instead incrementally updates the output (AlgorithmВ 1 line
12) after processing each block, so only one copy
of the output is needed (instead of KрқҗҫK copies for KрқҗҫK blocks).
This means that FlashAttention has smaller total memory requirement compared to Rabe and Staats [66].


The final major difference is the way the backward pass is computed.
Rabe and Staats [66] uses gradient checkpointing to recompute the attention
matrix and the temporary output of each block.
FlashAttention instead simplifies the backward pass analytically (SectionsВ B.2 andВ B.4).
It only recomputes the attention matrix and does not recompute the
temporary output of each block.
This reduces the memory requirement for the backward pass and yields speedup.


## 
Appendix C Proofs


###### Proof of TheoremВ 1.


We first count the number of FLOPs and extra memory required.


The dominating FLOPs are from matrix multiplication.
In the inner loop, (AlgorithmВ 1 line 9), we
compute рқҗҗiвҖӢрқҗҠjвҠӨвҲҲв„қBrГ—Bcsubscriptрқҗҗрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқҗҠрқ‘—topsuperscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\mathbf{Q}_{i}\mathbf{K}_{j}^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}} for рқҗҗiвҲҲв„қBrГ—dsubscriptрқҗҗрқ‘–superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘‘\mathbf{Q}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times d} and
рқҗҠjвҲҲв„қBcГ—dsubscriptрқҗҠрқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘\mathbf{K}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{c}\times d}, which takes OвҖӢ(BrвҖӢBcвҖӢd)рқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘O(B_{r}B_{c}d) FLOPs.
We also compute (AlgorithmВ 1 line 12) рқҗҸ~iвҖӢjвҖӢрқҗ•jвҲҲв„қBrГ—dsubscript~рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptрқҗ•рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹрқ‘‘\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij}\mathbf{V}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times d} for
рқҗҸ~iвҖӢjвҲҲв„қBrГ—Bcsubscript~рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җ\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_{ij}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{r}\times B_{c}} and рқҗ•jвҲҲв„қBcГ—dsubscriptрқҗ•рқ‘—superscriptв„қsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘\mathbf{V}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{B_{c}\times d}, which takes OвҖӢ(BrвҖӢBcвҖӢd)рқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘O(B_{r}B_{c}d) FLOPs.
We execute the inner loops
TcвҖӢTr=вҢҲNBcвҢүвҖӢвҢҲNBrвҢүsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җsubscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹT_{c}T_{r}=\left\lceil\frac{N}{B_{c}}\right\rceil\left\lceil\frac{N}{B_{r}}\right\rceil times.
Therefore the total number of FLOPs is

 | OвҖӢ(N2BcвҖӢBrвҖӢBrвҖӢBcвҖӢd)=OвҖӢ(N2вҖӢd).рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2subscriptрқҗөрқ‘җsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқҗөрқ‘җрқ‘‘рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2рқ‘‘O\left(\frac{N^{2}}{B_{c}B_{r}}B_{r}B_{c}d\right)=O(N^{2}d).
 | 


In terms of extra memory required, we see that we need OвҖӢ(N)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒO(N) memory to store
the statistics (в„“,m)в„“рқ‘ҡ(\ell,m).


We now prove the algorithmвҖҷs correctness by induction on jрқ‘—j for
0вүӨjвүӨTc0рқ‘—subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘җ0\leq j\leq T_{c}.

We then see that the claim is also true for j+1
