 | 
 | (2)


where the discrete-time parameters are each a function, specified by the discretization method, of the continuous-time parameters. See Iserles (2009) for more information on discretization methods.


### 
2.2 Parallelizing Linear State Space Models With Scans


We use parallel scans to efficiently compute the states of a discretized linear SSM. Given a binary associative operator вҲҷвҲҷ\bullet (i.e. (aвҲҷb)вҲҷc=aвҲҷ(bвҲҷc)вҲҷвҲҷрқ‘Һрқ‘Ҹрқ‘җвҲҷрқ‘ҺвҲҷрқ‘Ҹрқ‘җ(a\bullet b)\bullet c=a\bullet(b\bullet c)) and a sequence of LрқҗҝL elements [a1,a2,вҖҰ,aL]
- subscriptрқ‘Һ1subscriptрқ‘Һ2вҖҰsubscriptрқ‘Һрқҗҝ[a_{1},a_{2},...,a_{L}], the scan operation (sometimes referred to as all-prefix-sum) returns the sequence


 | 
 | [a1,(a1вҲҷa2),вҖҰ,(a1вҲҷa2вҲҷвҖҰвҲҷaL)].
- subscriptрқ‘Һ1вҲҷsubscriptрқ‘Һ1subscriptрқ‘Һ2вҖҰвҲҷsubscriptрқ‘Һ1subscriptрқ‘Һ2вҖҰsubscriptрқ‘Һрқҗҝ\displaystyle[a_{1},\ (a_{1}\bullet a_{2}),\ ...,\ (a_{1}\bullet a_{2}\bullet...\bullet a_{L})].
 | 
 | (3)


Computing a length LрқҗҝL linear recurrence of a discretized SSM, рқҗұk=рқҗҖВҜвҖӢрқҗұkвҲ’1+рқҗҒВҜвҖӢрқҗ®ksubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ
ВҜрқҗҖsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ1ВҜрқҗҒsubscriptрқҗ®рқ‘ҳ\mathbf{x}_{k}=\overline{\mathbf{A}}\mathbf{x}_{k-1}+\overline{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{u}_{k} as in (2), is a specific example of a scan operation. As discussed in Section 1.4 of Blelloch (1990), parallelizing the linear recurrence of the latent transitions in the discretized SSM above can be computed in a parallel time of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(TвҠҷвҖӢlogвҒЎL)рқ’Әsubscriptрқ‘Үdirect-productрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(T_{\odot}\log L), assuming LрқҗҝL processors, where TвҠҷsubscriptрқ‘Үdirect-productT_{\odot} represents the cost of matrix-matrix multiplication. For a general matrix рқҗҖВҜвҲҲв„қPГ—PВҜрқҗҖsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ғрқ‘ғ\overline{\mathbf{A}}\in\mathbb{R}^{P\times P}, TвҠҷsubscriptрқ‘Үdirect-productT_{\odot} is рқ’ӘвҖӢ(P3)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘ғ3\mathcal{O}(P^{3}). This can be prohibitively expensive in deep learning settings. However, if рқҗҖВҜВҜрқҗҖ\overline{\mathbf{A}} is a diagonal matrix, the parallel time becomes рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢlogвҒЎL)рқ’Әрқ‘ғрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(P\log L) with LрқҗҝL processors and only requires рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢL)рқ’Әрқ‘ғрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(PL) space. Finally, we note that efficient parallel scans are implemented in a work-efficient manner, thus the total computational cost of the parallel scan with a diagonal matrix is рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢL)рқ’Әрқ‘ғрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(PL) operations. See Appendix H for more information on parallel scans.


### 
2.3 S4: Structured State Space Sequence Layers


The S4 layer (Gu etВ al., 2021a) defines a nonlinear sequence-to-sequence transformation, mapping from an input sequence рқҗ®1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—Hsubscriptрқҗ®:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{u}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H} to an output sequence рқҗ®1:LвҖІвҲҲв„қLГ—Hsubscriptsuperscriptрқҗ®вҖІ:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{u}^{\prime}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}. An S4 layer contains a bank of Hрқҗ»H independent single-input, single-output (SISO) SSMs with Nрқ‘ҒN-dimensional states. Each S4 SSM is applied to one dimension of the input sequence. This results in an independent linear transformation from each input channel to each preactivation channel. A nonlinear activation function is then applied to the preactivations. Finally, a position-wise linear mixing layer is applied to combine the independent features and produce the output sequence рқҗ®1:LвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptрқҗ®вҖІ:1рқҗҝ\mathbf{u}^{\prime}_{1:L}. Figure 4(a) in the appendix illustrates the view of the S4 layer as a bank of independent SSMs. Figure 2(a) shows an alternative view of S4 as one large SSM with state size HвҖӢNрқҗ»рқ‘ҒHN and block-diagonal state, input and output matrices.


Each S4 SSM leverages the HiPPO framework for online function approximationВ (Gu etВ al., 2020a) by initializing the state matrices with a HiPPO matrix (most often the HiPPO-LegS matrix). This was demonstrated empirically to lead to strong performanceВ (Gu etВ al., 2021b; a), and can be shown as approximating long-range dependencies with respect to an infinitely long, exponentially-decaying measureВ (Gu etВ al., 2023). While the HiPPO-LegS matrix is not stably diagonalizableВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a), it can be represented as a normal plus low-rank (NPLR) matrix. The normal component, referred to as HiPPO-N and denoted рқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormal\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}, can be diagonalized. Thus, the HiPPO-LegS can be conjugated into a diagonal plus low-rank (DPLR) form, which S4 then utilizes to derive an efficient form of the convolution kernel. This motivates S4вҖҷs DPLR parameterization.


Efficiently applying the S4 layer requires two separate implementations depending on context: a recurrent mode and a convolution mode. For online generation, the SSM is iterated recurrently, much like other RNNs. However, when the entire sequence is available and the observations are evenly spaced, a more efficient convolution mode is used. This takes advantage of the ability to represent the linear recurrence as a one-dimensional convolution between the inputs and a convolution kernel for each of the SSMs. Fast Fourier transforms (FFTs) can then be applied to efficiently parallelize this application. Figure 4(a) in the appendix illustrates the convolution approach of the S4 layer for offline processing. We note that while parallel scans could, in principle, allow a recurrent approach to be used in offline scenarios, applying the parallel scan to all Hрқҗ»H of the Nрқ‘ҒN-dimensional SSMs would in general be much more expensive than the convolution approach S4 actually uses.


The trainable parameters of each S4 layer are the Hрқҗ»H independent copies of the learnable SSM parameters and the рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H2)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқҗ»2\mathcal{O}(H^{2}) parameters of the mixing layer. For each of the hвҲҲ{1,вҖҰ,H}в„Һ1вҖҰрқҗ»h\in\{1,...,H\} S4 SSMs, given a scalar input signal u(h)вҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„Һрқ‘Ўв„қu^{(h)}(t)\in\mathbb{R}, an S4 SSM uses an input matrix рқҗҒ(h)вҲҲв„ӮNГ—1superscriptрқҗҒв„Һsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғ1\mathbf{B}^{(h)}\in\mathbb{C}^{N\times 1}, a DPLR parameterized transition matrix рқҗҖ(h)вҲҲв„ӮNГ—NsuperscriptрқҗҖв„Һsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}^{(h)}\in\mathbb{C}^{N\times N}, an output matrix рқҗӮ(h)вҲҲв„Ӯ1Г—NsuperscriptрқҗӮв„Һsuperscriptв„Ӯ1рқ‘Ғ\mathbf{C}^{(h)}\in\mathbb{C}^{1\times N}, and feedthrough matrix рқҗғ(h)вҲҲв„қ1Г—1superscriptрқҗғв„Һsuperscriptв„қ11\mathbf{D}^{(h)}\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times 1}, to produce a signal y(h)вҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қsuperscriptрқ‘Ұв„Һрқ‘Ўв„қy^{(h)}(t)\in\mathbb{R}. To apply the S4 SSMs to discrete sequences, each continuous-time SSM is discretized using a constant timescale parameter О”(h)вҲҲв„қ+superscriptО”в„Һsubscriptв„қ
\Delta^{(h)}\in\mathbb{R}_{+}. The learnable parameters of each SSM are the timescale parameter О”(h)вҲҲв„қ+superscriptО”в„Һsubscriptв„қ
\Delta^{(h)}\in\mathbb{R}_{+}, the continuous-time parameters рқҗҒ(h)superscriptрқҗҒв„Һ\mathbf{B}^{(h)}, рқҗӮ(h)superscriptрқҗӮв„Һ\mathbf{C}^{(h)}, рқҗғ(h)superscriptрқҗғв„Һ\mathbf{D}^{(h)}, and the DPLR matrix, parameterized by vectors рқҡІ(h)вҲҲв„ӮNsuperscriptрқҡІв„Һsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{\Lambda}^{(h)}\in\mathbb{C}^{N} and рқҗ©(h),рқҗӘ(h)вҲҲв„ӮN
- superscriptрқҗ©в„ҺsuperscriptрқҗӘв„Һsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{p}^{(h)},\mathbf{q}^{(h)}\in\mathbb{C}^{N} representing the diagonal matrix and low-rank terms respectively. For notational compactness we denote the concatenation of the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSM states at discrete time index kрқ‘ҳk as рқҗұk(1:H)=[(рқҗұk(1))вҠӨ,вҖҰ,(рқҗұk(H))вҠӨ]вҠӨsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ:1рқҗ»superscript
- superscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ1topвҖҰsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳрқҗ»toptop\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(1:H)}=\big{[}(\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(1)})^{\top},\ldots,(\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(H)})^{\top}\big{]}^{\top}, and the Hрқҗ»H SSM outputs as рқҗІk=[рқҗІk(1),вҖҰ,рқҗІk(H)]вҠӨsubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳsuperscript
- superscriptsubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳ1вҖҰsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳрқҗ»top\mathbf{y}_{k}=\big{[}\mathbf{y}_{k}^{(1)},\ldots,\mathbf{y}_{k}^{(H)}\big{]}^{\top}.


(a) Internal structure of a single S4 layerВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a) when viewed as a block-diagonal system.


(b) Internal structure of a single S5 layer. 


Figure 2: Schematic of the internal structure of a discretized S4 layerВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a) (top) and S5 layer (bottom). Note рқҗғрқҗғ\mathbf{D} is omitted for simplicity. We view an S4 layer as a single block-diagonal SSM with a latent state of size HвҖӢNрқҗ»рқ‘ҒHN, followed by a nonlinearity and mixing layer to mix the independent features. (b) In contrast, the S5 layer uses a dense, MIMO linear SSM with latent size PвүӘHвҖӢNmuch-less-thanрқ‘ғрқҗ»рқ‘ҒP\ll HN. 


## 
3 The S5 Layer


In this section we present the S5 layer. We describe its structure, parameterization and computation, particularly focusing on how each of these differ from S4.


### 
3.1 S5 Structure: From SISO to MIMO


The S5 layer replaces the bank of SISO SSMs (or large block-diagonal system) in S4 with a multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) SSM, as in (1), with a latent state size Pрқ‘ғP, and input and output dimension Hрқҗ»H. The discretized version of this MIMO SSM can be applied to a vector-valued input sequence рқҗ®1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—Hsubscriptрқҗ®:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{u}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}, to produce a vector-valued sequence of SSM outputs (or preactivations) рқҗІ1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—HsubscriptрқҗІ:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{y}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}, using latent states рқҗұkвҲҲв„қPsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ғ\mathbf{x}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{P}. A nonlinear activation function is then applied to produce a sequence of layer outputs рқҗ®1:LвҖІвҲҲв„қLГ—Hsubscriptsuperscriptрқҗ®вҖІ:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{u}^{\prime}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}. See Figure 2(b) for an illustration. Unlike S4, we do not require an additional position-wise linear layer, since these features are already mixed. We note here that compared to the HвҖӢNрқҗ»рқ‘ҒHN latent size of the block-diagonal SSM in the S4 layer, S5вҖҷs latent size Pрқ‘ғP can be significantly smaller, allowing for the use of efficient parallel scans, as we discuss in Section 3.3.


### 
3.2 S5 Parameterization: Diagonalized Dynamics


The parameterization of the S5 layerвҖҷs MIMO SSM is motivated by the desire to use efficient parallel scans. As discussed in Section 2.2, a diagonal state matrix is required to efficiently compute the linear recurrence using a parallel scan. Thus, we diagonalize the system, writing the continuous-time state matrix as рқҗҖ=рқҗ•вҖӢрқҡІвҖӢрқҗ•вҲ’1рқҗҖрқҗ•рқҡІsuperscriptрқҗ•1\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{-1}, where рқҡІвҲҲв„ӮPГ—PрқҡІsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘ғрқ‘ғ\mathbf{\Lambda}\in\mathbb{C}^{P\times P} denotes the diagonal matrix containing the eigenvalues and рқҗ•вҲҲв„ӮPГ—Pрқҗ•superscriptв„Ӯрқ‘ғрқ‘ғ\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{C}^{P\times P} corresponds to the eigenvectors. Therefore, we can diagonalize the continuous-time latent dynamics from (1) as


 | 
 | dвҖӢрқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=рқҡІвҖӢрқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)+рқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒрқҗ®вҖӢ(t).dsuperscriptрқҗ•1рқҗұрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў
рқҡІsuperscriptрқҗ•1рқҗұрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқҗ•1рқҗҒрқҗ®рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{x}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{x}(t)+\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t).
 | 
 | (4)


Defining рқҗұ~вҖӢ(t)=рқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)~рқҗұрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқҗ•1рқҗұрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathbf{x}}(t)=\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{x}(t), рқҗҒ~=рқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ~рқҗҒsuperscriptрқҗ•1рқҗҒ\tilde{\mathbf{B}}=\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{B}, and рқҗӮ~=рқҗӮрқҗ•~рқҗӮрқҗӮрқҗ•\tilde{\mathbf{C}}=\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V} gives a reparameterized system,


 | 
 | dвҖӢрқҗұ~вҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=рқҡІвҖӢрқҗұ~вҖӢ(t)+рқҗҒ~вҖӢрқҗ®вҖӢ(t),рқҗІвҖӢ(t)d~рқҗұрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў
- 
рқҡІ~рқҗұрқ‘Ў~рқҗҒрқҗ®рқ‘ЎрқҗІрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\mathbf{\Lambda}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}(t)+\tilde{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{u}(t),\quad\quad\mathbf{y}(t)
 | =рқҗӮ~вҖӢрқҗұ~вҖӢ(t)+рқҗғрқҗ®вҖӢ(t).absent
~рқҗӮ~рқҗұрқ‘Ўрқҗғрқҗ®рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\tilde{\mathbf{C}}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}(t)+\mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t).
 | 
 | (5)


This is a linear SSM with a diagonal state matrix. This diagonalized system can be discretized with a timescale parameter О”вҲҲв„қ+О”subscriptв„қ
\Delta\in\mathbb{R}_{+} using the ZOH method to give another diagonalized system with parameters


 | 
 | рқҡІВҜ=eрқҡІвҖӢО”,рқҗҒВҜ=рқҡІвҲ’1вҖӢ(рқҡІВҜвҲ’рқҗҲ)вҖӢрқҗҒ~,рқҗӮВҜ=рқҗӮ~,рқҗғВҜ=рқҗғ.formulae-sequenceВҜрқҡІsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҡІО”formulae-sequenceВҜрқҗҒsuperscriptрқҡІ1ВҜрқҡІрқҗҲ~рқҗҒformulae-sequenceВҜрқҗӮ~рқҗӮВҜрқҗғрқҗғ\displaystyle\overline{\mathbf{\Lambda}}=e^{\mathbf{\Lambda}\Delta},\quad\overline{\mathbf{B}}=\mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1}(\overline{\mathbf{\Lambda}}-\mathbf{I})\tilde{\mathbf{B}},\quad\overline{\mathbf{C}}=\tilde{\mathbf{C}},\quad\overline{\mathbf{D}}=\mathbf{D}.
 | 
 | (6)


In practice, we use a vector of learnable timescale parameters рқҡ«вҲҲв„қPрқҡ«superscriptв„қрқ‘ғ\mathbf{\Delta}\in\mathbb{R}^{P} (see Section 4.3) and restrict the feedthrough matrix рқҗғрқҗғ\mathbf{D} to be diagonal. The S5 layer therefore has the learnable parameters: рқҗҒ~вҲҲв„ӮPГ—H~рқҗҒsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘ғрқҗ»\tilde{\mathbf{B}}\in\mathbb{C}^{P\times H}, рқҗӮ~вҲҲв„ӮHГ—P~рқҗӮsuperscriptв„Ӯрқҗ»рқ‘ғ\tilde{\mathbf{C}}\in\mathbb{C}^{H\times P}, diagвҖӢ(рқҗғ)вҲҲв„қHdiagрқҗғsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»\mathrm{diag}(\mathbf{D})\in\mathbb{R}^{H}, diagвҖӢ(рқҡІ)вҲҲв„ӮPdiagрқҡІsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘ғ\mathrm{diag}(\mathbf{\Lambda})\in\mathbb{C}^{P}, and рқҡ«вҲҲв„қPрқҡ«superscriptв„қрқ‘ғ\mathbf{\Delta}\in\mathbb{R}^{P}.


##### Initialization


Prior work showed that the performance of deep state space models are sensitive to the initialization of the state matrixВ (Gu etВ al., 2021b; a). We discussed in Section 2.2 that state matrices must be diagonal for efficient application of parallel scans. We also discussed in Section 2.3 that the HiPPO-LegS matrix cannot be diagonalized stably, but that the HiPPO-N matrix can be. In Section 4 we connect the dynamics of S5 to S4 to suggest why initializing with HiPPO-like matrices may also work well in the MIMO setting. We support this empirically, finding that diagonalizing the HiPPO-N matrix leads to good performance, and perform ablations in Appendix E to compare to other initializations. We note that DSSВ (Gupta etВ al., 2022) and S4DВ (Gu etВ al., 2022) layers also found strong performance in the SISO setting by using a diagonalization of the HiPPO-N matrix.


##### Conjugate Symmetry


The complex eigenvalues of a diagonalizable matrix with real entries always occur in conjugate pairs. We enforce this conjugate symmetry by using half the number of eigenvalues and latent states. This ensures real outputs and reduces the runtime and memory usage of the parallel scan by a factor of two. This idea is also discussed in Gu etВ al. (2022).


### 
3.3 S5 Computation: Fully Recurrent


Compared to the large HвҖӢNрқҗ»рқ‘ҒHN effective latent size of the block-diagonal S4 layer, the smaller latent dimension of the S5 layer (Pрқ‘ғP) allows the use of efficient parallel scans when the entire sequence is available. The S5 layer can therefore be efficiently used as a recurrence in the time domain for both online generation and offline processing. Parallel scans and the continuous-time parameterization also allow for efficient handling of irregularly sampled time series and other time-varying SSMs, by simply supplying a different рқҗҖВҜksubscriptВҜрқҗҖрқ‘ҳ\overline{\mathbf{A}}_{k} matrix at each step. We leverage this feature and apply S5 to irregularly sampled data in Section 6.3. In contrast, the convolution of the S4 layer requires a time invariant system and regularly spaced observations.


### 
3.4 Matching the Computational Efficiency of S4 and S5


A key design desiderata for S5 was matching the computational complexity of S4 for both online generation and offline recurrence. The following proposition guarantees that their complexities are of the same order if S5вҖҷs latent size P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H).


###### 
Proposition 1.


Given an S4 layer with Hрқҗ»H input/output features, an S5 layer with Hрқҗ»H input/output features and a latent size P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H) has the same order of magnitude complexity as an S4 layer in terms of both runtime and memory usage.


###### Proof.


See Appendix C.1.
вҲҺ


We also support this proposition with empirical comparisons in Appendix C.2.


## 
4 Relationship Between S4 and S5


We now establish a relationship between the dynamics of S5 and S4. In Section 4.1 we show that, under certain conditions, the outputs of the S5 SSM can be interpreted as a projection of the latent states computed by a particular S4 system. This interpretation motivates using HiPPO initializations for S5, which we discuss in more detail in Section 4.2. In Section 4.3 we discuss how the conditions required to relate the dynamics further motivate initialization and parameterization choices.


### 
4.1 Different Output Projections of Equivalent Dynamics


We compare the dynamics of S4 and S5 under some simplifying assumptions:


###### 
Assumption 1.


We consider only Hрқҗ»H-dimensional to Hрқҗ»H-dimensional sequence maps.


###### 
Assumption 2.


We assume the state matrix of each S4 SSM is identical, рқҗҖ(h)=рқҗҖвҲҲв„ӮNГ—NsuperscriptрқҗҖв„ҺрқҗҖsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}^{(h)}=\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{C}^{N\times N}.


###### 
Assumption 3.


We assume the timescales of each S4 SSM are identical, О”(h)=О”вҲҲв„қ+superscriptО”в„ҺО”subscriptв„қ
\Delta^{(h)}=\Delta\in\mathbb{R}_{+}


###### 
Assumption 4.


We assume that the same state matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} is used in S5 as in S4 (also cf. Assumption 2). Note this also specifies the S5 latent size P=Nрқ‘ғрқ‘ҒP=N. We also assume the S5 input matrix is the horizontal concatenation of the column input vectors used by S4: рқҗҒвүң[рқҗҒ(1)вҖӢвҲЈвҖҰвҲЈвҖӢрқҗҒ(H)]вүңрқҗҒdelimited-[]superscriptрқҗҒ1delimited-вҲЈвҲЈвҖҰsuperscriptрқҗҒрқҗ»\mathbf{B}\triangleq\left[\mathbf{B}^{(1)}\mid\ldots\mid\mathbf{B}^{(H)}\right].


We will discuss relaxing these assumptions shortly, but under these conditions it is straightforward to derive a relationship between the dynamics of S4 and S5:


###### 
Proposition 2.


Consider an S5 layer, with state matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A}, input matrix рқҗҒрқҗҒ\mathbf{B} and some output matrix рқҗӮрқҗӮ\mathbf{C} (cf. Assumption 1); and an S4 layer, where each of the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSMs has state matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} (cf. Assumption 2, 4) and input vector рқҗҒ(h)superscriptрқҗҒв„Һ\mathbf{B}^{(h)} (cf. Assumption 4). If the S4 and S5 layers are discretized with the same timescales (cf. Assumption 3), then the S5 SSM produces outputs, рқҗІksubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳ\mathbf{y}_{k}, equivalent to a linear combination of the latent states of the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSMs, рқҗІk=рқҗӮequivвҖӢрқҗұk(1:H)subscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқҗӮequivsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ:1рқҗ»\mathbf{y}_{k}=\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(1:H)}, whereВ рқҗӮequiv=[рқҗӮвҖӢвӢҜвҖӢрқҗӮ]superscriptрқҗӮequivdelimited-[]рқҗӮвӢҜрқҗӮ\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}=\left[\ \mathbf{C}\ \cdots\ \mathbf{C}\ \right].


###### Proof.


See Appendix D.2.
вҲҺ


Importantly, the S5 SSM outputs are not equal to the outputs of the block-diagonal S4 SSM. Instead they are equivalent to the outputs of the block-diagonal S4 SSM with modified output matrix рқҗӮequivsuperscriptрқҗӮequiv\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}. Under the assumptions, however, the underlying state dynamics are equivalent. Recalling that initializing the S4 dynamics with HiPPO was key to performanceВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a), the relationship established in PropositionВ 2 motivates using HiPPO initializations for S5, as we now discuss.


### 
4.2 Diagonalizable Initialization


Ideally, given the interpretation above, we would initialize S5 with the exact HiPPO-LegS matrix. Unfortunately, as discussed in Section 2.3, this matrix is not stably diagonalizable, as is required for the efficient parallel scans used for S5. However, Gupta etВ al. (2022) and Gu etВ al. (2022) showed empirically that removing the low rank terms and initializing with the diagonalized HiPPO-N matrix still performed well. Gu etВ al. (2022) offered a theoretical justification for the use of this normal approximation for single-input systems: in the limit of infinite state dimension, the linear ODE with HiPPO-N state matrix produces the same dynamics as an ODE with the HiPPO-LegS matrix. Using linearity, it is straightforward to extend this result to the multi-input system that S5 uses:


###### 
Corollary 1 (Extension of Theorem 3 in Gu etВ al. (2022)).


Consider рқҗҖLegSвҲҲв„қNГ—NsubscriptрқҗҖLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗҖLegSNormalвҲҲв„қNГ—NsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗҒLegSвҲҲв„қNГ—H,рқҗҸLegSвҲҲв„қNformulae-sequencesubscriptрқҗҒLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқҗ»subscriptрқҗҸLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times H},\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N} as defined in Appendix B.1.1. Given vector-valued inputs рқҗ®вҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қHрқҗ®рқ‘Ўsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»\mathbf{u}(t)\in\mathbb{R}^{H}, the ordinary differential equation dвҖӢрқҗұвҖІвҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=рқҗҖLegSNormalвҖӢрқҗұвҖІвҖӢ(t)+12вҖӢрқҗҒLegSвҖӢрқҗ®вҖӢ(t)dsuperscriptрқҗұвҖІрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў
superscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptрқҗұвҖІрқ‘Ў12subscriptрқҗҒLegSрқҗ®рқ‘Ў\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\prime}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\mathbf{x}^{\prime}(t)+\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\mathbf{u}(t) converges to dвҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=рқҗҖLegSвҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)+рқҗҒLegSвҖӢрқҗ®вҖӢ(t)dрқҗұрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў
subscriptрқҗҖLegSрқҗұрқ‘ЎsubscriptрқҗҒLegSрқҗ®рқ‘Ў\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\mathbf{x}(t)+\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\mathbf{u}(t) as NвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ҒN\rightarrow\infty.


We include a simple proof of this extension in Appendix D.3. This extension motivates the use of HiPPO-N to initialize S5вҖҷs MIMO SSM. Note that S4D (the diagonal extension of S4) uses the same HiPPO-N matrix. Thus, when under the assumptions in Proposition 2, an S5 SSM in fact produces outputs that are equivalent to a linear combination of the latent states produced by S4DвҖҷs SSMs. Our empirical results in Section 6 suggest that S5 initialized with the HiPPO-N matrix performs just as well as S4 initialized with the HiPPO-LegS matrix.


### 
4.3 Relaxing the Assumptions


We now revisit the assumptions required for Proposition 2, since they only relate a constrained version of S5 to a constrained version of S4. Regarding Assumption 2, Gu etВ al. (2021a) report that S4 models with tied state matrices can still perform well, though allowing different state matrices often yields higher performance. Likewise, requiring a single scalar timescale across all of the S4 SSMs, per Assumption 3, is restrictive. S4 typically learns different timescale parameters for each SSMВ (Gu etВ al., 2023) to capture different timescales in the data. To relax these assumptions, note that Assumption 4 constrains S5 to have dimension P=Nрқ‘ғрқ‘ҒP=N, and Nрқ‘ҒN is typically much smaller than the dimensionality of the inputs, Hрқҗ»H. PropositionВ 1 established that S5 can match S4вҖҷs complexity with P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H). By allowing for larger latent state sizes, Assumptions 2 and 3 can be relaxed, as discussed in Appendix D.4. We also discuss how this relaxation motivates a block-diagonal initialization with HiPPO-N matrices on the diagonal. Finally, to further relax the tied timescale assumptions, we note that in practice, we find improved performance by learning Pрқ‘ғP different timescales (one per state). See Appendix D.5 for further discussion of this empirical finding and the ablations in Appendix E.1.


## 
5 Related work


S5 is most directly related to S4 and its other extensions, which we have discussed thoroughly. However, there is prior literature that uses similar ideas to those developed here. For example, prior work studied approximating nonlinear RNNs with stacks of linear RNNs connected by nonlinear layers, while also using parallel scansВ (Martin & Cundy, 2018). Martin & Cundy (2018) showed that several efficient RNNs, such as QRNNsВ (Bradbury etВ al., 2017) and SRUsВ (Lei etВ al., 2018), fall into a class of linear surrogate RNNs that can leverage parallel scans. Kaul (2020) also used parallel scans for an approach that approximates RNNs with stacks of discrete-time single-input, multi-output (SIMO) SSMs. However, S4 and S5 are the only methods to significantly outperform other comparable state-of-the-art nonlinear RNNs, transformers and convolution approaches. Our ablation study in Appendix E.2 suggests that this performance gain over prior attempts at parallelized linear RNNs is likely due to the continuous-time parameterization and the HiPPO initialization.


## 
6 Experiments


We now compare empirically the performance of the S5 layer to the S4 layer and other baseline methods. We use the S5 layer as a drop-in replacement for the S4 layer. The architecture consists of a linear input encoder, stacks of S5 layers, and a linear output decoderВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a). For all experiments we choose the S5 dimensions to ensure similar computational complexities as S4, following the conditions discussed in Section 3.3, as well as comparable parameter counts. The results we present show that the S5 layer matches the performance and efficiency of the S4 layer. We include in the appendix further ablations, baselines and runtime comparisons.


### 
6.1 Long Range Arena


The long range arena (LRA) benchmarkВ (Tay etВ al., 2021) is a suite of six sequence modeling tasks, with sequence lengths from 1,024 to over 16,000. The suite was specifically developed to benchmark the performance of architectures on long-range modeling tasks (see Appendix G for more details). Table 1 presents S5вҖҷs LRA performance in comparison to other methods. S5 achieves the highest average score among methods that have linear complexity in sequence length (most notably S4, S4D, and the concurrent works: Liquid-S4В (Hasani etВ al., 2023) and Mega-chunkВ (Ma etВ al., 2023)). Most significantly, S5 achieves the highest score among all models (including MegaВ (Ma etВ al., 2023)) on the Path-X task, which has by far the longest sequence length of the tasks in the benchmark.

(2021a; 2022) and report means across three seeds for S4, S4D (as reported by Gu etВ al. (2021a; 2022)) and S5. Bold scores indicate highest performance, underlined scores indicate second placed performance. We also include the results for the concurrent methods Liquid-S4В (Hasani etВ al., 2023) and MegaВ (Ma etВ al., 2023). Unlike S4 methods and S5, the best Mega model retains the transformerвҖҷs рқ’ӘвҖӢ(L2)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқҗҝ2\mathcal{O}(L^{2}) complexity.

### 
6.2 Raw Speech Classification


The Speech Commands datasetВ (Warden, 2018) contains high-fidelity sound recordings of different human readers reciting a word from a vocabulary of 35 words. The task is to classify which word was spoken. We show in Table 2 that S5 outperforms the baselines, outperforms previous S4 methods and performs similarly to the concurrent Liquid-S4 methodВ (Hasani etВ al., 2023). As S4 and S5 methods are parameterized in continuous-time, these models can be applied to datasets with different sampling rates without the need for re-training, simply by globally re-scaling the timescale parameter О”О”\Delta by the ratio between the new and old sampling rates. The result of applying the best S5 model trained on 16kHz data, to the speech data sampled (via decimation) at 8kHz, without any additional fine-tuning, is also presented in Table 2. S5 also improves this metric over the baseline methods.

(2022), the mean across three random seeds is reported. Performance for the baselines InceptionNet through to S4D-Lin are reported from Gu etВ al. (2022).

### 
6.3 Variable Observation Interval


The final application we study here highlights how S5 can naturally handle observations received at irregular intervals. S5 does so by supplying a different О”tsubscriptО”рқ‘Ў\Delta_{t} value to the discretization at each step. We use the pendulum regression example presented by Becker etВ al. (2019) and Schirmer etВ al. (2022), illustrated in Figure 3. The input sequence is a sequence of L=50рқҗҝ50L=50 images, each 24Г—24242424\times 24 pixels in size, that has been corrupted with a correlated noise process and sampled at irregular intervals from a continuous trajectory of duration T=100рқ‘Ү100T=100. The targets are the sine and cosine of the angle of the pendulum, which follows a nonlinear dynamical system. The velocity is unobserved. We match the architecture, parameter count and training procedure of Schirmer etВ al. (2022). Table LABEL:tab:results:pendulum_mse summarizes the results of this experiment. S5 outperforms CRU on the regression task, recovering a lower mean error. Furthermore, S5 is markedly faster than CRU on the same hardware.


### 
6.4 Pixel-level 1-D Image Classification


TableВ 10 in Appendix F.4 shows results of S5 on other common benchmarks including sequential MNIST, permuted sequential MNIST and sequential CIFAR (color). We see that S5 broadly matches the performance of S4, and outperforms a range of state-of-the-art RNN-based methods.


Figure 3: Illustration of the pendulum regression example. Shown in the top row are the images used as input at the time points indicated. Shown on the bottom are the values of sinвҒЎ(Оёt)subscriptрқңғрқ‘Ў\sin(\theta_{t}) and cosвҒЎ(Оёt)subscriptрқңғрқ‘Ў\cos(\theta_{t}), where Оёtsubscriptрқңғрқ‘Ў\theta_{t} is the angle of the pendulum at time tрқ‘Ўt, that are used as the regression targets.

## 
7 Conclusion


We introduce the S5 layer for long-range sequence modeling. The S5 layer modifies the internal structure of the S4 layer, and replaces the frequency-domain approach used by S4 with a purely recurrent, time-domain approach leveraging parallel scans. S5 achieves high performance while retaining the computational efficiency of S4. S5 also provides further opportunities. For instance, unlike the convolutional S4 methods, the parallel scan unlocks the ability to efficiently and easily process time-varying SSMs whose parameters can vary with time. Section 6.3 illustrated an example of this for sequences sampled at variable sampling rates. The concurrently developed method, Liquid-S4В (Hasani etВ al., 2023), uses an input-dependent bilinear dynamical system and highlights further opportunities for time-varying SSMs. The more general MIMO SSM design will also enable connections to be made with classical probabilistic state space modeling as well as more recent work on parallelizing filtering and smoothing operationsВ (SГӨrkkГӨ & GarcГӯa-FernГЎndez, 2020). More broadly, we hope the simplicity and generality of the S5 layer can expand the use of state space layers in deep sequence modeling and lead to new formulations and extensions.

вҖў

вҖў

## 
Appendix A JAX Implementation of S5 Layer


в¬Ү

1import jax


2import jax.numpy as np


3parallel_scan = jax.lax.associative_scan


4


5def discretize(Lambda, B_tilde, Delta):


6 """ Discretize a diagonalized, continuous-time linear SSM


7 Args:


8 Lambda (complex64): diagonal state matrix (P,)


9 B_tilde (complex64): input matrix (P, H)


10 Delta (float32): discretization step sizes (P,)


11 Returns:


12 discretized Lambda_bar (complex64), B_bar (complex64) (P,), (P,H)"""


13 Identity = np.ones(Lambda.shape[0])


14 Lambda_bar = np.exp(Lambda * Delta)


15 B_bar = (1 / Lambda * (Lambda_bar - Identity))[..., None] * B_tilde


16 return Lambda_bar, B_bar


17


18def binary_operator(element_i, element_j):


19 """ Binary operator for parallel scan of linear recurrence. Assumes a diagonal matrix A.


20 Args:


21 element_i: tuple containing A_i and Bu_i at position i (P,), (P,)


22 element_j: tuple containing A_j and Bu_j at position j (P,), (P,)


23 Returns:


24 new element ( A_out, Bu_out ) """


25 A_i, Bu_i = element_i


26 A_j, Bu_j = element_j


27 return A_j * A_i, A_j * Bu_i + Bu_j


28


29def apply_ssm(Lambda_bar, B_bar, C_tilde, D, input_sequence):


30 """ Compute the LxH output of discretized SSM given an LxH input.


31 Args:


32 Lambda_bar (complex64): discretized diagonal state matrix (P,)


33 B_bar (complex64): discretized input matrix (P, H)


34 C_tilde (complex64): output matrix (H, P)


35 D (float32): feedthrough matrix (H,)


36 input_sequence (float32): input sequence of features (L, H)


37 Returns:


38 ys (float32): the SSM outputs (S5 layer preactivations) (L, H) """


39 # Prepare elements required to initialize parallel scan


40 Lambda_elements = np.repeat(Lambda_bar[None, ...], input_sequence.shape[0], axis=0)


41 Bu_elements = jax.vmap(lambda u: B_bar @ u)(input_sequence)


42 elements = (Lambda_elements, Bu_elements) # (L, P), (L, P)


43


44 # Compute latent state sequence given input sequence using parallel scan


45 _, xs = parallel_scan(binary_operator, elements) # (L, P)


46


47 # Compute SSM output sequence


48 ys = jax.vmap(lambda x, u: (C_tilde @ x + D * u).real)(xs, input_sequence)


49 return ys


50


51def apply_S5_layer(params, input_sequence):


52 """ Computes LxH output sequence of an S5 layer given LxH input sequence.


53 Args:


54 params: tuple of the continuous time SSM parameters


55 input_sequence: input sequence of features (L, H)


56 Returns:


57 The S5 layer output sequence (L, H) """


58 Lambda, B_tilde, C_tilde, D, log_Delta = params


59 Lambda_bar, B_bar = discretize(Lambda, B_tilde, np.exp(log_Delta))


60 preactivations = apply_ssm(Lambda_bar, B_bar, C_tilde, D, input_sequence)


61 return jax.nn.gelu(preactivations)


62


63def batch_apply_S5_layer(params, input_sequences):


64 """ Computes BxLxH output sequence of an S5 layer given BxLxH input sequence.


65 Args:


66 params: tuple of the continuous time SSM parameters


67 input_sequences: batch of input feature sequences (B, L ,H)


68 Returns:


69 Batch of S5 layer output sequences (B, L, H) """


70 return jax.vmap(apply_S5_layer, in_axes=(None, 0))(params, input_sequences)


ListingВ 1: JAX implementation to apply a single S5 layer to a batch of input sequences.


## 
Appendix B S5 Layer Details


### 
B.1 Initialization Details


#### 
B.1.1 Initialization of the State Matrix


Here we provide additional details to supplement the discussion of initialization in Section 3.2. Gu etВ al. (2023) explains the ability of S4 to capture long-range dependencies when using the HiPPO-LegS matrix via decomposing the input with respect to an infinitely long, exponentially decaying measure. The HiPPO-LegS matrix and corresponding SISO input vector are defined as


 | 
 | (рқҗҖLegS)nвҖӢksubscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\left(\mathbf{A_{\mathrm{LegS}}}\right)_{nk}
 | =вҲ’{(2вҖӢn+1)1/2вҖӢ(2вҖӢk+1)1/2,n>kn+1,n=k0,n<k.absentcasessuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ112superscript
2рқ‘ҳ112рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ӣ1рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ0рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=-\begin{cases}(2n+1)^{1/2}(2k+1)^{1/2},&n>k\\
n+1,&n=k\\
0,&n<k\end{cases}.
 | 
 | (7)


 | 
 | (рқҗӣLegS)nsubscriptsubscriptрқҗӣLegSрқ‘ӣ\displaystyle\left(\mathbf{b_{\mathrm{LegS}}}\right)_{n}
 | =(2вҖӢn+1)12.absentsuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ112\displaystyle=(2n+1)^{\frac{1}{2}}.
 | 
 | (8)


Note that in Section 4.2, the input matrix рқҗҒLegSвҲҲв„қNГ—HsubscriptрқҗҒLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқҗ»\mathbf{B_{\mathrm{LegS}}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times H} used in Corollary 1 is formed by concatenating Hрқҗ»H copies of рқҗӣLegSвҲҲв„қNsubscriptрқҗӣLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{b_{\mathrm{LegS}}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N}.


Theorem 1 ofВ Gu etВ al. (2021a) then shows that the HiPPO matrices in Gu etВ al. (2020a), рқҗҖHiPPOвҲҲв„қNГ—NsubscriptрқҗҖHiPPOsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{HiPPO}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} can be represented with a normal plus low-rank (NPLR) form consisting of a normal matrix, рқҗҖHiPPONormal=рқҗ•вҖӢрқҡІвҖӢрқҗ•вҲ—вҲҲв„қNГ—NsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖHiPPONormalрқҗ•рқҡІsuperscriptрқҗ•superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{HiPPO}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{*}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, and a low-rank term


 | 
 | рқҗҖHiPPO=рқҗҖHiPPONormalвҲ’рқҗҸрқҗҗвҠӨ=рқҗ•вҖӢ(рқҡІвҲ’(рқҗ•вҲ—вҖӢрқҗҸ)вҖӢ(рқҗ•вҲ—вҖӢрқҗҗ)вҲ—)вҖӢрқҗ•вҲ—subscriptрқҗҖHiPPOsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖHiPPONormalsuperscriptрқҗҸрқҗҗtopрқҗ•рқҡІsuperscriptрқҗ•рқҗҸsuperscriptsuperscriptрқҗ•рқҗҗsuperscriptрқҗ•\displaystyle\mathbf{A_{\mathrm{HiPPO}}}=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{HiPPO}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}-\mathbf{P}\mathbf{Q}^{\top}=\mathbf{V}\left(\mathbf{\Lambda}-(\mathbf{V}^{*}\mathbf{P})(\mathbf{V}^{*}\mathbf{Q})^{*}\right)\mathbf{V}^{*}
 | 
 | (9)


for unitary рқҗ•вҲҲв„ӮNГ—Nрқҗ•superscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{V}\in\mathbb{C}^{N\times N}, diagonal рқҡІвҲҲв„ӮNГ—NрқҡІsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{\Lambda}\in\mathbb{C}^{N\times N}, and low-rank factorization рқҗҸ,рқҗҗвҲҲв„қNГ—r
- рқҗҸрқҗҗsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹ\mathbf{P},\mathbf{Q}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times r}. The right hand side of this equation shows HiPPO matrices can be conjugated into a diagonal plus low-rank (DPLR) form. The HiPPO-LegS matrix can therefore be written in terms of the normal HiPPO-N matrix and low-rank term рқҗҸLegSвҲҲв„қNsubscriptрқҗҸLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N}В (Goel etВ al., 2022) as


 | 
 | рқҗҖLegSsubscriptрқҗҖLegS\displaystyle\mathbf{A_{\mathrm{LegS}}}
 | =рқҗҖLegSNormalвҲ’рқҗҸLegsвҖӢрқҗҸLegsвҠӨabsentsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsubscriptрқҗҸLegssuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҸLegstop\displaystyle=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}-\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{Legs}}\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{Legs}}^{\top}
 | 
 | (10)


where


 | 
 | рқҗҖLegSnвҖӢkNormalsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖsubscriptLegSрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳNormal\displaystyle\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}_{nk}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}
 | =вҲ’{(n+12)1/2вҖӢ(k+12)1/2,n>k12,n=k(n+12)1/2вҖӢ(k+12)1/2,n<k.absentcasessuperscript
рқ‘ӣ1212superscript
рқ‘ҳ1212рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ12рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳsuperscript
рқ‘ӣ1212superscript
рқ‘ҳ1212рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=-\begin{cases}(n+\frac{1}{2})^{1/2}(k+\frac{1}{2})^{1/2},&n>k\\
\frac{1}{2},&n=k\\
(n+\frac{1}{2})^{1/2}(k+\frac{1}{2})^{1/2},&n<k\end{cases}.
 | 
 | (11)


 | 
 | рқҗҸLegsnsubscriptsubscriptрқҗҸLegsрқ‘ӣ\displaystyle\mathbf{P_{\mathrm{Legs}}}_{n}
 | =(n+12)12absentsuperscript
рқ‘ӣ1212\displaystyle=(n+\frac{1}{2})^{\frac{1}{2}}
 | 
 | (12)


Our default is to set the S5 layer state matrix рқҗҖ=рқҗҖLegSNormalвҲҲв„қPГ—PрқҗҖsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ғрқ‘ғ\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\in\mathbb{R}^{P\times P}, and take the eigendecomposition of this matrix to recover the initial рқҡІрқҡІ\mathbf{\Lambda}. We often find it beneficial to also use рқҗ•рқҗ•\mathbf{V} and рқҗ•вҲ’1=рқҗ•вҲ—superscriptрқҗ•1superscriptрқҗ•\mathbf{V}^{-1}=\mathbf{V}^{*} to initialize рқҗҒ~~рқҗҒ\mathbf{\tilde{B}} and рқҗӮ~~рқҗӮ\mathbf{\tilde{C}}, as described below.


As mentioned in Section 4.3, we also found that performance on many tasks benefited from initializing the S5 state matrix as block-diagonal, with each block on the diagonal equal to рқҗҖLegSNormalвҲҲв„қRГ—RsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptв„қрқ‘…рқ‘…\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\in\mathbb{R}^{R\times R}, where Rрқ‘…R here is less than the state dimension Pрқ‘ғP, e.g. R=P4рқ‘…рқ‘ғ4R=\frac{P}{4} when 4 blocks are used on the diagonal. We then take the eigendecomposition of this matrix to initialize рқҡІрқҡІ\mathbf{\Lambda}, as well as рқҗҒ~~рқҗҒ\mathbf{\tilde{B}} and рқҗӮ~~рқҗӮ\mathbf{\tilde{C}}. We note that even in this case, рқҗҒ~~рқҗҒ\mathbf{\tilde{B}} and рқҗӮ~~рқҗӮ\mathbf{\tilde{C}} are still initialized in dense form and there is no constraint that requires рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} to remain block-diagonal during learning. In the hyperparameter table in Appendix G, the JрқҗҪJ hyperparameter indicates the number of these HiPPO-N blocks used on the diagonal for initialization, where J=1рқҗҪ1J=1 indicates we used the default case of initializing with a single HiPPO-N matrix. We discuss the motivation for this block-diagonal initialization further in Appendix D.4.


#### 
B.1.2 Initialization of Input, Output and Feed-through Matrices


In general, we explicitly initialize the input matrix рқҗҒ~~рқҗҒ\mathbf{\tilde{B}} and output matrix рқҗӮ~~рқҗӮ\mathbf{\tilde{C}} using the eigenvectors from the diagonalization of the initial state matrix. Specifically, we sample рқҗҒрқҗҒ\mathbf{B} and рқҗӮрқҗӮ\mathbf{C} and then initialize the (complex) learnable parameters рқҗҒ~~рқҗҒ\mathbf{\tilde{B}} as рқҗҒ~=рқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ~рқҗҒsuperscriptрқҗ•1рқҗҒ\mathbf{\tilde{B}}=\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{B} and рқҗӮ~=рқҗӮрқҗ•~рқҗӮрқҗӮрқҗ•\mathbf{\tilde{C}}=\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}.


We initialize рқҗғвҲҲв„қHрқҗғsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»\mathbf{D}\in\mathbb{R}^{H} by independently sampling each element from a standard normal distribution.


#### 
B.1.3 Initialization of the Timescales


Prior workВ (Gupta etВ al., 2022; Gu etВ al., 2023) found the initialization of this timescale parameter to be important. This is studied in detail in Gu etВ al. (2023). We sample these parameters in line with S4 and sample each element of logвҒЎрқҡ«вҲҲв„қPрқҡ«superscriptв„қрқ‘ғ\log\mathbf{\Delta}\in\mathbb{R}^{P} from a uniform distribution on the interval [logвҒЎОҙmin,logвҒЎОҙmax)subscriptрқӣҝsubscriptрқӣҝ[\log\delta_{\min},\log\delta_{\max}), where the default range is Оҙmin=0.001subscriptрқӣҝ0.001\delta_{\min}=0.001 and Оҙmax=0.1subscriptрқӣҝ0.1\delta_{\max}=0.1. The only exception is the Path-X experiment, where we initialize from Оҙmin=0.0001subscriptрқӣҝ0.0001\delta_{\min}=0.0001 and Оҙmax=0.1subscriptрқӣҝ0.1\delta_{\max}=0.1 to account for the longer timescales as discussed in Gu etВ al. (2023).


### 
B.2 Comparison of S4 and S5 Computational Elements


In Figure 4 we illustrate a comparison of the computational details of the S4 and S5 layers for efficient, parallelized offline processing.


(a) S4 layerВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a) offline processing.


(b) S5 layer offline processing. Duplicated from the main text. 


Figure 4: The computational components of the S4 layerВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a) (top) and the S5 layer (bottom) for offline application to a sequence. (a) The S4 layer applies an independent SSM to each dimension of the input sequence рқҗ®1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—Hsubscriptрқҗ®:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{u}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}. This requires a Cauchy kernel computation to compute the convolution kernel coefficients in the frequency domain. Convolutions are computed using FFTs to produce the independent SSM outputs рқҗІ1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—HsubscriptрқҗІ:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{y}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}. A nonlinear activation function that includes a mixing layer is applied to the SSM outputs to produce the layer outputs. (b) (Reproduced from Figure 1) The S5 layer uses a parallel scan on a diagonalized linear SSM to compute the SSM outputs рқҗІ1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—HsubscriptрқҗІ:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{y}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}. A nonlinear activation function is applied to the SSM outputs to produce the layer outputs.


## 
Appendix C Computational Efficiency of S5


### 
C.1 Theoretical Computational Efficiency


###### 
Proposition 1.


Given an S4 layer with Hрқҗ»H input/output features, an S5 layer with Hрқҗ»H input/output features and a latent size P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H) has the same order of magnitude complexity as an S4 layer in terms of both runtime and memory usage.


###### Proof.


We first consider the case where the entire sequence is available and compare the S4 layerвҖҷs convolution mode to the S5 layerвҖҷs use of a parallel scan. We then consider the online generation case where each method operates recurrently.


##### Parallelized offline processing


We consider the application of both the S4 and S5 layer to a vector-valued sequence рқҗ®1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—Hsubscriptрқҗ®:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{u}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}. Note that there are multiple ways to compute the convolution kernel for S4/S4D and the exact computational complexity depends on the implementation (Gu etВ al., 2021a; 2022). However, the overall complexity of computing the S4 SSM outputs given the inputs is lower bounded by the FFTs used to convert into the frequency domain to apply the convolutions. Therefore, we can lower bound the cost of applying a single SISO S4 SSM to a scalar input sequence as рқ’ӘвҖӢ(LвҖӢlogвҒЎL)рқ’Әрқҗҝрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(L\log L) operations and рқ’ӘвҖӢ(L)рқ’Әрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(L) space. The S4 layer then consists of Hрқҗ»H different S4 SSMs, and therefore requires рқ’ӘвҖӢ(HвҖӢLвҖӢlogвҒЎL)рқ’Әрқҗ»рқҗҝрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(HL\log L) operations and рқ’ӘвҖӢ(HвҖӢL)рқ’Әрқҗ»рқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(HL) space. Finally, mixing the Hрқҗ»H activations at each timestep requires LрқҗҝL independent matrix-vector multiplications. Thus, the S4 layer sequence-to-sequence transformation requires a total of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H2вҖӢL+HвҖӢLвҖӢlogвҒЎL)рқ’Ә
superscriptрқҗ»2рқҗҝрқҗ»рқҗҝрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(H^{2}L+HL\log L) operations when run in the efficient convolution mode. Given H2вҖӢLsuperscriptрқҗ»2рқҗҝH^{2}L processors, these operations can be parallelized to a minimum of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(logвҒЎH+logвҒЎL)рқ’Ә
рқҗ»рқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(\log H+\log L) parallel time.


We now consider the S5 layer. As discussed in Section 2.2, the parallel scan requires рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢL)рқ’Әрқ‘ғрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(PL) operations and рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢL)рқ’Әрқ‘ғрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(PL) space to perform the linear recurrence that computes the discretized states рқҗұ1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—Psubscriptрқҗұ:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқ‘ғ\mathbf{x}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times P}. In addition, рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢHвҖӢL)рқ’Әрқ‘ғрқҗ»рқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(PHL) operations are required for the independent matrix-vector multiplications that compute рқҗҒВҜвҖӢрқҗ®1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—PВҜрқҗҒsubscriptрқҗ®:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқ‘ғ\overline{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{u}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times P} and the SSM outputsВ рқҗӮ~вҖӢрқҗұ1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—H~рқҗӮsubscriptрқҗұ:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\tilde{\mathbf{C}}\mathbf{x}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}. Therefore, the S5 layer requires рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢHвҖӢL+PвҖӢL)рқ’Ә
рқ‘ғрқҗ»рқҗҝрқ‘ғрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}\left(PHL+PL\right) operations. Given PвҖӢHвҖӢLрқ‘ғрқҗ»рқҗҝPHL processors, these operations can be parallelized to a minimum of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(logвҒЎP+logвҒЎL)рқ’Ә
рқ‘ғрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(\log P+\log L) parallel time. Thus, we see that when the S5 state dimension P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H), the S5 layer requires рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H2вҖӢL+HвҖӢL)рқ’Ә
superscriptрқҗ»2рқҗҝрқҗ»рқҗҝ\mathcal{O}\left(H^{2}L+HL\right) operations compared to the S4 layerвҖҷs рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H2вҖӢL+HвҖӢLвҖӢlogвҒЎL)рқ’Ә
superscriptрқҗ»2рқҗҝрқҗ»рқҗҝрқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(H^{2}L+HL\log L) operations. Crucially, when P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H), S4 and S5 each have parallel complexity of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(logвҒЎH+logвҒЎL)рқ’Ә
рқҗ»рқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(\log H+\log L) (when H2вҖӢLsuperscriptрқҗ»2рқҗҝH^{2}L processors are available). In addition, when P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H), the space complexity for the parallel scan is рқ’ӘвҖӢ(HвҖӢL)рқ’Әрқҗ»рқҗҝ\mathcal{O}(HL) which matches the space complexity of S4вҖҷs FFTs. Both methods then also perform identical broadcasted matrix vector multiplications, and hence have the same space complexity.


##### Online generation


For online generation, both the S4 and S5 layers are run recurrently. The S4 layer requires рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H2+HвҖӢN)рқ’Ә
superscriptрқҗ»2рқҗ»рқ‘Ғ\mathcal{O}(H^{2}+HN) operations per step due to its рқ’ӘвҖӢ(HвҖӢN)рқ’Әрқҗ»рқ‘Ғ\mathcal{O}(HN) DPLR-matrix-vector multiplicationВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a) and the рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H2)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқҗ»2\mathcal{O}(H^{2}) matrix-vector multiplication of its mixing layer. The S5 layer requires рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢH+P)рқ’Ә
рқ‘ғрқҗ»рқ‘ғ\mathcal{O}(PH+P) operations per step due to its рқ’ӘвҖӢ(P)рқ’Әрқ‘ғ\mathcal{O}(P) matrix-vector multiplication with its diagonal matrix and its рқ’ӘвҖӢ(PвҖӢH)рқ’Әрқ‘ғрқҗ»\mathcal{O}(PH) matrix-vector multiplications to compute рқҗҒВҜвҖӢрқҗ®kвҲҲв„қPВҜрқҗҒsubscriptрқҗ®рқ‘ҳsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ғ\overline{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{u}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{P} and рқҗӮ~вҖӢрқҗұkвҲҲв„қH~рқҗӮsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»\tilde{\mathbf{C}}\mathbf{x}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{H}. Thus, we see the two approaches have the same per step complexity of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H2)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқҗ»2\mathcal{O}(H^{2}) when P=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ‘ғрқ’Әрқҗ»P=\mathcal{O}(H) and the individual S4 SSM state sizes Nрқ‘ҒN are рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқ’Әрқҗ»\mathcal{O}(H).


Thus, S4 and S5 have the same order computational complexity and memory requirements in both cases.
вҲҺ

Table 4 provides an empirical evaluation of the runtime performance, in terms of speed and memory, between S4, S4D and S5 across a range of sequence lengths from the LRA tasks. We compared the JAX implementation of S5 to a JAX implementation of S4 and S4D, based on the JAX implementation from Rush & Karamcheti (2022). For a fair comparison, we modified these existing JAX implementations of S4 and S4D to allow them both to enforce conjugate symmetry and use bidirectionality. For each task, models use bidirectionality and conjugate symmetry as reported in Gu etВ al. (2022). All models, except for the italicised S5 row, use the same input/output features Hрқҗ»H and number of layers as reported in Gu etВ al. (2022). The S4 and S4D layers also use the same S4 SSM latent size as reported in Gu etВ al. (2022). All methods used the same batch size and all comparisons were made using a 16GB NVIDIA V100 GPU. Note we observed the JAX S4D implementation to in general be faster than the JAX S4 implementation (possibly due to this specific S4 implementationвҖҷs use of the naive Cauchy kernel computationВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a)). For this reason, we consider S4D as the baseline.


We consider three configurations of S5 for comparison. The first two configurations, corresponding to lines 3 and 4 for each metric in Table 4, show how the runtime metrics vary as S5вҖҷs latent size is adjusted, with all other architecture choices equal to those of S4. In line 3 of each metric, we denote the S5 вҖңArchitectureвҖқ as вҖң(P==H) Matched to Gu etВ al. (2022)вҖқ to indicate that this configuration of S5 sets the latent size equal to the number of input/output features, P=Hрқ‘ғрқҗ»P=H. This line empirically supports the complexity argument presented in Appendix C.1. In line 4 of each metric, we denote the S5 вҖңArchitectureвҖқ as вҖң (P==N) Matched to Gu etВ al. (2022)вҖқ to indicate that this configuration of S5 sets the latent size Pрқ‘ғP equal to the latent size Nрқ‘ҒN S4 uses for each of its SISO SSMs. This line also corresponds to the constrained version of S5 that performs similarly to S4/S4D as presented in the ablation study in Table 5. The runtime results of both of these configurations supports the claim in Section 4.3 that the latent size of S5 can be increased while maintaining S4вҖҷs computational efficiency.


Finally, we include a third configuration of S5, presented in the fifth line of each metric and italicized. This configuration of S5 uses the best architectural dimensions from Table 11 and was used for the corresponding LRA results in Table 1.


Importantly, the broad takeaway from this empirical study is that the runtime and memory usage of S5 and S4/S4D are broadly similar, as suggested by the complexity analysis in the main text.


## 
Appendix D Relationship Between S4 and S5


We now describe in more detail the connection between the S4 and S5 architectures. This connection allowed us to develop more performant architectures and extend theoretical results from existing work.


We break this analysis down into three parts:


- 
1. 


In Section D.2 we prove Proposition 2. We exploit the linearity of the systems to identify that the latent states computed by the S5 SSM are equivalent to a linear combination of latent states computed by the Hрқҗ»H SISO S4 SSMs, and that the outputs of the S5 SSM are a further linear transformation of these states. We then highlight how S4 and S5 effectively define different output matrices in the block-diagonal perspective shown in Figure 2.


- 
2. 


In Section D.3 we provide a simple extension of the proof provided by Gu etВ al. (2022). The original proof shows that in the SISO case, in the limit of large Nрқ‘ҒN, the dynamics arising from a (non-diagonalizable) HiPPO-LegS matrix, are faithfully approximated by the (diagonalizable) normal component of the HiPPO-LegS matrix. We extend this proof to apply to the MIMO setting. This motivates initialization with the HiPPO-N matrix, which in-turn allows us to use parallel scans efficiently.


- 
3. 


In Section D.4 we conclude by showing that, by judicious choice of initialization of the S5 state matrix, S5 can implement multiple independent S4 systems and relax the assumptions made. We also discuss the vector of timescale parameters, which we found to improve performance.


We note that many of these results follow straightforwardly from the linearity of the recurrence.


### 
D.1 Assumptions


For these following sections we will use the following assumptions, until otherwise stated:


###### 
Assumption 1.


We consider only Hрқҗ»H-dimensional to Hрқҗ»H-dimensional sequence maps.


###### 
Assumption 2.


We assume the state matrix of each S4 SSM is identical, рқҗҖ(h)=рқҗҖвҲҲв„ӮNГ—NsuperscriptрқҗҖв„ҺрқҗҖsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}^{(h)}=\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{C}^{N\times N}.


###### 
Assumption 3.


We assume the timescales of each S4 SSM are identical, О”(h)=О”вҲҲв„қ+superscriptО”в„ҺО”subscriptв„қ
\Delta^{(h)}=\Delta\in\mathbb{R}_{+}


###### 
Assumption 4.


We assume that the same state matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} is used in S5 as in S4 (also cf. Assumption 2). Note this also specifies the S5 latent size P=Nрқ‘ғрқ‘ҒP=N. We also assume the S5 input matrix is the horizontal concatenation of the column input vectors used by S4, рқҗҒвүң[рқҗҒ(1)вҖӢвҲЈвҖҰвҲЈвҖӢрқҗҒ(H)]вүңрқҗҒdelimited-[]superscriptрқҗҒ1delimited-вҲЈвҲЈвҖҰsuperscriptрқҗҒрқҗ»\mathbf{B}\triangleq\left[\mathbf{B}^{(1)}\mid\ldots\mid\mathbf{B}^{(H)}\right].


### 
D.2 Different Output Projections of Equivalent Dynamics


We provide a proof of Proposition 2.


###### 
Proposition 2.


Consider an S5 layer, with state matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A}, input matrix рқҗҒрқҗҒ\mathbf{B} and some output matrix рқҗӮрқҗӮ\mathbf{C} (cf. Assumption 1); and an S4 layer, where each of the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSMs has state matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} (cf. Assumption 2, 4) and input vector рқҗҒ(h)superscriptрқҗҒв„Һ\mathbf{B}^{(h)} (cf. Assumption 4). If the S4 and S5 layers are discretized with the same timescales (cf. Assumption 3), then the S5 SSM produces outputs, рқҗІksubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳ\mathbf{y}_{k}, equivalent to a linear combination of the latent states of the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSMs, рқҗІk=рқҗӮequivвҖӢрқҗұk(1:H)subscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқҗӮequivsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ:1рқҗ»\mathbf{y}_{k}=\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(1:H)}, whereВ рқҗӮequiv=[рқҗӮвҖӢвӢҜвҖӢрқҗӮ]superscriptрқҗӮequivdelimited-[]рқҗӮвӢҜрқҗӮ\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}=\left[\ \mathbf{C}\ \cdots\ \mathbf{C}\ \right].


###### Proof.


For a single S4 SSM, the discretized latent states can be expressed as a function of the input sequence рқҗ®1:LвҲҲв„қLГ—Hsubscriptрқҗ®:1рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»\mathbf{u}_{1:L}\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H}


 | 
 | рқҗұk(h)=вҲ‘i=1kрқҗҖВҜkвҲ’iвҖӢрқҗҒВҜ(h)вҖӢui(h).superscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳв„Һsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳsuperscriptВҜрқҗҖрқ‘ҳрқ‘–superscriptВҜрқҗҒв„Һsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘–в„Һ\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(h)}=\sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{k}\overline{\mathbf{A}}^{k-i}\overline{\mathbf{B}}^{(h)}u_{i}^{(h)}.
 | 
 | (13)


For an S5 layer, the latent states are expressible as


 | 
 | рқҗұk=вҲ‘i=1kрқҗҖВҜkвҲ’iвҖӢрқҗҒВҜвҖӢрқҗ®i,subscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳsuperscriptВҜрқҗҖрқ‘ҳрқ‘–ВҜрқҗҒsubscriptрқҗ®рқ‘–\mathbf{x}_{k}=\sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{k}\overline{\mathbf{A}}^{k-i}\overline{\mathbf{B}}\mathbf{u}_{i},
 | 
 | (14)


where we index as рқҗҒВҜвүң[рқҗҒВҜ(1)вҖӢвҲЈвҖҰвҲЈвҖӢрқҗҒВҜ(H)]вүңВҜрқҗҒdelimited-[]superscriptВҜрқҗҒ1delimited-вҲЈвҲЈвҖҰsuperscriptВҜрқҗҒрқҗ»\overline{\mathbf{B}}\triangleq\left[\overline{\mathbf{B}}^{(1)}\mid\ldots\mid\overline{\mathbf{B}}^{(H)}\right] and рқҗ®iвүң[ui(1),вҖҰ,ui(H)]вҠӨвүңsubscriptрқҗ®рқ‘–superscript
- subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ў1рқ‘–вҖҰsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ўрқҗ»рқ‘–top\mathbf{u}_{i}\triangleq\left[u^{(1)}_{i},\ldots,u^{(H)}_{i}\right]^{\top}


Here we make the observation:


 | 
 | рқҗұk=вҲ‘h=1Hрқҗұk(h),subscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»superscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳв„Һ\mathbf{x}_{k}=\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(h)},
 | 
 | (15)


where this result follows directly from the linearity of (13) and (14). This shows that (under the assumptions outlined above) the states of the MIMO S5 SSM are equivalent to the summation of the states across the Hрқҗ»H different SISO S4 SSMs.


We can then consider the effect of the output matrix рқҗӮрқҗӮ\mathbf{C}. For S5, the output matrix is a single dense matrix


 | 
 | рқҗІk=рқҗӮрқҗұk.subscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқҗӮрқҗұрқ‘ҳ\mathbf{y}_{k}=\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}_{k}.
 | 
 | (16)


We can substitute the relationship in (15) into (16) to cast the outputs of the MIMO S5 SSM in terms of the state of the Hрқҗ»H SISO S4 SSMs:


 | 
 | рқҗІksubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\mathbf{y}_{k}
 | =рқҗӮвҖӢвҲ‘h=1Hрқҗұk(h),absentрқҗӮsuperscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»subscriptsuperscriptрқҗұв„Һрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\mathbf{C}\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}_{k},
 | 
 | (17)


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ‘h=1HрқҗӮрқҗұk(h).absentsuperscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»subscriptsuperscriptрқҗӮрқҗұв„Һрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}_{k}.
 | 
 | (18)


Denoting the vertical concatenation of the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSM state vectors рқҗұk(1:H)=[рқҗұk(1)вҠӨ,вҖҰ,рқҗұk(H)вҠӨ]вҠӨsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ:1рқҗ»superscript
- superscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳsuperscript1topвҖҰsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқҗ»toptop\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(1:H)}=\left[\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(1)^{\top}},\ldots,\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(H)^{\top}}\right]^{\top}, we see that the outputs of the S5 SSM are expressible as:


 | 
 | рқҗІk=рқҗӮequivвҖӢрқҗұk(1:H),whereрқҗӮequiv=[рқҗӮвҖӢвҲЈвӢҜвҲЈвҖӢрқҗӮ],formulae-sequencesubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳ
- superscriptрқҗӮequivsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳ:1рқҗ»wheresuperscriptрқҗӮequivdelimited-[]рқҗӮdelimited-вҲЈвҲЈвӢҜрқҗӮ\mathbf{y}_{k}=\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(1:H)},\quad\mathrm{where}\quad\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}=\left[\ \mathbf{C}\mid\cdots\mid\mathbf{C}\ \right],
 | 
 | (19)


and hence are equivalent to a linear combination of the HвҖӢNрқҗ»рқ‘ҒHN states computed by the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSMs.
вҲҺ


This shows the outputs of the constrained S5 SSM under consideration (cf. Assumption 4) can be interpreted as a linear combination of the latent states computed by Hрқҗ»H constrained S4 SSMs with the same state matrices and timescale parameters. Note however, it does not show that the outputs of the S5 SSM directly equal the outputs of the effective block-diagonal S4 SSM. Indeed, they are not equal, and we can repeat this analysis for the S4 layer to concretely identify the difference. For comparison we assume that the output vector for each S4 SSM is given as a row in the S5 output matrix, i.e. рқҗӮ=[рқҗӮ(1)вҠӨвҖӢвҲЈвҖҰвҲЈвҖӢрқҗӮ(H)вҠӨ]вҠӨрқҗӮsuperscriptdelimited-[]superscriptрқҗӮsuperscript1topdelimited-вҲЈвҲЈвҖҰsuperscriptрқҗӮsuperscriptрқҗ»toptop\mathbf{C}=\left[\mathbf{C}^{(1)^{\top}}\mid\ldots\mid\mathbf{C}^{(H)^{\top}}\right]^{\top}. We can express the output of each S4 SSM as


 | 
 | yk(h)=рқҗӮ(h)вҖӢрқҗұk(h),superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳв„ҺsuperscriptрқҗӮв„Һsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳв„Һ\displaystyle y_{k}^{(h)}=\mathbf{C}^{(h)}\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(h)},
 | 
 | (20)


where yk(h)вҲҲв„қsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳв„Һв„қy_{k}^{(h)}\in\mathbb{R}. We can then define the effective output matrix that operates on the entire latent space (the dashed box labelled рқҗӮрқҗӮ\mathbf{C} in Figure 2(a)) in S4 as


 | 
 | yk(h)=(рқҗӮS4вҖӢрқҗұk)(h)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳв„ҺsuperscriptsuperscriptрқҗӮS4subscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳв„Һy_{k}^{(h)}=\left(\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{S4}}\mathbf{x}_{k}\right)^{(h)}
 | 
 | (21)


By inspecting (19) and (21), we can concretely express the difference in the equivalent output matrix used by both layers


 | 
 | рқҗӮS4superscriptрқҗӮS4\displaystyle\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{S4}}
 | =[рқҗӮ(1)вӢҜрқҹҺвӢ®вӢұвӢ®рқҹҺвӢҜрқҗӮ(H)],absentdelimited-[]superscriptрқҗӮ1вӢҜ0вӢ®вӢұвӢ®0вӢҜsuperscriptрқҗӮрқҗ»\displaystyle=\left[\begin{array}[]{ccc}\mathbf{C}^{(1)}&\cdots&\mathbf{0}\\
\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
\mathbf{0}&\cdots&\mathbf{C}^{(H)}\end{array}\right],
 | рқҗӮequiv=[рқҗӮ(1)вӢҜрқҗӮ(1)вӢ®вӢұвӢ®рқҗӮ(H)вӢҜрқҗӮ(H)]=[рқҗӮвҖӢвҲЈвӢҜвҲЈвҖӢрқҗӮ].superscriptрқҗӮequivdelimited-[]superscriptрқҗӮ1вӢҜsuperscriptрқҗӮ1вӢ®вӢұвӢ®superscriptрқҗӮрқҗ»вӢҜsuperscriptрқҗӮрқҗ»delimited-[]рқҗӮdelimited-вҲЈвҲЈвӢҜрқҗӮ\displaystyle\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}}=\left[\begin{array}[]{ccc}\mathbf{C}^{(1)}&\cdots&\mathbf{C}^{(1)}\\
\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
\mathbf{C}^{(H)}&\cdots&\mathbf{C}^{(H)}\end{array}\right]=\left[\ \mathbf{C}\mid\cdots\mid\mathbf{C}\ \right].
 | 
 | (28)


In S4, the effective output matrix consists of independent vectors on the leading diagonal (as is pictured in Figure 2(a)). In contrast, the effective output matrix used by S5 instead ties dense output matrices across the Hрқҗ»H S4 SSMs. As such, S5 can be interpreted as simply defining a different projection of the Hрқҗ»H independent SISO SSMs than is used by S4. Note that both projection matrices have the same number of parameters.


Although the projection is different, the fact that the latent dynamics can still be interpreted as a linear projection of the same underlying S4 latent dynamics suggests that initializing the state dynamics in S5 with the HiPPO-LegS matrix may lead to good performance, similarly to what was observed in S4. We discuss this in the next section. We note that it is not obvious whether tying the dense output matrices is any more or less expressive than S4вҖҷs use of a single untied output vector for each SSM, and it is unlikely that one approach is universally better than the other. We also stress that one would never implement S4 using the block diagonal matrix in (28), or, implement S5 using the repeated matrix in (28). These matrices are simply constructs for understanding the equivalence between S4 and S5.


Finally, we note an alternative view: the block-diagonal S4 with output matrix рқҗӮequivsuperscriptрқҗӮequiv\mathbf{C}^{\mathrm{equiv}} of Proposition 2 is equivalent to a version of S4 that uses a bank of single-input, multi-output (SIMO) SSMs with tied state matrices, timescales and multi-channel output matrices рқҗӮ(h)=рқҗӮвҲҲв„қHГ—NsuperscriptрқҗӮв„ҺрқҗӮsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»рқ‘Ғ\mathbf{C}^{(h)}=\mathbf{C}\in\mathbb{R}^{H\times N} and sums the individual SSM outputs. See appendix of Gu etВ al. (2021b) for a discussion of how the linear state space layer (LSSL), a predecessor of S4, can have multiple output channels.


### 
D.3 Diagonalizable Initialization


Proposition 2 suggests that initializing with the HiPPO-LegS matrix may yield good performance in S5, just as it does in S4 (because the constrained version of S5 under consideration is effectively a different linear projection of the same latent dynamics). However, the HiPPO-LegS matrix is not stably diagonalizable. Corollary 1 allows us to initialize MIMO SSMs with the diagonalizable HiPPO-N matrix to approximate the HiPPO-LegS matrix and expect the performance to be comparable.


###### 
Corollary 1 (Extension of Theorem 3 in Gu etВ al. (2022)).


Consider рқҗҖLegSвҲҲв„қNГ—NsubscriptрқҗҖLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗҖLegSNormalвҲҲв„қNГ—NsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗҒLegSвҲҲв„қNГ—H,рқҗҸLegSвҲҲв„қNformulae-sequencesubscriptрқҗҒLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқҗ»subscriptрқҗҸLegSsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times H},\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\in\mathbb{R}^{N} as defined in Appendix B.1.1. Given vector-valued inputs рқҗ®вҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қHрқҗ®рқ‘Ўsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»\mathbf{u}(t)\in\mathbb{R}^{H}, the ordinary differential equation dвҖӢрқҗұвҖІвҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=рқҗҖLegSNormalвҖӢрқҗұвҖІвҖӢ(t)+12вҖӢрқҗҒLegSвҖӢрқҗ®вҖӢ(t)dsuperscriptрқҗұвҖІрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў
superscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptрқҗұвҖІрқ‘Ў12subscriptрқҗҒLegSрқҗ®рқ‘Ў\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\prime}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\mathbf{x}^{\prime}(t)+\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\mathbf{u}(t) converges to dвҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=рқҗҖLegSвҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)+рқҗҒLegSвҖӢрқҗ®вҖӢ(t)dрқҗұрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў
subscriptрқҗҖLegSрқҗұрқ‘ЎsubscriptрқҗҒLegSрқҗ®рқ‘Ў\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\mathbf{x}(t)+\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\mathbf{u}(t) as NвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ҒN\rightarrow\infty.


###### Proof.


Theorem 3 in Gu etВ al. (2022) shows the following relationship for scalar input signals as NвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ҒN\rightarrow\infty:


 | 
 | dвҖӢрқҗұ(h)вҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=рқҗҖLegSNormalвҖӢрқҗұ(h)вҖӢ(t)+12вҖӢрқҗҒLegS(h)вҖӢu(h)вҖӢ(t),dsuperscriptрқҗұв„Һрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў
superscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptрқҗұв„Һрқ‘Ў12subscriptsuperscriptрқҗҒв„ҺLegSsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„Һрқ‘Ў\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}(t)+\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{(h)}_{\mathrm{LegS}}u^{(h)}(t),
 | 
 | (29)


where the only modification we have made is introducing the (h)в„Һ(h) superscript to allow us to explicitly index dimensions later. We define рқҗҒ=[рқҗҒLegS(1)вҖӢвҲЈвҖҰвҲЈвҖӢрқҗҒLegS(H)]рқҗҒdelimited-[]subscriptsuperscriptрқҗҒ1LegSdelimited-вҲЈвҲЈвҖҰsubscriptsuperscriptрқҗҒрқҗ»LegS\mathbf{B}=\left[\mathbf{B}^{(1)}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\mid\ldots\mid\mathbf{B}^{(H)}_{\mathrm{LegS}}\right]. We wish to extend this to the case of vector-valued input signals.


We first recall (15), which shows that the latent states of the MIMO S5 SSM are the summation of the latent states of the Hрқҗ»H SISO S4 SSMs (to which Theorem 3 from Gu etВ al. (2021a) applies). Although we derived (15) in discrete time, it applies equally in continuous time:


 | 
 | рқҗұвҖӢ(t)=вҲ‘h=1Hрқҗұ(h)вҖӢ(t).рқҗұрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»superscriptрқҗұв„Һрқ‘Ў\mathbf{x}(t)=\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}(t).
 | 
 | (30)


We can therefore define the derivative of the S5 state as:


 | 
 | dвҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt=вҲ‘h=1HdвҖӢрқҗұ(h)вҖӢ(t)dвҖӢt.dрқҗұрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»dsuperscriptрқҗұв„Һрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}=\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}.
 | 
 | (31)


Substituting (29) into this then yields:


 | 
 | dвҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)dвҖӢtdрқҗұрқ‘Ўdрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}(t)}{\mathrm{d}t}
 | =вҲ‘h=1H[рқҗҖLegSNormalвҖӢрқҗұ(h)вҖӢ(t)+12вҖӢрқҗҒLegS(h)вҖӢu(h)вҖӢ(t)],absentsuperscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»delimited-[]
superscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptрқҗұв„Һрқ‘Ў12superscriptsubscriptрқҗҒLegSв„Һsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„Һрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\left[\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}(t)+\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{(h)}u^{(h)}(t)\right],
 | 
 | (32)


 | 
 | 
 | =рқҗҖLegSNormalвҖӢвҲ‘h=1Hрқҗұ(h)вҖӢ(t)+12вҖӢвҲ‘h=1HрқҗҒLegS(h)вҖӢu(h)вҖӢ(t),absent
superscriptsubscriptрқҗҖLegSNormalsuperscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»superscriptрқҗұв„Һрқ‘Ў12superscriptsubscriptв„Һ1рқҗ»superscriptsubscriptрқҗҒLegSв„Һsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„Һрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{\mathrm{Normal}}\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\mathbf{x}^{(h)}(t)+\frac{1}{2}\sum\nolimits_{h=1}^{H}\mathbf{B}_{\mathrm{LegS}}^{(h)}u^{(h)}(t),
 | 
 | (33)


 | 
 | 
 | =рқҗҖLegSNormalвҖӢрқҗұвҖӢ(t)+12вҖӢрқҗҒLegSвҖӢрқҗ®вҖӢ(t).absent

 | 
 | (34)


вҲҺ


This equivalence motivates initializing S5 state matrices with the diagonalizable HiPPO-N matrix and suggests that we can expect to see similar performance gains.


### 
D.4 Relaxing the Assumptions


Here we discuss how relaxing the constraint on S5вҖҷs latent size from Assumption 4 helps to relax the assumptions on the tied S4 SSM state matrices (Assumption 2) and timescales (Assumption 3) as well as the tied output matrices that result from Proposition 2.


We start by considering the case when the S5 SSM state matrix is block-diagonal. Consider an S5 SSM with latent size JвҖӢN=рқ’ӘвҖӢ(H)рқҗҪрқ‘Ғрқ’Әрқҗ»JN=\mathcal{O}(H) and block-diagonal рқҗҖвҲҲв„қJвҖӢNГ—JвҖӢNрқҗҖsuperscriptв„қрқҗҪрқ‘ҒрқҗҪрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{R}^{JN\times JN}, dense рқҗҒвҲҲв„қJвҖӢNГ—HрқҗҒsuperscriptв„қрқҗҪрқ‘Ғрқҗ»\mathbf{B}\in\mathbb{R}^{JN\times H}, dense рқҗӮвҲҲв„қHГ—JвҖӢNрқҗӮsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»рқҗҪрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{C}\in\mathbb{R}^{H\times JN}, and JрқҗҪJ different timescale parameters рқҡ«вҲҲв„қJрқҡ«superscriptв„қрқҗҪ\mathbf{\Delta}\in\mathbb{R}^{J}. As a result of the block-diagonal state matrix, this system has a latent state рқҗұkвҲҲв„қJвҖӢNsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳsuperscriptв„қрқҗҪрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{x}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{JN} that can be partitioned into JрқҗҪJ different states рқҗұk(j)вҲҲв„қNsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳрқ‘—superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(j)}\in\mathbb{R}^{N}. We can then partition this system into JрқҗҪJ different subsystems and discretize each subsystem with one of the О”(j)superscriptО”рқ‘—\Delta^{(j)} to get the following discretized system:


 | 
 | рқҗҖВҜ=[рқҗҖВҜ(1)вӢұрқҗҖВҜ(J)],рқҗҒВҜ=[рқҗҒВҜ(1)вӢ®рқҗҒВҜ(J)],рқҗӮ=[рқҗӮ(1)вҖӢвҲЈвӢҜвҲЈвҖӢрқҗӮ(J)],formulae-sequenceВҜрқҗҖdelimited-[]superscriptВҜрқҗҖ1missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionвӢұmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionsuperscriptВҜрқҗҖрқҗҪformulae-sequenceВҜрқҗҒdelimited-[]superscriptВҜрқҗҒ1missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionвӢ®missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionsuperscriptВҜрқҗҒрқҗҪmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionрқҗӮdelimited-[]superscriptрқҗӮ1delimited-вҲЈвҲЈвӢҜsuperscriptрқҗӮрқҗҪmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression\overline{\mathbf{A}}=\left[\begin{array}[]{ccc}\overline{\mathbf{A}}^{(1)}&&\\
&\ddots&\\
&&\overline{\mathbf{A}}^{(J)}\end{array}\right],\quad\overline{\mathbf{B}}=\left[\begin{array}[]{ccc}\overline{\mathbf{B}}^{(1)}\\
\vdots\\
\overline{\mathbf{B}}^{(J)}\end{array}\right],\quad\mathbf{C}=\left[\begin{array}[]{ccc}\mathbf{C}^{(1)}\mid\cdots\mid\mathbf{C}^{(J)}\end{array}\right],
 | 
 | (35)


where рқҗҖВҜ(j)вҲҲв„қNГ—NsuperscriptВҜрқҗҖрқ‘—superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\overline{\mathbf{A}}^{(j)}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗҒВҜ(j)вҲҲв„қNГ—HsuperscriptВҜрқҗҒрқ‘—superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқҗ»\overline{\mathbf{B}}^{(j)}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times H} and рқҗӮ(j)вҲҲв„қHГ—NsuperscriptрқҗӮрқ‘—superscriptв„қрқҗ»рқ‘Ғ\mathbf{C}^{(j)}\in\mathbb{R}^{H\times N}. It follows that this partitioned system can be also be viewed as JрқҗҪJ independent Nрқ‘ҒN-dimensional S5 SSM subsystems and the output of the overall system is the sum of the output of the JрқҗҪJ subsystems


 | 
 | рқҗІksubscriptрқҗІрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\mathbf{y}_{k}
 | =рқҗӮрқҗұkabsentsubscriptрқҗӮрқҗұрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}_{k}
 | 
 | (36)


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ‘j=1JрқҗӮ(j)вҖӢрқҗұk(j).absentsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—1рқҗҪsuperscriptрқҗӮрқ‘—superscriptsubscriptрқҗұрқ‘ҳрқ‘—\displaystyle=\sum_{j=1}^{J}\mathbf{C}^{(j)}\mathbf{x}_{k}^{(j)}.
 | 
 | (37)


It follows from Proposition 2 that the dynamics of each of these JрқҗҪJ S5 SSM subsystems can be related to the dynamics of a different S4 system from Proposition 2. Each of these S4 systems has its own bank of tied S4 SSMs (cf. Assumptions 2, 3). Importantly, each of the JрқҗҪJ S4 systems can have its own state matrix, timescale parameter and output matrix shared across its Hрқҗ»H S4 SSMs. Thus, the outputs of a JвҖӢNрқҗҪрқ‘ҒJN dimensional S5 SSM can be equivalent to the linear combination of the latent states of JрқҗҪJ different S4 systems from Proposition 2. This fact motivates the option to initialize a block-diagonal S5 state matrix with several HiPPO-N matrices on the blocks, rather than just initializing with one larger HiPPO-N matrix. In practice we found the block-diagonal initialization to improve performance on many tasks, see Appendix E.


### 
D.5 Timescale Parameterization


Finally, we take a closer look at the parameterization of the timescale parameters рқҡ«рқҡ«\mathbf{\Delta}. As discussed in Section 4.3, S4 can learn a different timescale parameter for each S4 SSM, potentially allowing it to capture different timescales of the data. Further, the initialization of the timescales can be importantВ (Gu etВ al., 2023; Gupta etВ al., 2022), and limiting to sampling a single initial parameter may lead to poor initialization. The discussion in the previous section motivates potentially learning JрқҗҪJ different timescale parameters, one for each of the JрқҗҪJ subsystems. However, in practice, we found better performance when using Pрқ‘ғP different timescale parameters, one for each of the states. On the one hand, this can be viewed simply as learning a different scaling for each of the eigenvalues in the diagonalized system (see Eq. (6)). On the other hand, this could be viewed as increasing the number of timescale parameters sampled at initialization, helping to combat the possibility of poor initialization. Of course, the system could learn to use just a single timescale by setting all of the timescales to be the same. See further discussion in the ablation study in Appendix E.


## 
Appendix E Ablations


We perform several ablations to empirically explore different aspects of S5.


### 
E.1 S5 latent size, block-diagonal initialization, and timescale parameterization


The discussion in Section 4 and Appendix D raises several interesting questions: How does S5 perform when the latent size Pрқ‘ғP is restricted to be equal to the latent size Nрқ‘ҒN used by each of S4вҖҷs SSMs? How important is the timescale parameterization discussed in Appendix D.5? How important is the block-diagonal initialization? Table 5 displays the results of an ablation study performed on the LRA tasks to get a better sense of this. We consider 3 versions of S5.


The first version uses the same general architecture (e.g. number of input/output features Hрқҗ»H, number of layers, etc) as reported for the S4/S4D variants in Gu etВ al. (2022), sets the S5 SSM latent size Pрқ‘ғP to be equal to the latent size N=64рқ‘Ғ64N=64 used by each of the S4 SSMs, and uses only a single scalar timescale parameter О”вҲҲв„қО”в„қ\Delta\in\mathbb{R}. This is essentially the version of S5 we consider in Proposition 2. We observe that this constrained version of S5 actually performs well on most tasks, though struggles to perform comparably to the S4 baselines on Image and ListOps.


The second version of S5 is exactly the same as the first except we parameterize the timescale parameter as a vector рқҡ«вҲҲв„қNрқҡ«superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{\Delta}\in\mathbb{R}^{N}. We observe uniform improvements over the scalar timescale parameterization and this reflects our general findings when training S5.


Finally, the complexity analysis and runtime comparison in Appendix C.2 suggests the latent size of S5 can be increased while maintaining similar complexity and practical runtimes as S4. We include the unconstrained version of S5 reported in our main results that uses the settings reported in the hyperparameter Table 11. These models were allowed to be parameterized with fewer input/output features Hрқҗ»H (to ensure similar parameter counts to the S4 baselines) and generally used larger latent sizes P>Nрқ‘ғрқ‘ҒP>N. Further, we swept over the use of a block-diagonal initialization or not and the number of blocks to use (where J=1рқҗҪ1J=1 indicates no block-diagonal initialization was used). All models benefited from the block-diagonal initialization for the LRA tasks (See Table 11 ).


Table 5: Ablations on the LRA benchmark tasksВ (Tay etВ al., 2021). S4 results were taken from Gu etВ al. (2022; 2021a). Note that the total parameter count for all models in this table are commensurate, and hence variations in performance cannot be attributed to a model having drastically more parameters.

### 
E.2 Importance of HiPPO-N and continuous-time parameterization


We perform a further ablation study to gain insight into the differences between S5 and prior attempts at parallelized linear RNNs (discussed in Section 5) focusing on what appears to be the distinguishing features: continuous-time parameterizations and HiPPO initializations. We compare different initializations of the state matrix: random Gaussian, random antisymmetric, and HiPPO-N. The antisymmetric initialization is interesting because prior work considered these matrices in RNNs for long-range dependenciesВ (Chang etВ al., 2019), and because the HiPPO-LegS matrix can be parameterized in a way related to antisymmetric matricesВ (Gu etВ al., 2021a). Moreover, to compare to a setup more akin to the previous parallelized linear RNN work, we also consider a direct discrete-time parameterization of S5 that does not perform repeated discretization during training or learn the timescale parameterВ рқҡ«рқҡ«\mathbf{\Delta}. We present the results of this ablation study in Table 6 (along with S5). We consider three of the LRA tasks that vary in length and difficulty.


Table 6: S5 Initialization and Parameterization Ablation Study. вң—В  indicates the model did not improve over random guessing.

The main takeaway is that the only approach that consistently performs well on all tasks, including the ability to solve Path-X, is the S5 approach that uses the continuous-time parameterization and HiPPO initialization. We also note that we observed the discrete time/HiPPO-N matrix configuration to be difficult to train due to stability issues, typically requiring a much lower learning rate.


### 
E.3 S4D Initialization ablations


Finally, Gu etВ al. (2022) propose several alternative diagonal matrices to the diagonalized HiPPO-N matrix, including the S4D-Inv and S4D-Lin matrices. They perform an ablation on the LRA tasks where they simply replace the diagonalized HiPPO-N matrix with the S4D-Inv and S4D-Lin matrices while keeping all other factors the same. We include these results in Table 7. In Table 7, we also include results for a similar ablation in S5 by using these matrices to initialize S5 in place of the HiPPO-N matrix while keeping all other factors constant. Both matrices perform well on most tasks with the exception of the S4D-Lin matrix on Path-X. Interestingly, one of these runs reached 96.79%percent96.7996.79\%, however the other runs did not exceed random guessing on this task. Future exploration of these and other matrices are an interesting direction for future work.
