# The Rust Programming Language вҖ” Working with Environment Variables

## Working with Environment Variables

WeвҖҷll improve the `minigrep` binary by adding an extra feature: an option for
case-insensitive searching that the user can turn on via an environment
variable. We could make this feature a command line option and require that
users enter it each time they want it to apply, but by instead making it an
environment variable, we allow our users to set the environment variable once
and have all their searches be case insensitive in that terminal session.


<a id="writing-a-failing-test-for-the-case-insensitive-search-function"></a>

### Writing a Failing Test for Case-Insensitive Search

We first add a new `search_case_insensitive` function to the `minigrep` library
that will be called when the environment variable has a value. WeвҖҷll continue
to follow the TDD process, so the first step is again to write a failing test.
WeвҖҷll add a new test for the new `search_case_insensitive` function and rename
our old test from `one_result` to `case_sensitive` to clarify the differences
between the two tests, as shown in Listing 12-20.

<Listing number="12-20" file-name="src/lib.rs" caption="Adding a new failing test for the case-insensitive function weвҖҷre about to add">

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

Note that weвҖҷve edited the old testвҖҷs `contents` too. WeвҖҷve added a new line
with the text `"Duct tape."` using a capital _D_ that shouldnвҖҷt match the query
`"duct"` when weвҖҷre searching in a case-sensitive manner. Changing the old test
in this way helps ensure that we donвҖҷt accidentally break the case-sensitive
search functionality that weвҖҷve already implemented. This test should pass now
and should continue to pass as we work on the case-insensitive search.

The new test for the case-_insensitive_ search uses `"rUsT"` as its query. In
the `search_case_insensitive` function weвҖҷre about to add, the query `"rUsT"`
should match the line containing `"Rust:"` with a capital _R_ and match the
line `"Trust me."` even though both have different casing from the query. This
is our failing test, and it will fail to compile because we havenвҖҷt yet defined
the `search_case_insensitive` function. Feel free to add a skeleton
implementation that always returns an empty vector, similar to the way we did
for the `search` function in Listing 12-16 to see the test compile and fail.

### Implementing the `search_case_insensitive` Function

The `search_case_insensitive` function, shown in Listing 12-21, will be almost
the same as the `search` function. The only difference is that weвҖҷll lowercase
the `query` and each `line` so that whatever the case of the input arguments,
theyвҖҷll be the same case when we check whether the line contains the query.

<Listing number="12-21" file-name="src/lib.rs" caption="Defining the `search_case_insensitive` function to lowercase the query and the line before comparing them">

```rust,noplayground
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

First, we lowercase the `query` string and store it in a new variable with the
same name, shadowing the original `query`. Calling `to_lowercase` on the query
is necessary so that no matter whether the userвҖҷs query is `"rust"`, `"RUST"`,
`"Rust"`, or `"rUsT"`, weвҖҷll treat the query as if it were `"rust"` and be
insensitive to the case. While `to_lowercase` will handle basic Unicode, it
wonвҖҷt be 100 percent accurate. If we were writing a real application, weвҖҷd want
to do a bit more work here, but this section is about environment variables,
not Unicode, so weвҖҷll leave it at that here.

Note that `query` is now a `String` rather than a string slice because calling
`to_lowercase` creates new data rather than referencing existing data. Say the
query is `"rUsT"`, as an example: That string slice doesnвҖҷt contain a lowercase
`u` or `t` for us to use, so we have to allocate a new `String` containing
`"rust"`. When we pass `query` as an argument to the `contains` method now, we
need to add an ampersand because the signature of `contains` is defined to take
a string slice.

Next, we add a call to `to_lowercase` on each `line` to lowercase all
characters. Now that weвҖҷve converted `line` and `query` to lowercase, weвҖҷll
find matches no matter what the case of the query is.

LetвҖҷs see if this implementation passes the tests:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

Great! They passed. Now letвҖҷs call the new `search_case_insensitive` function
from the `run` function. First, weвҖҷll add a configuration option to the `Config`
struct to switch between case-sensitive and case-insensitive search. Adding
this field will cause compiler errors because we arenвҖҷt initializing this field
anywhere yet:

<span class="filename">Filename: src/main.rs</span>

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

We added the `ignore_case` field that holds a Boolean. Next, we need the `run`
function to check the `ignore_case` fieldвҖҷs value and use that to decide
whether to call the `search` function or the `search_case_insensitive`
function, as shown in Listing 12-22. This still wonвҖҷt compile yet.

<Listing number="12-22" file-name="src/main.rs" caption="Calling either `search` or `search_case_insensitive` based on the value in `config.ignore_case`">

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

Finally, we need to check for the environment variable. The functions for
working with environment variables are in the `env` module in the standard
library, which is already in scope at the top of _src/main.rs_. WeвҖҷll use the
`var` function from the `env` module to check to see if any value has been set
for an environment variable named `IGNORE_CASE`, as shown in Listing 12-23.

<Listing number="12-23" file-name="src/main.rs" caption="Checking for any value in an environment variable named `IGNORE_CASE`">

```rust,ignore,noplayground
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

Here, we create a new variable, `ignore_case`. To set its value, we call the
`env::var` function and pass it the name of the `IGNORE_CASE` environment
variable. The `env::var` function returns a `Result` that will be the
successful `Ok` variant that contains the value of the environment variable if
the environment variable is set to any value. It will return the `Err` variant
if the environment variable is not set.

WeвҖҷre using the `is_ok` method on the `Result` to check whether the environment
variable is set, which means the program should do a case-insensitive search.
If the `IGNORE_CASE` environment variable isnвҖҷt set to anything, `is_ok` will
return `false` and the program will perform a case-sensitive search. We donвҖҷt
care about the _value_ of the environment variable, just whether itвҖҷs set or
unset, so weвҖҷre checking `is_ok` rather than using `unwrap`, `expect`, or any
of the other methods weвҖҷve seen on `Result`.

We pass the value in the `ignore_case` variable to the `Config` instance so
that the `run` function can read that value and decide whether to call
`search_case_insensitive` or `search`, as we implemented in Listing 12-22.

LetвҖҷs give it a try! First, weвҖҷll run our program without the environment
variable set and with the query `to`, which should match any line that contains
the word _to_ in all lowercase:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

Looks like that still works! Now letвҖҷs run the program with `IGNORE_CASE` set
to `1` but with the same query `to`:

```console
$ IGNORE_CASE=1 cargo run -- to poem.txt
```

If youвҖҷre using PowerShell, you will need to set the environment variable and
run the program as separate commands:

```console
PS> $Env:IGNORE_CASE=1; cargo run -- to poem.txt
```

This will make `IGNORE_CASE` persist for the remainder of your shell session.
It can be unset with the `Remove-Item` cmdlet:

```console
PS> Remove-Item Env:IGNORE_CASE
```

We should get lines that contain _to_ that might have uppercase letters:

<!-- manual-regeneration
cd listings/ch12-an-io-project/listing-12-23
IGNORE_CASE=1 cargo run -- to poem.txt
can't extract because of the environment variable
-->

```console
Are you nobody, too?
How dreary to be somebody!
To tell your name the livelong day
To an admiring bog!
```

Excellent, we also got lines containing _To_! Our `minigrep` program can now do
case-insensitive searching controlled by an environment variable. Now you know
how to manage options set using either command line arguments or environment
variables.

Some programs allow arguments _and_ environment variables for the same
configuration. In those cases, the programs decide that one or the other takes
precedence. For another exercise on your own, try controlling case sensitivity
through either a command line argument or an environment variable. Decide
whether the command line argument or the environment variable should take
precedence if the program is run with one set to case sensitive and one set to
ignore case.

The `std::env` module contains many more useful features for dealing with
environment variables: Check out its documentation to see what is available.

<|endoftext|>

# Combining Recurrent, Convolutional, and Continuous-time Models (LRU/LSSL) вҖ” Gu et al. 2022
# ArXiv: 2110.13985
# Fuente: arXiv (acceso abierto)

## Abstract


Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), temporal convolutions, and neural differential equations (NDEs) are popular families of deep learning models for time-series data,
each with unique strengths and tradeoffs in modeling power and computational efficiency.
We introduce a simple sequence model inspired by control systems that generalizes these approaches while addressing their shortcomings.
The Linear State-Space Layer (LSSL) maps a sequence uвҶҰymaps-toрқ‘ўрқ‘Ұu\mapsto y by simply simulating a linear continuous-time state-space representation xЛҷ=AвҖӢx+BвҖӢu,y=CвҖӢx+DвҖӢuformulae-sequenceЛҷрқ‘Ҙ
рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқҗөрқ‘ўрқ‘Ұ
рқҗ¶рқ‘Ҙрқҗ·рқ‘ў\dot{x}=Ax+Bu,y=Cx+Du.
Theoretically, we show that LSSL models are closely related to the three aforementioned families of models and inherit their strengths.
For example, they generalize convolutions to continuous-time, explain common RNN heuristics,
and share features of NDEs such as time-scale adaptation.
We then incorporate and generalize recent theory on continuous-time memorization to introduce a trainable subset of structured matrices AрқҗҙA that endow LSSLs with long-range memory.
Empirically, stacking LSSL layers into a simple deep neural network obtains state-of-the-art results across time series benchmarks for long dependencies in sequential image classification, real-world healthcare regression tasks, and speech.
On a difficult speech classification task with length-16000 sequences, LSSL outperforms prior approaches by 24 accuracy points, and even outperforms
baselines that use hand-crafted features on 100x shorter sequences.


## 
1 Introduction


A longstanding challenge in machine learning is efficiently modeling sequential data longer than a few thousand time steps.
The usual paradigms for designing sequence models involve recurrence (e.g. RNNs), convolutions (e.g. CNNs), or differential equations (e.g. NDEs),
which each come with tradeoffs.
For example, RNNs are a natural stateful model for sequential data that require only constant computation/storage per time step, but are slow to train and suffer from optimization difficulties (e.g., the "vanishing gradient problem" [39]), which empirically limits their ability to handle long sequences.
CNNs encode local context and enjoy fast, parallelizable training, but are not sequential, resulting in more expensive inference and an inherent limitation on the context length.
NDEs are a principled mathematical model that can theoretically address continuous-time problems and long-term dependencies [37], but are
very inefficient.


Ideally, a model family would combine the strengths of these paradigms, providing properties like parallelizable training (convolutional), stateful inference (recurrence) and time-scale adaptation (differential equations), while handling very long sequences in a computationally efficient way.
Several recent works have turned to this question.
These include the CKConv, which models a continuous convolution kernelВ [44]; several ODE-inspired RNNs, such as the UnICORNNВ [47];
the LMU, which speeds up
a specific linear recurrence using convolutionsВ [58, 12];
and HiPPOВ [24], a generalization of the LMU that introduces a theoretical framework for continuous-time memorization.
However, these model families come at the price of reduced expressivity:
intuitively, a family that is both convolutional and recurrent should be more restrictive than either.


Figure 1: (Three views of the LSSL)
A Linear State Space Layer layer is a map utвҲҲв„қвҶ’ytвҲҲв„қsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўв„қвҶ’subscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўв„қu_{t}\in\mathbb{R}\to y_{t}\in\mathbb{R}, where each feature utвҶҰytmaps-tosubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўu_{t}\mapsto y_{t} is defined by discretizing a state-space model A,B,C,D
- рқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶рқҗ·A,B,C,D with a parameter О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t. The underlying state space model defines a discrete recurrence through combining the state matrix AрқҗҙA and timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t into a transition matrix AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A}.
(Left)
As an implicit continuous model, irregularly-spaced data can be handled by discretizing the same matrix AрқҗҙA using a different timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t.
(Center) As a recurrent model, inference can be performed efficiently by computing the layer timewise (i.e., one vertical slice at a time (ut,xt,yt),(ut+1,xt+1,yt+1),вҖҰ
- subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘ў
рқ‘Ў1subscriptрқ‘Ҙ
рқ‘Ў1subscriptрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ў1вҖҰ(u_{t},x_{t},y_{t}),(u_{t+1},x_{t+1},y_{t+1}),\dots), by unrolling the linear recurrence.
(Right) As a convolutional model, training can be performed efficiently by computing the layer depthwise in parallel (i.e., one horizontal slice at a time (ut)tвҲҲ[L],(yt)tвҲҲ[L],вҖҰ
- subscriptsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўdelimited-[]рқҗҝsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўdelimited-[]рқҗҝвҖҰ(u_{t})_{t\in[L]},(y_{t})_{t\in[L]},\dots), by convolving with a particular filter.


Our first goal is to construct an expressive model family that
combines all 3 paradigms while preserving their strengths.
The Linear State-Space Layer (LSSL) is a simple sequence model that maps a 1-dimensional function or sequence uвҖӢ(t)вҶҰyвҖӢ(t)maps-toрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўu(t)\mapsto y(t) through an implicit state xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) by simulating a linear continuous-time state-space representation in discrete-time


 | 
 | xЛҷвҖӢ(t)Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}(t)
 | =AвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+BвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)absent
рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗөрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=Ax(t)+Bu(t)
 | 
 | (1)


 | 
 | yвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў\displaystyle y(t)
 | =CвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+DвҖӢuвҖӢ(t),absent
рқҗ¶рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗ·рқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=Cx(t)+Du(t),
 | 
 | (2)


where AрқҗҙA controls the evolution of the system and B,C,D
- рқҗөрқҗ¶рқҗ·B,C,D are projection parameters.
The LSSL can be viewed as an instantiation of each family, inheriting their strengths (Fig.В 1):


- 
вҖў 


LSSLs are recurrent.
If a discrete step-size О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t is specified, the LSSL can be discretized into a linear recurrence using standard techniques, and simulated during inference as a stateful recurrent model with constant memory and computation per time step.


- 
вҖў 


LSSLs are convolutional.
The linear time-invariant systems defined by (1)+(2) are known to be explicitly representable as a continuous convolution.
Moreover, the discrete-time version can be parallelized during training using convolutionsВ [12, 44].


- 
вҖў 


LSSLs are continuous-time.
The LSSL itself is a differential equation. As such, it can perform unique applications of continuous-time models, such as simulating continuous processes, handling missing dataВ [45], and adapting to different timescales.


Surprisingly, we show that LSSLs do not sacrifice expressivity, and in fact generalize convolutions and RNNs.
First, classical results from control theory imply that all 1-D convolutional kernels can be approximated by an LSSLВ [59].
Additionally, we provide two results relating RNNs and ODEs that may be of broader interest,
e.g.В showing that some RNN architectural heuristics (such as gating mechanisms) are related to the step-size О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t and can actually be derived from ODE approximations.
As corollaries of these results, we show that popular RNN methods are special cases of LSSLs.


The generality of LSSLs does come with tradeoffs.
In particular, we describe and address two challenges that naive LSSL instantiations face when handling long sequences:
(i) they inherit the limitations of both RNNs and CNNs at remembering long dependencies, and
(ii) choosing the state matrix AрқҗҙA and timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t appropriately are critical to their performance, yet learning them is computationally infeasible.
We simultaneously address these challenges by specializing LSSLs using a carefully chosen class of structured matrices AрқҗҙA,
such that
(i) these matrices generalize prior work on continuous-time memory [24] and mathematically capture long dependencies with respect to a learnable family of measures, and
(ii) with new algorithms, LSSLs with these matrices AрқҗҙA can be theoretically sped up under certain computation models, even while learning the measure AрқҗҙA and timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t.


We empirically validate that LSSLs are widely effective on benchmark datasets and very long time series from healthcare sensor data, images, and speech.


- 
вҖў 


On benchmark datasets, LSSLs obtain
SoTA over recent RNN, CNN, and NDE-based methods
across sequential image classification tasks (e.g., by over 10% accuracy on sequential CIFAR)
and healthcare regression tasks with length-4000 time series (by up to 80% reduction in RMSE).


- 
вҖў 


To showcase the potential of LSSLs to unlock applications with extremely long sequences,
we introduce a new sequential CelebA classification task with length-38000 sequences.
A small LSSL
comes within 2.16 accuracy points of a specialized ResNet-18 vision architecture that has 10x more parameters and is trained directly on images.


- 
вҖў 


Finally, we test LSSLs on a difficult dataset of high-resolution speech clips,
where usual speech pipelines pre-process the signals to reduce the length by 100x.
When training on the raw length-16000 signals,
the LSSL not only (i) outperforms previous methods by over 20 accuracy points in 1/5 the training time,
but (ii) outperforms all baselines that use the pre-processed length-160 sequences,
overcoming the limitations of hand-crafted feature engineering.


Summary of Contributions


- 
вҖў 


We introduce Linear State-Space Layers (LSSLs), a simple sequence-to-sequence transformation that shares the modeling advantages of recurrent, convolutional, and continuous-time methods.
Conversely, we show that RNNs and CNNs can be seen as special cases of LSSLs (SectionВ 3).


- 
вҖў 


We prove that a structured subclass of LSSLs can learn representations that solve continuous-time memorization, allowing it to adapt its measure and timescale (SectionВ 4.1).
We also provide new algorithms for these LSSLs, showing that they can be sped up computationally under an arithmetic complexity model SectionВ 4.2.


- 
вҖў 


Empirically, we show that LSSLs stacked into a deep neural network are widely effective on time series data, even (or especially) on extremely long sequences (SectionВ 5).


## 
2 Technical Background


We summarize the preliminaries on differential equations that are necessary for this work.
We first
introduce two standard approximation schemes for differential equations that we will use to convert continuous-time models to discrete-time, and will be used in our results on understanding RNNs.
We give further context on the step size or timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t, which is a particularly important parameter involved in this approximation process.
Finally, we provide a summary of the HiPPO framework for continuous-time memorizationВ [24],
which will give us a mathematical tool for constructing LSSLs that can address long-term dependencies.


##### Approximations of differential equations.


Any differential equation xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=fвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\dot{x}(t)=f(t,x(t)) has an equivalent integral equation
xвҖӢ(t)=xвҖӢ(t0)+вҲ«t0tfвҖӢ(s,xвҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘sрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ў0superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ x(t)=x(t_{0})+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}f(s,x(s))\mathop{}\!ds.
This can be numerically solved by storing some approximation for xрқ‘Ҙx,
and keeping it fixed inside fвҖӢ(t,x)рқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙf(t,x) while iterating the equation.
For example, Picard iteration is often used to prove the existence of solutions to ODEs by iterating the equation
xi+1вҖӢ(t):=xiвҖӢ(t0)+вҲ«t0tfвҖӢ(s,xiвҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘sassignsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ
рқ‘–1рқ‘Ў
subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ў0superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘ subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–рқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ x_{i+1}(t):=x_{i}(t_{0})+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}f(s,x_{i}(s))\mathop{}\!ds
.
In other words, it finds a sequence of functions x0вҖӢ(t),x1вҖӢ(t),вҖҰ
- subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ЎвҖҰx_{0}(t),x_{1}(t),\dots that approximate the solution xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) of the integral equation.


##### Discretization.


On the other hand,
for a desired sequence of discrete times tisubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–t_{i}, approximations to xвҖӢ(t0),xвҖӢ(t1),вҖҰ
- рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ў1вҖҰx(t_{0}),x(t_{1}),\dots can be found
by iterating the equation
xвҖӢ(ti+1)=xвҖӢ(ti)+вҲ«titi+1fвҖӢ(s,xвҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘sрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ў
рқ‘–1
рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ў
рқ‘–1рқ‘“рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ x(t_{i+1})=x(t_{i})+\int_{t_{i}}^{t_{i+1}}f(s,x(s))\mathop{}\!ds.
Different ways of approximating the RHS integral lead to different discretization schemes.
We single out a discretization method called the generalized bilinear transform (GBT) which is specialized to linear ODEs of the form (1).
Given a step size О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t, the GBT update is


 | 
 | xвҖӢ(t+О”вҖӢt)=(IвҲ’ОұвҖӢО”вҖӢtвӢ…A)вҲ’1вҖӢ(I+(1вҲ’Оұ)вҖӢО”вҖӢtвӢ…A)вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+О”вҖӢtвҖӢ(IвҲ’ОұвҖӢО”вҖӢtвӢ…A)вҲ’1вҖӢBвӢ…uвҖӢ(t).рқ‘Ҙ

 | (3)


Three important cases are:
Оұ=0рқӣј0\alpha=0 becomes the classic Euler method which is simply the first-order approximation xвҖӢ(t+О”вҖӢt)=xвҖӢ(t)+О”вҖӢtвӢ…xвҖІвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙ
рқ‘ЎО”рқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ЎвӢ…О”рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ҘвҖІрқ‘Ўx(t+\Delta t)=x(t)+\Delta t\cdot x^{\prime}(t);
Оұ=1рқӣј1\alpha=1 is called the backward Euler method;
and Оұ=12рқӣј12\alpha=\frac{1}{2} is called the bilinear method, which preserves the stability of the systemВ [61].


In SectionВ 3.2 we will show that the backward Euler method and Picard iteration are actually related to RNNs.
On the other hand, the bilinear discretization will be our main method for computing accurate discrete-time approximations of our continuous-time models.
In particular, define AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} and BВҜВҜрқҗө\overline{B} to be the matrices appearing in (3) for Оұ=12рқӣј12\alpha=\frac{1}{2}.
Then the discrete-time state-space model
is


 | 
 | xtsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle x_{t}
 | =AВҜвҖӢxtвҲ’1+BВҜвҖӢutabsent
ВҜрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў1ВҜрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\overline{A}x_{t-1}+\overline{B}u_{t}
 | 
 | (4)


 | 
 | ytsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў\displaystyle y_{t}
 | =CвҖӢxt+DвҖӢut.absent
рқҗ¶subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗ·subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=Cx_{t}+Du_{t}.
 | 
 | (5)


##### 
О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t as a timescale.


In most models, the length of dependencies they can capture is roughly proportional to 1О”вҖӢt1О”рқ‘Ў\frac{1}{\Delta t}.
Thus we also refer to the step size О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t as a timescale.
This is an intrinsic part of converting a continuous-time ODE into a discrete-time recurrence,
and most ODE-based RNN models have it as an important and non-trainable hyperparameterВ [58, 24, 47].
On the other hand, in SectionВ 3.2 we show that the gating mechanism of classical RNNs is a version of learning О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t.
Moreover when viewed as a CNN, the timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t can be viewed as controlling the width of the convolution kernel (SectionВ 3.2).
Ideally, all ODE-based sequence models would be able to automatically learn the proper timescales.


##### Continuous-time memory.


Consider an input function uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t), a fixed probability measure ПүвҖӢ(t)рқң”рқ‘Ў\omega(t), and a sequence of Nрқ‘ҒN basis functions such as polynomials.
At every time tрқ‘Ўt, the history of uрқ‘ўu before time tрқ‘Ўt can be projected onto this basis,
which yields a vector of coefficients xвҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қNрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғx(t)\in\mathbb{R}^{N} that represents an optimal approximation of the history of uрқ‘ўu with respect to the provided measure Пүрқң”\omega.
The map taking the function uвҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўв„қu(t)\in\mathbb{R} to coefficients xвҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қNрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғx(t)\in\mathbb{R}^{N} is called the High-Order Polynomial Projection Operator (HiPPO) with respect to the measure Пүрқң”\omega.
In special cases such as the uniform measure Пү=рқ•ҖвҖӢ{[0,1]}рқң”рқ•Җ01\omega=\mathbb{I}\{[0,1]\} and the exponentially-decaying measure ПүвҖӢ(t)=expвҒЎ(вҲ’t)рқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў\omega(t)=\exp(-t),
Gu etВ al. [24] showed that xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) satisfies a differential equation xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=AвҖӢ(t)вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+BвҖӢ(t)вҖӢuвҖӢ(t)Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗөрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\dot{x}(t)=A(t)x(t)+B(t)u(t) (i.e., (1)) and derived closed forms for the matrix AрқҗҙA.
Their framework provides a principled way to design memory models handling long dependencies;
however, they prove only these few special cases.


## 
3 Linear State-Space Layers (LSSL)


We define our main abstraction, a model family that generalizes recurrence and convolutions.
SectionВ 3.1 first formally defines the LSSL, then discusses how to compute it
with multiple views.
Conversely, SectionВ 3.2 shows that LSSLs are related to mechanisms of the most popular RNNs.


### 
3.1 Different Views of the LSSL


Given a fixed state space representation A,B,C,D
- рқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶рқҗ·A,B,C,D, an LSSL is the sequence-to-sequence mapping defined by discretizing the linear state-space model (1) and (2).


Concretely, an LSSL layer has parameters
A,B,C,D
- рқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶рқҗ·A,B,C,D, and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t.
It operates on an input uвҲҲв„қLГ—Hрқ‘ўsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ»u\in\mathbb{R}^{L\times H} representing a sequence of length LрқҗҝL where each timestep has an Hрқҗ»H-dimensional feature vector.
Each feature hвҲҲ[H]в„Һdelimited-[]рқҗ»h\in[H] defines a sequence (ut(h))tвҲҲ[L]subscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўв„Һрқ‘Ўdelimited-[]рқҗҝ(u_{t}^{(h)})_{t\in[L]},
which is combined with a timescale О”вҖӢthО”subscriptрқ‘Ўв„Һ\Delta t_{h} to define an output y(h)вҲҲв„қLsuperscriptрқ‘Ұв„Һsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝy^{(h)}\in\mathbb{R}^{L} via the discretized state-space model (4)+(5).


Computationally, the discrete-time LSSL can be viewed in multiple ways (Fig.В 1).


##### As a recurrence.


The recurrent state
xtвҲ’1вҲҲв„қHГ—Nsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў1superscriptв„қрқҗ»рқ‘Ғx_{t-1}\in\mathbb{R}^{H\times N}
carries the context of all inputs before time tрқ‘Ўt.
The current state xtsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx_{t} and output ytsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўy_{t} can be computed by simply following equations (4)+(5). Thus the LSSL is a recurrent model
with efficient and stateful inference,
which can consume a (potentially unbounded) sequence of inputs while requiring fixed computation/storage per time step.


##### As a convolution.


For simplicity let the initial state be xвҲ’1=0subscriptрқ‘Ҙ10x_{-1}=0. Then (4)+(5) explicitly yields


 | 
 | yk=CвҖӢ(AВҜ)kвҖӢBВҜвҖӢu0+CвҖӢ(AВҜ)kвҲ’1вҖӢBВҜвҖӢu1+вӢҜ+CвҖӢAВҜвҖӢBВҜвҖӢukвҲ’1+BВҜвҖӢuk+DвҖӢuk.subscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
рқҗ¶superscriptВҜрқҗҙрқ‘ҳВҜрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ў0рқҗ¶superscriptВҜрқҗҙрқ‘ҳ1ВҜрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ў1вӢҜрқҗ¶ВҜрқҗҙВҜрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ1ВҜрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳрқҗ·subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳy_{k}=C\left(\overline{A}\right)^{k}\overline{B}u_{0}+C\left(\overline{A}\right)^{k-1}\overline{B}u_{1}+\dots+C\overline{A}\overline{B}u_{k-1}+\overline{B}u_{k}+Du_{k}.
 | 
 | (6)


Then yрқ‘Ұy is simply the (non-circular) convolution y=рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(AВҜ,BВҜ,C)вҲ—u+DвҖӢuрқ‘Ұ
вҲ—subscriptрқ’ҰрқҗҝВҜрқҗҙВҜрқҗөрқҗ¶рқ‘ўрқҗ·рқ‘ўy=\mathcal{K}_{L}(\overline{A},\overline{B},C)\ast u+Du,
where


 | 
 | рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(A,B,C)=(CвҖӢAiвҖӢB)iвҲҲ[L]вҲҲв„қL=(CвҖӢB,CвҖӢAвҖӢB,вҖҰ,CвҖӢALвҲ’1вҖӢB).subscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝрқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶subscriptрқҗ¶superscriptрқҗҙрқ‘–рқҗөрқ‘–delimited-[]рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ¶рқҗөрқҗ¶рқҗҙрқҗөвҖҰрқҗ¶superscriptрқҗҙрқҗҝ1рқҗө\mathcal{K}_{L}(A,B,C)=\left(CA^{i}B\right)_{i\in[L]}\in\mathbb{R}^{L}=(CB,CAB,\dots,CA^{L-1}B).
 | 
 | (7)


Thus the LSSL can be viewed as a convolutional model where
the entire output yвҲҲв„қHГ—Lрқ‘Ұsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»рқҗҝy\in\mathbb{R}^{H\times L} can be computed at once by a convolution,
which can be efficiently implemented with three FFTs.


##### The computational bottleneck.


We make a note that the bottleneck of (i) the recurrence view is matrix-vector multiplication (MVM) by the discretized state matrix AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} when simulating (4),
and (ii) the convolutional view is computing the Krylov function рқ’ҰLsubscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝ\mathcal{K}_{L} (7).
Throughout this section we assumed the LSSL parameters were fixed, which means that AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} and рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(A,B,C)subscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝрқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶\mathcal{K}_{L}(A,B,C) can be cached for efficiency.
However, learning the parameters AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t would involve repeatedly re-computing these, which is infeasible in practice.
We revisit and solve this problem in SectionВ 4.2.


### 
3.2 Expressivity of LSSLs


For a model to be both recurrent and convolutional, one might expect it to be limited in other ways.
Indeed, while [12, 44] also observe that certain recurrences can be replaced with a convolution, they note that it is not obvious if convolutions can be replaced by recurrences.
Moreover, while the LSSL is a linear recurrence, popular RNN models are nonlinear sequence models with activation functions between each time step.
We now show that LSSLs surprisingly do not have limited expressivity.


##### Convolutions are LSSLs.


A well-known fact about state-space systems (1)+(2) is that the output yрқ‘Ұy is related to the input uрқ‘ўu by a convolution yвҖӢ(t)=вҲ«hвҖӢ(П„)вҖӢuвҖӢ(tвҲ’П„)вҖӢрқ‘‘П„рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўв„ҺрқңҸрқ‘ўрқ‘ЎрқңҸdifferential-dрқңҸy(t)=\int h(\tau)u(t-\tau)d\tau with the impulse response hв„Һh of the system.
Conversely, a convolutional filter hв„Һh that is a rational function of degree Nрқ‘ҒN can be represented by a state-space model of size Nрқ‘ҒNВ [59].
Thus, an arbitrary convolutional filter hв„Һh can be approximated by a rational function (e.g., by PadГ© approximants) and represented by an LSSL.


In the particular case of LSSLs with HiPPO matrices (SectionsВ 2 andВ 4.1), there is another intuitive interpretation of how LSSL relate to convolutions.
Consider the special case when AрқҗҙA corresponds to a uniform measure (in the literature known as the LMU [58] or HiPPO-LegT [24] matrix).
Then for a fixed dвҖӢtрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўdt, equation (1) is simply memorizing the input within sliding windows of 1О”вҖӢt1О”рқ‘Ў\frac{1}{\Delta t} elements,
and equation (2) extracts features from this window.
Thus the LSSL can be interpreted as automatically learning convolution filters with a learnable kernel width.


##### RNNs are LSSLs.


We show two results about RNNs that may be of broader interest.
Our first result says that the ubiquitous gating mechanism of RNNs, commonly perceived as a heuristic to smooth optimization [28], is actually the analog of a step size or timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t.


###### 
Lemma 3.1.


A (1-D) gated recurrence xt=(1вҲ’ПғвҖӢ(z))вҖӢxtвҲ’1+ПғвҖӢ(z)вҖӢutsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
1рқңҺрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў1рқңҺрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўx_{t}=(1-\sigma(z))x_{t-1}+\sigma(z)u_{t}, where ПғрқңҺ\sigma is the sigmoid function and zрқ‘§z is an arbitrary expression,
can be viewed as the GBT(Оұ=1рқӣј1\alpha=1) (i.e., backwards-Euler) discretization of a 1-D linear ODE xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’xвҖӢ(t)+uвҖӢ(t)Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\dot{x}(t)=-x(t)+u(t).


###### Proof.


Applying a discretization requires a positive step size О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t.
The simplest way to parameterize a positive function is via the exponential function О”вҖӢt=expвҒЎ(z)О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘§\Delta t=\exp(z) applied to any expression zрқ‘§z.
Substituting this into (3) with A=вҲ’1,B=1,Оұ=1formulae-sequenceрқҗҙ1formulae-sequenceрқҗө1рқӣј1A=-1,B=1,\alpha=1
exactly produces the gated recurrence.
вҲҺ


WhileВ LemmaВ 3.1 involves approximating continuous systems using discretization, the second result is about approximating them using Picard iteration (SectionВ 2).
Roughly speaking, each layer of a deep linear RNN can be viewed as successive Picard iterates x0вҖӢ(t),x1вҖӢ(t),вҖҰ
- subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘ЎвҖҰx_{0}(t),x_{1}(t),... approximating a function xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) defined by a non-linear ODE.
This shows that we do not lose modeling power by using linear instead of non-linear recurrences, and that the nonlinearity can instead be вҖңmovedвҖқ to the depth direction of deep neural networks to improve speed without sacrificing expressivity.


###### 
Lemma 3.2.


(Infinitely) deep stacked LSSL layers of order N=1рқ‘Ғ1N=1 with position-wise non-linear functions can approximate any non-linear ODE xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’x+fвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\dot{x}(t)=-x+f(t,x(t)).


We note that many of the most popular and effective RNN variants
such as the LSTMВ [28], GRUВ [14], QRNNВ [5], and SRUВ [33],
involve a hidden state xtвҲҲв„қHsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptв„қрқҗ»x_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{H} that involves independently вҖңgatingвҖқ the Hрқҗ»H hidden units.
Applying LemmaВ 3.1, they actually also approximate an ODE of the form in LemmaВ 3.2.
Thus LSSLs and these popular RNN models can be seen to all approximate the same type of underlying continuous dynamics,
by using Picard approximations in the depth direction and discretization (gates) in the time direction.
AppendixВ C gives precise statements and proofs.


### 
3.3 Deep LSSLs


The basic LSSL is defined as a sequence-to-sequence map from в„қLвҶ’в„қLвҶ’superscriptв„қрқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝ\mathbbm{R}^{L}\to\mathbbm{R}^{L} on 1D sequences of length LрқҗҝL,
parameterized by parameters AвҲҲв„қNГ—N,BвҲҲв„қNГ—1,CвҲҲв„қ1Г—N,DвҲҲв„қ1Г—1,О”вҖӢtвҲҲв„қformulae-sequenceрқҗҙsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғformulae-sequenceрқҗөsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ1formulae-sequenceрқҗ¶superscriptв„қ1рқ‘Ғformulae-sequenceрқҗ·superscriptв„қ11О”рқ‘Ўв„қA\in\mathbbm{R}^{N\times N},B\in\mathbbm{R}^{N\times 1},C\in\mathbbm{R}^{1\times N},D\in\mathbbm{R}^{1\times 1},\Delta t\in\mathbbm{R}.
Given an input sequence with hidden dimension Hрқҗ»H (in other words a feature dimension greater than 111),
we simply broadcast the parameters B,C,D,О”вҖӢt
- рқҗөрқҗ¶рқҗ·О”рқ‘ЎB,C,D,\Delta t with an extra dimension Hрқҗ»H.
Each of these Hрқҗ»H copies is learned independently, so that there are Hрқҗ»H different versions of a 1D LSSL processing each of the input features independently.
Overall, the standalone LSSL layer is a sequence-to-sequence map with the same interface as standard sequence model layers such as RNNs, CNNs, and Transformers.


The full LSSL architecture in a deep neural network is defined similarly to standard sequence models such as deep ResNets and Transformers,
involving stacking LSSL layers connected with normalization layers and residual connections.
Full architecture details are described in AppendixВ B, including the initialization of AрқҗҙA and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t,
computational details,
and other architectural details.


## 
4 Combining LSSLs with Continuous-time Memorization


In SectionВ 3 we introduced the LSSL model and showed that it shares the strengths of convolutions and recurrences while also generalizing them.
We now discuss and address its main limitations,
in particular handling long dependencies (SectionВ 4.1) and efficient computation (SectionВ 4.2).


### 
4.1 Incorporating Long Dependencies into LSSLs


The generality of LSSLs means they can inherit the issues of recurrences and convolutions at addressing long dependencies (SectionВ 1). For example, viewed as a recurrence, repeated multiplication by AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} could suffer from the vanishing gradients problem [39, 44].
We confirm empirically that LSSLs with random state matrices AрқҗҙA are actually not effective (SectionВ 5.4) as a generic sequence model.


However, one advantage of these mathematical continuous-time models is that they are theoretically analyzable, and specific AрқҗҙA matrices can be derived to address this issue.
In particular, the HiPPO framework (SectionВ 2) describes how to memorize a function in continuous time with respect to a measure Пүрқң”\omegaВ [24].
This operator mapping a function to a continuous representation of its past is denoted рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—ҲвҒЎ(Пү)рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳрқң”\operatorname{\mathsf{hippo}}(\omega), and was shown to have the form of equationВ (1) in three special cases.
However, these matrices are non-trainable in the sense that no other AрқҗҙA matrices were known to be рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳрқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳ\operatorname{\mathsf{hippo}} operators.


To address this, we theoretically
resolve the open question from [24], showing that рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—ҲвҒЎ(Пү)рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳрқң”\operatorname{\mathsf{hippo}}(\omega) for any measure Пүрқң”\omega
111To be precise, the measures that correspond to orthogonal polynomials [52].
results in (1) with a structured matrix AрқҗҙA.


###### 
Theorem 1 (Informal).


For an arbitrary measure Пүрқң”\omega, the optimal memorization operator hippo(Пү)hippoрқң”\operatorname*{hippo}(\omega) has the form xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=AвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+BвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗөрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\dot{x}(t)=Ax(t)+Bu(t) (1) for a low recurrence-width (LRW)В [17] state matrix AрқҗҙA.


For measures covering the classical orthogonal polynomials (OPs)В [52] (in particular, corresponding to Jacobi and Laguerre polynomials), there is even more structure.


###### 
Corollary 4.1.


For Пүрқң”\omega corresponding to the classical OPs,
рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—ҲвҒЎ(Пү)рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳрқң”\operatorname{\mathsf{hippo}}(\omega) is 333-quasiseparable.


Although beyond the scope of this section, we mention that LRW matrices are a type of structured matrix that have linear MVMВ [17].
In AppendixВ D we define this class and prove TheoremВ 1.
Quasi-separable matrices are a related class of structured matrices with additional algorithmic properties.
We define these matrices in DefinitionВ 4 and prove CorollaryВ 4.1 in SectionВ D.3.


TheoremВ 1 tells us that a LSSL that uses a state matrix AрқҗҙA
within a particular class of structured matrices
would carry the theoretical interpretation of continuous-time memorization.
Ideally, we would be able to automatically learn the best AрқҗҙA within this class;
however, this runs into computational challenges which we address next (SectionВ 4.2).
For now, we define the LSSL-fixed or LSSL-f to be one where the AрқҗҙA matrix is fixed to one of the HiPPO matrices prescribed by [24].


### 
4.2 Theoretically Efficient Algorithms for the LSSL


Although AрқҗҙA and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t are the most critical parameters of an LSSL which govern the state-space (c.f.В SectionВ 4.1) and timescale (SectionsВ 2 andВ 3.2), they are not feasible to train in a naive LSSL.
In particular, SectionВ 3.1 noted that it would require efficient matrix-vector multiplication (MVM) and Krylov function (7) for AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} to compute the recurrent and convolutional views, respectively.
However, the former seems to involve a matrix inversion (3),
while the latter seems to require powering AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} up LрқҗҝL times.


In this section, we show that the same restriction of AрқҗҙA to the class of quasiseparable (CorollaryВ 4.1), which gives an LSSL the ability to theoretically remember long dependencies,
simultaneously grants it computational efficiency.


First of all, it is known that quasiseparable matrices have efficient (linear-time) MVMВ [40].
We show that they also have fast Krylov functions, allowing efficient training with convolutions.


###### 
Theorem 2.


For any kрқ‘ҳk-quasiseparable matrix AрқҗҙA (with constant kрқ‘ҳk)
and arbitrary B,C
- рқҗөрқҗ¶B,C, the Krylov function рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(A,B,C)subscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝрқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶\mathcal{K}_{L}(A,B,C) can be computed in quasi-linear time and space O~вҖӢ(N+L)~рқ‘Ӯ
рқ‘Ғрқҗҝ\tilde{O}(N+L) and logarithmic depth (i.e., is parallelizable).
The operation count is in an exact arithmetic model, not accounting for bit complexity or numerical stability.


We remark that TheoremВ 2 is non-obvious.
To illustrate, it is easy to see that unrolling (7) for a general matrix AрқҗҙA takes time LвҖӢN2рқҗҝsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2LN^{2}.
Even if AрқҗҙA is extremely structured with linear computation, it requires LвҖӢNрқҗҝрқ‘ҒLN operations and linear depth.
The depth can be reduced with the squaring technique (batch multiply by A,A2,A4,вҖҰ
- рқҗҙsuperscriptрқҗҙ2superscriptрқҗҙ4вҖҰA,A^{2},A^{4},\dots),
but this then requires LвҖӢNрқҗҝрқ‘ҒLN intermediate storage.
In fact, the algorithm for TheoremВ 2 is quite sophisticated (AppendixВ E) and
involves a divide-and-conquer recursion over matrices of polynomials, using the observation that (7) is related to the power series CвҖӢ(IвҲ’AвҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢBрқҗ¶superscriptрқҗјрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ1рқҗөC(I-Ax)^{-1}B .


Unless specified otherwise, the full LSSL refers to an LSSL with AрқҗҙA
satisfying CorollaryВ 4.1.
In conclusion, learning within this structured matrix family simultaneously endows LSSLs with long-range memory through TheoremВ 1 and is theoretically computationally feasible through TheoremВ 2.
We note the caveat that TheoremВ 2 is over exact arithmetic and not floating point numbers, and thus is treated more as a proof of concept that LSSLs can be computationally efficient in theory.
We comment more on the limitations of the LSSL in SectionВ 6.

We test LSSLs empirically on a range of time series datasets with sequences from length 160 up to 38000 (SectionsВ 5.1 andВ 5.2),
where they substantially improve over prior work.
We additionally validate the computational and modeling benefits of LSSLs from generalizing all three main model families (SectionВ 5.3),
and analyze the benefits of incorporating principled memory representations that can be learned (SectionВ 5.4).


##### Baselines.


Our tasks have extensive prior work and we evaluate against previously reported best results.
We highlight our primary baselines, three very recent works explicitly designed for long sequences:
CKConv (a continuous-time CNN)В [44], UnICORNN (an ODE-inspired RNN)В [47],
and Neural Controlled/Rough Differential Equations (NCDE/NRDE) (a sophisticated NDE)В [31, 37]. These are the only models we are aware of that have experimented with sequences of length >10k.


### 
5.1 Image and Time Series Benchmarks


Table 1: 
(Sequential CelebA Classification.)


 | 
 | LSSL-f
 | ResNet


 | Att.

 | MSO
 | 92.36

 | Smil.
 | 90.95
 | 92.89

 | 90.57
 | 93.25


We test on the sequential MNIST, permuted MNIST, and sequential CIFAR tasks (Fig.В 3),
popular benchmarks which were originally designed to test the ability of recurrent models to capture long-term dependencies of length up to 1k [2].
LSSL sets SoTA on sCIFAR by more than 10 points.
We note that all results were achieved with at least 5x fewer parameters than the previous SoTA (AppendixВ F).


We additionally use the BDIMC healthcare datasets (Fig.В 3), a suite of widely studied time series regression problems of length 4000 on estimating vital signs.
LSSL reduces RMSE by more than two-thirds on all datasets.


### 
5.2 Speech and Image Classification for Very Long Time Series


Raw speech is challenging for ML models due to high-frequency sampling resulting in very long sequences. Traditional systems involve complex pipelines that require
feeding mixed-and-matched hand-crafted features into DNNsВ [42].
TableВ 2 reports results for the Speech Commands (SC) datasetВ [31] for classification of 1-second audio clips.
Few methods have made progress on the raw speech signal,
instead requiring pre-processing with standard mel-frequency cepstrum coefficients (MFCC).
By contrast, LSSL sets SoTA on this dataset while training on the raw signal.
We note that MFCC extracts sliding window frequency coefficients and thus is related to the coefficients xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) defined by LSSL-f (SectionВ 2, SectionВ 4.1, [24], AppendixВ D).
Consequently, LSSL may be interpreted as automatically learning MFCC-type features in a trainable basis.


To stress-test the LSSLвҖҷs ability to handle extremely long sequences, we create a challenging new sequential-CelebA task, where we classify 178Г—218178218178\times 218 images = 38000-length sequences for 4 facial attributes: Attractive (Att.), Mouth Slightly Open (MSO), Smiling (Smil.), Wearing Lipstick (WL) [36]. We chose the 4 most class-balanced attributes to avoid well-known problems with class imbalance. LSSL-f comes close to matching the performance of a specialized ResNet-18 image classification architecture that has 10Г—10\times the parameters (TableВ 1). We emphasize we are the first to demonstrate that this is possible to do with a generic sequence model.

### 
5.3 Advantages of Recurrent, Convolutional, and Continuous-time Models


We validate that the generality of LSSLs endows it with the strengths of all three families.


Convergence Speed.
As a recurrent and NDE model that incorporates new theory for continuous-time memory (SectionВ 4.1), the LSSL has strong inductive bias for sequential data,
and converges rapidly to SoTA results on our benchmarks.
With its convolutional view, training can be parallelized and it is also computationally efficient in practice.
TableВ 3 compares the time it takes the LSSL-f to achieve SoTA, in either sample (measured by epochs) or computational (measured by wall clock) complexity.
In all cases, LSSLs reached the target in a fraction of the time of the previous model.

Timescale Adaptation.
TableВ 2 also reports the results of continuous-time models that are able to handle unique settings such as
missing data in time series,
or test-time shift in timescale (we note that this is a realistic problem, e.g., when deployed healthcare models are tested on EEG signals that are sampled at a different rateВ [48, 49]).
We note that many of these baselines were custom designed for such settings, which is of independent interest.
On the other hand, LSSLs perform timescale adaptation by simply changing its О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t values at inference time, while still outperforming the performance of prior methods with no shift.
Additional results on the CharacterTrajectories dataset from prior workВ [31, 44] are in AppendixВ F, where LSSL is competitive with the best baselines.


### 
5.4 LSSL Ablations: Learning the Memory Dynamics and Timescale


We demonstrate that the О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t and AрқҗҙA parameters, which LSSLs are able to automatically learn in contrast to prior work, are indeed critical to the performance of these continuous-time models.
We note that learning О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t adds only OвҖӢ(H)рқ‘Ӯрқҗ»O(H) parameters and learning AрқҗҙA adds OвҖӢ(N)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒO(N) parameters, adding less than 1% parameter count compared to the base models with OвҖӢ(HвҖӢN)рқ‘Ӯрқҗ»рқ‘ҒO(HN) parameters.


Memory dynamics AрқҗҙA.
We validate that vanilla LSSLs suffer from the modeling issues described in SectionВ 4. We tested that LSSLs with random AрқҗҙA matrices (normalized appropriately) perform very poorly (e.g., 62% on pMNIST).
Further, we note the consistent increase in performance from LSSL-f to LSSL despite the negligible parameter difference.
These ablations show that
(i) incorporating the theory of TheoremВ 1 is actually necessary for LSSLs,
and (ii) further training the structured AрқҗҙA is additionally helpful, which can be interpreted as learning the measure for memorization (SectionВ 4.1).


Timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t.
SectionВ 3.2 showed that LSSLвҖҷs ability to learn О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t is its direct generalization of the critical gating mechanism of popular RNNs,
which previous ODE-based RNN models [58, 24, 12, 47] cannot learn.
We note that on sCIFAR, LSSL-f with poorly-specified О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t gets only 49.3%percent49.349.3\% accuracy.
Additional results in AppendixВ F show that learning О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t alone provides an orthogonal boost to learning AрқҗҙA,
and visualizes the noticeable change in О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t over the course of training.


## 
6 Discussion


In this work we introduced a simple and principled model (LSSL) inspired by a fundamental representation of physical systems.
We showed theoretically and empirically that it generalizes and inherits the strengths of the main families of modern time series models,
that its main limitations of long-term memory can be resolved with new theory on continuous-time memorization,
and that it is empirically effective on difficult tasks with very long sequences.


Related work.
The LSSL is related to several rich lines of work on recurrent, convolutional, and continuous-time models,
as well as sequence models addressing long dependencies.
AppendixВ A provides an extended related work connecting these topics.


Tuning.
Our models are very simple, consisting of identical L(inear)SSL layers with simple position-wise non-linear modules between layers (AppendixВ B).
Our models were able to train at much higher learning rates than baselines and were not sensitive to hyperparameters, of which we did light tuning primarily on learning rate and dropout.
In contrast to previous baselinesВ [4, 31, 44], we did not use hyperparameters for improving stability and regularization such as weight decay, gradient clipping, weight norm, input dropout, etc.
While the most competitive recent works introduce at least one hyperparameter of critical importance (e.g. depth and step sizeВ [37], Оұрқӣј\alpha and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta tВ [47], Пү0subscriptрқң”0\omega_{0}В [44]) that are difficult to tune, the LSSL-fixed has only О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t, which the full LSSL can even learn automatically (at the expense of speed).


Limitations.
SectionsВ 1, 3 andВ 1 mention that a potential benefit of having the recurrent representation of LSSLs may endow it with efficient inference.
While this is theoretically possible, this work did not experiment on any applications that leverage this.
Follow-up work showed that it is indeed possible in practice to speed up some applications at inference time.


TheoremВ 2вҖҷs algorithm is sophisticated (AppendixВ D) and was not implemented in the first version of this work.
A follow-up to this paper found that it is not numerically stable and thus not usable on hardware.
Thus the algorithmic contributions in TheoremВ 2 serve the purpose of a proof-of-concept that fast algorithms for the LSSL do exist in other computation models (i.e., arithmetic operations instead of floating point operations), and leave an open question as to whether fast, numerically stable, and practical algorithms for the LSSL exist.


As described in AppendixВ B,
by freezing the AрқҗҙA matrix and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t timescale,
the LSSL-fixed is able to be computed much faster than the full LSSL,
and is comparable to prior models in practice (TableВ 3).
However, beyond computational complexity, there is also a consideration of space efficiency.
Both the LSSL and LSSL-fixed suffer from a large amount of space overhead (described in AppendixВ B) вҖ“ using OвҖӢ(NвҖӢL)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒрқҗҝO(NL) instead of OвҖӢ(L)рқ‘ӮрқҗҝO(L) space when working on a 1D sequence of length LрқҗҝL вҖ“ that essentially stems from using the latent state representation of dimension Nрқ‘ҒN.
Consequently, the LSSL can be space inefficient and we used multi-GPU training for our largest experiments (speech and high resolution images, TablesВ 2 andВ 1).


These fundamental issues with computation and space complexity were revisited and resolved in follow-up work to this paper, where a new state space model (the Structured State Space) provided a new parameterization and algorithms for state spaces.


Conclusion and future work.
Modern deep learning models struggle in applications with very long temporal data such as speech, videos, and medical time-series.
We hope that our conceptual and technical contributions can lead to new capabilities
with simple, principled, and less engineered models.
We note that our pixel-level image classification experiments, which use no heuristics (batch norm, auxiliary losses) or extra information (data augmentation),
perform similar to early convnet models with vastly more parameters,
and is in the spirit of recent attempts at unifying data modalities with a generic sequence modelВ [18].
Our speech results demonstrate the possibility of learning better features than hand-crafted processing pipelines used widely in speech applications.
We are excited about potential downstream applications, such as training other downstream models on top of pre-trained state space features.
