## 
Appendix A Related Work


We provide an extended related work comparing the LSSL to previous recurrent, convolutional, and continuous-time models.


##### HiPPO


The LSSL is most closely related to the HiPPO framework for continuous-time memory [24] and its predecessor, the Legendre Memory Unit (LMU) [58].
The HiPPO-RNN and the LMU define dynamics of the form of equation (1), and incorporate it into an RNN architecture.
A successor to the LMU, the LMU-FFT [12] keeps the original linear dynamics, allowing the LMU to be computed with a cached convolution kernel.


These methods all suffer from two main limitations.
First, the state matrix AрқҗҙA and discretization timescale О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t cannot be trained due to both limitations in theoretical understanding of which AрқҗҙA matrices are effective, as well as computational limitations.
Second, (1) is a 1-D to Nрқ‘ҒN-D map, requiring states to be projected back down to 1-D.
This creates an overall 1-D bottleneck in the state, limiting the expressivity of the model.


Compared to these, the LSSL does not use a conventional RNN architecture, instead keeping the linear recurrence (4) and downprojecting it with the second part of the state space representation (5).
To avoid the 1-D feature bottlneck, it simply computes Hрқҗ»H copies of this 1-D to 1-D independently, creating an overall Hрқҗ»H-dimensional sequence-to-sequence model.
However, this exacerbates the computational issue, since the work is increased by a factor of Hрқҗ»H.


This work resolves the expressivity issue with new theory.
Compared to HiPPO and the LMU, LSSL allows training the AрқҗҙA matrix by showing generalized theoretical results for the HiPPO framework,
showing that there is a parameterized class of structured state spaces that are HiPPO operators.


The LSSL makes progress towards the second issue with new algorithms for these structured matrices (TheoremВ 2).
However, as noted in SectionsВ 4.2 andВ 6, the algorithm presented in TheoremВ 2 was later found to be not practical,
and an improved representation and algorithm was found in subsequent work.


##### Continuous-time CNNs.


The CKConv is the only example of a continuous-time CNN that we are aware of, and is perhaps the strongest baseline in our experiments.
Rather than storing a finite sequence of weights for a convolution kernel,
the CKConv parameterizes it as an implicit function from [0,1]вҶ’в„қвҶ’01в„қ[0,1]\to\mathbb{R} which allows sampling it at any resolution.
A successor to the CKConv is the FlexConvВ [43],
which learns convolutional kernels with a flexible width.
This is similar to the convolution interpretation of LSSL when using certain HiPPO bases (SectionВ 3.2).


##### Continuous-time RNNs.


The connection from RNNs to continuous-time models have been known since their inception, and recent years have seen an explosion of CT-RNN (continuous-time RNN) models based on dynamical systems or ODEs. We briefly mention a few classic and modern works along these lines, categorizing them into a few main topics.


First are theoretical works that analyze the expressivity of RNNs from a continuous-time perspective.
The connection between RNNs and dynamical systems has been studied since the 90s [22], fleshing out the correspondence between different dynamical systems and RNN architectures [38].
Modern treatments have focused on analyzing the stabilityВ [62] and dynamicsВ [29] of RNNs.


Second, a large class of modern RNNs have been designed that aim to combat vanishing gradients from a dynamical systems analysis.
These include include the AntisymmetricRNNВ [7], iRNNВ [30], and LipschitzRNNВ [20], which address the exploding/vanishing gradient problem by reparatermizing the architecture or recurrent matrix based on insights from an underlying dynamical system.


Third is a class of models that are based on an explicit underlying ODE introduced to satisfy various properties.
This category includes the UnICORNNВ [47] and its predecessor coRNNВ [46] which discretize a second-order ODE inspired by oscillatory systems.
Other models include the Liquid Time-Constant Networks (LTC)В [27] and successor CfCВ [26], which use underlying dynamical systems with varying time-constants with stable behavior and provable rates of expressivity measured by trajectory length.
The LTC is based on earlier dynamic causal models (DCM)В [21], which are a particular ODE related to state spaces with an extra bilinear term.
Finally, the LMUВ [58] and HiPPOВ [24] also fall in this category, whose underlying ODEs are mathematically derived for continuous-time memorization.


Fourth, the recent family of neural ODEsВ [10], originally introduced as continuous-depth models, have been adapted to continuous-time, spawning a series of вҖңODE-RNNвҖқ models.
Examples include the ODE-RNNВ [45], GRU-ODE-BayesВ [16], and ODE-LSTMВ [32], which extend adjoint-based neural ODEs to the discrete input setting as an alternative to standard RNNs.
Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDE)В [31] and Neural Rough Differential Equations (NRDE)В [37] are memory efficient versions that integrate observations more smoothly and can be extended to very long time series.


##### Gating mechanisms.


As a special case of continuous-time RNNs, some works have observed the relation between gating mechanisms and damped dynamical systemsВ [54].
Some examples of continuous-time RNNs based on such damped dynamical systems include the LTCВ [27] and iRNNВ [30].
Compared to these, LemmaВ 3.1 shows a stronger result that sigmoid gates are not just motivated by being an arbitrary monotonic function with range (0,1)01(0,1), but the exact formula appears out of discretizing a damped ODE.


## 
Appendix B Model Details


### 
B.1 (M)LSSL Computation


SectionВ 3.1 noted that some of the computations for using the LSSL are expensive to compute.
When the LSSL fixes the AрқҗҙA and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t parameters (e.g. when they are not trained, or at inference time),
these computational difficulties can be circumvented by caching particular computations.
In particular, this case applies to the LSSL-f.
Note that in this case, the other state-space matrices Cрқҗ¶C and Dрқҗ·D comprise the OвҖӢ(HвҖӢN)рқ‘Ӯрқҗ»рқ‘ҒO(HN) trainable parameters of the fixed-transition LSSL.


In particular, we assume that there is a black-box inference algorithm for this system, i.e. matrix-vector multiplication by AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} (an example of implementing this black box for a particular structured class is in SectionВ E.2).
We then compute and cache


- 
вҖў 


the transition matrix AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A}, which is computed by applying the black-box AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} MVM algorithm to the identity matrix IрқҗјI.


- 
вҖў 


the Krylov matrix


 | 
 | KвҖӢ(AВҜ,BВҜ)=(BВҜ,AВҜвҖӢBВҜ,(AВҜ)2вҖӢBВҜ,вҖҰ)вҲҲв„қNГ—L,рқҗҫВҜрқҗҙВҜрқҗөВҜрқҗөВҜрқҗҙВҜрқҗөsuperscriptВҜрқҗҙ2ВҜрқҗөвҖҰsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ҒрқҗҝK(\overline{A},\overline{B})=(\overline{B},\overline{A}\overline{B},(\overline{A})^{2}\overline{B},...)\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times L},
 | 
 | (8)


which is computed in a parallelized manner by the squaring technique for exponentiation, i.e.В batch multiply by AВҜ,(AВҜ)2,(AВҜ)4,вҖҰ
- ВҜрқҗҙsuperscriptВҜрқҗҙ2superscriptВҜрқҗҙ4вҖҰ\overline{A},(\overline{A})^{2},(\overline{A})^{4},\dots.


At inference time, the model can be unrolled recurrently with AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A}.
At training time, the convolutional filter KLвҖӢ(AВҜ,BВҜ,C)subscriptрқҗҫрқҗҝВҜрқҗҙВҜрқҗөрқҗ¶K_{L}(\overline{A},\overline{B},C) (equation (7)) is computed with a matrix multiplication CвӢ…KвҖӢ(AВҜ,BВҜ)вӢ…рқҗ¶рқҗҫВҜрқҗҙВҜрқҗөC\cdot K(\overline{A},\overline{B}) before convolving with the input uрқ‘ўu.


TableВ 5 provides more detailed complexity of this version of the LSSL with fixed A,О”вҖӢt
- рқҗҙО”рқ‘ЎA,\Delta t.


Note that as mentioned in SectionВ 6, this cached algorithm is fairly fast, but the main drawback is that materializing the Krylov matrix (8) requires OвҖӢ(NвҖӢL)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒрқҗҝO(NL) instead of OвҖӢ(L)рқ‘ӮрқҗҝO(L) space.


### 
B.2 Initialization of AрқҗҙA


The LSSL initializes the AрқҗҙA parameter in (1) to the HiPPO-LegS operator,
which was derived to solve a particular continuous-time memorization problem.
This matrix AвҲҲв„қNГ—Nрқҗҙsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒA\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} is


 | 
 | AnвҖӢksubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle A_{nk}
 | ={(2вҖӢn+1)1/2вҖӢ(2вҖӢk+1)1/2ifВ вҖӢn>kn+1ifВ вҖӢn=k0ifВ вҖӢn<k.absentcasessuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ112superscript
2рқ‘ҳ112ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ӣ1ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ0ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\begin{cases}(2n+1)^{1/2}(2k+1)^{1/2}&\mbox{if }n>k\\
n+1&\mbox{if }n=k\\
0&\mbox{if }n<k\end{cases}.
 | 


Note that the LSSL-f is the LSSL with a non-trainable AрқҗҙA (and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t),
so that AрқҗҙA is fixed to the above matrix.


### 
B.3 Initialization of О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t


One distinction between the LSSL and the most related prior work is that the inclusion of the projection (2) makes the layer a 1-dimensional to 1-dimensional map, instead of 1-D to Nрқ‘ҒN-DВ [58, 24].
This enables us to concatenate Hрқҗ»H copies of this map (at the expense of computation, cf.В SectionsВ 4.2 andВ D).
Even when О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t is not trained as in the LSSL-f, these Hрқҗ»H copies allow multiple timescales to be considered by setting О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t differently for each copy.


In particular, we initialize О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t log-uniformly in a range О”вҖӢtmвҖӢiвҖӢn,О”вҖӢtmвҖӢaвҖӢx
- О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡрқ‘–рқ‘ӣО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡрқ‘Һрқ‘Ҙ\Delta t_{min},\Delta t_{max} (i.e., О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t is initialized within this range, such that logвҒЎО”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\log\Delta t is uniformly distributed).
The maximum and minimum values were generally chosen to be a factor of 100100100 apart such that the length of the sequences in the dataset are contained in this range.
Specific values for each model and dataset are in
AppendixВ F.
We did not search over these as a hyperparameter, but we note that it can be tuned for additional performance improvements in our experiments.


### 
B.4 Deep Neural Network Architecture


The Deep LSSL models used in our experiments simply stack together LSSL layers in a simple deep neural network architecture.
We note the following architecture details.


##### Channels.


The state-space model (1)+(2) accepts a 1-dimensional input uрқ‘ўu, but does not strictly have to return a 1-dimensional output yрқ‘Ұy.
By making the matrices in (2) dimension CвҲҲв„қMГ—N,DвҲҲв„қMГ—1formulae-sequenceрқҗ¶superscriptв„қрқ‘Җрқ‘Ғрқҗ·superscriptв„қрқ‘Җ1C\in\mathbb{R}^{M\times N},D\in\mathbb{R}^{M\times 1},
the output yрқ‘Ұy will be dimension Mрқ‘ҖM instead of 111.


We call Mрқ‘ҖM the number of channels in the model.


##### Feedforward.


There are two drawbacks with the current definition of LSSL:


- 
вҖў 


They are defined by running Hрқҗ»H independent copies of a state-space model, which means the Hрқҗ»H input features do not interact at all.


- 
вҖў 


If the channel dimension is M>1рқ‘Җ1M>1, then the LSSL is a map from dimension 111 to Mрқ‘ҖM, which means residuals cannot be applied.


These are both addressed by introducing a position-wise feedforward layer after the LSSL of shape HвӢ…MвҶ’HвҶ’вӢ…рқҗ»рқ‘Җрқҗ»H\cdot M\to H.
This simultaneously mixes the hidden features, and projects the output back to dimension 111 if necessary.
There is also an optional non-linearity in between the LSSL and this feedforward projection; we fix it to the GeLU activation function in our models.


We note that this factorization of parallel convolutions on the Hрқҗ»H features followed by a position-wise linear map is very similar to depth-wise separable convolutionsВ [13].


##### Residuals and normalization.


To stack multiple layers of LSSLs together, we use very standard architectures for deep neural networks.
In particular, we use residual connections and a layer normalization (either pre-norm or post-norm) in the style of standard Transformer architectures.
Whether to use pre-norm or post-norm was chosen on a per-dataset basis, and depended on whether the model overfit; recent results have shown that pre-norm architectures are more stable [35, 15], so we used it on harder datasets with less overfitting.
We note that we could have additionally inserted MLP modules in between LSSL layers, in the style of TransformersВ [57], but did not experiment with this.


##### Parameter count.


The overall parameter count of an LSSL model is MвӢ…HвӢ…(H+N)вӢ…рқ‘Җрқҗ»
рқҗ»рқ‘ҒM\cdot H\cdot(H+N).


We primarily used two model sizes in our experiments, which were chosen simply to produce round numbers of parameters:


- 
вҖў 


LSSL small (вүҲ200вҖӢKabsent200рқҗҫ\approx 200K parameters): 666 layers, H=128,N=128,M=1formulae-sequenceрқҗ»128formulae-sequenceрқ‘Ғ128рқ‘Җ1H=128,N=128,M=1.


- 
вҖў 


LSSL large (вүҲ2вҖӢMabsent2рқ‘Җ\approx 2M parameters): 444 layers, H=256,N=256,M=4formulae-sequenceрқҗ»256formulae-sequenceрқ‘Ғ256рқ‘Җ4H=256,N=256,M=4.


We did not search over additional sizes, but for some datasets reduced the model size for computational reasons.


## 
Appendix C LSSL Proofs


This section gives refinements of the statements in SectionВ 3, additional results, and proofs of all results.


SectionВ C.1 has a more detailed (and self-contained) summary of basic methods in ODE approximation which will be used in the results and proofs.


SectionВ C.2 give more general statements and proofs of LemmaВ 3.1 and LemmaВ 3.2 in LemmaВ C.1 and TheoremВ 4, respectively.


### 
C.1 Approximations of ODEs


We consider the standard setting of a first-order initial value problem (IVP) ordinary differential equation (ODE) for a continuous function fвҖӢ(t,x)рқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙf(t,x)


 | 
 | xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=fвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))xвҖӢ(t0)=x0.Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўabsentрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ў0absentsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ0\begin{aligned} \dot{x}(t)&=f(t,x(t))\\
x(t_{0})&=x_{0}\\
\end{aligned}.
 | 
 | (9)


This differential form has an equivalent integral form


 | 
 | xвҖӢ(t)=x0+вҲ«t0tfвҖӢ(s,xвҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘s.рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ x(t)=x_{0}+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}f(s,x(s))\mathop{}\!ds.
 | 
 | (10)


SectionsВ C.1.1 andВ C.1.2 overview the Picard theorem and first-order numerical integration methods,
which apply to any IVP (9).
SectionВ C.1.3 then shows how to specialize it to linear systems as in equation (1).


At a high level, the basic approximation methods considered here use the integral form (10) and approximate the integral in the right-hand side by simple techniques.


#### 
C.1.1 Picard Iteration


The Picard-LindelГ¶f Theorem gives sufficient conditions for the existence and uniqueness of solutions to an IVP.
As part of the proof, it provides an iteration scheme to compute this solution.


###### 
Theorem 3 (Picard-LindelГ¶f).


In the IVP (9), if there is an interval around t0subscriptрқ‘Ў0t_{0} such that fрқ‘“f is Lipschitz in its second argument,
then there is an open interval IвҲӢt0subscriptрқ‘Ў0рқҗјI\ni t_{0} such that there exists a unique solution xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) to the IVP in IрқҗјI.
Furthermore, the sequence of Picard iterates x(0),x(1),вҖҰ
- superscriptрқ‘Ҙ0superscriptрқ‘Ҙ1italic-вҖҰx^{(0)},x^{(1)},\dots defined by


 | 
 | x(0)вҖӢ(t)superscriptрқ‘Ҙ0рқ‘Ў\displaystyle x^{(0)}(t)
 | =x0absentsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ0\displaystyle=x_{0}
 | 


 | 
 | x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle x^{(\ell)}(t)
 | =x0+вҲ«t0tfвҖӢ(s,x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘sabsent
subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘ superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ \displaystyle=x_{0}+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}f(s,x^{(\ell-1)}(s))\mathop{}\!ds
 | 


converges to xрқ‘Ҙx.


The Picard iteration can be viewed as approximating (10) by holding the previous estimate of the solution x(в„“вҲ’1)superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1x^{(\ell-1)} fixed inside the RHS integral.


#### 
C.1.2 Numerical Integration Methods


Many methods for numerical integration of ODEs exist, which calculate discrete-time approximations of the solution.
We discuss a few of the simplest methods, which are first-order methods with local error OвҖӢ(h2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptв„Һ2O(h^{2}) [6].


These methods start by discretizing (10) into the form


 | 
 | xвҖӢ(tk)вҲ’xвҖӢ(tkвҲ’1)=вҲ«tkвҲ’1tkfвҖӢ(s,xвҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘s.рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘“рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ x(t_{k})-x(t_{k-1})=\int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_{k}}f(s,x(s))\mathop{}\!ds.
 | 
 | (11)


Here we assume a sequence of discrete times t0,t1,t2,вҖҰ
- subscriptрқ‘Ў0subscriptрқ‘Ў1subscriptрқ‘Ў2вҖҰt_{0},t_{1},t_{2},\dots is fixed.
For convenience, let xksubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳx_{k} denote xвҖӢ(tk)рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳx(t_{k}) and let О”вҖӢtk:=tkвҲ’tkвҲ’1assignО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1\Delta t_{k}:=t_{k}-t_{k-1}.
The goal is now to approximate the integral in the RHS of (11).


##### Euler method.


The Euler method approximates (11) by holding the left endpoint constant throughout the integral (i.e., the вҖңrectangle ruleвҖқ with left endpoint), fвҖӢ(s,xвҖӢ(s))вүҲfвҖӢ(tkвҲ’1,xвҖӢ(tkвҲ’1))рқ‘“рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1f(s,x(s))\approx f(t_{k-1},x(t_{k-1})).
The discrete-time update becomes


 | 
 | xkвҲ’xkвҲ’1subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1\displaystyle x_{k}-x_{k-1}
 | =(tkвҲ’tkвҲ’1)вҖӢfвҖӢ(tkвҲ’1,xвҖӢ(tkвҲ’1))absentsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1рқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1\displaystyle=(t_{k}-t_{k-1})f(t_{k-1},x(t_{k-1}))
 | 
 | (12)


 | 
 | 
 | =О”вҖӢtkвҖӢfвҖӢ(tkвҲ’1,xkвҲ’1).absentО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1\displaystyle=\Delta t_{k}f(t_{k-1},x_{k-1}).
 | 


##### Backward Euler method.


The backward Euler method approximates (11) by holding the right endpoint constant throughout the integral (i.e., the вҖңrectangle ruleвҖқ with right endpoint), fвҖӢ(s,xвҖӢ(s))вүҲfвҖӢ(tk,xвҖӢ(tk))рқ‘“рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ рқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳf(s,x(s))\approx f(t_{k},x(t_{k})).
The discrete-time update becomes


 | 
 | xkвҲ’xkвҲ’1subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1\displaystyle x_{k}-x_{k-1}
 | =(tkвҲ’tkвҲ’1)вҖӢfвҖӢ(tk,xвҖӢ(tk))absentsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1рқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=(t_{k}-t_{k-1})f(t_{k},x(t_{k}))
 | 
 | (13)


 | 
 | 
 | =О”вҖӢtkвҖӢfвҖӢ(tk,xk).absentО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\Delta t_{k}f(t_{k},x_{k}).
 | 


#### 
C.1.3 Discretization of State-Space Models


In the case of a linear system, the IVP is specialized to the case


 | 
 | fвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))=AвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+BвҖӢuвҖӢ(t).рқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗөрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle f(t,x(t))=Ax(t)+Bu(t).
 | 


Note that here uрқ‘ўu is treated as a fixed external input, which is constant from the point of view of this ODE in xрқ‘Ҙx.
Let uksubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳu_{k} denote the average value in each discrete time interval,


 | 
 | uk=1О”вҖӢtkвҖӢвҲ«tkвҲ’1tkuвҖӢ(s)вҖӢрқ‘‘s.subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘ўрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ \displaystyle u_{k}=\frac{1}{\Delta t_{k}}\int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_{k}}u(s)\mathop{}\!ds.
 | 


The integral equation (11) can be specialized to this case, and more generally a convex combination of the left and right endpoints can be taken to approximate the integral,
weighing them by 1вҲ’Оұ1рқӣј1-\alpha and Оұрқӣј\alpha respectively.
Note that the case Оұ=0,1рқӣј
- 01\alpha=0,1 are specializations of the forward and backward Euler method,
and the case Оұ=12рқӣј12\alpha=\frac{1}{2} is the classic вҖңtrapezoid ruleвҖқ for numerical integration.


 | 
 | xвҖӢ(tk)вҲ’xвҖӢ(tkвҲ’1)рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1\displaystyle x(t_{k})-x(t_{k-1})
 | =вҲ«tkвҲ’1tkAвҖӢxвҖӢ(s)вҖӢрқ‘‘s+вҲ«tkвҲ’1tkBвҖӢuвҖӢ(s)вҖӢрқ‘‘sabsent
superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗөрқ‘ўрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ \displaystyle=\int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_{k}}Ax(s)\mathop{}\!ds+\int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_{k}}Bu(s)\mathop{}\!ds
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ«tkвҲ’1tkAвҖӢxвҖӢ(s)вҖӢрқ‘‘s+О”вҖӢtkвҖӢBвҖӢukabsent
superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_{k}}Ax(s)\mathop{}\!ds+\Delta t_{k}Bu_{k}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | вүҲО”вҖӢtkвҖӢ[(1вҲ’Оұ)вҖӢAвҖӢxkвҲ’1+ОұвҖӢAвҖӢxk]+О”вҖӢtkвҖӢBвҖӢuk.absent
О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳdelimited-[]
1рқӣјрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1рқӣјрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\approx\Delta t_{k}\left[(1-\alpha)Ax_{k-1}+\alpha Ax_{k}\right]+\Delta t_{k}Bu_{k}.
 | 


Rearranging yields


 | 
 | (IвҲ’ОұвҖӢО”вҖӢtkвӢ…A)вҖӢxkрқҗјвӢ…рқӣјО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle(I-\alpha\Delta t_{k}\cdot A)x_{k}
 | =(I+(1вҲ’Оұ)вҖӢО”вҖӢtkвӢ…A)вҖӢxkвҲ’1+О”вҖӢtkвӢ…BвҖӢukabsent

рқҗјвӢ…1рқӣјО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1вӢ…О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=(I+(1-\alpha)\Delta t_{k}\cdot A)x_{k-1}+\Delta t_{k}\cdot Bu_{k}
 | 


 | 
 | xksubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle x_{k}
 | =(IвҲ’ОұвҖӢО”вҖӢtkвӢ…A)вҲ’1вҖӢ(I+(1вҲ’Оұ)вҖӢО”вҖӢtkвӢ…A)вҖӢxkвҲ’1+(IвҲ’ОұвҖӢО”вҖӢtkвӢ…A)вҲ’1вҖӢО”вҖӢtkвӢ…BвҖӢukabsent
superscriptрқҗјвӢ…рқӣјО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗҙ1
рқҗјвӢ…1рқӣјО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1вӢ…superscriptрқҗјвӢ…рқӣјО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗҙ1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=(I-\alpha\Delta t_{k}\cdot A)^{-1}(I+(1-\alpha)\Delta t_{k}\cdot A)x_{k-1}+(I-\alpha\Delta t_{k}\cdot A)^{-1}\Delta t_{k}\cdot Bu_{k}
 | 


This derives the generalized bilinear transform (GBT)В [61].
The bilinear method is the case Оұ=12рқӣј12\alpha=\frac{1}{2} of special significance, and was numerically found to be better than the forward and backward Euler methods Оұ=0,1рқӣј
- 01\alpha=0,1 both in synthetic function approximation settings and in end-to-end experiments [24, Figure 4].


### 
C.2 RNNs are LSSLs: Proof of Results in SectionВ 3.2


We provide more detailed statements of LemmasВ 3.1 andВ 3.2 from SectionВ 3.2.
In summary, LSSLs and popular families of RNN methods all approximate the same continuous-time dynamics


 | 
 | xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’x+fвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\dot{x}(t)=-x+f(t,x(t))
 | 
 | (14)


by viewing them with a combination of two techniques.


We note that these results are about two of the most commonly used architecture modifications for RNNs.
First, the gating mechanism is ubiquitous in RNNs, and usually thought of as a heuristic for smoothing optimizationВ [28].
Second, many of the effective large-scale RNNs use linear (gated) recurrences and deeper models, which is usually thought of as a heuristic for computational efficiencyВ [5].
Our results suggest that neither of these are heuristics after all, and arise from standard ways to approximate ODEs.


To be more specific, we show that:


- 
вҖў 


Non-linear RNNs discretize the dynamics (14) by applying backwards Euler discretization to the linear term, which arises in the gating mechanism of RNNs (SectionВ C.2.2, LemmaВ C.1).


- 
вҖў 


A special case of LSSLs approximates the dynamics (14) (in continuous-time) by applying Picard iteration to the non-linear term (SectionВ C.2.3, TheoremВ 4).


- 
вҖў 


Deep linear RNNs approximate the dynamics (14) with both Picard iteration in the depth direction to linearize the non-linear term, and discretization (gates) in the time direction to discretize the equation (SectionВ C.2.4, CorollaryВ C.3).


A comparison is summarized in TableВ 4.


In the remainder of this section, we assume that there is an underlying function xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) that satisfies (14) on some interval for any initial condition,
and that fрқ‘“f is continuous and Lipschitz in its second argument.
Our goal is to show that several families of models approximate this in various ways.


Table 4: A summary of the characteristics of popular RNN methods and their approximation mechanisms for capturing the dynamics xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’xвҖӢ(t)+fвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\dot{x}(t)=-x(t)+f(t,x(t)) (equation (14)).
The LSSL entries are for the very specific case with order N=1рқ‘Ғ1N=1 and A=вҲ’1,B=1,C=1,D=0formulae-sequenceрқҗҙ1formulae-sequenceрқҗө1formulae-sequenceрқҗ¶1рқҗ·0A=-1,B=1,C=1,D=0; LSSLs are more general.

 | 


 | Method
 | RNN
 | RNN
 | LSSL
 | LSSL


 | Variant
 | Gated
 | Gated, linear
 | 
Discrete (4)+(5)

 | 
Continuous (1)+(2)


 | Special cases
 | 
LSTMВ [28], GRUВ [14]

 | 
QRNNВ [5], SRUВ [33]

 | 
 | 


 | Deep?
 | Single-layer
 | Deep
 | Deep
 | Deep


 | Continuous?
 | Discrete-time
 | Discrete-time
 | Discrete-time
 | Continuous-time


 | Linear?
 | Non-linear
 | Linear
 | Linear
 | Linear


 | Approximation
 | 
 | 
 | 
 | 


 | Depth-wise
 | -
 | Picard iteration
 | Picard iteration
 | Picard iteration


 | Time-wise
 | Backwards Euler
 | 
GBT(Оұ=1рқӣј1\alpha=1)

 | 
GBT(Оұ=12рқӣј12\alpha=\frac{1}{2}) (i.e.В Bilinear)

 | -


#### 
C.2.1 Intuition / Proof Sketches


We sketch the idea of how LSSLs capture popular RNNs. More precisely, we will show how approximating the dynamics (14) in various ways lead to types of RNNs and LSSLs.


The first step is to look at the simpler dynamics


 | 
 | xЛҷвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’xвҖӢ(t)+uвҖӢ(t)Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}(t)=-x(t)+u(t)
 | 


where there is some input uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t) that is independent of xрқ‘Ҙx.
(In other words, in (14), the function fвҖӢ(t,x)рқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙf(t,x) does not depend on the second argument.)


By directing applying the GBT discretization with Оұ=1рқӣј1\alpha=1, this leads to a gated recurrence (LemmaВ 3.1).


The second step is that by applying the backwards Euler discretization more directly to (14),
this leads to a gated RNN where the input can depend on the state (LemmaВ C.1).


Alternatively, we can apply Picard iteration on (14), which says that the iteration


 | 
 | x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=x0+вҲ«t0tвҲ’x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(s)вҖӢdвҖӢs+вҲ«t0tfвҖӢ(s,x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘ssuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў

subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘ рқ‘‘рқ‘ superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘ superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ \displaystyle x^{(\ell)}(t)=x_{0}+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}-x^{(\ell-1)}(s)\mathop{}\!ds+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}f(s,x^{(\ell-1)}(s))\mathop{}\!ds
 | 


converges to the solution xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t).


However, the first integral term is simple and can be tightened.
We can instead try to apply Picard iteration on only the second term, leaving the first integral in terms of x(в„“)superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“x^{(\ell)}.
Intuitively this should still converge to the right solution, since this is a weaker iteration; weвҖҷre only using the Picard approximation on the second term.


 | 
 | x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=x0+вҲ«t0tвҲ’x(в„“)вҖӢ(s)вҖӢdвҖӢs+вҲ«t0tfвҖӢ(s,x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘ssuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў

subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘ рқ‘‘рқ‘ superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘ superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ \displaystyle x^{(\ell)}(t)=x_{0}+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}-x^{(\ell)}(s)\mathop{}\!ds+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}f(s,x^{(\ell-1)}(s))\mathop{}\!ds
 | 


Differentiating, this equation is the ODE


 | 
 | xЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=вҲ’x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)+fвҖӢ(t,x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(t))superscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў
superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}^{(\ell)}(t)=-x^{(\ell)}(t)+f(t,x^{(\ell-1)}(t))
 | 


This implies that alternating point-wise functions with a simple linear ODE xЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=вҲ’x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)+u(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў
superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\dot{x}^{(\ell)}(t)=-x^{(\ell)}(t)+u^{(\ell)}(t)
also captures the dynamics (14).
But this is essentially what an LSSL is.


To move to discrete-time, this continuous-time layer can be discretized with gates as in LemmaВ 3.1, leading to deep linear RNNs such as the QRNN,
or with the bilinear discretization, leading to the discrete-time LSSL.
We note again that in the discrete-time LSSL, AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} and BВҜВҜрқҗө\overline{B} play the role of the gates Пғ,1вҲ’Пғ
- рқңҺ1рқңҺ\sigma,1-\sigma.


#### 
C.2.2 Capturing gates through discretization


###### 
Lemma C.1.


Consider an RNN of the form


 | 
 | xk=(1вҲ’ПғвҖӢ(zk))вҖӢxkвҲ’1+ПғвҖӢ(zk)вҖӢfВҜвҖӢ(k,xkвҲ’1),subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ
1рқңҺsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1рқңҺsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳВҜрқ‘“рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1x_{k}=(1-\sigma(z_{k}))x_{k-1}+\sigma(z_{k})\overline{f}(k,x_{k-1}),
 | 
 | (15)


where fВҜвҖӢ(k,x)ВҜрқ‘“рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙ\overline{f}(k,x) is an arbitrary function that is Lipschitz in its second argument (e.g., it may depend on an external input uksubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳu_{k}).


Then equation (15) is a discretization of the dynamics (14)
with step sizes О”вҖӢtk=expвҒЎ(zk)О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳ\Delta t_{k}=\exp(z_{k}), i.e. xkвүҲxвҖӢ(tk)subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳx_{k}\approx x(t_{k}) where tk=вҲ‘i=1kО”вҖӢtisubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–t_{k}=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\Delta t_{i}.


###### Proof.


Apply the backwards Euler discretization (13) to equation (14) to get


 | 
 | xkвҲ’xkвҲ’1subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1\displaystyle x_{k}-x_{k-1}
 | =О”вҖӢtkвҖӢ[вҲ’xk+fвҖӢ(tk,xk)]absentО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳdelimited-[]
subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\Delta t_{k}\left[-x_{k}+f(t_{k},x_{k})\right]
 | 


 | 
 | (1+О”вҖӢtk)вҖӢxk
1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle(1+\Delta t_{k})x_{k}
 | =xkвҲ’1+О”вҖӢtkвҖӢfвҖӢ(tk,xk)absent
subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=x_{k-1}+\Delta t_{k}f(t_{k},x_{k})
 | 


 | 
 | xksubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle x_{k}
 | =11+О”вҖӢtkвҖӢxkвҲ’1+О”вҖӢtk1+О”вҖӢtkвҖӢfвҖӢ(tk,xk).absent
1
1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ
1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\frac{1}{1+\Delta t_{k}}x_{k-1}+\frac{\Delta t_{k}}{1+\Delta t_{k}}f(t_{k},x_{k}).
 | 


Note that О”вҖӢtk1+О”вҖӢtk=ezk1+ezk=11+eвҲ’zkО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ
1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳ
1superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳ1
1superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳ\frac{\Delta t_{k}}{1+\Delta t_{k}}=\frac{e^{z_{k}}}{1+e^{z_{k}}}=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z_{k}}}
and 11+О”вҖӢtk=1вҲ’О”вҖӢtk1+О”вҖӢtk1
1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ
1О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ\frac{1}{1+\Delta t_{k}}=1-\frac{\Delta t_{k}}{1+\Delta t_{k}}, thus


 | 
 | xksubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle x_{k}
 | =(1вҲ’ПғвҖӢ(zk))вҖӢxkвҲ’1+ПғвҖӢ(zk)вҖӢfВҜвҖӢ(k,xkвҲ’1).absent
1рқңҺsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1рқңҺsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳВҜрқ‘“рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳ1\displaystyle=(1-\sigma(z_{k}))x_{k-1}+\sigma(z_{k})\overline{f}(k,x_{k-1}).
 | 


Here we are denoting fВҜвҖӢ(k,x)=fвҖӢ(tk,x)ВҜрқ‘“рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙ\overline{f}(k,x)=f(t_{k},x) to be a discrete-time version of fрқ‘“f evaluatable at the given timesteps tksubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳt_{k}.
вҲҺ


Note that a potential external input function uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t) or sequence uksubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳu_{k} is captured through the abstraction fвҖӢ(t,x)рқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙf(t,x).
For example, a basic RNN could define fВҜвҖӢ(k,x)=fвҖӢ(tk,x)=tanhвҖӢ(WвҖӢx+UвҖӢuk)ВҜрқ‘“рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙtanh
рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҳsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ\overline{f}(k,x)=f(t_{k},x)=\text{tanh}(Wx+Uu_{k}).


#### 
C.2.3 Capturing non-linearities through Picard iteration


The main result of this section is TheoremВ 4 showing that LSSLs can approximate the same dynamics as the RNNs in the previous section.
This follows from a technical lemma.


###### 
Lemma C.2.


Let fвҖӢ(t,x)рқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙf(t,x) be any function that satisfies the conditions of the Picard-LindelГ¶f Theorem (TheoremВ 3).


Define a sequence of functions x(в„“)superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“x^{(\ell)} by alternating the (point-wise) function fрқ‘“f with solving an ODE


 | 
 | x(0)вҖӢ(t)superscriptрқ‘Ҙ0рқ‘Ў\displaystyle x^{(0)}(t)
 | =x0absentsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ0\displaystyle=x_{0}
 | 


 | 
 | u(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle u^{(\ell)}(t)
 | =fвҖӢ(t,x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(t))absentрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=f(t,x^{(\ell-1)}(t))
 | 


 | 
 | xЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}^{(\ell)}(t)
 | =AвҖӢx(в„“)вҖӢ(t)+u(в„“)вҖӢ(t).absent
рқҗҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=Ax^{(\ell)}(t)+u^{(\ell)}(t).
 | 


Then x(в„“)superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“x^{(\ell)} converges to a solution x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)вҶ’xвҖӢ(t)вҶ’superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx^{(\ell)}(t)\to x(t) of the IVP


 | 
 | xЛҷвҖӢ(t)Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}(t)
 | =AвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+fвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))absent
рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=Ax(t)+f(t,x(t))
 | 


 | 
 | xвҖӢ(t0)рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ў0\displaystyle x(t_{0})
 | =x0.absentsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ0\displaystyle=x_{0}.
 | 


###### 
Theorem 4.


A (continuous-time) deep LSSL with order N=1рқ‘Ғ1N=1 and A=вҲ’1,B=1,C=1,D=0formulae-sequenceрқҗҙ1formulae-sequenceрқҗө1formulae-sequenceрқҗ¶1рқҗ·0A=-1,B=1,C=1,D=0 approximates the non-linear dynamics (14).


###### Proof.


Applying the definition of an LSSL (equations (1)+(2)) with these parameters results in a layer mapping uвҖӢ(t)вҶҰyвҖӢ(t)maps-toрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўu(t)\mapsto y(t) where yрқ‘Ұy is defined implicitly through the ODE


 | 
 | yЛҷвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’yвҖӢ(t)+uвҖӢ(t).Лҷрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{y}(t)=-y(t)+u(t).
 | 


This can be seen since the choice of C,D
- рқҗ¶рқҗ·C,D implies yвҖӢ(t)=xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўy(t)=x(t) and the choice of A,B
- рқҗҙрқҗөA,B gives the above equation.


Consider the deep LSSL defined by alternating this LSSL with position-wise (in time) non-linear functions


 | 
 | u(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle u^{(\ell)}(t)
 | =fвҖӢ(t,y(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(t))absentрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ұв„“1рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=f(t,y^{(\ell-1)}(t))
 | 


 | 
 | yЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptЛҷрқ‘Ұв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{y}^{(\ell)}(t)
 | =вҲ’y(в„“)вҖӢ(t)+u(в„“)вҖӢ(t).absent
superscriptрқ‘Ұв„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=-y^{(\ell)}(t)+u^{(\ell)}(t).
 | 


But this is exactly a special case of LemmaВ C.2,
so that we know y(в„“)вҖӢ(t)вҶ’yвҖӢ(t)вҶ’superscriptрқ‘Ұв„“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўy^{(\ell)}(t)\to y(t) such that yвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўy(t) satisfies


 | 
 | yЛҷвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’yвҖӢ(t)+fвҖӢ(t,yвҖӢ(t))Лҷрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў
рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{y}(t)=-y(t)+f(t,y(t))
 | 


as desired.
вҲҺ


###### Proof of LemmaВ C.2.


Let


 | 
 | zвҖӢ(t)=eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)рқ‘§рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle z(t)=e^{-At}x(t)
 | 


(and z0=zвҖӢ(t0)=xвҖӢ(t0)=x0subscriptрқ‘§0рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ў0subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0z_{0}=z(t_{0})=x(t_{0})=x_{0}).
Note that


 | 
 | zЛҷвҖӢ(t)Лҷрқ‘§рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{z}(t)
 | =eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢ[xЛҷвҖӢ(t)вҲ’AвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)]absentsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўdelimited-[]Лҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=e^{-At}\left[\dot{x}(t)-Ax(t)\right]
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢfвҖӢ(t,xвҖӢ(t))absentsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=e^{-At}f(t,x(t))
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢfвҖӢ(t,eAвҖӢtвҖӢzвҖӢ(t)).absentsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘§рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=e^{-At}f(t,e^{At}z(t)).
 | 


Since fрқ‘“f satisfies the conditions of the Picard Theorem (i.e., is continuous in the first argument and Lipschitz in the second),
so does the function gрқ‘”g where gвҖӢ(t,x):=eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢfвҖӢ(t,eAвҖӢtвҖӢx)assignрқ‘”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙg(t,x):=e^{-At}f(t,e^{At}x) for some interval around the initial time.


By TheoremВ 3, the iterates z(в„“)superscriptрқ‘§в„“z^{(\ell)} defined by


 | 
 | z(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=z0+вҲ«t0teвҲ’AвҖӢsвҖӢfвҖӢ(s,eAвҖӢsвҖӢz(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(s))вҖӢрқ‘‘ssuperscriptрқ‘§в„“рқ‘Ў
subscriptрқ‘§0superscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў0рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘ рқ‘“рқ‘ superscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘ superscriptрқ‘§в„“1рқ‘ differential-dрқ‘ z^{(\ell)}(t)=z_{0}+\int_{t_{0}}^{t}e^{-As}f(s,e^{As}z^{(\ell-1)}(s))\mathop{}\!ds
 | 
 | (16)


converges to zрқ‘§z.


Define x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=eAвҖӢtвҖӢz(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘§в„“рқ‘Ўx^{(\ell)}(t)=e^{At}z^{(\ell)}(t).
Differentiate (16) to get


 | 
 | zЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptЛҷрқ‘§в„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{z}^{(\ell)}(t)
 | =eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢfвҖӢ(t,eAвҖӢtвҖӢz(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(t))absentsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘§в„“1рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=e^{-At}f(t,e^{At}z^{(\ell-1)}(t))
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢfвҖӢ(t,x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(t))absentsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=e^{-At}f(t,x^{(\ell-1)}(t))
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢu(в„“)вҖӢ(t).absentsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=e^{-At}u^{(\ell)}(t).
 | 


But


 | 
 | zЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=eвҲ’AвҖӢtвҖӢ[xЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)вҲ’AвҖӢx(в„“)вҖӢ(t)],superscriptЛҷрқ‘§в„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўdelimited-[]superscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўрқҗҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{z}^{(\ell)}(t)=e^{-At}\left[\dot{x}^{(\ell)}(t)-Ax^{(\ell)}(t)\right],
 | 


so


 | 
 | xЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=AвҖӢx(в„“)вҖӢ(t)+u(в„“)вҖӢ(t).superscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў
рқҗҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}^{(\ell)}(t)=Ax^{(\ell)}(t)+u^{(\ell)}(t).
 | 


Since z(в„“)вҶ’zвҶ’superscriptрқ‘§в„“рқ‘§z^{(\ell)}\to z and x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=eAвҖӢtвҖӢz(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘§в„“рқ‘Ўx^{(\ell)}(t)=e^{At}z^{(\ell)}(t) and xвҖӢ(t)=eAвҖӢtвҖӢzвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’рқҗҙрқ‘Ўрқ‘§рқ‘Ўx(t)=e^{At}z(t),
we have x(в„“)вҶ’xвҶ’superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ҙx^{(\ell)}\to x.
вҲҺ


#### 
C.2.4 Capturing Deep, Linear, Gated RNNs


We finally note that several types of RNNs exist which were originally motivated by approximating linearizing gated RNNs for speed.
Although these were treated as a heuristic for efficiency reasons,
they are explained by combining our two main technical results.


LemmaВ C.1 shows that a single-layer, discrete-time, non-linear RNN approximates the dynamics (14) through discretization, which arises in the gating mechanism.


TheoremВ 4 shows that a deep, continuous-time, linear RNN approximates (14) through Picard iteration, where the non-linearity is moved to the depth direction.


Combining these two results leads to CorollaryВ C.3, which says that a deep, discrete-time, linear RNN can also approximate the same dynamics (14).


###### 
Corollary C.3.


Consider a deep, linear RNN of the form


 | 
 | xk(в„“)subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle x^{(\ell)}_{k}
 | =(1вҲ’ПғвҖӢ(zk))вҖӢxkвҲ’1(в„“)+ПғвҖӢ(zk)вҖӢuk(в„“)absent
1рқңҺsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘ҳ1рқңҺsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=(1-\sigma(z_{k}))x^{(\ell)}_{k-1}+\sigma(z_{k})u^{(\ell)}_{k}
 | 


 | 
 | uk(в„“)subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle u^{(\ell)}_{k}
 | =fВҜвҖӢ(k,xk(в„“вҲ’1)).absentВҜрқ‘“рқ‘ҳsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=\overline{f}(k,x^{(\ell-1)}_{k}).
 | 


This is a discretization of the dynamics (14)
with step sizes О”вҖӢtk=expвҒЎ(zk)О”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘ҳ\Delta t_{k}=\exp(z_{k}), i.e. xkвүҲxвҖӢ(tk)subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳx_{k}\approx x(t_{k}) where tk=вҲ‘i=1kО”вҖӢtisubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳО”subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–t_{k}=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\Delta t_{i}.


###### Proof.


By LemmaВ C.1, the first equation is a discretization of the continuous-time equation


 | 
 | xЛҷ(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=вҲ’x(в„“)вҖӢ(t)+u(в„“)вҖӢ(t)superscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ў
superscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}^{(\ell)}(t)=-x^{(\ell)}(t)+u^{(\ell)}(t)
 | 


where


 | 
 | u(в„“)вҖӢ(t)=fвҖӢ(t,x(в„“вҲ’1)вҖӢ(t))superscriptрқ‘ўв„“рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙв„“1рқ‘Ў\displaystyle u^{(\ell)}(t)=f(t,x^{(\ell-1)}(t))
 | 


uses the continuous-time version fрқ‘“f of fВҜВҜрқ‘“\overline{f}.
But by LemmaВ C.2, this is an approximation of the dynamics (14) using Picard iteration.
вҲҺ


Notable examples of this type of model include the Quasi-RNN or QRNN [5] and the Simple Recurrent Unit (SRU) [33],
which are among the most effective models in practice.
We remark that these are the closest models to the LSSL and suggest that their efficacy is a consequence of the results of this section,
which shows that they are not heuristics.


We note that there are many more RNN variants that use a combination of these gating and linearization techniques that were not mentioned in this section, and can be explained similarly.


## 
Appendix D LSSL Proofs and Algorithms


This section proves the results in SectionВ 4.1, and is organized as follows:


- 
вҖў 


SectionВ D.1 gives a self-contained synopsis of the HiPPO frameworkВ [24].


- 
вҖў 


SectionВ D.2 proves TheoremВ 1, which shows that the рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳрқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳ\operatorname{\mathsf{hippo}} operators for any measure lead to a simple linear ODE of the form of equation (1).


- 
вҖў 


SectionВ D.3 proves CorollaryВ 4.1, including a formal definition of quasiseparable matrices (i.e., how LSSL matrices are defined) in DefinitionВ 4.


##### Notation


This section is technically involved and we adopt notation to simplify reasoning about the shapes of objects.
In particular, we use bold capitals (e.g.В рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A}) to denote matrices and bold lowercase (e.g.В рқҗӣрқҗӣ\mathbf{b}) to denote vectors.
For example, equation (1) becomes xЛҷ=рқҗҖвҖӢx+рқҗӣвҖӢuЛҷрқ‘Ҙ
рқҗҖрқ‘Ҙрқҗӣрқ‘ў\dot{x}=\mathbf{A}x+\mathbf{b}u.
These conventions are adopted throughout AppendicesВ D andВ E.


### 
D.1 Preliminaries: HiPPO Framework and Recurrence Width


This section summarizes technical preliminaries taken directly from prior work.
We include this section so that this work is self-contained and uses consistent notation, which may deviate from prior work.
For example, we use modified notation from Gu etВ al. [24] in order to follow conventions in control theory
(e.g., we denote input by uрқ‘ўu and state by xрқ‘Ҙx as in (1)).


SectionВ D.1.1 formally defines the HiPPO operator mathematically as in [24, Section 2.2],
and SectionВ D.1.2 overviews the steps to derive the HiPPO operator as in [24, Appendix C].
SectionВ D.1.3 defines the class of Low Recurrence Width (LRW) matrices, which is the class of matrices that our generalization of the HiPPO results (TheoremВ 1) uses.


#### 
D.1.1 Definition of HiPPO Operator


###### 
Definition 1 ([24], Definition 1).


Given a time-varying measure Ој(t)superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ў\mu^{(t)} supported on (вҲ’вҲһ,t]рқ‘Ў(-\infty,t], an N-dimensional subspace рқ’ўрқ’ў\mathcal{G} of polynomials, and a continuous function u:в„қвүҘ0вҶ’в„қ:рқ‘ўвҶ’subscriptв„қabsent0в„қu:\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}, HiPPO defines a projection operator рқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғtsubscriptрқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғрқ‘Ў\mathsf{proj}_{t} and a coefficient extraction operator рқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝtsubscriptрқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝрқ‘Ў\mathsf{coef}_{t} at every time tрқ‘Ўt, with the following properties:


- 
1. 


рқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғtsubscriptрқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғрқ‘Ў\mathsf{proj}_{t} takes a function uрқ‘ўu restricted up to time tрқ‘Ўt, uвүӨt:=uвҖӢ(x)|xвүӨtassignsubscriptрқ‘ўabsentрқ‘Ўevaluated-atрқ‘ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўu_{\leq t}:=u(x)|_{x\leq t}, and maps it to a polynomial g(t)вҲҲрқ’ўsuperscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Ўрқ’ўg^{(t)}\in\mathcal{G}, that minimizes the approximation error вҖ–uвүӨtвҲ’g(t)вҖ–L2вҖӢ(Ој(t))subscriptnormsubscriptрқ‘ўabsentрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗҝ2superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ў\|u_{\leq t}-g^{(t)}\|_{L_{2}(\mu^{(t)})}.


- 
2. 


рқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝt:рқ’ўвҶ’в„қN:subscriptрқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ’ўsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathsf{coef}_{t}:\mathcal{G}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{N} maps the polynomial g(t)superscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Ўg^{(t)} to the coefficients cвҖӢ(t)вҲҲв„қNрқ‘җрқ‘Ўsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғc(t)\in\mathbb{R}^{N} of the basis of orthogonal polynomials defined with respect to the measure Ој(t)superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ў\mu^{(t)}.


The composition рқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝtвҲҳрқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғtsubscriptрқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғрқ‘Ў\mathsf{coef}_{t}\circ\mathsf{proj}_{t} is called рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳрқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳ\operatorname{\mathsf{hippo}}, which is an operator mapping a function u:в„қвүҘ0вҶ’в„қ:рқ‘ўвҶ’subscriptв„қabsent0в„қu:\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} to the optimal projection coefficients c:в„қвүҘ0вҶ’в„қN:рқ‘җвҶ’subscriptв„қabsent0superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғc:\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{N} (i.e (рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—ҲвҒЎ(u))вҖӢ(t)=рқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝtвҖӢ(рқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғtвҖӢ(f))рқ—Ғрқ—Ӯрқ—үрқ—үрқ—Ҳрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ–јрқ—Ҳрқ–ҫрқ–ҝрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ—үрқ—Ӣрқ—Ҳрқ—ғрқ‘Ўрқ‘“(\operatorname{\mathsf{hippo}}(u))(t)=\mathsf{coef}_{t}(\mathsf{proj}_{t}(f)).


#### 
D.1.2 HiPPO Framework for Deriving the HiPPO Operator


The main ingredients of HiPPO consists of an approximation measure and an orthogonal polynomial basis. We recall how they are defined in [24] (we note that compared to Gu etВ al. [24], our notation has changed from input fвҖӢ(t)рқ‘“рқ‘Ўf(t) coefficients (state) cвҖӢ(t)рқ‘җрқ‘Ўc(t) to input uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t) and coefficients (state) xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t), following conventions in controls).


##### Approximation Measures


At every tрқ‘Ўt, the approximation quality is defined with respect to a measure Ој(t)superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ў\mu^{(t)} supported on (вҲ’вҲһ,t]рқ‘Ў(-\infty,t]. We assume that the measures Ој(t)superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ў\mu^{(t)} have densities ПүвҖӢ(t,Y):=dвҖӢОј(t)dвҖӢYassignрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқ‘‘superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ўрқ‘‘рқ‘Ң\omega(t,Y):=\frac{\mathop{}\!d\mu^{(t)}}{\mathop{}\!dY}. Note that this implies that integrating with respect to dвҖӢОј(t)рқ‘‘superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ў\mathop{}\!d\mu^{(t)} is the same as integrating with respect to ПүвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢdвҖӢYрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқ‘‘рқ‘Ң\omega(t,Y)\mathop{}\!dY.


##### Orthogonal Polynomial basis


Let {Pn(t)}nвҲҲв„•subscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣв„•\{P_{n}^{(t)}\}_{n\in\mathbb{N}} denote a sequence of orthogonal polynomials with respect to some time-varying measure Ој(t)superscriptрқңҮрқ‘Ў\mu^{(t)}. Let pn(t)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўp_{n}^{(t)} be the normalized version of of orthogonal Pn(t)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘ӣрқ‘ЎP_{n}^{(t)}, and define


 | 
 | pnвҖӢ(t,Y)=pn(t)вҖӢ(Y).subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңp_{n}(t,Y)=p_{n}^{(t)}(Y).
 | 


In particular, the above implies that


 | 
 | вҲ«вҲ’вҲһtpn(t)вҖӢ(Y)вӢ…pm(t)вҖӢ(Y)вҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢрқ‘‘Y=Оҙm,n.superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ЎвӢ…superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқӣҝ
- рқ‘ҡрқ‘ӣ\int_{-\infty}^{t}p_{n}^{(t)}(Y)\cdot p_{m}^{(t)}(Y)\omega(t,Y)\mathop{}\!dY=\delta_{m,n}.
 | 


In the general framework, HiPPO does not require an orthogonal polynomial basis as the selected basis.
The choice of basis is generalized by tilting with ПҮрқң’\chi.


##### Tilted measure and basis


For any scaling function ПҮвҖӢ(t,Y)рқң’рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ң\chi(t,Y), the functions pnвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢПҮвҖӢ(t,Y)subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң’рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңp_{n}(t,Y)\chi(t,Y) are orthogonal with respect to the density ПүПҮ2рқң”superscriptрқң’2\frac{\omega}{\chi^{2}} at every time tрқ‘Ўt.
Define ОҪвҖӢ(t)рқңҲрқ‘Ў\nu(t) to be the normalized measure with density proportional to ПүПҮ2рқң”superscriptрқң’2\frac{\omega}{\chi^{2}}, with normalization constant О¶вҖӢ(t)=вҲ«0tПүвҖӢ(t,Y)ПҮвҖӢ(t,Y)2вҖӢdxрқңҒрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ўрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң’superscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ң2differential-dрқ‘Ҙ\zeta(t)=\int_{0}^{t}\frac{\omega(t,Y)}{\chi(t,Y)^{2}}\mathrm{d}x.


We express the coefficients xnвҖӢ(t)subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўx_{n}(t) calculated by the HiPPO framework as:


 | 
 | xnвҖӢ(t)=1О¶вҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ«0tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,Y)ПҮвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢрқ‘‘Y.subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў1рқңҒрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң’рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ңx_{n}(t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\zeta(t)}}\int_{0}^{t}u(Y)p_{n}(t,Y)\frac{\omega(t,Y)}{\chi(t,Y)}\mathop{}\!dY.
 | 
 | (17)


To use this to derive xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\dot{x}_{n}(t), let hвҖӢ(t,Y)=uвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,Y)в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңh(t,Y)=u(Y)p_{n}(t,Y)\omega(t,Y). We see that


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)
 | =ddвҖӢtвҖӢвҲ«0tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢdYabsentddрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\displaystyle=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t}\int_{0}^{t}u(Y)p_{n}(t,Y)\omega(t,Y)\mathrm{d}Y
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =ddвҖӢtвҖӢвҲ«0thвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢdYabsentddрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ўв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\displaystyle=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t}\int_{0}^{t}h(t,Y)\mathrm{d}Y
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ«0tвҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢhвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢdY+hвҖӢ(t,t)absent
superscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ў

 |

 | =вҲ«0tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢ(вҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,Y))вҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢdY+вҲ«0tfвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢ(вҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,Y))вҖӢdYabsent

рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ңsuperscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ўрқ‘“рқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ң

 | 


 | 
 | 
 | +uвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,t)вҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,t).

This allows xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\dot{x}_{n}(t) to be written as


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)

рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқң”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ң

 | 


 |

 | +вҲ«0tfвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢ(вҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢПүвҖӢ(t,Y))вҖӢdY.

 | 
 | (18)


Although Gu etВ al. [24] describe the framework in the full generality above and use ПҮрқң’\chi as another degree of freedom, in their concrete derivations they always fix ПҮ=Пүрқң’рқң”\chi=\omega.
Our general results also use this setting.
For the remainder of this section, we assume the вҖңfull tiltingвҖқ case ПҮ=Пүрқң’рқң”\chi=\omega.
In particular, this means that in Eq.В 18, we essentially substitute Пүрқң”\omega above with 111 and divide each term by the inverse square root of our normalization constant, О¶рқңҒ\zeta, to get the coefficient dynamics that we will use in our arguments:


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=1О¶вҖӢ(t)вҖӢuвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,t)+1О¶вҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ«0tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢ(вҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,Y))вҖӢdYsubscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
1рқңҒрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў1рқңҒрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscript0рқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ң

рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\dot{x}_{n}(t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\zeta(t)}}u(t)p_{n}(t,t)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\zeta(t)}}\int_{0}^{t}u(Y)\left({\frac{\partial}{\partial t}p_{n}\left({t,Y}\right)}\right)\mathrm{d}Y
 | 
 | (19)


Now, if we can show that each of the integrated terms in (18) are linear combinations of xnвҖӢ(t)subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўx_{n}(t), this would be the same as saying that xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=рқҗҖвҖӢ(t)вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+рқҗӣвҖӢ(t)вҖӢuвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
рқҗҖрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\dot{x}_{n}(t)=\mathbf{A}(t)x(t)+\mathbf{b}(t)u(t) for some рқҗҖвҖӢ(t)рқҗҖрқ‘Ў\mathbf{A}(t). Therefore, the incremental update operation would be bounded by the runtime of the matrix-vector operation рқҗҖвҖӢ(t)вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)рқҗҖрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\mathbf{A}(t)x(t).


#### 
D.1.3 Recurrence Width


Our final goal is to show that
xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=рқҗҖвҖӢ(t)вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+рқҗӣвҖӢ(t)вҖӢuвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
рқҗҖрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\dot{x}_{n}(t)=\mathbf{A}(t)x(t)+\mathbf{b}(t)u(t) for some рқҗҖвҖӢ(t)рқҗҖрқ‘Ў\mathbf{A}(t) with constant recurrence width (see Definition 2).
This will show TheoremВ 1, and also imply that the MVM рқҗҖвҖӢ(t)вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)рқҗҖрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\mathbf{A}(t)x(t) can be computed in O~вҖӢ(N)~рқ‘Ӯрқ‘Ғ\tilde{O}(N) time.
To build this argument, we borrow the fact that OPs all have recurrence width 2 and results regarding matrix-vector multiplication of matrices with constant recurrence width along with their inverses.


###### 
Definition 2 ([17]).


An NГ—Nрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒN\times N matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} has recurrence width tрқ‘Ўt if the polynomials aiвҖӢ(X)=вҲ‘j=0NвҲ’1рқҗҖвҖӢ[i,j]вҖӢXjsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘–рқ‘Ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—0рқ‘Ғ1рқҗҖрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptрқ‘Ӣрқ‘—a_{i}(X)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\mathbf{A}[i,j]X^{j} satisfy degвҒЎ(ai)вүӨidegreesubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘–рқ‘–\deg(a_{i})\leq i for i<tрқ‘–рқ‘Ўi<t, and


 | 
 | aiвҖӢ(X)=вҲ‘j=1tgi,jвҖӢ(X)вҖӢaiвҲ’jвҖӢ(X)subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘–рқ‘Ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—1рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘”
- рқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘Ӣsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘Ӣa_{i}(X)=\sum_{j=1}^{t}g_{i,j}(X)a_{i-j}(X)
 | 


for iвүҘtрқ‘–рқ‘Ўi\geq t, where the polynomials gi,jвҲҲв„қвҖӢ[X]subscriptрқ‘”
- рқ‘–рқ‘—в„қdelimited-[]рқ‘Ӣg_{i,j}\in\mathbb{R}[X] have degree at most jрқ‘—j.


###### 
Theorem 5 ([17], Theorem 4.4).


For any NГ—Nрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒN\times N matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} with constant recurrence width, any vector рқҗұвҲҲв„қnрқҗұsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ӣ\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}, рқҗҖрқҗұрқҗҖрқҗұ\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} can be computed with O~вҖӢ(N)~рқ‘Ӯрқ‘Ғ\tilde{O}(N) operations over в„қв„қ\mathbb{R}.


###### 
Theorem 6 ([17], Theorem 7.1).


For any NГ—Nрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒN\times N matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} with constant recurrence width, any vector рқҗұвҲҲв„қnрқҗұsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ӣ\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}, рқҗҖвҲ’1вҖӢрқҗұsuperscriptрқҗҖ1рқҗұ\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{x} can be computed with O~вҖӢ(N)~рқ‘Ӯрқ‘Ғ\tilde{O}(N) operations over в„қв„қ\mathbb{R}.


For the rest of the note weвҖҷll assume that any operation over в„қв„қ\mathbb{R} can be done in constant time. It would be useful for us to define рқҗҸвҲҲв„қNГ—NрқҗҸsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{P}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} such that the coefficients of the OP piвҖӢ(X)subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘–рқ‘Ӣp_{i}(X), i.e. piвҖӢ(X)=вҲ‘j=0NвҲ’1рқҗҸвҖӢ[i,j]вҖӢXjsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘–рқ‘Ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—0рқ‘Ғ1рқҗҸрқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptрқ‘Ӣрқ‘—p_{i}(X)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\mathbf{P}[i,j]X^{j}.


### 
D.2 Proof of TheoremВ 1


This section proves TheoremВ 1, which is restated formally in CorollaryВ D.4.
SectionВ D.2.1 proves some results relating orthogonal polynomials to recurrence width (SectionВ D.1.3).
SectionВ D.2.2 proves CorollaryВ D.4.
SectionsВ D.2.3 andВ D.2.4 provides examples showing how CorollaryВ D.4 can be specialized to exactly recover the HiPPO-LegT, HiPPO-LagT, HiPPO-LegS methods [24].


#### 
D.2.1 Relating Orthogonal Polynomials and Recurrence Width


Next we introduce the following lemma, which will be useful in our arguments:


###### 
Lemma D.1.


For any nрқ‘ӣn, there exists ordered sets of coefficients Оұn={Оұn,i},ОІn={ОІn,i}formulae-sequencesubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘–subscriptрқӣҪрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘–\alpha_{n}=\{\alpha_{n,i}\},\beta_{n}=\{\beta_{n,i}\},


- 
(i)

###### Proof.


Follows from the fact that piвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘–рқ‘§p_{i}(z) for 0вүӨi<N0рқ‘–рқ‘Ғ0\leq i<N forms a basis and the observation of the degrees of the polynomials on the LHS.
вҲҺ


The following matrices will aid in showing verifying that matrix vector multiplication with a given matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} can be computed in OвҖӢ(N~)рқ‘Ӯ~рқ‘ҒO(\tilde{N}) time.


###### 
Definition 3.


рқҗғ1,рқҗғ2вҲҲв„қNГ—N
- subscriptрқҗғ1subscriptрқҗғ2superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{D}_{1},\mathbf{D}_{2}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} are the matrices such that


 | 
 | piвҖІвҖӢ(Z)=вҲ‘j=0NвҲ’1рқҗғ1вҖӢ[i,j]вҖӢZj,В andВ вҖӢZвҖӢpiвҖІвҖӢ(Z)=вҲ‘j=0NвҲ’1рқҗғ2вҖӢ[i,j]вҖӢZj.formulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘–рқ‘Қsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—0рқ‘Ғ1subscriptрқҗғ1рқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘—В andВ рқ‘Қsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘–рқ‘Қsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—0рқ‘Ғ1subscriptрқҗғ2рқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘—p^{\prime}_{i}(Z)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\mathbf{D}_{1}[i,j]Z^{j},\text{ and }\ Zp^{\prime}_{i}(Z)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\mathbf{D}_{2}[i,j]Z^{j}.
 | 


Let рқҗ’рқҗ’\mathbf{S} be the вҖңright shift" matrix, i.e. for any matrix рқҗҢрқҗҢ\mathbf{M}, рқҗҢрқҗ’рқҗҢрқҗ’\mathbf{M}\mathbf{S} has the columns of рқҗҢрқҗҢ\mathbf{M} shifted to right by one. Note that рқҗ’Tsuperscriptрқҗ’рқ‘Ү\mathbf{S}^{T} corresponds to the вҖңleft shift" matrix.


We now note that:


###### 
Lemma D.2.


рқҗғ1=рқҗҸвӢ…diagвҖӢ(0,1,вҖҰ,NвҲ’1)вӢ…рқҗ’Tsubscriptрқҗғ1вӢ…вӢ…рқҗҸdiag01вҖҰрқ‘Ғ1superscriptрқҗ’рқ‘Ү\mathbf{D}_{1}=\mathbf{P}\cdot\mathrm{diag}(0,1,\dots,N-1)\cdot\mathbf{S}^{T} and рқҗғ2=рқҗҸвӢ…diagвҖӢ(0,1,вҖҰ,NвҲ’1)subscriptрқҗғ2вӢ…рқҗҸdiag01вҖҰрқ‘Ғ1\mathbf{D}_{2}=\mathbf{P}\cdot\mathrm{diag}(0,1,\dots,N-1). In particular,
рқҗғ1вҖӢрқҗіsubscriptрқҗғ1рқҗі\mathbf{D}_{1}\mathbf{z} and рқҗғ2вҖӢрқҗіsubscriptрқҗғ2рқҗі\mathbf{D}_{2}\mathbf{z} can be computed in O~вҖӢ(N)~рқ‘Ӯрқ‘Ғ\tilde{O}(N) time for any рқҗівҲҲв„қNрқҗіsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{z}\in\mathbb{R}^{N}.


###### Proof.


Recall that рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P} has the coefficients of the OP polynomials p0вҖӢ(Z),вҖҰ,pNвҲ’1вҖӢ(Z)
- subscriptрқ‘қ0рқ‘ҚвҖҰsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘Ғ1рқ‘Қp_{0}(Z),\dots,p_{N-1}(Z) as its rows. Then note that


 | 
 | pnвҖІвҖӢ(Z)=вҲ‘i=0nвҲ’1iвӢ…рқҗҸвҖӢ[n,i]вӢ…ZiвҲ’1.subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘Қsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–0рқ‘ӣ1вӢ…вӢ…рқ‘–рқҗҸрқ‘ӣрқ‘–superscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘–1p^{\prime}_{n}(Z)=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}i\cdot\mathbf{P}[n,i]\cdot Z^{i-1}.
 | 
 | (20)


The claim on рқҗғ1=рқҗҸвӢ…diagвҖӢ(0,1,вҖҰ,NвҲ’1)вӢ…рқҗ’Tsubscriptрқҗғ1вӢ…вӢ…рқҗҸdiag01вҖҰрқ‘Ғ1superscriptрқҗ’рқ‘Ү\mathbf{D}_{1}=\mathbf{P}\cdot\mathrm{diag}(0,1,\dots,N-1)\cdot\mathbf{S}^{T} follows from the above.
Recall that рқҗғрқҗғ\mathbf{D} has recurrence width of 222. The claim on the runtime of computing рқҗғ1вҖӢрқҗіsubscriptрқҗғ1рқҗі\mathbf{D}_{1}\mathbf{z} then follows from
Theorem 5 and the fact that both diagвҖӢ(0,1,вҖҰ,NвҲ’1)diag01вҖҰрқ‘Ғ1\mathrm{diag}(0,1,\dots,N-1) and рқҗ’Tsuperscriptрқҗ’рқ‘Ү\mathbf{S}^{T} is nрқ‘ӣn-sparse.


From Eq.В 20, it is easy to see that


 | 
 | ZвҖӢpnвҖІвҖӢ(Z)=вҲ‘i=0nвҲ’1iвӢ…рқҗҸвҖӢ[n,i]вӢ…Zi.рқ‘Қsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘Қsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–0рқ‘ӣ1вӢ…вӢ…рқ‘–рқҗҸрқ‘ӣрқ‘–superscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘–Zp^{\prime}_{n}(Z)=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}i\cdot\mathbf{P}[n,i]\cdot Z^{i}.
 | 


The claim on the structure of рқҗғ2subscriptрқҗғ2\mathbf{D}_{2} then follows from the above expression. The claim on runtime of computing рқҗғ2вҖӢрқҗіsubscriptрқҗғ2рқҗі\mathbf{D}_{2}\mathbf{z} follows from essentially the same argument as for рқҗғ1вҖӢрқҗіsubscriptрқҗғ1рқҗі\mathbf{D}_{1}\mathbf{z}.


вҲҺ


Finally, we make the following observation:


###### 
Lemma D.3.


Let рқҗҖвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҖвҖІ\mathbf{A}^{\prime} and рқҗҒвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҒвҖІ\mathbf{B}^{\prime} be defined such that рқҗҖвҖІвҖӢ[n,i]=Оұn,isuperscriptрқҗҖвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘–subscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘–\mathbf{A}^{\prime}[n,i]=\alpha_{n,i} and рқҗҒвҖІвҖӢ[n,i]=ОІn,isuperscriptрқҗҒвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘–subscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘–\mathbf{B}^{\prime}[n,i]=\beta_{n,i}. Then both рқҗҖвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҖвҖІ\mathbf{A}^{\prime} and рқҗҒвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҒвҖІ\mathbf{B}^{\prime} are both products of three matrices: two of which have recurrence width at most 222 and the third is the inverse of a matrix that has recurrence width 222.


###### Proof.


We note that since рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P} expresses the orthogonal polynomials in standard basis, рқҗҸвҲ’1superscriptрқҗҸ1\mathbf{P}^{-1} changes from OP basis to standard basis. This along with LemmaВ D.2 implies that рқҗҖ=рқҗҸвӢ…(diagвҖӢ(0,1,вҖҰ,NвҲ’1)вӢ…рқҗ’T)вӢ…рқҗҸвҲ’1рқҗҖвӢ…рқҗҸвӢ…diag01вҖҰрқ‘Ғ1superscriptрқҗ’рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҸ1\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{P}\cdot\left({\mathrm{diag}(0,1,\dots,N-1)\cdot\mathbf{S}^{T}}\right)\cdot\mathbf{P}^{-1}. It is easy to check that diagвҖӢ(0,1,вҖҰ,NвҲ’1)вӢ…рқҗ’TвӢ…diag01вҖҰрқ‘Ғ1superscriptрқҗ’рқ‘Ү\mathrm{diag}(0,1,\dots,N-1)\cdot\mathbf{S}^{T} has recurrence width 111 and the claim on рқҗҖвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҖвҖІ\mathbf{A}^{\prime} follows since рқҗҸрқҗҸ\mathbf{P} has recurrence width 222. A similar argument proves the claim on рқҗҒвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҒвҖІ\mathbf{B}^{\prime}.
вҲҺ


#### 
D.2.2 HiPPO for General Measures


Let Оё:в„қвүҘ0вҶҰв„қвүҘ0:рқңғmaps-tosubscriptв„қabsent0subscriptв„қabsent0\theta:\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}\mapsto\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} be a function such that for all tрқ‘Ўt, ОёвҖӢ(t)вүӨtрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў\theta(t)\leq t and ОёвҖӢ(t)рқңғрқ‘Ў\theta(t) is differentiable.


In what follows, define


 | 
 | z=2вҖӢ(YвҲ’t)ОёвҖӢ(t)+1.рқ‘§
2рқ‘Ңрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ў1z=\frac{2({Y}-t)}{\theta(t)}+1.
 | 


We note that


 | 
 | dвҖӢzdвҖӢY=2ОёвҖӢ(t).dрқ‘§dрқ‘Ң2рқңғрқ‘Ў\frac{\mathrm{d}z}{\mathrm{d}{Y}}=\frac{2}{\theta(t)}.
 | 
 | (21)


Further, note that:


 | 
 | dвҖӢzdвҖӢtdрқ‘§dрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}z}{\mathrm{d}t}
 | =ddвҖӢtвҖӢ(2вҖӢ(YвҲ’t)ОёвҖӢ(t)+1)absentddрқ‘Ў

 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ’2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҲ’2вҖӢ(YвҲ’t)вҖӢОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)Оё2вҖӢ(t)absent2рқңғрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ңрқ‘ЎsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқңғ2рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=-\frac{2}{\theta(t)}-\frac{2({Y}-t)\theta^{\prime}(t)}{\theta^{2}(t)}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ’2Оё2вҖӢ(t)вҖӢ(ОёвҖӢ(t)+(YвҲ’t)вҖӢОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)).absent2superscriptрқңғ2рқ‘Ў
рқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқ‘ЎsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=-\frac{2}{\theta^{2}(t)}\left({\theta(t)+({Y}-t)\theta^{\prime}(t)}\right).
 | 


From the definition of zрқ‘§z, we see that YвҲ’t=(zвҲ’1)вҖӢОёвҖӢ(t)2рқ‘Ңрқ‘Ўрқ‘§1рқңғрқ‘Ў2{Y}-t=\frac{(z-1)\theta(t)}{2}. Then


 | 
 | dвҖӢzdвҖӢtdрқ‘§dрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}z}{\mathrm{d}t}
 | =вҲ’2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ(1+(zвҲ’12)вҖӢОёвҖІвҖӢ(t))absent2рқңғрқ‘Ў
1рқ‘§12superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=-\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\left({1+\left({\frac{z-1}{2}}\right)\theta^{\prime}(t)}\right)
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ’2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҲ’(zвҲ’1)вҖӢОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t).absent2рқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘§1superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=-\frac{2}{\theta(t)}-\frac{(z-1)\theta^{\prime}(t)}{\theta(t)}.
 | 
 | (22)


Additionally, given a measure Пүрқң”\omega on [-1,1] and OP family p0вҖӢ(Y),p1вҖӢ(Y),вҖҰ
- subscriptрқ‘қ0рқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қ1рқ‘ҢвҖҰp_{0}({Y}),\ p_{1}({Y}),\dots such that for all iвү jрқ‘–рқ‘—i\neq j,


 | 
 | вҲ«вҲ’11piвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpjвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢПүвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢdY=Оҙi,j,subscriptsuperscript11subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘–рқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘—рқ‘Ңрқң”рқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқӣҝ
- рқ‘–рқ‘—\int^{1}_{-1}p_{i}({Y})p_{j}({Y})\omega({Y})\mathrm{d}{Y}=\delta_{i,j},
 | 


define


 | 
 | ПүвҖӢ(Y,t)=2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢПүвҖӢ(z)вҖӢВ andВ вҖӢpnвҖӢ(Y,t)=pnвҖӢ(z).рқң”рқ‘Ңрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқ‘Ўрқң”рқ‘§В andВ subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ңрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§\omega({Y},t)=\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\omega(z)\text{ and }p_{n}({Y},t)=p_{n}(z).
 | 


Then we can adjust (17) to:


 | 
 | xnвҖӢ(t)=вҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdY.subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘Ңx_{n}(t)=\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})p_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}.
 | 
 | (23)


The Leibniz integral rule states that


 | 
 | вҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢвҲ«ОұвҖӢ(t)ОІвҖӢ(t)hвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢdY=вҲ«ОұвҖӢ(t)ОІвҖӢ(t)вҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢhвҖӢ(t,Y)вҖӢdYвҲ’ОұвҖІвҖӢ(t)вҖӢhвҖӢ(ОұвҖӢ(t),t)+ОІвҖІвҖӢ(t)вҖӢhвҖӢ(t,t).

рқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ЎрқӣҪрқ‘Ўв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘Ң
superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ЎрқӣҪрқ‘Ў

рқ‘Ўв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңdifferential-dрқ‘ҢsuperscriptрқӣјвҖІрқ‘Ўв„Һрқӣјрқ‘Ўрқ‘ЎsuperscriptрқӣҪвҖІрқ‘Ўв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{\alpha(t)}^{\beta(t)}h(t,{Y})\mathrm{d}{Y}=\int_{\alpha(t)}^{\beta(t)}\frac{\partial}{\partial t}h(t,{Y})\mathrm{d}{Y}-\alpha^{\prime}(t)h(\alpha(t),t)+\beta^{\prime}(t)h(t,t).
 | 


If we let ОұвҖӢ(t)=tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)рқӣјрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ў\alpha(t)=t-\theta(t) and ОІвҖӢ(t)=tрқӣҪрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў\beta(t)=t, then applying the Leibniz rule to (23) we get:


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)
 | =вҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢвҲӮвҲӮtвҖӢ(pnвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t))вҖӢdYвҲ’(1вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t))вҖӢuвҖӢ(tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t))вҖӢpnвҖӢ(tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t),t)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)absentsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ң

рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘Ң1superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left({p_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}}\right)\mathrm{d}{Y}-(1-\theta^{\prime}(t))u(t-\theta(t))p_{n}(t-\theta(t),t)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | +uвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(t,t)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)
рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\ \ \ \ +u(t)p_{n}(t,t)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ’(1вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t))вҖӢuвҖӢ(tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t))вҖӢpnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)+uвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(1)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)absent
1superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ12рқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ12рқңғрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=-(1-\theta^{\prime}(t))u(t-\theta(t))p_{n}(-1)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}+u(t)p_{n}(1)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | +вҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢdвҖӢzdвҖӢtвҖӢpnвҖІвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdYвҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdY.
subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңdрқ‘§dрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘ҢsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\displaystyle\ \ \ \ \ +\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})\frac{\mathrm{d}z}{\mathrm{d}t}p^{\prime}_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}-\frac{\theta^{\prime}(t)}{\theta(t)}\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})p_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}.
 | 


From (22), it follows that


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)=-
 | 2вҖӢ(1вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t))вҖӢuвҖӢ(tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t))вҖӢpnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)ОёвҖӢ(t)+2вӢ…uвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(1)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҲ’2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖІвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdYвҲ’
21superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1рқңғрқ‘ЎвӢ…2рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1рқңғрқ‘Ўlimit-from2рқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\displaystyle\frac{2(1-\theta^{\prime}(t))u(t-\theta(t))p_{n}(-1)}{\theta(t)}+\frac{2\cdot u(t)p_{n}(1)}{\theta(t)}-\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})p^{\prime}_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}-
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢ(zвҲ’1)вҖӢpnвҖІвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdYвҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdY.superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңрқ‘§1subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘ҢsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\displaystyle\frac{\theta^{\prime}(t)}{\theta(t)}\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})(z-1)p^{\prime}_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}-\frac{\theta^{\prime}(t)}{\theta(t)}\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})p_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}.
 | 
 | (24)


Because degвҒЎ(pnвҖІвҖӢ(z))вүӨnвҲ’1degreesubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқ‘ӣ1\deg\left({p^{\prime}_{n}(z)}\right)\leq n-1 and degвҒЎ((zвҲ’1)вҖӢpnвҖІвҖӢ(z))вүӨndegreeрқ‘§1subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқ‘ӣ\deg\left({(z-1)p^{\prime}_{n}(z)}\right)\leq n, they can be written as a linear combination of {piвҖІ}iвүӨnsubscriptsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘–рқ‘–рқ‘ӣ\{p^{\prime}_{i}\}_{i\leq n}. Let us define {Оұn,j}subscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘—\{\alpha_{n,j}\}, {ОІn,j}subscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘—\{\beta_{n,j}\} such that


 | 
 | pnвҖІвҖӢ(z)=вҲ‘j=0nвҲ’1Оұn,jвҖӢpjвҖӢ(z)вҖӢВ andВ вҖӢ(zвҲ’1)вҖӢpnвҖІвҖӢ(z)=вҲ‘j=0nОІn,jвҖӢpjвҖӢ(z).subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘§superscriptsubscriptрқ‘—0рқ‘ӣ1subscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘—рқ‘§В andВ рқ‘§1subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘қвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘§superscriptsubscriptрқ‘—0рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘—рқ‘§p^{\prime}_{n}(z)=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\alpha_{n,j}p_{j}(z)\text{ and }(z-1)p^{\prime}_{n}(z)=\sum_{j=0}^{n}\beta_{n,j}p_{j}(z).
 | 
 | (25)


Then by using (25) in (24), we get:


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)
 | =вҲ’2вҖӢ(1вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t))вҖӢuвҖӢ(t+ОёвҖӢ(t))вҖӢpnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)ОёвҖӢ(t)+2вӢ…uвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(1)ОёвҖӢ(t)absent

 |

- рқ‘ӣрқ‘—subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘—рқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\displaystyle\ \ \ \ -\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\sum^{n-1}_{j=0}\alpha_{n,j}\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})p_{j}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ‘j=0nОІn,jвҖӢвҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpjвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdYвҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ«tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t)tuвҖӢ(Y)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(z)вҖӢ2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢdYsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘—0subscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘—subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘—рқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘ҢsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ңsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқңғрқ‘Ўdifferential-dрқ‘Ң\displaystyle\ \ \ \ -\frac{\theta^{\prime}(t)}{\theta(t)}\sum^{n}_{j=0}\beta_{n,j}\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})p_{j}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}-\frac{\theta^{\prime}(t)}{\theta(t)}\int^{t}_{t-\theta(t)}u({Y})p_{n}(z)\frac{2}{\theta(t)}\mathrm{d}{Y}
 | 


 |

 | =вҲ’2вҖӢ(1вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t))вҖӢuвҖӢ(t+ОёвҖӢ(t))вҖӢpnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)ОёвҖӢ(t)+2вӢ…uвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpnвҖӢ(1)ОёвҖӢ(t)absent

 | вҲ’2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ‘j=0nвҲ’1Оұn,jвҖӢxjвҖӢ(t)вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢвҲ‘j=0nОІn,jвҖӢxjвҖӢ(t)вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢxnвҖӢ(t).2рқңғрқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ӣ1рқ‘—0subscriptрқӣј

 | 


Thus, in vector form we get


###### 
Theorem 7.


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’1ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢрқҗҖ1вҖӢ(t)вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)вҲ’2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ(1вҲ’ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t))вҖӢuвҖӢ(tвҲ’ОёвҖӢ(t))вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)вӢ®]+2ОёвҖӢ(t)вҖӢuвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(1)вӢ®]subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
1рқңғрқ‘ЎsubscriptрқҗҖ1рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқ‘Ў1superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқңғрқ‘ЎmatrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®2рқңғрқ‘Ўрқ‘ўрқ‘ЎmatrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®\dot{x}_{n}(t)=-\frac{1}{\theta(t)}\mathbf{A}_{1}(t)x(t)-\frac{2}{\theta(t)}(1-\theta^{\prime}(t))u(t-\theta(t))\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(-1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}+\frac{2}{\theta(t)}u(t)\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}
 | 


where рқҗҖ1вҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[n,k]={2вҖӢОұn,k+ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)вҖӢОІn,kВ ifВ вҖӢk<nОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)вҖӢОІn,n+ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)В ifВ вҖӢk=n0В otherwiseВ subscriptрқҗҖ1рқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcases
2subscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘ЎsubscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳВ ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ
superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘ЎsubscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ӣsuperscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘ЎВ ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ0В otherwiseВ \mathbf{A}_{1}(t)[n,k]=\begin{cases}2\alpha_{n,k}+\theta^{\prime}(t)\beta_{n,k}&\text{ if }k<n\\
\theta^{\prime}(t)\beta_{n,n}+\theta^{\prime}(t)&\text{ if }k=n\\
0\ \ &\text{ otherwise }\end{cases} for Оұn,ksubscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\alpha_{n,k},ОІn,ksubscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\beta_{n,k} as defined in (25).


###### 
Corollary D.4.


The matrix рқҗҖ1subscriptрқҗҖ1\mathbf{A}_{1} in TheoremВ 7 can be re-written as


 | 
 | рқҗҖ1=2вӢ…рқҗҖвҖІ+ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)вӢ…рқҗҒвҖІ+ОёвҖІвҖӢ(t)вӢ…рқҗҲ.subscriptрқҗҖ1
вӢ…2superscriptрқҗҖвҖІвӢ…superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘ЎsuperscriptрқҗҒвҖІвӢ…superscriptрқңғвҖІрқ‘ЎрқҗҲ\mathbf{A}_{1}=2\cdot\mathbf{A}^{\prime}+\theta^{\prime}(t)\cdot\mathbf{B}^{\prime}+\theta^{\prime}(t)\cdot\mathbf{I}.
 | 
 | (26)


In particular, both рқҗҖвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҖвҖІ\mathbf{A}^{\prime} and рқҗҒвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҒвҖІ\mathbf{B}^{\prime} both products of three matrices: two of which have recurrence width at most 222 and the third is the inverse of a matrix that has recurrence width 222.


###### Proof.


Eq.В 26 follows from TheoremВ 7 and defining рқҗҖвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҖвҖІ\mathbf{A}^{\prime} and рқҗҒвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҒвҖІ\mathbf{B}^{\prime} to contain the Оұn,ksubscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\alpha_{n,k} and ОІn,ksubscriptрқӣҪ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\beta_{n,k} coefficients.

#### 
D.2.3 Translated HiPPO (Sliding Windows)


The case when ОёвҖӢ(t)=Оёрқңғрқ‘Ўрқңғ\theta(t)=\theta for all tрқ‘Ўt represents a constant-size sliding window, which Gu etВ al. [24] denote as the вҖңTranslated HiPPOвҖқ case with instantiations such as HiPPO-LegT (Translated Legendre) and HiPPO-LagT (Translated Laguerre).


We now state a corollary of Theorem 7 for the case of ОёвҖӢ(t)=Оёрқңғрқ‘Ўрқңғ\theta(t)=\theta for all t.


###### 
Corollary D.5.


Let ОёвҖӢ(t)=Оёрқңғрқ‘Ўрқңғ\theta(t)=\theta for all tрқ‘Ўt. Then


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’1ОёвҖӢрқҗҖ1вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)вҲ’2ОёвҖӢuвҖӢ(tвҲ’Оё)вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)вӢ®]+2ОёвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(1)вӢ®].subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
1рқңғsubscriptрқҗҖ1рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқ‘ўрқ‘ЎрқңғmatrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®2рқңғрқ‘ўрқ‘ЎmatrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®\dot{x}_{n}(t)=-\frac{1}{\theta}\mathbf{A}_{1}x(t)-\frac{2}{\theta}u(t-\theta)\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(-1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}+\frac{2}{\theta}u(t)\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}.
 | 


where рқҗҖ1вҖӢ[n,j]={2вҖӢОұn,kВ ifВ вҖӢk<n0В otherwiseВ subscriptрқҗҖ1рқ‘ӣрқ‘—cases2subscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳВ ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ0В otherwiseВ \mathbf{A}_{1}[n,j]=\begin{cases}2\alpha_{n,k}&\text{ if }k<n\\
0\ \ &\text{ otherwise }\end{cases}.


Next, we use the approximation


 | 
 | uвҖӢ(x)вүҲвҲ‘k=0NвҲ’1xkвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpkвҖӢ(z).рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҳ0рқ‘Ғ1subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ҳрқ‘§u(x)\approx\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x_{k}(t)p_{k}(z).
 | 


to handle the uвҖӢ(tвҲ’Оё)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқңғu(t-\theta) term in Corollary D.5.


###### 
Corollary D.6.


Let ОёвҖӢ(t)=Оёрқңғрқ‘Ўрқңғ\theta(t)=\theta for all tрқ‘Ўt. Then


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)вүҲвҲ’1ОёвҖӢрқҗҖвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+2ОёвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(1)вӢ®]subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
1рқңғрқҗҖрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқ‘ўрқ‘ЎmatrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®\dot{x}_{n}(t)\approx-\frac{1}{\theta}\mathbf{A}x(t)+\frac{2}{\theta}u(t)\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}
 | 


where рқҗҖ=рқҗҖ1+2вҖӢрқҗҖ2рқҗҖ
subscriptрқҗҖ12subscriptрқҗҖ2\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{A}_{1}+2\mathbf{A}_{2} for рқҗҖ1subscriptрқҗҖ1\mathbf{A}_{1} as defined in Corollary D.5 and рқҗҖ2вҖӢ[n,k]=pnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)вҖӢpkвҖӢ(вҲ’1)subscriptрқҗҖ2рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ҳ1\mathbf{A}_{2}[n,k]=p_{n}(-1)p_{k}(-1).


###### Proof.


To approximate uвҖӢ(tвҲ’Оё)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқңғu(t-\theta), we note that when Y=tвҲ’Оёрқ‘Ңрқ‘Ўрқңғ{Y}=t-\theta, z=вҲ’1рқ‘§1z=-1. Then


 | 
 | uвҖӢ(tвҲ’Оё)вүҲвҲ‘k=0NвҲ’1xkвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpkвҖӢ(вҲ’1).рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқңғsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҳ0рқ‘Ғ1subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ҳ1u(t-\theta)\approx\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x_{k}(t)p_{k}(-1).
 | 


Then by Corollary D.5,


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)
 | вүҲвҲ’1ОёвҖӢрқҗҖ1вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)вҲ’2ОёвҖӢ(вҲ‘k=0NвҲ’1xkвҖӢ(t)вҖӢpkвҖӢ(вҲ’1))вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)вӢ®]+2ОёвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)вӢ®].absent
1рқңғsubscriptрқҗҖ1рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў2рқңғsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҳ0рқ‘Ғ1subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ҳ1matrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®2рқңғрқ‘ўрқ‘ЎmatrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®\displaystyle\approx-\frac{1}{\theta}\mathbf{A}_{1}x(t)-\frac{2}{\theta}\left({\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x_{k}(t)p_{k}(-1)}\right)\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(-1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}+\frac{2}{\theta}u(t)\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(-1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}.
 | 
 | (27)


Let us define a matrix, рқҗҖ2вҲҲв„қNГ—NsubscriptрқҗҖ2superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}_{2}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} matrix such that рқҗҖ2вҖӢ[n,k]=pnвҖӢ(вҲ’1)вҖӢpkвҖӢ(вҲ’1)subscriptрқҗҖ2рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ҳ1\mathbf{A}_{2}[n,k]=p_{n}(-1)p_{k}(-1). Then the claim follows.


вҲҺ


We now show that the special case of Corollary D.6 for Legendre matches the results from [24].


###### 
Corollary D.7.


Let pnвҖӢ(z)=(2вҖӢn+12)1/2вҖӢPnвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§superscript
2рқ‘ӣ1212subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘ӣрқ‘§p_{n}(z)=\left({\frac{2n+1}{2}}\right)^{1/2}P_{n}(z) where PnвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘ӣрқ‘§P_{n}(z) are the Legendre polynomials. Then


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)вүҲ1ОёвҖӢрқҗҖвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+2ОёвҖӢрқҗӣвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
1рқңғрқҗҖрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқҗӣрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)\approx\frac{1}{\theta}\mathbf{A}x(t)+\frac{2}{\theta}\mathbf{b}u(t)
 | 


where


 | 
 | рқҗҖвҖӢ[n,k]=(2вҖӢn+1)12вҖӢ(2вҖӢk+1)12вҖӢ{1В ifВ вҖӢkвүӨn(вҲ’1)nвҲ’kВ ifВ вҖӢkвүҘn,рқҗҖрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳsuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ112superscript
2рқ‘ҳ112cases1В ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsuperscript1рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳВ ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ\mathbf{A}[n,k]=(2n+1)^{\frac{1}{2}}\left({2k+1}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\begin{cases}1&\text{ if }k\leq n\\
(-1)^{n-k}\ \ &\text{ if }k\geq n\end{cases},
 | 


and рқҗӣвҖӢ[n]=(2вҖӢn+12)12рқҗӣdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣsuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ1212\mathbf{b}[n]=\left({\frac{2n+1}{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}.


###### Proof.


From Corollary D.6,


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)вүҲвҲ’1ОёвҖӢрқҗҖвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+2ОёвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(1)вӢ®]subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
1рқңғрқҗҖрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў2рқңғрқ‘ўрқ‘ЎmatrixвӢ®subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ1вӢ®\dot{x}_{n}(t)\approx-\frac{1}{\theta}\mathbf{A}x(t)+\frac{2}{\theta}u(t)\begin{bmatrix}\vdots\\
p_{n}(1)\\
\vdots\end{bmatrix}
 |

#### 
D.2.4 Scaled HiPPO: Recovering HiPPO-LegS


We now use Theorem 7 to recover the HiPPO-LegS instantiation for the вҖңScaled LegendreвҖқ measure, the main method from Gu etВ al. [24].


###### 
Corollary D.8.


Let pnвҖӢ(z)=(2вҖӢn+12)1/2вҖӢPnвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§superscript

 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=1tвҖӢрқҗҖвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+2tвҖӢрқҗӣвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў

 | 


where


 | 
 | рқҗҖвҖӢ[n,k]={(2вҖӢn+1)12вҖӢ(2вҖӢk+1)12В ifВ вҖӢk<nn+1В ifВ вҖӢk=n0В ifВ вҖӢk>n,рқҗҖрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcasessuperscript

n+1&\text{ if }k=n\\
0\ \ &\text{ if }k>n\end{cases},
 | 


and рқҗӣвҖӢ[n]=(2вҖӢn+12)12рқҗӣdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣsuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ1212\mathbf{b}[n]=\left({\frac{2n+1}{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}.


###### Proof.

 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’1tвҖӢрқҗҖ1вҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+2tвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[вӢ®pnвҖӢ(1)вӢ®]subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў

p_{n}(1)\\

 | 


where


 | 
 | рқҗҖ1вҖӢ(t)вҖӢ[n,k]={2вҖӢОұn,k+ОІn,kВ ifВ вҖӢk<nОІn,n+1В ifВ вҖӢk=n0В otherwiseВ subscriptрқҗҖ1рқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcases

 | (30)


for Оұn,ksubscriptрқӣј
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\alpha_{n,k},ОІn,ksubscriptрқӣҪ

- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcasessuperscript

 |

 |

 |

n+1\ \ &\text{ if }k=n\\
0\ \ &\text{ if }k>n\\
\end{cases},
 | 


which completes our claim.
вҲҺ


### 
D.3 Proof of CorollaryВ 4.1: HiPPO for Classical Orthogonal Polynomials


This section proves CorollaryВ 4.1, showing that the HiPPO matrices for measures corresponding to classical families of orthogonal polynomials [11] are quasiseparable.
We define quasi-separability in SectionВ D.3.1.
TheoremВ 8 proves the claimed result for Jacobi polynomials and LemmaВ D.11 proves the claimed result for Laguerre polynomials.


We note that there is a third family of classical OPs, the Hermite polynomials [11], which have a two-sided infinite measure.
However, since HiPPO is about continuous-time memorization of a functionвҖҷs history, it requires a one-sided measure and therefore the Hermite polynomials are not appropriate.


#### 
D.3.1 Quasiseparable Matrices


###### 
Definition 4 (from [19]).


A matrix рқҗ‘вҲҲв„қNГ—Nрқҗ‘superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{R}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N} is (p,q)рқ‘қрқ‘һ(p,q)-quasiseparable if


- 
вҖў 


Every matrix contained strictly above the diagonal has rank at most pрқ‘қp.


- 
вҖў 


Every matrix contained strictly below the diagonal has rank at most qрқ‘һq.


A (q,q)рқ‘һрқ‘һ(q,q)-quasiseparable matrix is called qрқ‘һq-quasiseparable.


We are interested in showing the рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} matrices for a broad class of OPs in Corollary D.6 are OвҖӢ(1)рқ‘Ӯ1O(1)-quasiseperable. We now state some properties of qрқ‘һq-quasiseparable matrices:


###### 
Lemma D.9.


Let рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q} be qрқ‘һq-quasiseparable. Then:


- 
(i) 


For any qвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘һвҖІq^{\prime}-quasiseparable matrix рқҗҗвҖІвҲҲв„қNГ—NsuperscriptрқҗҗвҖІsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{Q}^{\prime}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗҗВұрқҗҗвҖІplus-or-minusрқҗҗsuperscriptрқҗҗвҖІ\mathbf{Q}\pm\mathbf{Q}^{\prime} is (q+qвҖІ)
рқ‘һsuperscriptрқ‘һвҖІ(q+q^{\prime})-quasiseparable.


- 
(ii) 


For any рқҗ„вҲҲв„қNГ—Nрқҗ„superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{E}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗ„рқҗ„\mathbf{E} is rрқ‘ҹr-quasiseparable where r=rankвҖӢ(рқҗ„)рқ‘ҹrankрқҗ„r=\text{rank}(\mathbf{E}).


- 
(iii) 


For any two diagonal matrices рқҗғ1,рқҗғ2вҲҲв„қNГ—N
- subscriptрқҗғ1subscriptрқҗғ2superscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{D}_{1},\ \mathbf{D}_{2}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}, рқҗғ1вҖӢрқҗҗрқҗғ2subscriptрқҗғ1subscriptрқҗҗрқҗғ2\mathbf{D}_{1}\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{D}_{2} is qрқ‘һq-quasiseparable.


###### Proof.


We argue each point separately:


- 
(i) 


Any submatrix contained strictly below or above the diagonal in рқҗҗрқҗҗ\mathbf{Q} has rank вүӨqabsentрқ‘һ\leq q and its corresponding submatrix in рқҗҗвҖІsuperscriptрқҗҗвҖІ\mathbf{Q}^{\prime} also has rank вүӨqвҖІabsentsuperscriptрқ‘һвҖІ\leq q^{\prime}. This implies that the corresponding submatrix in рқҗҗВұрқҗҗвҖІplus-or-minusрқҗҗsuperscriptрқҗҗвҖІ\mathbf{Q}\pm\mathbf{Q}^{\prime} has rank вүӨq+qвҖІabsent
рқ‘һsuperscriptрқ‘һвҖІ\leq q+q^{\prime}. Therefore рқҗҗВұрқҗҗвҖІplus-or-minusрқҗҗsuperscriptрқҗҗвҖІ\mathbf{Q}\pm\mathbf{Q}^{\prime} is (q+qвҖІ)
рқ‘һsuperscriptрқ‘һвҖІ(q+q^{\prime})-quasiseparable.


- 
(ii) 


Let the r=rankвҖӢ(рқҗ„)рқ‘ҹrankрқҗ„r=\text{rank}(\mathbf{E}). Thus any submatrix in рқҗ„рқҗ„\mathbf{E} has rank вүӨrabsentрқ‘ҹ\leq r. Then рқҗ„рқҗ„\mathbf{E} is rрқ‘ҹr-quasiseparable.


- 
(iii) 


Multiplication by diagonal matrices only scales the rows and columns, leaving the rank of each submatrix unchanged.


вҲҺ


####

#### 
D.3.3 HiPPO for Jacobi Polynomials


###### 
Theorem 8.
