(39) makes use of the fact that (вҲ’1)n+k=(вҲ’1)nвҲ’ksuperscript1
рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳsuperscript1рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ(-1)^{n+k}=(-1)^{n-k} along with the definitions above.


Any submatrix of рқҗҗ1subscriptрқҗҗ1\mathbf{Q}_{1} below the diagonal contains all 111s, and submatrix of рқҗҗ1subscriptрқҗҗ1\mathbf{Q}_{1} above the diagonal contains all 00s. Then any submatrix above or below the diagonal has rank 1. Therefore рқҗҗ1subscriptрқҗҗ1\mathbf{Q}_{1} is 1-quasiseparable. Since рқҗҗ1subscriptрқҗҗ1\mathbf{Q}_{1} is 1-quasiseparable and рқҗғ11,рқҗғ12,рқҗғ21,рқҗғ22
- subscriptрқҗғ11subscriptрқҗғ12subscriptрқҗғ21subscriptрқҗғ22\mathbf{D}_{11},\mathbf{D}_{12},\mathbf{D}_{21},\mathbf{D}_{22} are all diagonal matrices, part (iii) of Lemma D.9 implies that the matrices рқҗғ11вҖӢрқҗҗ1вҖӢрқҗғ12subscriptрқҗғ11subscriptрқҗҗ1subscriptрқҗғ12\mathbf{D}_{11}\mathbf{Q}_{1}\mathbf{D}_{12} and рқҗғ21вҖӢрқҗҗ1вҖӢрқҗғ22subscriptрқҗғ21subscriptрқҗҗ1subscriptрқҗғ22\mathbf{D}_{21}\mathbf{Q}_{1}\mathbf{D}_{22} are both 1-quasiseparable. Therefore part (i) of Lemma D.9 implies that рқҗҖ1subscriptрқҗҖ1\mathbf{A}_{1} is 2-quasiseparable.


From (4.1.1) and (4.1.4) in [53], it is known that


 | 
 | pnОұ,ОІвҖӢ(1)=1О»nОұ,ОІвҖӢ(n+Оұn)вҖӢВ andВ вҖӢpnОұ,ОІвҖӢ(1)=(вҲ’1)nО»nОұ,ОІвҖӢ(n+ОІn)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ
- рқӣјрқӣҪ11subscriptsuperscriptрқңҶ
- рқӣјрқӣҪрқ‘ӣbinomial
рқ‘ӣрқӣјрқ‘ӣВ andВ superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣ
- рқӣјрқӣҪ1superscript1рқ‘ӣsubscriptsuperscriptрқңҶ
- рқӣјрқӣҪрқ‘ӣbinomial
рқ‘ӣрқӣҪрқ‘ӣ{p_{n}^{\alpha,\beta}}(1)=\frac{1}{\lambda^{\alpha,\beta}_{n}}\binom{n+\alpha}{n}\text{ and }{p_{n}^{\alpha,\beta}}(1)=\frac{(-1)^{n}}{\lambda^{\alpha,\beta}_{n}}\binom{n+\beta}{n}
 | 


where


 | 
 | (zn)={О“вҖӢ(z+1)О“вҖӢ(n+1)вҖӢО“вҖӢ(zвҲ’n+1)В ifВ вҖӢnвүҘ0 0В ifВ вҖӢn<0.binomialрқ‘§рқ‘ӣcasesО“

\ 0\ \ \ &\text{ if }n<0\end{cases}.
 | 


Then рқҗҖ2subscriptрқҗҖ2\mathbf{A}_{2} can be written рқҗғ3вҖӢрқҗҗ2вҖӢрқҗғ4subscriptрқҗғ3subscriptрқҗҗ2subscriptрқҗғ4\mathbf{D}_{3}\mathbf{Q}_{2}\mathbf{D}_{4} where рқҗғ3,рқҗғ4
- subscriptрқҗғ3subscriptрқҗғ4\mathbf{D}_{3},\mathbf{D}_{4} are the diagonal matrices such that


 | 
 | рқҗғ3вҖӢ[n,n]=(вҲ’1)nО»nОұ,ОІвҖӢ(n+ОІn)вҖӢ,В вҖӢрқҗғ4вҖӢ[k,k]=(вҲ’1)kО»kОұ,ОІвҖӢ(k+ОІk),subscriptрқҗғ3рқ‘ӣрқ‘ӣsuperscript1рқ‘ӣsubscriptsuperscriptрқңҶ
- рқӣјрқӣҪрқ‘ӣbinomial
рқ‘ӣрқӣҪрқ‘ӣ,В subscriptрқҗғ4рқ‘ҳрқ‘ҳsuperscript1рқ‘ҳsubscriptsuperscriptрқңҶ
- рқӣјрқӣҪрқ‘ҳbinomial
рқ‘ҳрқӣҪрқ‘ҳ\mathbf{D}_{3}[n,n]=\frac{(-1)^{n}}{\lambda^{\alpha,\beta}_{n}}\binom{n+\beta}{n}\text{, }\mathbf{D}_{4}[k,k]=\frac{(-1)^{k}}{\lambda^{\alpha,\beta}_{k}}\binom{k+\beta}{k},
 | 


where рқҗҗ2вҖӢ[n,k]=1subscriptрқҗҗ2рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ1\mathbf{Q}_{2}[n,k]=1 for all 0вүӨn,k<Nformulae-sequence0рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ғ0\leq n,k<N. рқҗҗ2subscriptрқҗҗ2\mathbf{Q}_{2} has rank 1, and рқҗғ3,рқҗғ4
- subscriptрқҗғ3subscriptрқҗғ4\mathbf{D}_{3},\mathbf{D}_{4} are diagonal matrices. Hence by part (ii) and (iii) Lemma D.9, рқҗҖ2subscriptрқҗҖ2\mathbf{A}_{2} is 1-quasiseparable.


Since рқҗҖ1subscriptрқҗҖ1\mathbf{A}_{1} is 2-quasiseparable and рқҗҖ2subscriptрқҗҖ2\mathbf{A}_{2} is 1-quasiseparable, part (i) of Lemma D.9 implies that рқҗҖ=рқҗҖ1+2вҖӢрқҗҖ2рқҗҖ
subscriptрқҗҖ12subscriptрқҗҖ2\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{A}_{1}+2\mathbf{A}_{2} is 3-quasiseparable and the claim follows.

#### 
D.3.4 HiPPO-LagT


The Laguerre polynomial of degree nрқ‘ӣn with parameters Оұ>вҲ’1рқӣј1\alpha>-1 will be denoted LnОұвҖӢ(z)subscriptsuperscriptрқҗҝрқӣјрқ‘ӣрқ‘§L^{\alpha}_{n}\left({z}\right). The Laguerre polynomials are orthogonal with respect to measure zОұвҖӢeвҲ’zsuperscriptрқ‘§рқӣјsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘§z^{\alpha}e^{-z}. In particular, from (5.1.1) in [53] we know
that


 | 
 | вҲ«вҲ’1вҲһLnОұвҖӢ(z)вҖӢLmОұвҖӢ(z)вҖӢzОұвҖӢeвҲ’zвҖӢdz=О“вҖӢ(n+Оұ+1)!О“вҖӢ(n+1)вҖӢОҙn,m.superscriptsubscript1subscriptsuperscriptрқҗҝрқӣјрқ‘ӣрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқҗҝрқӣјрқ‘ҡрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘§рқӣјsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘§differential-dрқ‘§О“
рқ‘ӣрқӣј1О“
рқ‘ӣ1subscriptрқӣҝ
- рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҡ\int_{-1}^{\infty}L^{\alpha}_{n}\left({z}\right)L^{\alpha}_{m}\left({z}\right)z^{\alpha}e^{-z}\mathrm{d}z=\frac{\Gamma(n+\alpha+1)!}{\Gamma(n+1)}\delta_{n,m}.
 | 


Let О»n=(О“вҖӢ(n+1)О“вҖӢ(n+Оұ+1))12subscriptрқңҶрқ‘ӣsuperscriptО“
рқ‘ӣ1О“
рқ‘ӣрқӣј112\lambda_{n}=\left({\frac{\Gamma(n+1)}{\Gamma(n+\alpha+1)}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} be our normalization constant. We note that the normalized Laguerre polynomials


 | 
 | pnвҖӢ(z)=О»nвҖӢLnОұвҖӢ(tвҲ’Y)subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§subscriptрқңҶрқ‘ӣsubscriptsuperscriptрқҗҝрқӣјрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңp_{n}\left({z}\right)=\lambda_{n}L^{\alpha}_{n}(t-{Y})
 | 
 | (40)


form an orthonormal OP family with respect to measure Пү=(tвҲ’Y)ОұвҖӢeвҲ’(tвҲ’Y)вҖӢрқҹҷ(вҲ’вҲһ,t)рқң”superscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқӣјsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңsubscript1рқ‘Ў\omega=(t-{Y})^{\alpha}e^{-(t-{Y})}\mathbbm{1}_{(-\infty,t)} for a fixed Оұрқӣј\alpha and tilting ПҮ=(tвҲ’Y)ОұвҖӢexpвҒЎ(вҲ’1вҲ’ОІ2вҖӢ(tвҲ’Y))вҖӢрқҹҷ(вҲ’вҲһ,t)рқң’superscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңрқӣј1рқӣҪ2рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ңsubscript1рқ‘Ў\chi=(t-{Y})^{\alpha}\exp\left({-\frac{1-\beta}{2}(t-{Y})}\right)\mathbbm{1}_{(-\infty,t)} for a fixed ОІрқӣҪ\beta.


We use the following result from [24]:


###### 
Theorem 9.


Let pnвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣрқ‘§p_{n}(z) be defined as in (40)40(\ref{eq:lag}). Then


 | 
 | xЛҷnвҖӢ(t)=вҲ’рқҗҖвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+рқҗӣвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)subscriptЛҷрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ў
рқҗҖрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗӣрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\dot{x}_{n}(t)=-\mathbf{A}x(t)+\mathbf{b}u(t)
 | 


where


 | 
 | рқҗҖвҖӢ[n,k]={1+ОІ2В ifВ k=n1В ifВ k<n0В otherwise,рқҗҖрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcases
1рқӣҪ2
- В ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ1
- В ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ0В otherwise\mathbf{A}[n,k]=\begin{cases}\frac{1+\beta}{2}\ \ &\text{ if }\ \ k=n\\
1&\text{ if }\ \ k<n\\
0\ \ &\text{ otherwise}\\
\end{cases},
 | 


 | 
 | рқҗӣвҖӢ[n]=О»nвҖӢ(n+Оұn),рқҗӣdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқңҶрқ‘ӣbinomial
рқ‘ӣрқӣјрқ‘ӣ\mathbf{b}[n]=\lambda_{n}\binom{n+\alpha}{n},
 | 


We now show that рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} as defined in Theorem 9 is 111-quasiseperable.


###### 
Lemma D.11.


Let рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} be defined as in Theorem 9. Then рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} is 111-quasiseperable.


###### Proof.


From Theorem 9, we know that


 | 
 | рқҗҖвҖӢ[n,k]={1+ОІ2В ifВ k=n1В ifВ k<n0В otherwise,рқҗҖрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcases
1рқӣҪ2
- В ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ1
- В ifВ рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ0В otherwise\mathbf{A}[n,k]=\begin{cases}\frac{1+\beta}{2}\ \ &\text{ if }\ \ k=n\\
1&\text{ if }\ \ k<n\\
0\ \ &\text{ otherwise}\\
\end{cases},
 | 


 | 
 | рқҗӣвҖӢ[n]=О»nвҖӢ(n+Оұn).рқҗӣdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқңҶрқ‘ӣbinomial
рқ‘ӣрқӣјрқ‘ӣ\mathbf{b}[n]=\lambda_{n}\binom{n+\alpha}{n}.
 | 


Below the diagonal, all entries рқҗҖвҖӢ[n,k]=1рқҗҖрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ1\mathbf{A}[n,k]=1. Then any submatrix below the diagonal has rank 1. Similarly, above the diagonal, all entries рқҗҖвҖӢ[n,k]=0рқҗҖрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ0\mathbf{A}[n,k]=0. Then any submatrix above the diagonal also has rank 1. Then by Definition 4, the claim follows.


вҲҺ


## 
Appendix E LSSL Algorithms


- 
вҖў 


SectionВ E.1 proves TheoremВ 2, providing an algorithm to compute the Krylov function efficiently for LSSLs.


- 
вҖў 


SectionВ E.2 shows a further simplification of CorollaryВ 4.1, presenting an even simpler class of structured matrices that we use in our implementation of LSSL.


- 
вҖў 


SectionВ E.3 provides technical details of the implementation of LSSL, in particular for computing the MVM black box (multiplication by AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A}) and for computing gradients during backpropagation.


### 
E.1 Proof of TheoremВ 2


This section addresses the computational aspects of the LSSL.
In particular, we prove TheoremВ 2 for the computational speed of computing the Krylov function (7) for quasiseparable matrices AрқҗҙA,
by providing a concrete algorithm in SectionВ E.1.1.


We restate the Krylov function (7) here for convenience. Recall that LрқҗҝL is the length of the input sequence and Nрқ‘ҒN is the order of the LSSL internate state, e.g. AвҲҲв„қNГ—Nрқҗҙsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒA\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}.


 | 
 | рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(A,B,C)=(CвҖӢAiвҖӢB)iвҲҲ[L]вҲҲв„қL=(CвҖӢB,CвҖӢAвҖӢB,вҖҰ,CвҖӢALвҲ’1вҖӢB)subscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝрқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶subscriptрқҗ¶superscriptрқҗҙрқ‘–рқҗөрқ‘–delimited-[]рқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝрқҗ¶рқҗөрқҗ¶рқҗҙрқҗөвҖҰрқҗ¶superscriptрқҗҙрқҗҝ1рқҗө\displaystyle\mathcal{K}_{L}(A,B,C)=\left(CA^{i}B\right)_{i\in[L]}\in\mathbb{R}^{L}=(CB,CAB,\dots,CA^{L-1}B)
 | 


###### 
Remark E.1.


We call (7) the Krylov function following the notation of [17], since it can be written рқ’ҰвҖӢ(A,B)TвҖӢCрқ’Ұsuperscriptрқҗҙрқҗөрқ‘Үрқҗ¶\mathcal{K}(A,B)^{T}C where рқ’ҰвҖӢ(A,B)рқ’Ұрқҗҙрқҗө\mathcal{K}(A,B) is the Krylov matrix defined in (8).
Alternative naming suggestions are welcome.


#### 
E.1.1 The Algorithm


We follow the similar problem of [17, Lemma 6.6] but track the dependence on LрқҗҝL and the log factors more precisely, and optimize it in the case of stronger structure than quasiseparability, which holds in our setting (particularly TheoremВ 11).


The first step is to observe that the Krylov function рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(A,B,C)subscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝрқҗҙрқҗөрқҗ¶\mathcal{K}_{L}(A,B,C) is actually the coefficient vector of CвҖӢ(IвҲ’AвҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢB(modxL)annotatedрқҗ¶superscriptрқҗјрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ1рқҗөpmodsuperscriptрқ‘ҘрқҗҝC(I-Ax)^{-1}B\pmod{x^{L}} as a polynomial in xрқ‘Ҙx.
(Note that AвҖӢxрқҗҙрқ‘ҘAx means simply multiplying every entry in AрқҗҙA by a scalar variable xрқ‘Ҙx.)
This follows from expanding the power series (IвҲ’AвҖӢx)вҲ’1=I+AвҖӢx+A2вҖӢx2+вҖҰsuperscriptрқҗјрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ1
рқҗјрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқҗҙ2superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2вҖҰ(I-Ax)^{-1}=I+Ax+A^{2}x^{2}+\dots.
Thus we first compute CвҖӢ(IвҲ’AвҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢBрқҗ¶superscriptрқҗјрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ1рқҗөC(I-Ax)^{-1}B, which is a rational function of degree at most Nрқ‘ҒN in the numerator and denominator (which can be seen by the standard adjoint formula for the matrix inverse).


The second step is simply inverting the denominator of this rational function (modxL)pmodsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝ\pmod{x^{L}} and multiplying by the numerator, both of which are operations that need LвҖӢlogвҒЎ(L)рқҗҝрқҗҝL\log(L) time by standard results for polynomial arithmetic [51].


For the remainder of this section, we focus on computing the first part.
We make two notational changes:
First, we transpose рқҗӮрқҗӮ\mathbf{C} to make it have the same shape as рқҗҒрқҗҒ\mathbf{B}.
We consider the more general setting where рқҗҒрқҗҒ\mathbf{B} and рқҗӮрқҗӮ\mathbf{C} have multiple columns; this can be viewed as handling a вҖңbatchвҖқ problem with several queries for рқҗҒ,рқҗӮ
- рқҗҒрқҗӮ\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C} at the same time.


###### 
Lemma E.2.


Let рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} be a qрқ‘һq-quasiseparable matrix.
Then


 | 
 | рқҗӮTвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖвҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒwhereВ вҖӢрқҗҖвҲҲв„қNГ—NрқҗҒ,рқҗӮвҲҲв„қNГ—kformulae-sequence
- superscriptрқҗӮрқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲрқҗҖрқ‘Ҙ1рқҗҒwhereВ рқҗҖsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ
- рқҗҒрқҗӮsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\mathbf{C}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}x)^{-1}\mathbf{B}\qquad\text{where }\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times N}\qquad\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times k}
 | 


is a kГ—kрқ‘ҳрқ‘ҳk\times k matrix of rational functions of degree at most Nрқ‘ҒN,
which can be computed in OвҖӢ(q3вҖӢlog4вҒЎN)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘һ3superscript4рқ‘ҒO(q^{3}\log^{4}N) operations.


The main idea is that quasiseparable matrices are recursively вҖңself-similarвҖқ, in that the principal submatrices are also quasiseparable, which leads to a divide-and-conquer algorithm.
In particular, divide рқҗҖ=[рқҗҖ00рқҗҖ01рқҗҖ10рқҗҖ11]рқҗҖmatrixsubscriptрқҗҖ00subscriptрқҗҖ01subscriptрқҗҖ10subscriptрқҗҖ11\mathbf{A}=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{A}_{00}&\mathbf{A}_{01}\\
\mathbf{A}_{10}&\mathbf{A}_{11}\end{bmatrix} into quadrants.
Then by DefinitionВ 4, рқҗҖ00,рқҗҖ11
- subscriptрқҗҖ00subscriptрқҗҖ11\mathbf{A}_{00},\mathbf{A}_{11} are both qрқ‘һq-quasiseparable and рқҗҖ01,рқҗҖ10
- subscriptрқҗҖ01subscriptрқҗҖ10\mathbf{A}_{01},\mathbf{A}_{10} are rank qрқ‘һq.
Therefore the strategy is to view рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖвҖӢxрқҗҲрқҗҖрқ‘Ҙ\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}x as a low-rank perturbation of smaller quasiseparable matrices and reduce the problem to a simpler one.


###### 
Proposition 10 (Binomial Inverse Theorem or Woodbury matrix identityВ [60, 23]).


Over a commutative ring в„ӣв„ӣ\mathcal{R}, let рқҗҖвҲҲв„ӣNГ—NрқҗҖsuperscriptв„ӣрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\mathbf{A}\in\mathcal{R}^{N\times N} and рқҗ”,рқҗ•вҲҲв„ӣNГ—p
- рқҗ”рқҗ•superscriptв„ӣрқ‘Ғрқ‘қ\mathbf{U},\mathbf{V}\in\mathcal{R}^{N\times p}. Suppose рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} and рқҗҖ+рқҗ”рқҗ•T
рқҗҖsuperscriptрқҗ”рқҗ•рқ‘Ү\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^{T} are invertible. Then рқҗҲp+рқҗ•TвҖӢрқҗҖвҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ”вҲҲв„ӣpГ—p
subscriptрқҗҲрқ‘қsuperscriptрқҗ•рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҖ1рқҗ”superscriptв„ӣрқ‘қрқ‘қ\mathbf{I}_{p}+\mathbf{V}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{U}\in\mathcal{R}^{p\times p} is invertible and


 | 
 | (рқҗҖ+рқҗ”рқҗ•T)вҲ’1=рқҗҖвҲ’1вҲ’рқҗҖвҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ”вҖӢ(рқҗҲp+рқҗ•TвҖӢрқҗҖвҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ”)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ•TвҖӢрқҗҖвҲ’1superscript
рқҗҖsuperscriptрқҗ”рқҗ•рқ‘Ү1superscriptрқҗҖ1superscriptрқҗҖ1рқҗ”superscript
subscriptрқҗҲрқ‘қsuperscriptрқҗ•рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҖ1рқҗ”1superscriptрқҗ•рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҖ1(\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^{T})^{-1}=\mathbf{A}^{-1}-\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{I}_{p}+\mathbf{V}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{U})^{-1}\mathbf{V}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{-1}
 | 


For our purposes, в„ӣв„ӣ\mathcal{R} will be the ring of rational functions over в„қв„қ\mathbb{R}.


###### Proof of LemmaВ E.2.


Since рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} is qрқ‘һq-quasiseparable, we can write рқҗҖ10=рқҗ”LвҖӢрқҗ•LTsubscriptрқҗҖ10subscriptрқҗ”рқҗҝsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ•рқҗҝрқ‘Ү\mathbf{A}_{10}=\mathbf{U}_{L}\mathbf{V}_{L}^{T} and рқҗҖ01=рқҗ”UвҖӢрқҗ•UTsubscriptрқҗҖ01subscriptрқҗ”рқ‘Ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ•рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ү\mathbf{A}_{01}=\mathbf{U}_{U}\mathbf{V}_{U}^{T} where рқҗ”вӢ…,рқҗ•вӢ…вҲҲрқ”ҪNГ—q
- subscriptрқҗ”вӢ…subscriptрқҗ•вӢ…superscriptрқ”Ҫрқ‘Ғрқ‘һ\mathbf{U}_{\cdot},\mathbf{V}_{\cdot}\in\mathbb{F}^{N\times q}.
Notice that we can write рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖвҖӢxрқҗҲрқҗҖрқ‘Ҙ\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}x as


 | 
 | рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖвҖӢx=[рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢxрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx]+[рқҹҺрқҗ”Uрқҗ”LрқҹҺ]вҖӢ[рқҗ•LрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗ•U]TвҖӢx.рқҗҲрқҗҖрқ‘Ҙ
matrixрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ00рқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙmatrix0subscriptрқҗ”рқ‘Ҳsubscriptрқҗ”рқҗҝ0superscriptmatrixsubscriptрқҗ•рқҗҝ00subscriptрқҗ•рқ‘Ҳрқ‘Үрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}x=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x\end{bmatrix}+\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{U}_{U}\\
\mathbf{U}_{L}&\mathbf{0}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{V}_{L}&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{V}_{U}\end{bmatrix}^{T}x.
 | 


Suppose we know the expansions of each of


 | 
 | рқҗҢ1вҲҲв„ӣkГ—k=рқҗӮTвҖӢ[рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢxрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx]вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒsubscriptрқҗҢ1superscriptв„ӣрқ‘ҳрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқҗӮрқ‘ҮsuperscriptmatrixрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ00рқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1рқҗҒ\displaystyle\mathbf{M}_{1}\in\mathcal{R}^{k\times k}=\mathbf{C}^{T}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x\end{bmatrix}^{-1}\mathbf{B}
 | 
 | (41)


 | 
 | рқҗҢ2вҲҲв„ӣkГ—2вҖӢq=рқҗӮTвҖӢ[рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢxрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx]вҲ’1вҖӢ[рқҹҺрқҗ”Uрқҗ”LрқҹҺ]subscriptрқҗҢ2superscriptв„ӣрқ‘ҳ2рқ‘һsuperscriptрқҗӮрқ‘ҮsuperscriptmatrixрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ00рқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1matrix0subscriptрқҗ”рқ‘Ҳsubscriptрқҗ”рқҗҝ0\displaystyle\mathbf{M}_{2}\in\mathcal{R}^{k\times 2q}=\mathbf{C}^{T}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x\end{bmatrix}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{U}_{U}\\
\mathbf{U}_{L}&\mathbf{0}\end{bmatrix}
 | 
 | (42)


 | 
 | рқҗҢ3вҲҲв„ӣ2вҖӢqГ—2вҖӢq=[рқҗ•LрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗ•U]TвҖӢ[рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢxрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx]вҲ’1вҖӢ[рқҹҺрқҗ”Uрқҗ”LрқҹҺ]subscriptрқҗҢ3superscriptв„ӣ2рқ‘һ2рқ‘һsuperscriptmatrixsubscriptрқҗ•рқҗҝ00subscriptрқҗ•рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ҮsuperscriptmatrixрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ00рқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1matrix0subscriptрқҗ”рқ‘Ҳsubscriptрқҗ”рқҗҝ0\displaystyle\mathbf{M}_{3}\in\mathcal{R}^{2q\times 2q}=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{V}_{L}&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{V}_{U}\end{bmatrix}^{T}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x\end{bmatrix}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{U}_{U}\\
\mathbf{U}_{L}&\mathbf{0}\end{bmatrix}
 | 
 | (43)


 | 
 | рқҗҢ4вҲҲв„ӣ2вҖӢqГ—k=[рқҗ•LрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗ•U]TвҖӢ[рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢxрқҹҺрқҹҺрқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx]вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ.subscriptрқҗҢ4superscriptв„ӣ2рқ‘һрқ‘ҳsuperscriptmatrixsubscriptрқҗ•рқҗҝ00subscriptрқҗ•рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ҮsuperscriptmatrixрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ00рқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1рқҗҒ\displaystyle\mathbf{M}_{4}\in\mathcal{R}^{2q\times k}=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{V}_{L}&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{V}_{U}\end{bmatrix}^{T}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x&\mathbf{0}\\
\mathbf{0}&\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x\end{bmatrix}^{-1}\mathbf{B}.
 | 
 | (44)


By PropositionВ 10, the desired answer is


 | 
 | рқҗӮTвҖӢ(XвҲ’рқҗҖ)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ=рқҗҢ1вҲ’рқҗҢ2вҖӢ(рқҗҲ2вҖӢq+рқҗҢ3)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҢ4.superscriptрқҗӮрқ‘Үsuperscriptрқ‘ӢрқҗҖ1рқҗҒsubscriptрқҗҢ1subscriptрқҗҢ2superscript
subscriptрқҗҲ2рқ‘һsubscriptрқҗҢ31subscriptрқҗҢ4\mathbf{C}^{T}(X-\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}=\mathbf{M}_{1}-\mathbf{M}_{2}(\mathbf{I}_{2q}+\mathbf{M}_{3})^{-1}\mathbf{M}_{4}.
 | 


Then the final result can be computed by inverting рқҗҲ2вҖӢt+рқҗҢ3
subscriptрқҗҲ2рқ‘ЎsubscriptрқҗҢ3\mathbf{I}_{2t}+\mathbf{M}_{3} (OвҖӢ(q3вҖӢNвҖӢlogвҒЎ(N))рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘һ3рқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒO(q^{3}N\log(N)) operations),
multiplying by рқҗҢ2,рқҗҢ4
- subscriptрқҗҢ2subscriptрқҗҢ4\mathbf{M}_{2},\mathbf{M}_{4} (OвҖӢ((kвҖӢq2+k2вҖӢq)вҖӢNвҖӢlogвҒЎ(N))рқ‘Ӯ
рқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқ‘һ2superscriptрқ‘ҳ2рқ‘һрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒO((kq^{2}+k^{2}q)N\log(N)) operations),
and subtracting from рқҗҢ1subscriptрқҗҢ1\mathbf{M}_{1} (OвҖӢ(k2вҖӢNвҖӢlogвҒЎ(N))рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘ҳ2рқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒO(k^{2}N\log(N)) operations).
This is a total of OвҖӢ((q3+kвҖӢq2+k2вҖӢq)вҖӢNвҖӢlogвҒЎ(N))рқ‘Ӯ
superscriptрқ‘һ3рқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқ‘һ2superscriptрқ‘ҳ2рқ‘һрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒO((q^{3}+kq^{2}+k^{2}q)N\log(N)) operations.
Note that when k=OвҖӢ(qвҖӢlogвҒЎN)рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ӯрқ‘һрқ‘Ғk=O(q\log N), this becomes OвҖӢ(q3вҖӢNвҖӢlog3вҒЎN)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘һ3рқ‘Ғsuperscript3рқ‘ҒO(q^{3}N\log^{3}{N}); we will use this in the analysis shortly.


To compute рқҗҢ1,рқҗҢ2,рқҗҢ3,рқҗҢ4
- subscriptрқҗҢ1subscriptрқҗҢ2subscriptрқҗҢ3subscriptрқҗҢ4\mathbf{M}_{1},\mathbf{M}_{2},\mathbf{M}_{3},\mathbf{M}_{4}, it suffices to compute the following:


 | 
 | рқҗӮ1TвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ0superscriptsubscriptрқҗӮ1рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗҒ0\displaystyle\mathbf{C}_{1}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x)^{-1}\mathbf{B}_{0}
 | рқҗӮ1TвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ1superscriptsubscriptрқҗӮ1рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗҒ1\displaystyle\mathbf{C}_{1}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x)^{-1}\mathbf{B}_{1}
 | 
 | (45)


 | 
 | рқҗӮ0TвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ”UsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗӮ0рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗ”рқ‘Ҳ\displaystyle\mathbf{C}_{0}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x)^{-1}\mathbf{U}_{U}
 | рқҗӮ1TвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ”LsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗӮ1рқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗ”рқҗҝ\displaystyle\mathbf{C}_{1}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x)^{-1}\mathbf{U}_{L}
 | 


 | 
 | рқҗ•LTвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ”Usuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ•рқҗҝрқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗ”рқ‘Ҳ\displaystyle\mathbf{V}_{L}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x)^{-1}\mathbf{U}_{U}
 | рқҗ•UTвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗ”Lsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ•рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗ”рқҗҝ\displaystyle\mathbf{V}_{U}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x)^{-1}\mathbf{U}_{L}
 | 


 | 
 | рқҗ•LTвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ0superscriptsubscriptрқҗ•рқҗҝрқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗҒ0\displaystyle\mathbf{V}_{L}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x)^{-1}\mathbf{B}_{0}
 | рқҗ•UTвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ1.superscriptsubscriptрқҗ•рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқҗҒ1\displaystyle\mathbf{V}_{U}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x)^{-1}\mathbf{B}_{1}.
 | 


But to compute those, it suffices to compute the following (k+t)Г—(k+t)
рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ў
рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ў(k+t)\times(k+t) matrices:


 | 
 | 
 | [рқҗӮ0рқҗ•L]TвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ00вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢ[рқҗҒ0рқҗ”U]superscriptmatrixsubscriptрқҗӮ0subscriptрқҗ•рқҗҝрқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ00рқ‘Ҙ1matrixsubscriptрқҗҒ0subscriptрқҗ”рқ‘Ҳ\displaystyle\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{C}_{0}&\mathbf{V}_{L}\end{bmatrix}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{00}x)^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{B}_{0}&\mathbf{U}_{U}\end{bmatrix}
 | 
 | (46)


 | 
 | 
 | [рқҗӮ1рқҗ•U]TвҖӢ(рқҗҲвҲ’рқҗҖ11вҖӢx)вҲ’1вҖӢ[рқҗҒ1рқҗ”L]superscriptmatrixsubscriptрқҗӮ1subscriptрқҗ•рқ‘Ҳрқ‘ҮsuperscriptрқҗҲsubscriptрқҗҖ11рқ‘Ҙ1matrixsubscriptрқҗҒ1subscriptрқҗ”рқҗҝ\displaystyle\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{C}_{1}&\mathbf{V}_{U}\end{bmatrix}^{T}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}_{11}x)^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{B}_{1}&\mathbf{U}_{L}\end{bmatrix}
 | 


Since рқҗҖ00subscriptрқҗҖ00\mathbf{A}_{00} and рқҗҖ11subscriptрқҗҖ11\mathbf{A}_{11} have the same form as рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A}, this is two recursive calls of half the size.
Notice that the size of the other input (dimensions of рқҗҒ,рқҗӮ
- рқҗҒрқҗӮ\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C}) is growing, but when the initial input is k=1рқ‘ҳ1k=1, it never exceeds 1+qвҖӢlogвҒЎN
1рқ‘һрқ‘Ғ1+q\log{N} (since they increase by qрқ‘һq every time we go down a level).
Earlier, we noticed that when k=OвҖӢ(qвҖӢlogвҒЎN)рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ӯрқ‘һрқ‘Ғk=O(q\log N), the reduction step has complexity OвҖӢ(q3вҖӢNвҖӢlog3вҒЎ(N))рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘һ3рқ‘Ғsuperscript3рқ‘ҒO(q^{3}N\log^{3}(N)) for any recursive call.
The recursion adds an additional logвҒЎNрқ‘Ғ\log{N} multiplicative factor on top of this.
вҲҺ


###### 
Corollary E.3.


Suppose that рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} is semiseparable instead of quasiseparable,
and suppose qрқ‘һq is a small constant.
Then the cost of LemmaВ E.2 is OвҖӢ(NвҖӢlog2вҒЎ(N))рқ‘Ӯрқ‘Ғsuperscript2рқ‘ҒO(N\log^{2}(N)) operations.


This follows from the fact that in the recursion (45) and (46), the рқҗ”,рқҗ•
- рқҗ”рқҗ•\mathbf{U},\mathbf{V} matrices do not have to be appended if they already exist in рқҗҒ,рқҗӮ
- рқҗҒрқҗӮ\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C}.
For intuition, this happens in the case when рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} is tridiagonal, so that U,V
- рқ‘Ҳрқ‘үU,V have the structure (1,0,вҖҰ,0)10вҖҰ0(1,0,\dots,0), or the case when the off-diagonal part of рқҗҖрқҗҖ\mathbf{A} is all 111 (such as the HiPPO-LegT matrix).
The matrices in SectionВ D.3 and SectionВ E.2 (TheoremsВ 8, 9 andВ 11) actually satisfy this stronger structure, so CorollaryВ E.3 applies.


Combining everything, this proves TheoremВ 2 with the exact bound NвҖӢlog2вҒЎ(N)+LвҖӢlogвҒЎ(L)
рқ‘Ғsuperscript2рқ‘ҒрқҗҝрқҗҝN\log^{2}(N)+L\log(L) operations.
The memory claim follows similarly, and the depth of the algorithm is log2вҒЎ(N)+logвҒЎ(L)
superscript2рқ‘Ғрқҗҝ\log^{2}(N)+\log(L) from the divide-and-conquer recursions.


#### 
E.1.2 Summary of Computation Speed for LSSLs and other Mechanisms


We provide a summary of complexity requirements for various sequence model mechanisms, including several versions of the LSSL.
Note that these are over exact arithmetic as in TheoremВ 2.


First, the self-attention mechanism is another common sequence model that has an L2superscriptрқҗҝ2L^{2} dependence on the length of the sequence, so it is not suitable for the very long sequences we consider here.
(We do note that there is an active line of work on reducing this complexity.)


Second, we include additional variants of the LSSL.
In TableВ 5, LSSL-naive denotes learning AрқҗҙA and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t for unstructured AрқҗҙA;
LSSL-fixed denotes not learning A,О”вҖӢt
- рқҗҙО”рқ‘ЎA,\Delta t (see AppendixВ B for details);
LSSL denotes the learning AрқҗҙA and О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t for the structured class AрқҗҙA.

### 
E.2 Further Simplification with Tridiagonal Matrices


The algorithm for TheoremВ 2 for general quasiseparable matrices is still difficult to implement in practice, and we make a further simplification using a particular subclass of quasiseparable matrices.


###### 
Theorem 11.


The class of NГ—Nрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҒN\times N matrices рқ’®N={PвҖӢ(D+TвҲ’1)вҖӢQ}subscriptрқ’®рқ‘Ғрқ‘ғ
рқҗ·superscriptрқ‘Ү1рқ‘„\mathcal{S}_{N}=\{P(D+T^{-1})Q\} with diagonal D,P,Q
- рқҗ·рқ‘ғрқ‘„D,P,Q and tridiagonal Tрқ‘ҮT
includes the original HiPPO-LegS, HiPPO-LegT, and HiPPO-LagT matricesВ [24].


TheoremВ 11 shows that a simple representation involving tridiagonal and diagonal matrices captures all of the original HiPPO matrices.
In particular, our LSSL implementation initializes AрқҗҙA to be the HiPPO-LegS matrix (AppendixВ B) and learns within the class defined by TheoremВ 11.


We note that the matrices in TheoremВ 11 are all 1-quasiseparable and in particular also contain the HiPPO matrices for Gegenbauer and generalized Laguerre orthogonal polynomials derived in TheoremВ 9.
In fact, the notion of semiseparability, which is closely related to (and actually is the predecessor of) quasiseparability,
was originally motivated precisely to capture inverses of tridiagonal matrices.
Thus the structured class in TheoremВ 11 can be viewed as an approximation of 333-quasiseparable matrices (CorollaryВ 4.1) to 111-quasiseparable,
which still contains many of the HiPPO families of interest.


###### Proof.


We simply show that each of these specific matrices can be represented in the proposed form.


HiPPO-LegT.


Let AрқҗҙA denote the HiPPO-LegT transition matrix.
Up to row/column scaling (i.e.В left- and right- multiplication by diagonal Pрқ‘ғP and Qрқ‘„Q),
we can write


 | 
 | AnвҖӢk={(вҲ’1)nвҲ’kifВ вҖӢnвүӨk1ifВ вҖӢnвүҘk.subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcasessuperscript1рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ1ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle A_{nk}=\begin{cases}(-1)^{n-k}&\mbox{if }n\leq k\\
1&\mbox{if }n\geq k\end{cases}.
 | 


The main observation is that


 | 
 | AвҲ’1=12вҖӢ[110вҖҰ000вҲ’101вҖҰ0000вҲ’10вҖҰ000вӢ®вӢ®вӢ®вӢұвӢ®вӢ®вӢ®000вҖҰ010000вҖҰвҲ’101000вҖҰ0вҲ’11]superscriptрқҗҙ112matrix110вҖҰ000101вҖҰ000010вҖҰ000вӢ®вӢ®вӢ®вӢұвӢ®вӢ®вӢ®000вҖҰ010000вҖҰ101000вҖҰ011\displaystyle A^{-1}=\frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}1&1&0&\dots&0&0&0\\
-1&0&1&\dots&0&0&0\\
0&-1&0&\dots&0&0&0\\
\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots&\vdots&\vdots\\
0&0&0&\dots&0&1&0\\
0&0&0&\dots&-1&0&1\\
0&0&0&\dots&0&-1&1\\
\end{bmatrix}
 | 


##### HiPPO-LegS.


The HiPPO-LegS matrix is


 | 
 | AnвҖӢksubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle A_{nk}
 | =вҲ’{(2вҖӢn+1)1/2вҖӢ(2вҖӢk+1)1/2ifВ вҖӢn>kn+1ifВ вҖӢn=k0ifВ вҖӢn<k.absentcasessuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ112superscript
2рқ‘ҳ112ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ӣ1ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ0ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=-\begin{cases}(2n+1)^{1/2}(2k+1)^{1/2}&\mbox{if }n>k\\
n+1&\mbox{if }n=k\\
0&\mbox{if }n<k\end{cases}.
 | 


This can be written as вҲ’PвҖӢAвҖІвҖӢQрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқҗҙвҖІрқ‘„-PA^{\prime}Q where P=Q=diag((2вҖӢn+1)12)рқ‘ғрқ‘„diagsuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ112P=Q=\operatorname*{diag}((2n+1)^{\frac{1}{2}}) and


 | 
 | AnвҖӢkвҖІ={1ifВ вҖӢn>k0ifВ вҖӢn<k1вҲ’n2вҖӢn+1ifВ вҖӢn=k.subscriptsuperscriptрқҗҙвҖІрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcases1ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ0ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ1рқ‘ӣ
2рқ‘ӣ1ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle A^{\prime}_{nk}=\begin{cases}1&\mbox{if }n>k\\
0&\mbox{if }n<k\\
1-\frac{n}{2n+1}&\mbox{if }n=k\end{cases}.
 | 


Finally, AвҖІ=D+TвҲ’1superscriptрқҗҙвҖІ
рқҗ·superscriptрқ‘Ү1A^{\prime}=D+T^{-1} where D=вҲ’diag(n2вҖӢn+1)рқҗ·diagрқ‘ӣ
2рқ‘ӣ1D=-\operatorname*{diag}(\frac{n}{2n+1}) and Tрқ‘ҮT is the matrix with 111 on the main diagonal and вҲ’11-1 on the subdiagonal.


##### HiPPO-LagT.


The HiPPO-LagT matrix is


 | 
 | AnвҖӢksubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle A_{nk}
 | =вҲ’{1ifВ вҖӢn>k0ifВ вҖӢn<k12ifВ вҖӢn=k.absentcases1ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ0ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ12ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle=-\begin{cases}1&\mbox{if }n>k\\
0&\mbox{if }n<k\\
\frac{1}{2}&\mbox{if }n=k\\
\end{cases}.
 | 


This can be written as вҲ’PвҖӢ(D+TвҲ’1)вҖӢQрқ‘ғ
рқҗ·superscriptрқ‘Ү1рқ‘„-P(D+T^{-1})Q where P=Q=Iрқ‘ғрқ‘„рқҗјP=Q=I, D=вҲ’12вҖӢIрқҗ·12рқҗјD=-\frac{1}{2}I, and Tрқ‘ҮT is the same tridiagonal matrix as in the HiPPO-LegS case.
вҲҺ


### 
E.3 Implementation Details


In this section we provide several implementation details that are useful for implementing LSSLs in practice.


Recall that one of the main primitives of LSSLs is the matrix-vector multiplication y=AВҜвҖӢxрқ‘ҰВҜрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙy=\overline{A}x (SectionВ 3.1, AppendixВ B), where AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} is the state matrix AрқҗҙA discretized with step size О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t using the bilinear method (SectionВ C.1.3).
In SectionВ E.3.1, we describe how this MVM can be performed with simpler MVM primitives which we call the вҖңforward differenceвҖқ and вҖңbackward differenceвҖқ.


However, if these MVM primitives are implemented in a specialized way for particular classes of AрқҗҙA matrices (i.e., not using atoms in a standard autograd framework), then we also need to calculate several additional gradients by hand.
SectionВ E.3.2 shows that calculating gradients to A,О”вҖӢt,x
- рқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҘA,\Delta t,x during backpropagation can actually be reduced to those same forward/backward difference primitives.


Finally, in the case when AрқҗҙA is the structured class of matrices in TheoremВ 11, SectionВ E.2 shows how to efficiently calculate those primitives using a black-box tridiagonal solver.
Our code222Available at https://github.com/HazyResearch/state-spaces implements all the algorithms in this section, with bindings to the cuSPARSE library for efficient tridiagonal solving on GPU.


#### 
E.3.1 Matrix-vector Multiplication by the Bilinear Discretization


##### Bilinear Discretization


The discrete state-space system is given by (4) and (5), re-written here for convenience


 | 
 | xtsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle x_{t}
 | =AВҜвҖӢxtвҲ’1+BВҜвҖӢutabsent
ВҜрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў1ВҜрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\overline{A}x_{t-1}+\overline{B}u_{t}
 | 


 | 
 | ytsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў\displaystyle y_{t}
 | =CвҖӢxt+DвҖӢutabsent
рқҗ¶subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗ·subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=Cx_{t}+Du_{t}
 | 


where AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} is a function of A,Оҙt
- рқҗҙsubscriptрқӣҝрқ‘ЎA,\delta_{t} and AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} is a function of A,B,Оҙt
- рқҗҙрқҗөsubscriptрқӣҝрқ‘ЎA,B,\delta_{t}.
In particular, we define AВҜВҜрқҗҙ\overline{A} to be the matrix discretized using the bilinear method (SectionВ C.1.3),
and the system can be written explicitly:


 | 
 | xtsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle x_{t}
 | =(IвҲ’О”вҖӢtвҖӢA2)вҲ’1вҖӢ((I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA2)вҖӢxtвҲ’1+О”вҖӢtвҖӢBвҖӢut)absentsuperscriptрқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ21

рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ2subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў1О”рқ‘Ўрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\left(I-\frac{\Delta tA}{2}\right)^{-1}\left(\left(I+\frac{\Delta tA}{2}\right)x_{t-1}+\Delta tBu_{t}\right)
 | 


 | 
 | ytsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў\displaystyle y_{t}
 | =CвҖӢxt+DвҖӢutabsent
рқҗ¶subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗ·subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle=Cx_{t}+Du_{t}
 | 


Thus it suffices to compute the maps


 | 
 | FвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x):=(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҖӢxassignрқҗ№рқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle F(A,\Delta t,x):=(I+\Delta tA)x
 | 


and


 | 
 | BвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x):=(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҖӢx.assignрқҗөрқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle B(A,\Delta t,x):=(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}x.
 | 


We will call these functions the forward difference and backward difference maps, respectively.
(The Euler and backward Euler discretizations (SectionВ C.1.2) are also known as the вҖңforward differenceвҖқ and вҖңbackward differenceвҖқ methods, which in the case of linear systems reduces down to the maps Fрқҗ№F and BрқҗөB.)


#### 
E.3.2 Gradients through the Forward/Backward Difference Primitives


In this section we will let y=FвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x)рқ‘Ұрқҗ№рқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙy=F(A,\Delta t,x) or y=BвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x)рқ‘ҰрқҗөрқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙy=B(A,\Delta t,x) denote the computation of interest,
LвҖӢ(y)рқҗҝрқ‘ҰL(y) denote a generic loss function,
and dвҖӢx,dвҖӢy,вҖҰ
- рқ‘‘рқ‘Ҙрқ‘‘рқ‘ҰвҖҰdx,dy,\dots denote gradients to x,y,вҖҰ
- рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰвҖҰx,y,\dots (e.g., dвҖӢx=вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮxрқ‘‘рқ‘Ҙ
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ҙdx=\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial x}).


##### Derivatives of backward difference.


First we have the standard вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮx=вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮyвҖӢвҲӮyвҲӮx=вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮyвҖӢ(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ҙ
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ҙ
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ұsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial x}=\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial y}\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}=\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial y}(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}.
This corresponds to matrix-vector multiplication by (I+О”вҖӢA)вҲ’Tsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқҗҙрқ‘Ү(I+\Delta A)^{-T}.
In other words, it can be computed by the primitive BвҖӢ(AT,О”вҖӢt,dвҖӢy)рқҗөsuperscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ҮО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘‘рқ‘ҰB(A^{T},\Delta t,dy).


Similarly, in order to compute вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮО”вҖӢt
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
О”рқ‘Ў\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial\Delta t} we require вҲӮyвҲӮО”вҖӢt
рқ‘Ұ
О”рқ‘Ў\frac{\partial y}{\partial\Delta t}.
We need the result вҲӮYвҲ’1вҲӮx=вҲ’YвҲ’1вҖӢвҲӮYвҲӮxвҖӢYвҲ’1
superscriptрқ‘Ң1
рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ң1
рқ‘Ң
рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ң1\frac{\partial Y^{-1}}{\partial x}=-Y^{-1}\frac{\partial Y}{\partial x}Y^{-1}for an invertible matrix Yрқ‘ҢY [41, equation (59)].
Then


 | 
 | вҲӮyвҲӮО”вҖӢt
рқ‘Ұ
О”рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\frac{\partial y}{\partial\Delta t}
 | =вҲӮ(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҲӮО”вҖӢtвҖӢxabsent
superscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1
О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=\frac{\partial(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}}{\partial\Delta t}x
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ’(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҖӢвҲӮ(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲӮО”вҖӢtвҖӢ(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҖӢxabsentsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1

рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ
О”рқ‘Ўsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=-(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}\frac{\partial(I+\Delta tA)}{\partial\Delta t}(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}x
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ’(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҖӢAвҖӢ(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҖӢxabsentsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1рқҗҙsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=-(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}A(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}x
 | 


and


 | 
 | вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮО”вҖӢt
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
О”рқ‘Ў\displaystyle\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial\Delta t}
 | =вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮyвҖӢвҲӮyвҲӮО”вҖӢtabsent
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ұ
О”рқ‘Ў\displaystyle=\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial y}\frac{\partial y}{\partial\Delta t}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =вҲ’[вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)вҲӮyвҖӢ(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1]вҖӢAвҖӢ[(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҖӢx]absentdelimited-[]
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ұsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1рқҗҙdelimited-[]superscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=-\left[\frac{\partial L(y)}{\partial y}(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}\right]A\left[(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}x\right]
 | 


We can summarize this as follows. Let y=BвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x)=(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҲ’1вҖӢxрқ‘ҰрқҗөрқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ1рқ‘Ҙy=B(A,\Delta t,x)=(I+\Delta tA)^{-1}x and dвҖӢy=вҲӮLвҖӢ(y)/вҲӮyрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ
рқҗҝрқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ұdy=\partial L(y)/\partial y (as a column vector).
Then


 | 
 | yрқ‘Ұ\displaystyle y
 | =BвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x)absentрқҗөрқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=B(A,\Delta t,x)
 | 


 | 
 | dвҖӢxрқ‘‘рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle dx
 | =BвҖӢ(AT,О”вҖӢt,dвҖӢy)absentрқҗөsuperscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ҮО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ\displaystyle=B(A^{T},\Delta t,dy)
 | 


 | 
 | dвҖӢО”вҖӢtрқ‘‘О”рқ‘Ў\displaystyle d\Delta t
 | =вҲ’dвҖӢxTвҖӢAвҖӢy.absentрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Үрқҗҙрқ‘Ұ\displaystyle=-dx^{T}Ay.
 | 


##### Derivatives of forward difference.


The forward case is simpler.
Let y=FвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x)=(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)вҖӢxрқ‘Ұрқҗ№рқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙy=F(A,\Delta t,x)=(I+\Delta tA)x.
Then вҲӮyвҲӮx=I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA
рқ‘Ұ
рқ‘Ҙ
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙ\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}=I+\Delta tA and вҲӮyвҲӮО”вҖӢt=AвҖӢx
рқ‘Ұ
О”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ\frac{\partial y}{\partial\Delta t}=Ax.
Thus


 | 
 | yрқ‘Ұ\displaystyle y
 | =FвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x)absentрқҗ№рқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=F(A,\Delta t,x)
 | 


 | 
 | dвҖӢxрқ‘‘рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle dx
 | =(I+О”вҖӢtвҖӢA)TвҖӢdвҖӢy=FвҖӢ(AT,О”вҖӢt,dвҖӢy)absentsuperscript
рқҗјО”рқ‘Ўрқҗҙрқ‘Үрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұрқҗ№superscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ҮО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ\displaystyle=(I+\Delta tA)^{T}dy=F(A^{T},\Delta t,dy)
 | 


 | 
 | dвҖӢО”вҖӢtрқ‘‘О”рқ‘Ў\displaystyle d\Delta t
 | =dвҖӢyTвҖӢAвҖӢx.absentрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘Үрқҗҙрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=dy^{T}Ax.
 | 


#### 
E.3.3 Computing the Forward/Backward Difference for Tridiagonal Inverse Matrices


TheoremВ 11 uses the classes of matrices A=PвҖӢ(D+TвҲ’1)вҖӢQрқҗҙрқ‘ғ
рқҗ·superscriptрқ‘Ү1рқ‘„A=P(D+T^{-1})Q for diagonal D,P,Q
- рқҗ·рқ‘ғрқ‘„D,P,Q and tridiagonal Tрқ‘ҮT.
We describe how the forward and backward difference MVMs can be performed efficiently for this class of matrices by reducing to a black-box tridiagonal solver.


##### Forward difference.


It is straightforward to compute


 | 
 | FвҖӢ(A,О”вҖӢt,x)=(I+О”вҖӢtвӢ…PвҖӢ(D+TвҲ’1)вҖӢQ)вҖӢx=x+О”вҖӢtвӢ…PвҖӢDвҖӢQвҖӢx+О”вҖӢtвӢ…PвҖӢTвҲ’1вҖӢQвҖӢxрқҗ№рқҗҙО”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ
рқҗјвӢ…О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘ғ
рқҗ·superscriptрқ‘Ү1рқ‘„рқ‘Ҙ
рқ‘ҘвӢ…О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘ғрқҗ·рқ‘„рқ‘ҘвӢ…О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘Ү1рқ‘„рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle F(A,\Delta t,x)=(I+\Delta t\cdot P(D+T^{-1})Q)x=x+\Delta t\cdot PDQx+\Delta t\cdot PT^{-1}Qx
 | 


in terms of multiplication by diagonal matrices xвҶҰDвҖӢxmaps-toрқ‘Ҙрқҗ·рқ‘Ҙx\mapsto Dx and tridiagonal solving xвҶҰTвҲ’1вҖӢxmaps-toрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ү1рқ‘Ҙx\mapsto T^{-1}x.


##### Backward difference.


We will explicitly rewrite the inverse of the matrix G=I+О”вҖӢtвӢ…PвҖӢ(D+TвҲ’1)вҖӢQрқҗә
рқҗјвӢ…О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘ғ
рқҗ·superscriptрқ‘Ү1рқ‘„G=I+\Delta t\cdot P(D+T^{-1})Q.


The core observation is to multiply GрқҗәG by a choice selection of matrices to cancel out the TвҲ’1superscriptрқ‘Ү1T^{-1} term:


 | 
 | TвҖӢPвҲ’1вҖӢGвҖӢQвҲ’1=TвҖӢPвҲ’1вҖӢQвҲ’1+О”вҖӢtвҖӢTвҖӢD+О”вҖӢtвҖӢI.рқ‘Үsuperscriptрқ‘ғ1рқҗәsuperscriptрқ‘„1
рқ‘Үsuperscriptрқ‘ғ1superscriptрқ‘„1О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Үрқҗ·О”рқ‘Ўрқҗј\displaystyle TP^{-1}GQ^{-1}=TP^{-1}Q^{-1}+\Delta tTD+\Delta tI.
 | 


Rearranging yields


 | 
 | GвҲ’1=QвҲ’1вҖӢ(TвҖӢPвҲ’1вҖӢQвҲ’1+О”вҖӢtвҖӢTвҖӢD+О”вҖӢtвҖӢI)вҲ’1вҖӢTвҖӢPвҲ’1.superscriptрқҗә1superscriptрқ‘„1superscript
рқ‘Үsuperscriptрқ‘ғ1superscriptрқ‘„1О”рқ‘Ўрқ‘Үрқҗ·О”рқ‘Ўрқҗј1рқ‘Үsuperscriptрқ‘ғ1\displaystyle G^{-1}=Q^{-1}(TP^{-1}Q^{-1}+\Delta tTD+\Delta tI)^{-1}TP^{-1}.
 | 


Now note that the matrix in the middle is tridiagonal.
Hence we have reduced MVM by GвҲ’1superscriptрқҗә1G^{-1}, i.e. the backward difference problem, to a series of diagonal and tridiagonal MVMs (easy), and a tridiagonal inverse MVM (a.k.a. a tridiagonal solve).

SectionВ F.2 describes our training methodology in more detail for each dataset.
The hyperparameters for all reported results are in TableВ 9.

Finally, Fig.В 4 plots the О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t values at the beginning and end of training on the SpeechCommands-Raw dataset, confirming that training О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t does noticeably change their values to better model the data.
In particular, the О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t values spread over time to cover a larger range of timescales.


Figure 4: 
We visualize the 32 largest and smallest О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t values at the start and end of training for the first layer of our state-of-the-art LSSL model on the Speech Commands Raw dataset.
The plots visualize 1О”вҖӢt1О”рқ‘Ў\frac{1}{\Delta t}, which can be interpreted as the timescale at which they operate (SectionВ 2).
The plots confirm that LSSL does modify the dt values in order to more appropriately model the speech data.


### 
F.2 Methodology


We describe our training procedure on each dataset for our model and any relevant baselines.


##### General


All models and datasets used the Adam optimizer with a LR decay scheduler that reduced LR by 5x upon validation plateau for 10 or 20 epochs.
We fixed the batch size to 505050 for the MNIST/CIFAR datasets and 323232 for other datasets, reducing if necessary to fit in memory.


For all models, we chose the hyperparameters that achieved the highest validation accuracy/RMSE (values in Table 9).


##### Error Bars


We note that the results in SectionВ 5 do not include standard deviations for formatting reasons, since most of the baselines were best results reported in previous papers without error bars.
As SectionВ 6 noted, the LSSL was actually quite stable in performance and not particularly sensitive to hyperparameters.
We note that for every result in SectionВ 5, the LSSL with error bars was at least one standard deviation above the baseline results.


#### 
F.2.1 Sequential and Permuted MNIST


The model architecture of LSSL(-f) was fixed to the small architecture with 200K parameters (AppendixВ B).
Following [44], we fixed the learning rate scheduler to decay on plateau by with a factor of 0.20.20.2, and the number of epochs to 200.
We searched hyperparameters over the product of the following learning rate values: {0.001,0.002,0.004,0.01}0.0010.0020.0040.01\{0.001,0.002,0.004,0.01\}, and dropout values: {0.1,0.2}0.10.2\{0.1,0.2\}.


#### 
F.2.2 Sequential CIFAR


The model architecture of LSSL(-f) was fixed to the large architecture with 2M parameters (AppendixВ B).
We searched over the product of the following learning rate values: {0.001,0.002,0.004,0.01,0.02}0.0010.0020.0040.010.02\{0.001,0.002,0.004,0.01,0.02\}, and dropout values: {0.2,0.3,0.4}0.20.30.4\{0.2,0.3,0.4\}.


#### 
F.2.3 BIDMC Healthcare


The BIDMC tasks aim at predicting three vital signs of a patient, respiratory rate (RR), heart rate (HR), and oxygen saturation (SpO2), based on PPG and ECG signals.
The clinical data is provided by the Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center.
The PPG and ECG signals were sampled at 125125125Hz and have a sequence length of 400040004000.


For this dataset, we fixed the small LSSL(-f) model (AppendixВ B).
Following [47], we changed the scheduler to a multistep scheduler that decays on fixed epochs, and trained for 500 epochs.


For our methods, we searched over the product of the following learning rate values: {0.004,0.01,0.02}0.0040.010.02\{0.004,0.01,0.02\}, and dropout values: {0.1,0.2}0.10.2\{0.1,0.2\}.


##### Baseline parameters.


For CKConv, we searched over Пү0вҲҲ[10,50]subscriptрқң”01050\omega_{0}\in[10,50] following the guidelines of Romero etВ al. [44] (best value Пү0=20subscriptрқң”020\omega_{0}=20).
Since we tuned the sensitive Пү0subscriptрқң”0\omega_{0}, we fixed the learning rate to 0.0010.0010.001 and dropout to 0.10.10.1 which was the default used in [44].


The transformer model we used was a vanilla transformer with a hidden dimension of 256256256, 888 attention heads, 444 layers, and a feedforward dimension of 102410241024.
We used a learning rate of 0.0010.0010.001 and a dropout of 00.
We tried a few variants, but no transformer model was effective at all.


#### 
F.2.4 CelebA


For these larger datasets, we reduced the size of the order Nрқ‘ҒN and did not tie it to Hрқҗ»H.
These experiments were computationally heavy and we did not do any tuning (i.e., TableВ 9 are the only runs).
The model size was picked to train in a reasonable amount of time,
and the learning rate for the first attribute was picked based on general best hyperparameters for other datasets,
and then reduced for subsequent experiments on the other attributes.


Baseline parameters.
For ResNet-18, we used the standard implementation with a learning rate of 0.0010.0010.001.


#### 
F.2.5 Speech Commands


For Speech Commands, we use the same dataset and preprocessing code from Kidger etВ al. [31], Romero etВ al. [44].
We consider the two settings from Kidger etВ al. [31]:
SC-Raw uses very long time-series raw speech signals of 16000 timesteps each,
while SC-MFCC uses standard MFCC features of 161 timesteps.


For our models trained over the raw data, we searched over the product of the following learning rate values: {0.002,0.004,0.01}0.0020.0040.01\{0.002,0.004,0.01\}, and dropout values: {0.1,0.2}0.10.2\{0.1,0.2\}.
For our models trained over the MFCC features, we searched over the product of the following learning rate values: {0.0001,0.001,0.002,0.004,0.01}0.00010.0010.0020.0040.01\{0.0001,0.001,0.002,0.004,0.01\}, and dropout values: {0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4}0.10.20.30.4\{0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4\}.


Baseline parameters.
To get more results for the strongest baselines on very long sequences in the literature,
we ran the UniCORNNВ [47] baseline on both Raw and MFCC variants,
and the Neural Rough Differential EquationsВ [37] baseline on the Raw variant.


For UniCORNN trained over the raw data, we searched over multiple hyperparameters.
Specifically, we searched over alpha: {0,10,20,30,40}010203040\{0,10,20,30,40\}, О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t values: {0.00001,0.0001,0.001,0.01}0.000010.00010.0010.01\{0.00001,0.0001,0.001,0.01\}, and learning rate values: {0.0001,0.0004,0.001,0.004}0.00010.00040.0010.004\{0.0001,0.0004,0.001,0.004\}.
However, since the method was not able to generalize to the validation set for any hyperparameter combination, we used the authorsвҖҷ reported hyperparameters for the Eigenworms dataset as it also contains very long sequences (вүҲ18000absent18000\approx 18000).
In particular, we used a learning rate of 0.020.020.02, hidden dimension of 256256256, 333 layers with dt values [0.0000281,0.0343,0.0343]
- 0.00002810.03430.0343[0.0000281,0.0343,0.0343], dropout of 0.10.10.1, and alpha of 00.


For UniCORNN trained over the MFCC features, we used the authorsвҖҷ reported hyperparameters for the MNIST dataset (again due to similarly sized sequence lengths), and further tuned the learning rate over the values: {0.0001,0.001,0.005,0.01,0.02}0.00010.0010.0050.010.02\{0.0001,0.001,0.005,0.01,0.02\}, О”вҖӢtО”рқ‘Ў\Delta t values: {0.01,0.1}0.010.1\{0.01,0.1\}, and alpha values: {10,20,30}102030\{10,20,30\}.


The best model used a learning rate of 0.020.020.02, hidden dimension of 256256256, 333 layers with dt values of 0.190.190.19, dropout of 0.10.10.1, and alpha of 30.6530.6530.65.


For NRDE on SC-Raw, we used depth 222, step size 444, hidden dimension 323232, and 333 layers.
Our results were better than unofficial numbers reported in correspondence with the authors,
so we did not tune further.


#### 
F.2.6 Convergence Speed (TableВ 3)


The convergence table compared against logs directly from the corresponding baselineвҖҷs SoTA models [47, 44], which were either released publicly or found in direct correspondence with the authors.
To generate the wall clock numbers, we ran the baseline models on the same hardware as our models and extrapolated to the target epoch.

# The Rust Programming Language вҖ” Patterns and Matching

# Patterns and Matching

Patterns are a special syntax in Rust for matching against the structure of
types, both complex and simple. Using patterns in conjunction with `match`
expressions and other constructs gives you more control over a programвҖҷs
control flow. A pattern consists of some combination of the following:

- Literals
- Destructured arrays, enums, structs, or tuples
- Variables
- Wildcards
- Placeholders

Some example patterns include `x`, `(a, 3)`, and `Some(Color::Red)`. In the
contexts in which patterns are valid, these components describe the shape of
data. Our program then matches values against the patterns to determine whether
it has the correct shape of data to continue running a particular piece of code.

To use a pattern, we compare it to some value. If the pattern matches the
value, we use the value parts in our code. Recall the `match` expressions in
Chapter 6 that used patterns, such as the coin-sorting machine example. If the
value fits the shape of the pattern, we can use the named pieces. If it
doesnвҖҷt, the code associated with the pattern wonвҖҷt run.

This chapter is a reference on all things related to patterns. WeвҖҷll cover the
valid places to use patterns, the difference between refutable and irrefutable
patterns, and the different kinds of pattern syntax that you might see. By the
end of the chapter, youвҖҷll know how to use patterns to express many concepts in
a clear way.

<|endoftext|>

# The Rust Programming Language вҖ” A Closer Look at the Traits for Async

<a id="digging-into-the-traits-for-async"></a>

## A Closer Look at the Traits for Async

Throughout the chapter, weвҖҷve used the `Future`, `Stream`, and `StreamExt`
traits in various ways. So far, though, weвҖҷve avoided getting too far into the
details of how they work or how they fit together, which is fine most of the
time for your day-to-day Rust work. Sometimes, though, youвҖҷll encounter
situations where youвҖҷll need to understand a few more of these traitsвҖҷ details,
along with the `Pin` type and the `Unpin` trait. In this section, weвҖҷll dig in
just enough to help in those scenarios, still leaving the _really_ deep dive
for other documentation.



<a id="future"></a>

### The `Future` Trait

LetвҖҷs start by taking a closer look at how the `Future` trait works. HereвҖҷs how
Rust defines it:

```rust
use std::pin::Pin;
use std::task::{Context, Poll};

pub trait Future {
    type Output;

    fn poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Self::Output>;
}
```

That trait definition includes a bunch of new types and also some syntax we
havenвҖҷt seen before, so letвҖҷs walk through the definition piece by piece.

First, `Future`вҖҷs associated type `Output` says what the future resolves to.
This is analogous to the `Item` associated type for the `Iterator` trait.
Second, `Future` has the `poll` method, which takes a special `Pin` reference
for its `self` parameter and a mutable reference to a `Context` type, and
returns a `Poll<Self::Output>`. WeвҖҷll talk more about `Pin` and `Context` in a
moment. For now, letвҖҷs focus on what the method returns, the `Poll` type:

```rust
pub enum Poll<T> {
    Ready(T),
    Pending,
}
```

This `Poll` type is similar to an `Option`. It has one variant that has a value,
`Ready(T)`, and one that does not, `Pending`. `Poll` means something quite
different from `Option`, though! The `Pending` variant indicates that the future
still has work to do, so the caller will need to check again later. The `Ready`
variant indicates that the `Future` has finished its work and the `T` value is
available.

> Note: ItвҖҷs rare to need to call `poll` directly, but if you do need to, keep
> in mind that with most futures, the caller should not call `poll` again after
> the future has returned `Ready`. Many futures will panic if polled again after
> becoming ready. Futures that are safe to poll again will say so explicitly in
> their documentation. This is similar to how `Iterator::next` behaves.

When you see code that uses `await`, Rust compiles it under the hood to code
that calls `poll`. If you look back at Listing 17-4, where we printed out the
page title for a single URL once it resolved, Rust compiles it into something
kind of (although not exactly) like this:

```rust,ignore
match page_title(url).poll() {
    Ready(page_title) => match page_title {
        Some(title) => println!("The title for {url} was {title}"),
        None => println!("{url} had no title"),
    }
    Pending => {
        // But what goes here?
    }
}
```

What should we do when the future is still `Pending`? We need some way to try
again, and again, and again, until the future is finally ready. In other words,
we need a loop:

```rust,ignore
let mut page_title_fut = page_title(url);
loop {
    match page_title_fut.poll() {
        Ready(value) => match page_title {
            Some(title) => println!("The title for {url} was {title}"),
            None => println!("{url} had no title"),
        }
        Pending => {
            // continue
        }
    }
}
```

If Rust compiled it to exactly that code, though, every `await` would be
blockingвҖ”exactly the opposite of what we were going for! Instead, Rust ensures
that the loop can hand off control to something that can pause work on this
future to work on other futures and then check this one again later. As weвҖҷve
seen, that something is an async runtime, and this scheduling and coordination
work is one of its main jobs.

In the [вҖңSending Data Between Two Tasks Using Message
PassingвҖқ][message-passing] section, we described waiting on
`rx.recv`. The `recv` call returns a future, and awaiting the future polls it.
We noted that a runtime will pause the future until itвҖҷs ready with either
`Some(message)` or `None` when the channel closes. With our deeper
understanding of the `Future` trait, and specifically `Future::poll`, we can
see how that works. The runtime knows the future isnвҖҷt ready when it returns
`Poll::Pending`. Conversely, the runtime knows the future _is_ ready and
advances it when `poll` returns `Poll::Ready(Some(message))` or
`Poll::Ready(None)`.

The exact details of how a runtime does that are beyond the scope of this book,
but the key is to see the basic mechanics of futures: a runtime _polls_ each
future it is responsible for, putting the future back to sleep when it is not
yet ready.



<a id="pinning-and-the-pin-and-unpin-traits"></a>
<a id="the-pin-and-unpin-traits"></a>

### The `Pin` Type and the `Unpin` Trait

Back in Listing 17-13, we used the `trpl::join!` macro to await three
futures. However, itвҖҷs common to have a collection such as a vector containing
some number futures that wonвҖҷt be known until runtime. LetвҖҷs change Listing
17-13 to the code in Listing 17-23 that puts the three futures into a vector
and calls the `trpl::join_all` function instead, which wonвҖҷt compile yet.

<Listing number="17-23" caption="Awaiting futures in a collection"  file-name="src/main.rs">

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

We put each future within a `Box` to make them into _trait objects_, just as
we did in the вҖңReturning Errors from `run`вҖқ section in Chapter 12. (WeвҖҷll cover
trait objects in detail in Chapter 18.) Using trait objects lets us treat each
of the anonymous futures produced by these types as the same type, because all
of them implement the `Future` trait.

This might be surprising. After all, none of the async blocks returns anything,
so each one produces a `Future<Output = ()>`. Remember that `Future` is a
trait, though, and that the compiler creates a unique enum for each async
block, even when they have identical output types. Just as you canвҖҷt put two
different handwritten structs in a `Vec`, you canвҖҷt mix compiler-generated
enums.

Then we pass the collection of futures to the `trpl::join_all` function and
await the result. However, this doesnвҖҷt compile; hereвҖҷs the relevant part of
the error messages.

 src/main.rs:48:33
   |
48 |         trpl::join_all(futures).await;
   |                                 ^^^^^ the trait `Unpin` is not implemented for `dyn Future<Output = ()>`
   |
   = note: consider using the `pin!` macro
           consider using `Box::pin` if you need to access the pinned value outside of the current scope
   = note: required for `Box<dyn Future<Output = ()>>` to implement `Future`
note: required by a bound in `futures_util::future::join_all::JoinAll`
  --> file:///home/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/futures-util-0.3.30/src/future/join_all.rs:29:8
   |
27 | pub struct JoinAll<F>
   |            ------- required by a bound in this struct
28 | where
29 |     F: Future,
   |        ^^^^^^ required by this bound in `JoinAll`
```

The note in this error message tells us that we should use the `pin!` macro to
_pin_ the values, which means putting them inside the `Pin` type that
guarantees the values wonвҖҷt be moved in memory. The error message says pinning
is required because `dyn Future<Output = ()>` needs to implement the `Unpin`
trait and it currently does not.

The `trpl::join_all` function returns a struct called `JoinAll`. That struct is
generic over a type `F`, which is constrained to implement the `Future` trait.
Directly awaiting a future with `await` pins the future implicitly. ThatвҖҷs why
we donвҖҷt need to use `pin!` everywhere we want to await futures.

However, weвҖҷre not directly awaiting a future here. Instead, we construct a new
future, JoinAll, by passing a collection of futures to the `join_all` function.
The signature for `join_all` requires that the types of the items in the
collection all implement the `Future` trait, and `Box<T>` implements `Future`
only if the `T` it wraps is a future that implements the `Unpin` trait.

ThatвҖҷs a lot to absorb! To really understand it, letвҖҷs dive a little further
into how the `Future` trait actually works, in particular around pinning. Look
again at the definition of the `Future` trait:

```rust
use std::pin::Pin;
use std::task::{Context, Poll};

pub trait Future {
    type Output;

    // Required method
    fn poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Self::Output>;
}
```

The `cx` parameter and its `Context` type are the key to how a runtime actually
knows when to check any given future while still being lazy. Again, the details
of how that works are beyond the scope of this chapter, and you generally only
need to think about this when writing a custom `Future` implementation. WeвҖҷll
focus instead on the type for `self`, as this is the first time weвҖҷve seen a
method where `self` has a type annotation. A type annotation for `self` works
like type annotations for other function parameters but with two key
differences:

- It tells Rust what type `self` must be for the method to be called.
- It canвҖҷt be just any type. ItвҖҷs restricted to the type on which the method is
  implemented, a reference or smart pointer to that type, or a `Pin` wrapping a
  reference to that type.

WeвҖҷll see more on this syntax in [Chapter 18][ch-18]. For now,
itвҖҷs enough to know that if we want to poll a future to check whether it is
`Pending` or `Ready(Output)`, we need a `Pin`-wrapped mutable reference to the
type.

`Pin` is a wrapper for pointer-like types such as `&`, `&mut`, `Box`, and `Rc`.
(Technically, `Pin` works with types that implement the `Deref` or `DerefMut`
traits, but this is effectively equivalent to working only with references and
smart pointers.) `Pin` is not a pointer itself and doesnвҖҷt have any behavior of
its own like `Rc` and `Arc` do with reference counting; itвҖҷs purely a tool the
compiler can use to enforce constraints on pointer usage.

Recalling that `await` is implemented in terms of calls to `poll` starts to
explain the error message we saw earlier, but that was in terms of `Unpin`, not
`Pin`. So how exactly does `Pin` relate to `Unpin`, and why does `Future` need
`self` to be in a `Pin` type to call `poll`?

Remember from earlier in this chapter that a series of await points in a future
get compiled into a state machine, and the compiler makes sure that state
machine follows all of RustвҖҷs normal rules around safety, including borrowing
and ownership. To make that work, Rust looks at what data is needed between one
await point and either the next await point or the end of the async block. It
then creates a corresponding variant in the compiled state machine. Each
variant gets the access it needs to the data that will be used in that section
of the source code, whether by taking ownership of that data or by getting a
mutable or immutable reference to it.

So far, so good: if we get anything wrong about the ownership or references in
a given async block, the borrow checker will tell us. When we want to move
around the future that corresponds to that blockвҖ”like moving it into a `Vec` to
pass to `join_all`вҖ”things get trickier.

When we move a futureвҖ”whether by pushing it into a data structure to use as an
iterator with `join_all` or by returning it from a functionвҖ”that actually means
moving the state machine Rust creates for us. And unlike most other types in
Rust, the futures Rust creates for async blocks can end up with references to
themselves in the fields of any given variant, as shown in the simplified illustration in Figure 17-4.

<figure>

<img alt="A single-column, three-row table representing a future, fut1, which has data values 0 and 1 in the first two rows and an arrow pointing from the third row back to the second row, representing an internal reference within the future." src="img/trpl17-04.svg" class="center" />

<figcaption>Figure 17-4: A self-referential data type</figcaption>

</figure>

By default, though, any object that has a reference to itself is unsafe to move,
because references always point to the actual memory address of whatever they
refer to (see Figure 17-5). If you move the data structure itself, those
internal references will be left pointing to the old location. However, that
memory location is now invalid. For one thing, its value will not be updated
when you make changes to the data structure. For anotherвҖ”more importantвҖ”thing,
the computer is now free to reuse that memory for other purposes! You could end
up reading completely unrelated data later.

<figure>

<img alt="Two tables, depicting two futures, fut1 and fut2, each of which has one column and three rows, representing the result of having moved a future out of fut1 into fut2. The first, fut1, is grayed out, with a question mark in each index, representing unknown memory. The second, fut2, has 0 and 1 in the first and second rows and an arrow pointing from its third row back to the second row of fut1, representing a pointer that is referencing the old location in memory of the future before it was moved." src="img/trpl17-05.svg" class="center" />

<figcaption>Figure 17-5: The unsafe result of moving a self-referential data type</figcaption>

</figure>

Theoretically, the Rust compiler could try to update every reference to an
object whenever it gets moved, but that could add a lot of performance overhead,
especially if a whole web of references needs updating. If we could instead make
sure the data structure in question _doesnвҖҷt move in memory_, we wouldnвҖҷt have
to update any references. This is exactly what RustвҖҷs borrow checker is for:
in safe code, it prevents you from moving any item with an active reference to
it.

`Pin` builds on that to give us the exact guarantee we need. When we _pin_ a
value by wrapping a pointer to that value in `Pin`, it can no longer move. Thus,
if you have `Pin<Box<SomeType>>`, you actually pin the `SomeType` value, _not_
the `Box` pointer. Figure 17-6 illustrates this process.

<figure>

<img alt="Three boxes laid out side by side. The first is labeled вҖңPinвҖқ, the second вҖңb1вҖқ, and the third вҖңpinnedвҖқ. Within вҖңpinnedвҖқ is a table labeled вҖңfutвҖқ, with a single column; it represents a future with cells for each part of the data structure. Its first cell has the value вҖң0вҖқ, its second cell has an arrow coming out of it and pointing to the fourth and final cell, which has the value вҖң1вҖқ in it, and the third cell has dashed lines and an ellipsis to indicate there may be other parts to the data structure. All together, the вҖңfutвҖқ table represents a future which is self-referential. An arrow leaves the box labeled вҖңPinвҖқ, goes through the box labeled вҖңb1вҖқ and terminates inside the вҖңpinnedвҖқ box at the вҖңfutвҖқ table." src="img/trpl17-06.svg" class="center" />

<figcaption>Figure 17-6: Pinning a `Box` that points to a self-referential future type</figcaption>

</figure>

In fact, the `Box` pointer can still move around freely. Remember: we care about
making sure the data ultimately being referenced stays in place. If a pointer
moves around, _but the data it points to_ is in the same place, as in Figure
17-7, thereвҖҷs no potential problem. (As an independent exercise, look at the docs
for the types as well as the `std::pin` module and try to work out how youвҖҷd do
this with a `Pin` wrapping a `Box`.) The key is that the self-referential type
itself cannot move, because it is still pinned.

<figure>

<img alt="Four boxes laid out in three rough columns, identical to the previous diagram with a change to the second column. Now there are two boxes in the second column, labeled вҖңb1вҖқ and вҖңb2вҖқ, вҖңb1вҖқ is grayed out, and the arrow from вҖңPinвҖқ goes through вҖңb2вҖқ instead of вҖңb1вҖқ, indicating that the pointer has moved from вҖңb1вҖқ to вҖңb2вҖқ, but the data in вҖңpinnedвҖқ has not moved." src="img/trpl17-07.svg" class="center" />

<figcaption>Figure 17-7: Moving a `Box` which points to a self-referential future type</figcaption>

</figure>

However, most types are perfectly safe to move around, even if they happen to be
behind a `Pin` pointer. We only need to think about pinning when items have
internal references. Primitive values such as numbers and Booleans are safe
because they obviously donвҖҷt have any internal references.
Neither do most types you normally work with in Rust. You can move around
a `Vec`, for example, without worrying. Given what we have seen so far, if
you have a `Pin<Vec<String>>`, youвҖҷd have to do everything via the safe but
restrictive APIs provided by `Pin`, even though a `Vec<String>` is always safe
to move if there are no other references to it. We need a way to tell the
compiler that itвҖҷs fine to move items around in cases like thisвҖ”and thatвҖҷs
where `Unpin` comes into play.

`Unpin` is a marker trait, similar to the `Send` and `Sync` traits we saw in
Chapter 16, and thus has no functionality of its own. Marker traits exist only
to tell the compiler itвҖҷs safe to use the type implementing a given trait in a
particular context. `Unpin` informs the compiler that a given type does _not_
need to uphold any guarantees about whether the value in question can be safely
moved.

<!--
  The inline `<code>` in the next block is to allow the inline `<em>` inside it,
  matching what NoStarch does style-wise, and emphasizing within the text here
  that it is something distinct from a normal type.
-->

Just as with `Send` and `Sync`, the compiler implements `Unpin` automatically
for all types where it can prove it is safe. A special case, again similar to
`Send` and `Sync`, is where `Unpin` is _not_ implemented for a type. The
notation for this is <code>impl !Unpin for <em>SomeType</em></code>, where
<code><em>SomeType</em></code> is the name of a type that _does_ need to uphold
those guarantees to be safe whenever a pointer to that type is used in a `Pin`.

In other words, there are two things to keep in mind about the relationship
between `Pin` and `Unpin`. First, `Unpin` is the вҖңnormalвҖқ case, and `!Unpin` is
the special case. Second, whether a type implements `Unpin` or `!Unpin` _only_
matters when youвҖҷre using a pinned pointer to that type like <code>Pin<&mut
<em>SomeType</em>></code>.

To make that concrete, think about a `String`: it has a length and the Unicode
characters that make it up. We can wrap a `String` in `Pin`, as seen in Figure
17-8. However, `String` automatically implements `Unpin`, as do most other types
in Rust.

<figure>

<img alt="A box labeled вҖңPinвҖқ on the left with an arrow going from it to a box labeled вҖңStringвҖқ on the right. The вҖңStringвҖқ box contains the data 5usize, representing the length of the string, and the letters вҖңhвҖқ, вҖңeвҖқ, вҖңlвҖқ, вҖңlвҖқ, and вҖңoвҖқ representing the characters of the string вҖңhelloвҖқ stored in this String instance. A dotted rectangle surrounds the вҖңStringвҖқ box and its label, but not the вҖңPinвҖқ box." src="img/trpl17-08.svg" class="center" />

<figcaption>Figure 17-8: Pinning a `String`; the dotted line indicates that the `String` implements the `Unpin` trait and thus is not pinned</figcaption>

</figure>

As a result, we can do things that would be illegal if `String` implemented
`!Unpin` instead, such as replacing one string with another at the exact same
location in memory as in Figure 17-9. This doesnвҖҷt violate the `Pin` contract,
because `String` has no internal references that make it unsafe to move around.
That is precisely why it implements `Unpin` rather than `!Unpin`.

<figure>

<img alt="The same вҖңhelloвҖқ string data from the previous example, now labeled вҖңs1вҖқ and grayed out. The вҖңPinвҖқ box from the previous example now points to a different String instance, one that is labeled вҖңs2вҖқ, is valid, has a length of 7usize, and contains the characters of the string вҖңgoodbyeвҖқ. s2 is surrounded by a dotted rectangle because it, too, implements the Unpin trait." src="img/trpl17-09.svg" class="center" />

<figcaption>Figure 17-9: Replacing the `String` with an entirely different `String` in memory</figcaption>

</figure>

Now we know enough to understand the errors reported for that `join_all` call
from back in Listing 17-23. We originally tried to move the futures produced by
async blocks into a `Vec<Box<dyn Future<Output = ()>>>`, but as weвҖҷve seen,
those futures may have internal references, so they donвҖҷt automatically
implement `Unpin`. Once we pin them, we can pass the resulting `Pin` type into
the `Vec`, confident that the underlying data in the futures will _not_ be
moved. Listing 17-24 shows how to fix the code by calling the `pin!` macro
where each of the three futures are defined and adjusting the trait object type.

<Listing number="17-24" caption="Pinning the futures to enable moving them into the vector">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

This example now compiles and runs, and we could add or remove futures from the
vector at runtime and join them all.

`Pin` and `Unpin` are mostly important for building lower-level libraries, or
when youвҖҷre building a runtime itself, rather than for day-to-day Rust code.
When you see these traits in error messages, though, now youвҖҷll have a better
idea of how to fix your code!

> Note: This combination of `Pin` and `Unpin` makes it possible to safely
> implement a whole class of complex types in Rust that would otherwise prove
> challenging because theyвҖҷre self-referential. Types that require `Pin` show up
> most commonly in async Rust today, but every once in a while, you might see
> them in other contexts, too.
>
> The specifics of how `Pin` and `Unpin` work, and the rules theyвҖҷre required
> to uphold, are covered extensively in the API documentation for `std::pin`, so
> if youвҖҷre interested in learning more, thatвҖҷs a great place to start.
>
> If you want to understand how things work under the hood in even more detail,
> see Chapters [2][under-the-hood] and
> [4][pinning] of
> [_Asynchronous Programming in Rust_][async-book].

### The `Stream` Trait

Now that you have a deeper grasp on the `Future`, `Pin`, and `Unpin` traits, we
can turn our attention to the `Stream` trait. As you learned earlier in the
chapter, streams are similar to asynchronous iterators. Unlike `Iterator` and
`Future`, however, `Stream` has no definition in the standard library as of
this writing, but there _is_ a very common definition from the `futures` crate
used throughout the ecosystem.

LetвҖҷs review the definitions of the `Iterator` and `Future` traits before
looking at how a `Stream` trait might merge them together. From `Iterator`, we
have the idea of a sequence: its `next` method provides an
`Option<Self::Item>`. From `Future`, we have the idea of readiness over time:
its `poll` method provides a `Poll<Self::Output>`. To represent a sequence of
items that become ready over time, we define a `Stream` trait that puts those
features together:

```rust
use std::pin::Pin;
use std::task::{Context, Poll};

trait Stream {
    type Item;

    fn poll_next(
        self: Pin<&mut Self>,
        cx: &mut Context<'_>
    ) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>>;
}
```

The `Stream` trait defines an associated type called `Item` for the type of the
items produced by the stream. This is similar to `Iterator`, where there may be
zero to many items, and unlike `Future`, where there is always a single
`Output`, even if itвҖҷs the unit type `()`.

`Stream` also defines a method to get those items. We call it `poll_next`, to
make it clear that it polls in the same way `Future::poll` does and produces a
sequence of items in the same way `Iterator::next` does. Its return type
combines `Poll` with `Option`. The outer type is `Poll`, because it has to be
checked for readiness, just as a future does. The inner type is `Option`,
because it needs to signal whether there are more messages, just as an iterator
does.

Something very similar to this definition will likely end up as part of RustвҖҷs
standard library. In the meantime, itвҖҷs part of the toolkit of most runtimes,
so you can rely on it, and everything we cover next should generally apply!

In the examples we saw in the [вҖңStreams: Futures in SequenceвҖқ][streams]<!--
ignore --> section, though, we didnвҖҷt use `poll_next` _or_ `Stream`, but
instead used `next` and `StreamExt`. We _could_ work directly in terms of the
`poll_next` API by hand-writing our own `Stream` state machines, of course,
just as we _could_ work with futures directly via their `poll` method. Using
`await` is much nicer, though, and the `StreamExt` trait supplies the `next`
method so we can do just that:

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

<!--
TODO: update this if/when tokio/etc. update their MSRV and switch to using async functions
in traits, since the lack thereof is the reason they do not yet have this.
-->

> Note: The actual definition we used earlier in the chapter looks slightly
> different than this, because it supports versions of Rust that did not yet
> support using async functions in traits. As a result, it looks like this:
>
> ```rust,ignore
> fn next(&mut self) -> Next<'_, Self> where Self: Unpin;
> ```
>
> That `Next` type is a `struct` that implements `Future` and allows us to name
> the lifetime of the reference to `self` with `Next<'_, Self>`, so that `await`
> can work with this method.

The `StreamExt` trait is also the home of all the interesting methods available
to use with streams. `StreamExt` is automatically implemented for every type
that implements `Stream`, but these traits are defined separately to enable the
community to iterate on convenience APIs without affecting the foundational
trait.

In the version of `StreamExt` used in the `trpl` crate, the trait not only
defines the `next` method but also supplies a default implementation of `next`
that correctly handles the details of calling `Stream::poll_next`. This means
that even when you need to write your own streaming data type, you _only_ have
to implement `Stream`, and then anyone who uses your data type can use
`StreamExt` and its methods with it automatically.

ThatвҖҷs all weвҖҷre going to cover for the lower-level details on these traits. To
wrap up, letвҖҷs consider how futures (including streams), tasks, and threads all
fit together!

[ch-18]: ch18-00-oop.html
[async-book]: https://rust-lang.github.io/async-book/
[under-the-hood]: https://rust-lang.github.io/async-book/02_execution/01_chapter.html
[pinning]: https://rust-lang.github.io/async-book/04_pinning/01_chapter.html
[first-async]: ch17-01-futures-and-syntax.html#our-first-async-program
[any-number-futures]: ch17-03-more-futures.html#working-with-any-number-of-futures
[streams]: ch17-04-streams.html
