# The Rust Programming Language вҖ” Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match

## Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match

Patterns come in two forms: refutable and irrefutable. Patterns that will match
for any possible value passed are _irrefutable_. An example would be `x` in the
statement `let x = 5;` because `x` matches anything and therefore cannot fail
to match. Patterns that can fail to match for some possible value are
_refutable_. An example would be `Some(x)` in the expression `if let Some(x) =
a_value` because if the value in the `a_value` variable is `None` rather than
`Some`, the `Some(x)` pattern will not match.

Function parameters, `let` statements, and `for` loops can only accept
irrefutable patterns because the program cannot do anything meaningful when
values donвҖҷt match. The `if let` and `while let` expressions and the
`let...else` statement accept refutable and irrefutable patterns, but the
compiler warns against irrefutable patterns because, by definition, theyвҖҷre
intended to handle possible failure: The functionality of a conditional is in
its ability to perform differently depending on success or failure.

In general, you shouldnвҖҷt have to worry about the distinction between refutable
and irrefutable patterns; however, you do need to be familiar with the concept
of refutability so that you can respond when you see it in an error message. In
those cases, youвҖҷll need to change either the pattern or the construct youвҖҷre
using the pattern with, depending on the intended behavior of the code.

LetвҖҷs look at an example of what happens when we try to use a refutable pattern
where Rust requires an irrefutable pattern and vice versa. Listing 19-8 shows a
`let` statement, but for the pattern, weвҖҷve specified `Some(x)`, a refutable
pattern. As you might expect, this code will not compile.

<Listing number="19-8" caption="Attempting to use a refutable pattern with `let`">

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

If `some_option_value` were a `None` value, it would fail to match the pattern
`Some(x)`, meaning the pattern is refutable. However, the `let` statement can
only accept an irrefutable pattern because there is nothing valid the code can
do with a `None` value. At compile time, Rust will complain that weвҖҷve tried to
use a refutable pattern where an irrefutable pattern is required:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

Because we didnвҖҷt cover (and couldnвҖҷt cover!) every valid value with the
pattern `Some(x)`, Rust rightfully produces a compiler error.

If we have a refutable pattern where an irrefutable pattern is needed, we can
fix it by changing the code that uses the pattern: Instead of using `let`, we
can use `let...else`. Then, if the pattern doesnвҖҷt match, the code in the curly
brackets will handle the value. Listing 19-9 shows how to fix the code in
Listing 19-8.

<Listing number="19-9" caption="Using `let...else` and a block with refutable patterns instead of `let`">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

WeвҖҷve given the code an out! This code is perfectly valid, although it means we
cannot use an irrefutable pattern without receiving a warning. If we give
`let...else` a pattern that will always match, such as `x`, as shown in Listing
19-10, the compiler will give a warning.

<Listing number="19-10" caption="Attempting to use an irrefutable pattern with `let...else`">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

Rust complains that it doesnвҖҷt make sense to use `let...else` with an
irrefutable pattern:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

For this reason, match arms must use refutable patterns, except for the last
arm, which should match any remaining values with an irrefutable pattern. Rust
allows us to use an irrefutable pattern in a `match` with only one arm, but
this syntax isnвҖҷt particularly useful and could be replaced with a simpler
`let` statement.

Now that you know where to use patterns and the difference between refutable
and irrefutable patterns, letвҖҷs cover all the syntax we can use to create
patterns.

<|endoftext|>

# The Rust Programming Language вҖ” Concise Control Flow with `if let` and `let...else`

## Concise Control Flow with `if let` and `let...else`

The `if let` syntax lets you combine `if` and `let` into a less verbose way to
handle values that match one pattern while ignoring the rest. Consider the
program in Listing 6-6 that matches on an `Option<u8>` value in the
`config_max` variable but only wants to execute code if the value is the `Some`
variant.

<Listing number="6-6" caption="A `match` that only cares about executing code when the value is `Some`">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

If the value is `Some`, we print out the value in the `Some` variant by binding
the value to the variable `max` in the pattern. We donвҖҷt want to do anything
with the `None` value. To satisfy the `match` expression, we have to add `_ =>
()` after processing just one variant, which is annoying boilerplate code to
add.

Instead, we could write this in a shorter way using `if let`. The following
code behaves the same as the `match` in Listing 6-6:

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

The syntax `if let` takes a pattern and an expression separated by an equal
sign. It works the same way as a `match`, where the expression is given to the
`match` and the pattern is its first arm. In this case, the pattern is
`Some(max)`, and the `max` binds to the value inside the `Some`. We can then
use `max` in the body of the `if let` block in the same way we used `max` in
the corresponding `match` arm. The code in the `if let` block only runs if the
value matches the pattern.

Using `if let` means less typing, less indentation, and less boilerplate code.
However, you lose the exhaustive checking `match` enforces that ensures that
you arenвҖҷt forgetting to handle any cases. Choosing between `match` and `if
let` depends on what youвҖҷre doing in your particular situation and whether
gaining conciseness is an appropriate trade-off for losing exhaustive checking.

In other words, you can think of `if let` as syntax sugar for a `match` that
runs code when the value matches one pattern and then ignores all other values.

We can include an `else` with an `if let`. The block of code that goes with the
`else` is the same as the block of code that would go with the `_` case in the
`match` expression that is equivalent to the `if let` and `else`. Recall the
`Coin` enum definition in Listing 6-4, where the `Quarter` variant also held a
`UsState` value. If we wanted to count all non-quarter coins we see while also
announcing the state of the quarters, we could do that with a `match`
expression, like this:

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

Or we could use an `if let` and `else` expression, like this:

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

## Staying on the вҖңHappy PathвҖқ with `let...else`

The common pattern is to perform some computation when a value is present and
return a default value otherwise. Continuing with our example of coins with a
`UsState` value, if we wanted to say something funny depending on how old the
state on the quarter was, we might introduce a method on `UsState` to check the
age of a state, like so:

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

Then, we might use `if let` to match on the type of coin, introducing a `state`
variable within the body of the condition, as in Listing 6-7.

<Listing number="6-7" caption="Checking whether a state existed in 1900 by using conditionals nested inside an `if let`">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

That gets the job done, but it has pushed the work into the body of the `if
let` statement, and if the work to be done is more complicated, it might be
hard to follow exactly how the top-level branches relate. We could also take
advantage of the fact that expressions produce a value either to produce the
`state` from the `if let` or to return early, as in Listing 6-8. (You could do
something similar with a `match`, too.)

<Listing number="6-8" caption="Using `if let` to produce a value or return early">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

This is a bit annoying to follow in its own way, though! One branch of the `if
let` produces a value, and the other one returns from the function entirely.

To make this common pattern nicer to express, Rust has `let...else`. The
`let...else` syntax takes a pattern on the left side and an expression on the
right, very similar to `if let`, but it does not have an `if` branch, only an
`else` branch. If the pattern matches, it will bind the value from the pattern
in the outer scope. If the pattern does _not_ match, the program will flow into
the `else` arm, which must return from the function.

In Listing 6-9, you can see how Listing 6-8 looks when using `let...else` in
place of `if let`.

<Listing number="6-9" caption="Using `let...else` to clarify the flow through the function">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

Notice that it stays on the вҖңhappy pathвҖқ in the main body of the function this
way, without having significantly different control flow for two branches the
way the `if let` did.

If you have a situation in which your program has logic that is too verbose to
express using a `match`, remember that `if let` and `let...else` are in your
Rust toolbox as well.

## Summary

WeвҖҷve now covered how to use enums to create custom types that can be one of a
set of enumerated values. WeвҖҷve shown how the standard libraryвҖҷs `Option<T>`
type helps you use the type system to prevent errors. When enum values have
data inside them, you can use `match` or `if let` to extract and use those
values, depending on how many cases you need to handle.

Your Rust programs can now express concepts in your domain using structs and
enums. Creating custom types to use in your API ensures type safety: The
compiler will make certain your functions only get values of the type each
function expects.

In order to provide a well-organized API to your users that is straightforward
to use and only exposes exactly what your users will need, letвҖҷs now turn to
RustвҖҷs modules.

# The Rust Programming Language вҖ” `RefCell<T>` and the Interior Mutability Pattern

## `RefCell<T>` and the Interior Mutability Pattern

_Interior mutability_ is a design pattern in Rust that allows you to mutate
data even when there are immutable references to that data; normally, this
action is disallowed by the borrowing rules. To mutate data, the pattern uses
`unsafe` code inside a data structure to bend RustвҖҷs usual rules that govern
mutation and borrowing. Unsafe code indicates to the compiler that weвҖҷre
checking the rules manually instead of relying on the compiler to check them
for us; we will discuss unsafe code more in Chapter 20.

We can use types that use the interior mutability pattern only when we can
ensure that the borrowing rules will be followed at runtime, even though the
compiler canвҖҷt guarantee that. The `unsafe` code involved is then wrapped in a
safe API, and the outer type is still immutable.

LetвҖҷs explore this concept by looking at the `RefCell<T>` type that follows the
interior mutability pattern.



<a id="enforcing-borrowing-rules-at-runtime-with-refcellt"></a>

### Enforcing Borrowing Rules at Runtime

Unlike `Rc<T>`, the `RefCell<T>` type represents single ownership over the data
it holds. So, what makes `RefCell<T>` different from a type like `Box<T>`?
Recall the borrowing rules you learned in Chapter 4:

- At any given time, you can have _either_ one mutable reference or any number
  of immutable references (but not both).
- References must always be valid.

With references and `Box<T>`, the borrowing rulesвҖҷ invariants are enforced at
compile time. With `RefCell<T>`, these invariants are enforced _at runtime_.
With references, if you break these rules, youвҖҷll get a compiler error. With
`RefCell<T>`, if you break these rules, your program will panic and exit.

The advantages of checking the borrowing rules at compile time are that errors
will be caught sooner in the development process, and there is no impact on
runtime performance because all the analysis is completed beforehand. For those
reasons, checking the borrowing rules at compile time is the best choice in the
majority of cases, which is why this is RustвҖҷs default.

The advantage of checking the borrowing rules at runtime instead is that
certain memory-safe scenarios are then allowed, where they wouldвҖҷve been
disallowed by the compile-time checks. Static analysis, like the Rust compiler,
is inherently conservative. Some properties of code are impossible to detect by
analyzing the code: The most famous example is the Halting Problem, which is
beyond the scope of this book but is an interesting topic to research.

Because some analysis is impossible, if the Rust compiler canвҖҷt be sure the
code complies with the ownership rules, it might reject a correct program; in
this way, itвҖҷs conservative. If Rust accepted an incorrect program, users
wouldnвҖҷt be able to trust the guarantees Rust makes. However, if Rust rejects a
correct program, the programmer will be inconvenienced, but nothing
catastrophic can occur. The `RefCell<T>` type is useful when youвҖҷre sure your
code follows the borrowing rules but the compiler is unable to understand and
guarantee that.

Similar to `Rc<T>`, `RefCell<T>` is only for use in single-threaded scenarios
and will give you a compile-time error if you try using it in a multithreaded
context. WeвҖҷll talk about how to get the functionality of `RefCell<T>` in a
multithreaded program in Chapter 16.

Here is a recap of the reasons to choose `Box<T>`, `Rc<T>`, or `RefCell<T>`:

- `Rc<T>` enables multiple owners of the same data; `Box<T>` and `RefCell<T>`
  have single owners.
- `Box<T>` allows immutable or mutable borrows checked at compile time; `Rc<T>`
  allows only immutable borrows checked at compile time; `RefCell<T>` allows
  immutable or mutable borrows checked at runtime.
- Because `RefCell<T>` allows mutable borrows checked at runtime, you can
  mutate the value inside the `RefCell<T>` even when the `RefCell<T>` is
  immutable.

Mutating the value inside an immutable value is the interior mutability
pattern. LetвҖҷs look at a situation in which interior mutability is useful and
examine how itвҖҷs possible.



<a id="interior-mutability-a-mutable-borrow-to-an-immutable-value"></a>

### Using Interior Mutability

A consequence of the borrowing rules is that when you have an immutable value,
you canвҖҷt borrow it mutably. For example, this code wonвҖҷt compile:

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

If you tried to compile this code, youвҖҷd get the following error:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

However, there are situations in which it would be useful for a value to mutate
itself in its methods but appear immutable to other code. Code outside the
valueвҖҷs methods would not be able to mutate the value. Using `RefCell<T>` is
one way to get the ability to have interior mutability, but `RefCell<T>`
doesnвҖҷt get around the borrowing rules completely: The borrow checker in the
compiler allows this interior mutability, and the borrowing rules are checked
at runtime instead. If you violate the rules, youвҖҷll get a `panic!` instead of
a compiler error.

LetвҖҷs work through a practical example where we can use `RefCell<T>` to mutate
an immutable value and see why that is useful.



<a id="a-use-case-for-interior-mutability-mock-objects"></a>

#### Testing with Mock Objects

Sometimes during testing a programmer will use a type in place of another type,
in order to observe particular behavior and assert that itвҖҷs implemented
correctly. This placeholder type is called a _test double_. Think of it in the
sense of a stunt double in filmmaking, where a person steps in and substitutes
for an actor to do a particularly tricky scene. Test doubles stand in for other
types when weвҖҷre running tests. _Mock objects_ are specific types of test
doubles that record what happens during a test so that you can assert that the
correct actions took place.

Rust doesnвҖҷt have objects in the same sense as other languages have objects,
and Rust doesnвҖҷt have mock object functionality built into the standard library
as some other languages do. However, you can definitely create a struct that
will serve the same purposes as a mock object.

HereвҖҷs the scenario weвҖҷll test: WeвҖҷll create a library that tracks a value
against a maximum value and sends messages based on how close to the maximum
value the current value is. This library could be used to keep track of a
userвҖҷs quota for the number of API calls theyвҖҷre allowed to make, for example.

Our library will only provide the functionality of tracking how close to the
maximum a value is and what the messages should be at what times. Applications
that use our library will be expected to provide the mechanism for sending the
messages: The application could show the message to the user directly, send an
email, send a text message, or do something else. The library doesnвҖҷt need to
know that detail. All it needs is something that implements a trait weвҖҷll
provide, called `Messenger`. Listing 15-20 shows the library code.

<Listing number="15-20" file-name="src/lib.rs" caption="A library to keep track of how close a value is to a maximum value and warn when the value is at certain levels">

```rust,noplayground
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

One important part of this code is that the `Messenger` trait has one method
called `send` that takes an immutable reference to `self` and the text of the
message. This trait is the interface our mock object needs to implement so that
the mock can be used in the same way a real object is. The other important part
is that we want to test the behavior of the `set_value` method on the
`LimitTracker`. We can change what we pass in for the `value` parameter, but
`set_value` doesnвҖҷt return anything for us to make assertions on. We want to be
able to say that if we create a `LimitTracker` with something that implements
the `Messenger` trait and a particular value for `max`, the messenger is told
to send the appropriate messages when we pass different numbers for `value`.

We need a mock object that, instead of sending an email or text message when we
call `send`, will only keep track of the messages itвҖҷs told to send. We can
create a new instance of the mock object, create a `LimitTracker` that uses the
mock object, call the `set_value` method on `LimitTracker`, and then check that
the mock object has the messages we expect. Listing 15-21 shows an attempt to
implement a mock object to do just that, but the borrow checker wonвҖҷt allow it.

<Listing number="15-21" file-name="src/lib.rs" caption="An attempt to implement a `MockMessenger` that isnвҖҷt allowed by the borrow checker">

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

This test code defines a `MockMessenger` struct that has a `sent_messages`
field with a `Vec` of `String` values to keep track of the messages itвҖҷs told
to send. We also define an associated function `new` to make it convenient to
create new `MockMessenger` values that start with an empty list of messages. We
then implement the `Messenger` trait for `MockMessenger` so that we can give a
`MockMessenger` to a `LimitTracker`. In the definition of the `send` method, we
take the message passed in as a parameter and store it in the `MockMessenger`
list of `sent_messages`.

In the test, weвҖҷre testing what happens when the `LimitTracker` is told to set
`value` to something that is more than 75 percent of the `max` value. First, we
create a new `MockMessenger`, which will start with an empty list of messages.
Then, we create a new `LimitTracker` and give it a reference to the new
`MockMessenger` and a `max` value of `100`. We call the `set_value` method on
the `LimitTracker` with a value of `80`, which is more than 75 percent of 100.
Then, we assert that the list of messages that the `MockMessenger` is keeping
track of should now have one message in it.

However, thereвҖҷs one problem with this test, as shown here:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

We canвҖҷt modify the `MockMessenger` to keep track of the messages, because the
`send` method takes an immutable reference to `self`. We also canвҖҷt take the
suggestion from the error text to use `&mut self` in both the `impl` method and
the trait definition. We do not want to change the `Messenger` trait solely for
the sake of testing. Instead, we need to find a way to make our test code work
correctly with our existing design.

This is a situation in which interior mutability can help! WeвҖҷll store the
`sent_messages` within a `RefCell<T>`, and then the `send` method will be able
to modify `sent_messages` to store the messages weвҖҷve seen. Listing 15-22 shows
what that looks like.

<Listing number="15-22" file-name="src/lib.rs" caption="Using `RefCell<T>` to mutate an inner value while the outer value is considered immutable">

```rust,noplayground
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

The `sent_messages` field is now of type `RefCell<Vec<String>>` instead of
`Vec<String>`. In the `new` function, we create a new `RefCell<Vec<String>>`
instance around the empty vector.

For the implementation of the `send` method, the first parameter is still an
immutable borrow of `self`, which matches the trait definition. We call
`borrow_mut` on the `RefCell<Vec<String>>` in `self.sent_messages` to get a
mutable reference to the value inside the `RefCell<Vec<String>>`, which is the
vector. Then, we can call `push` on the mutable reference to the vector to keep
track of the messages sent during the test.

The last change we have to make is in the assertion: To see how many items are
in the inner vector, we call `borrow` on the `RefCell<Vec<String>>` to get an
immutable reference to the vector.

Now that youвҖҷve seen how to use `RefCell<T>`, letвҖҷs dig into how it works!



<a id="keeping-track-of-borrows-at-runtime-with-refcellt"></a>

#### Tracking Borrows at Runtime

When creating immutable and mutable references, we use the `&` and `&mut`
syntax, respectively. With `RefCell<T>`, we use the `borrow` and `borrow_mut`
methods, which are part of the safe API that belongs to `RefCell<T>`. The
`borrow` method returns the smart pointer type `Ref<T>`, and `borrow_mut`
returns the smart pointer type `RefMut<T>`. Both types implement `Deref`, so we
can treat them like regular references.

The `RefCell<T>` keeps track of how many `Ref<T>` and `RefMut<T>` smart
pointers are currently active. Every time we call `borrow`, the `RefCell<T>`
increases its count of how many immutable borrows are active. When a `Ref<T>`
value goes out of scope, the count of immutable borrows goes down by 1. Just
like the compile-time borrowing rules, `RefCell<T>` lets us have many immutable
borrows or one mutable borrow at any point in time.

If we try to violate these rules, rather than getting a compiler error as we
would with references, the implementation of `RefCell<T>` will panic at
runtime. Listing 15-23 shows a modification of the implementation of `send` in
Listing 15-22. WeвҖҷre deliberately trying to create two mutable borrows active
for the same scope to illustrate that `RefCell<T>` prevents us from doing this
at runtime.

<Listing number="15-23" file-name="src/lib.rs" caption="Creating two mutable references in the same scope to see that `RefCell<T>` will panic">

```rust,ignore,panics
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

We create a variable `one_borrow` for the `RefMut<T>` smart pointer returned
from `borrow_mut`. Then, we create another mutable borrow in the same way in
the variable `two_borrow`. This makes two mutable references in the same scope,
which isnвҖҷt allowed. When we run the tests for our library, the code in Listing
15-23 will compile without any errors, but the test will fail:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

Notice that the code panicked with the message `already borrowed:
BorrowMutError`. This is how `RefCell<T>` handles violations of the borrowing
rules at runtime.

Choosing to catch borrowing errors at runtime rather than compile time, as
weвҖҷve done here, means youвҖҷd potentially be finding mistakes in your code later
in the development process: possibly not until your code was deployed to
production. Also, your code would incur a small runtime performance penalty as
a result of keeping track of the borrows at runtime rather than compile time.
However, using `RefCell<T>` makes it possible to write a mock object that can
modify itself to keep track of the messages it has seen while youвҖҷre using it
in a context where only immutable values are allowed. You can use `RefCell<T>`
despite its trade-offs to get more functionality than regular references
provide.



<a id="having-multiple-owners-of-mutable-data-by-combining-rc-t-and-ref-cell-t"></a>
<a id="allowing-multiple-owners-of-mutable-data-with-rct-and-refcellt"></a>

### Allowing Multiple Owners of Mutable Data

A common way to use `RefCell<T>` is in combination with `Rc<T>`. Recall that
`Rc<T>` lets you have multiple owners of some data, but it only gives immutable
access to that data. If you have an `Rc<T>` that holds a `RefCell<T>`, you can
get a value that can have multiple owners _and_ that you can mutate!

For example, recall the cons list example in Listing 15-18 where we used
`Rc<T>` to allow multiple lists to share ownership of another list. Because
`Rc<T>` holds only immutable values, we canвҖҷt change any of the values in the
list once weвҖҷve created them. LetвҖҷs add in `RefCell<T>` for its ability to
change the values in the lists. Listing 15-24 shows that by using a
`RefCell<T>` in the `Cons` definition, we can modify the value stored in all
the lists.

<Listing number="15-24" file-name="src/main.rs" caption="Using `Rc<RefCell<i32>>` to create a `List` that we can mutate">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

We create a value that is an instance of `Rc<RefCell<i32>>` and store it in a
variable named `value` so that we can access it directly later. Then, we create
a `List` in `a` with a `Cons` variant that holds `value`. We need to clone
`value` so that both `a` and `value` have ownership of the inner `5` value
rather than transferring ownership from `value` to `a` or having `a` borrow
from `value`.

We wrap the list `a` in an `Rc<T>` so that when we create lists `b` and `c`,
they can both refer to `a`, which is what we did in Listing 15-18.

After weвҖҷve created the lists in `a`, `b`, and `c`, we want to add 10 to the
value in `value`. We do this by calling `borrow_mut` on `value`, which uses the
automatic dereferencing feature we discussed in [вҖңWhereвҖҷs the `->`
Operator?вҖқ][wheres-the---operator] in Chapter 5 to dereference
the `Rc<T>` to the inner `RefCell<T>` value. The `borrow_mut` method returns a
`RefMut<T>` smart pointer, and we use the dereference operator on it and change
the inner value.

When we print `a`, `b`, and `c`, we can see that they all have the modified
value of `15` rather than `5`:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

This technique is pretty neat! By using `RefCell<T>`, we have an outwardly
immutable `List` value. But we can use the methods on `RefCell<T>` that provide
access to its interior mutability so that we can modify our data when we need
to. The runtime checks of the borrowing rules protect us from data races, and
itвҖҷs sometimes worth trading a bit of speed for this flexibility in our data
structures. Note that `RefCell<T>` does not work for multithreaded code!
`Mutex<T>` is the thread-safe version of `RefCell<T>`, and weвҖҷll discuss
`Mutex<T>` in Chapter 16.

[wheres-the---operator]: ch05-03-method-syntax.html#wheres-the---operator

<|endoftext|>

# Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces (S4) вҖ” Gu et al. 2022
# ArXiv: 2111.00396
# Fuente: arXiv (acceso abierto)

## Abstract


A central goal of sequence modeling is designing a single principled model that can address sequence data across a range of modalities and tasks, particularly on long-range dependencies.
Although conventional models including RNNs, CNNs, and Transformers have specialized variants for capturing long dependencies, they still struggle to scale to very long sequences of 100001000010000 or more steps.
A promising recent approach proposed modeling sequences by simulating the fundamental state space model (SSM) xвҖІвҖӢ(t)=AвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+BвҖӢuвҖӢ(t),yвҖӢ(t)=CвҖӢxвҖӢ(t)+DвҖӢuвҖӢ(t)formulae-sequencesuperscriptрқ‘ҘвҖІрқ‘Ў
рқҗҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗөрқ‘ўрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ў
рқҗ¶рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўрқҗ·рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўx^{\prime}(t)=Ax(t)+Bu(t),y(t)=Cx(t)+Du(t), and showed that for appropriate choices of the state matrix AрқҗҙA, this system could handle long-range dependencies mathematically and empirically.
However, this method has prohibitive computation and memory requirements, rendering it infeasible as a general sequence modeling solution.
We propose the Structured State Space sequence model (S4) based on a new parameterization for the SSM, and show that it can be computed much more efficiently than prior approaches while preserving their theoretical strengths.
Our technique involves conditioning AрқҗҙA with a low-rank correction, allowing it to be diagonalized stably and reducing the SSM to the well-studied computation of a Cauchy kernel.
S4 achieves strong empirical results across a diverse range of established benchmarks, including (i) 91% accuracy on sequential CIFAR-10 with no data augmentation or auxiliary losses, on par with a larger 2-D ResNet, (ii) substantially closing the gap to Transformers on image and language modeling tasks, while performing generation 60Г—60\times faster (iii) SoTA on every task from the Long Range Arena benchmark, including solving the challenging Path-X task of length 16k that all prior work fails on, while being as efficient as all competitors.111Code is publicly available at https://github.com/HazyResearch/state-spaces.


## 
1 Introduction


A central problem in sequence modeling is efficiently handling data that contains long-range dependencies (LRDs).
Real-world time-series data often requires reasoning over tens of thousands of time steps, while few sequence models address even thousands of time steps.
For instance, results from the long-range arena (LRA) benchmarkВ [40] highlight that sequence models today perform poorly on LRD tasks,
including one (Path-X) where no model performs better than random guessing.


Figure 1: 
(Left) State Space Models (SSM) parameterized by matrices рқ‘Ё,рқ‘©,рқ‘Ә,рқ‘«
- рқ‘Ёрқ‘©рқ‘Әрқ‘«\bm{A},\bm{B},\bm{C},\bm{D} map an input signal uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t) to output yвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўy(t) through a latent state xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t).
(Center) Recent theory on continuous-time memorization derives special рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} matrices that allow SSMs to capture LRDs mathematically and empirically.
(Right) SSMs can be computed either as a recurrence (left) or convolution (right).
However, materializing these conceptual views requires utilizing different representations of its parameters (red, blue, green) which are very expensive to compute.
S4 introduces a novel parameterization that efficiently swaps between these representations, allowing it to handle a wide range of tasks, be efficient at both training and inference, and excel at long sequences.


Since LRDs are perhaps the foremost challenge for sequence models, all standard model families such as continuous-time models (CTMs), RNNs, CNNs, and Transformers include many specialized variants designed to address them.
Modern examples include orthogonal and Lipschitz RNNs [1, 13] to combat vanishing gradients, dilated convolutions to increase context size [3, 28], and an increasingly vast family of efficient Transformers that reduce the quadratic dependence on sequence length [22, 8].
Despite being designed for LRDs, these solutions still perform poorly on challenging benchmarks such as LRAВ [40] or raw audio classificationВ [18].


An alternative approach to LRDs was recently introduced based on the state space model (SSM) (Fig.В 1).
SSMs are a foundational scientific model used in fields such as control theory, computational neuroscience, and many more, but have not been applicable to deep learning for concrete theoretical reasons.
In particular, Gu etВ al. [18] showed that deep SSMs actually struggle even on simple tasks,
but can perform exceptionally well when equipped with special state matrices рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} recently derived to solve a problem of continuous-time memorization [45, 16].
Their Linear State Space Layer (LSSL) conceptually unifies the strengths of CTM, RNN and CNN models, and provides a proof of concept that deep SSMs can address LRDs in principle.


Unfortunately, the LSSL is infeasible to use in practice because of prohibitive computation and memory requirements induced by the state representation.
For state dimension Nрқ‘ҒN and sequence length LрқҗҝL, computing the latent state requires OвҖӢ(N2вҖӢL)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2рқҗҝO(N^{2}L) operations and OвҖӢ(NвҖӢL)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒрқҗҝO(NL) space вҖ“ compared to a О©вҖӢ(L+N)О©
рқҗҝрқ‘Ғ\Omega(L+N) lower bound for both.
Thus for reasonably sized models (e.g. N=256рқ‘Ғ256N=256 in Gu etВ al. [18]), the LSSL uses orders of magnitude more memory than comparably-sized RNNs or CNNs.
Although theoretically efficient algorithms for the LSSL were proposed, we show that these are numerically unstable.
In particular, the special рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} matrix is highly non-normal in the linear algebraic sense, which prevents the application of conventional algorithmic techniques. Consequently, although the LSSL showed that SSMs have strong performance, they are currently computationally impractical as a general sequence modeling solution.


In this work, we introduce the Structured State Space (S4) sequence model based on the SSM that solves the critical computational bottleneck in previous work.
Technically, S4 reparameterizes the structured state matrices рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} appearing in Voelker etВ al. [45], Gu etВ al. [16] by decomposing them as the sum of a low-rank and normal term.
Additionally, instead of expanding the standard SSM in coefficient space, we compute its truncated generating function in frequency space, which can be simplified into a multipole-like evaluation.
Combining these two ideas, we show that the low-rank term can be corrected by the Woodbury identity while the normal term can be diagonalized stably,
ultimately reducing to a well-studied and theoretically stable Cauchy kernel [29, 30].
This results in O~вҖӢ(N+L)~рқ‘Ӯ
рқ‘Ғрқҗҝ\tilde{O}(N+L) computation and OвҖӢ(N+L)рқ‘Ӯ
рқ‘ҒрқҗҝO(N+L) memory usage, which is essentially tight for sequence models.
Compared to the LSSL, S4 is up to 30Г—30\times faster with 400Г—400\times less memory usage, while exceeding the LSSLвҖҷs performance empirically.


Empirically, S4 significantly advances the state-of-the-art for LRD.
On the LRA benchmark for efficient sequence models, S4 is as fast as all baselines while outperforming them by 20+limit-from20
20+ points on average.
S4 is the first model to solve the difficult LRA Path-X task (length-163841638416384), achieving 88% accuracy compared to 50% random guessing for all prior work.
On speech classification with length-160001600016000 sequences, S4 halves the test error (1.7%percent1.71.7\%) of specialized Speech CNNs вҖ“
by contrast, all RNN and Transformer baselines fail to learn (вүҘ70%absentpercent70\geq 70\% error).


##### Towards a general-purpose sequence model.


Beyond LRD, a broad goal of machine learning is to develop a single model that can be used across a wide range of problems.
Models today are typically specialized to solve problems from a particular domain (e.g. images, audio, text, time-series), and enable a narrow range of capabilities (e.g. efficient training, fast generation, handling irregularly sampled data).
This specialization is typically expressed via domain-specific preprocessing, inductive biases, and architectures. Sequence models provide a general framework for solving many of these problems with reduced specialization
вҖ“ e.g. Vision Transformers for image classification with less 2D information [12]. However, most models such as Transformers generally still require substantial specialization per task to achieve high performance.


Deep SSMs in particular have conceptual strengths that suggest they may be promising as a general sequence modeling solution.
These strengths include a principled approach to handling LRDs, as well as the ability to move between continuous-time, convolutional, and recurrent model representations, each with distinct capabilities (Fig.В 1). Our technical contributions enable SSMs to be applied successfully to a varied set of benchmarks with minimal modification:


- 
вҖў 


Large-scale generative modeling.
On CIFAR-10 density estimation, S4 is competitive with the best autoregressive models (2.852.852.85 bits per dim). On WikiText-103 language modeling, S4 substantially closes the gap to Transformers (within 0.80.80.8 perplexity), setting SoTA for attention-free models.


- 
вҖў 


Fast autoregressive generation.
Like RNNs, S4 can use its latent state to perform 60Г—60\times faster pixel/token generation than standard autoregressive models on CIFAR-10 and WikiText-103.


- 
вҖў 


Sampling resolution change.
Like specialized CTMs, S4 can adapt to changes in time-series sampling frequency without retraining, e.g.В at 0.5Г—0.5\times frequency on speech classification.


- 
вҖў 


Learning with weaker inductive biases.
With no architectural changes, S4 surpasses Speech CNNs on speech classification, outperforms the specialized Informer model on time-series forecasting problems,
and matches a 2-D ResNet on sequential CIFAR with over 90%percent9090\% accuracy.


## 
2 Background: State Spaces


SectionsВ 2.1, 2.2, 2.4 andВ 2.3 describe the four properties of SSMs in Fig.В 1:
the classic continuous-time representation, addressing LRDs with the HiPPO framework, the discrete-time recurrent representation, and the parallelizable convolution representation.
In particular, SectionВ 2.4 introduces the SSM convolution kernel рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}},
which is
the focus of our theoretical contributions in SectionВ 3.


### 
2.1 State Space Models: A Continuous-time Latent State Model


The state space model is defined by the simple equation (1).
It maps a 1-D input signal uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t) to an Nрқ‘ҒN-D latent state xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t)
before projecting to a 1-D output signal yвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўy(t).

SSMs are broadly used in many scientific disciplines

### 
2.2 Addressing Long-Range Dependencies with HiPPO


Prior work found that the basic SSM (1) actually performs very poorly in practice.
Intuitively, one explanation is that linear first-order ODEs solve to an exponential function,
and thus may suffer from gradients scaling exponentially in the sequence length (i.e., the vanishing/exploding gradients problemВ [32]).
To address this problem, the LSSL leveraged the HiPPO theory of continuous-time memorization [16].
HiPPO specifies a class of certain matrices рқ‘ЁвҲҲв„қNГ—Nрқ‘Ёsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\bm{A}\in\mathbbm{R}^{N\times N} that when incorporated into (1), allows the state xвҖӢ(t)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx(t) to memorize the history of the input uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t).
The most important matrix in this class is defined by equation (2),
which we will call the HiPPO matrix. For example, the LSSL found that simply modifying an SSM from a random matrix рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} to equation (2) improved its performance on the sequential MNIST benchmark from 60%percent6060\% to 98%percent9898\%.


 | 
 | (HiPPO Matrix)рқ‘ЁnвҖӢk=вҲ’{(2вҖӢn+1)1/2вҖӢ(2вҖӢk+1)1/2ifВ вҖӢn>kn+1ifВ вҖӢn=k0ifВ вҖӢn<k.
- HiPPO Matrixsubscriptрқ‘Ёрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳcasessuperscript
2рқ‘ӣ112superscript
2рқ‘ҳ112ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ
рқ‘ӣ1ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ0ifВ рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ(\text{{HiPPO Matrix}})\qquad\bm{A}_{nk}=-\begin{cases}(2n+1)^{1/2}(2k+1)^{1/2}&\mbox{if }n>k\\
n+1&\mbox{if }n=k\\
0&\mbox{if }n<k\end{cases}.

### 
2.3 Discrete-time SSM: The Recurrent Representation


To be applied on a discrete input sequence (u0,u1,вҖҰ)subscriptрқ‘ў0subscriptрқ‘ў1вҖҰ(u_{0},u_{1},\dots) instead of continuous function uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t),

Conceptually, the inputs uksubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳu_{k} can be viewed as sampling an implicit underlying continuous signal uвҖӢ(t)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ўu(t), where uk=uвҖӢ(kвҖӢО”)subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳО”u_{k}=u(k\Delta).

 | =рқ‘Ә.absentрқ‘Ә\displaystyle=\bm{C}.
 | 


Equation (3) is now a sequence-to-sequence map ukвҶҰykmaps-tosubscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳu_{k}\mapsto y_{k} instead of function-to-function.
Moreover the state equation is now a recurrence in xksubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳx_{k},
allowing the discrete SSM to be computed like an RNN.
Concretely, xkвҲҲв„қNsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғx_{k}\in\mathbbm{R}^{N} can be viewed as a hidden state with transition matrix рқ‘ЁВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё\bm{\overline{A}}.


Notationally, throughout this paper we use рқ‘ЁВҜ,рқ‘©ВҜ,вҖҰ
- bold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©вҖҰ\bm{\overline{A}},\bm{\overline{B}},\dots to denote discretized SSM matrices defined by (3). Note that these matrices are a function of both рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} as well as a step size О”О”\Delta; we suppress this dependence for notational convenience when it is clear.


### 
2.4 Training SSMs: The Convolutional Representation


The recurrent SSM (3) is not practical for training on modern hardware due to its sequentiality.
Instead,
there is a well-known connection between linear time-invariant (LTI) SSMs such as (1) and continuous convolutions.
Correspondingly, (3) can actually be written as a discrete convolution.


For simplicity let the initial state be xвҲ’1=0subscriptрқ‘Ҙ10x_{-1}=0.
Then unrolling (3) explicitly yields


 | 
 | x0subscriptрқ‘Ҙ0\displaystyle x_{0}
 | =рқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu0absentbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў0\displaystyle=\bm{\overline{B}}u_{0}
 | x1subscriptрқ‘Ҙ1\displaystyle x_{1}
 | =рқ‘ЁВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu0+рқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu1absent
bold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў0bold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў1\displaystyle=\bm{\overline{A}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{0}+\bm{\overline{B}}u_{1}
 | x2subscriptрқ‘Ҙ2\displaystyle x_{2}
 | =рқ‘ЁВҜ2вҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu0+рқ‘ЁВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu1+рқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu2absent
superscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё2bold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў0bold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў1bold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў2\displaystyle=\bm{\overline{A}}^{2}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{0}+\bm{\overline{A}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{1}+\bm{\overline{B}}u_{2}
 | вҖҰвҖҰ\displaystyle\dots
 | 


 | 
 | y0subscriptрқ‘Ұ0\displaystyle y_{0}
 | =рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu0absentbold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў0\displaystyle=\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{0}
 | y1subscriptрқ‘Ұ1\displaystyle y_{1}
 | =рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu0+рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu1absent
bold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў0bold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў1\displaystyle=\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{0}+\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{1}
 | y2subscriptрқ‘Ұ2\displaystyle y_{2}
 | =рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜ2вҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu0+рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu1+рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu2absent
bold-ВҜрқ‘Әsuperscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё2bold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў0bold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў1bold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў2\displaystyle=\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}^{2}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{0}+\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{1}+\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{2}
 | вҖҰвҖҰ\displaystyle\dots
 | 


This can be vectorized into a convolution (4) with an explicit formula for the convolution kernel (5).


 | 
 | yk=рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜkвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu0+рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜkвҲ’1вҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢu1+вӢҜ+рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢukвҲ’1+рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜвҖӢuky=рқ‘ІВҜвҲ—u.subscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҳ
bold-ВҜрқ‘Әsuperscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёрқ‘ҳbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў0bold-ВҜрқ‘Әsuperscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёрқ‘ҳ1bold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ў1вӢҜbold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳ1bold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘©subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘ҰвҲ—bold-ВҜрқ‘Ірқ‘ў\begin{split}y_{k}&=\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}^{k}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{0}+\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}^{k-1}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{1}+\dots+\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{k-1}+\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{B}}u_{k}\\
y&=\bm{\overline{K}}\ast u.\end{split}
 | 
 | (4)


 | 
 | рқ‘ІВҜвҲҲв„қL:=рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(рқ‘ЁВҜ,рқ‘©ВҜ,рқ‘ӘВҜ):=(рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜiвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜ)iвҲҲ[L]=(рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜ,рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜвҖӢрқ‘©ВҜ,вҖҰ,рқ‘ӘВҜвҖӢрқ‘ЁВҜLвҲ’1вҖӢрқ‘©ВҜ).bold-ВҜрқ‘Іsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝassignsubscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©bold-ВҜрқ‘Әassignsubscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Әsuperscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёрқ‘–bold-ВҜрқ‘©рқ‘–delimited-[]рқҗҝbold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘©bold-ВҜрқ‘Әbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©вҖҰbold-ВҜрқ‘Әsuperscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёрқҗҝ1bold-ВҜрқ‘©\bm{\overline{K}}\in\mathbb{R}^{L}:=\mathcal{K}_{L}(\bm{\overline{A}},\bm{\overline{B}},\bm{\overline{C}}):=\left(\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}^{i}\bm{\overline{B}}\right)_{i\in[L]}=(\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{B}},\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}\bm{\overline{B}},\dots,\bm{\overline{C}}\bm{\overline{A}}^{L-1}\bm{\overline{B}}).
 | 
 | (5)


In other words, equation (4) is a single (non-circular) convolution and can be computed very efficiently with FFTs, provided that рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} is known. However, computing рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} in (5) is non-trivial and is the focus of our technical contributions in SectionВ 3.
We call рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} the SSM convolution kernel or filter.


## 
3 Method: Structured State Spaces (S4)


Our technical results focus on developing the S4 parameterization and showing how to efficiently compute all views of the SSM (SectionВ 2):
the continuous representation (рқ‘Ё,рқ‘©,рқ‘Ә)рқ‘Ёрқ‘©рқ‘Ә(\bm{A},\bm{B},\bm{C}) (1),
the recurrent representation (рқ‘ЁВҜ,рқ‘©ВҜ,рқ‘ӘВҜ)bold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©bold-ВҜрқ‘Ә(\bm{\overline{A}},\bm{\overline{B}},\bm{\overline{C}}) (3),
and the convolutional representation рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} (4).


SectionВ 3.1 motivates our approach, which is based on the linear algebraic concepts of conjugation and diagonalization, and discusses why the naive application of this approach does not work.
SectionВ 3.2 gives an overview of the key technical components of our approach and formally defines the S4 parameterization.
SectionВ 3.3 sketches the main results, showing that S4 is asymptotically efficient (up to log factors) for sequence models.
Proofs are in AppendicesВ B andВ C.


### 
3.1 Motivation: Diagonalization


The fundamental bottleneck in computing the discrete-time SSM (3) is that it involves repeated matrix multiplication by рқ‘ЁВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё\bm{\overline{A}}.
For example, computing (5) naively as in the LSSL involves LрқҗҝL successive multiplications by рқ‘ЁВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё\bm{\overline{A}}, requiring OвҖӢ(N2вҖӢL)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ғ2рқҗҝO(N^{2}L) operations and OвҖӢ(NвҖӢL)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒрқҗҝO(NL) space.


To overcome this bottleneck, we use a structural result that allows us to simplify SSMs.


###### 
Lemma 3.1.


Conjugation is an equivalence relation on SSMs (рқҗҖ,рқҗҒ,рқҗӮ)вҲј(рқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҖвҖӢрқҗ•,рқҗ•вҲ’1вҖӢрқҗҒ,рқҗӮвҖӢрқҗ•)similar-toрқҗҖрқҗҒрқҗӮsuperscriptрқҗ•1рқҗҖрқҗ•superscriptрқҗ•1рқҗҒрқҗӮрқҗ•(\bm{A},\bm{B},\bm{C})\sim(\bm{V}^{-1}\bm{A}\bm{V},\bm{V}^{-1}\bm{B},\bm{C}\bm{V}).


###### Proof.


Write out the two SSMs with state denoted by xрқ‘Ҙx and x~~рқ‘Ҙ\tilde{x} respectively:


 | 
 | xвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ҘвҖІ\displaystyle x^{\prime}
 | =рқ‘ЁвҖӢx+рқ‘©вҖӢuabsent
рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ҙрқ‘©рқ‘ў\displaystyle=\bm{A}x+\bm{B}u
 | x~вҖІsuperscript~рқ‘ҘвҖІ\displaystyle\tilde{x}^{\prime}
 | =рқ‘ҪвҲ’1вҖӢрқ‘ЁвҖӢрқ‘ҪвҖӢx~+рқ‘ҪвҲ’1вҖӢрқ‘©вҖӢuabsent
superscriptрқ‘Ҫ1рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ҫ~рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ҫ1рқ‘©рқ‘ў\displaystyle=\bm{V}^{-1}\bm{A}\bm{V}\tilde{x}+\bm{V}^{-1}\bm{B}u
 | 


 | 
 | yрқ‘Ұ\displaystyle y
 | =рқ‘ӘвҖӢxabsentрқ‘Әрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=\bm{C}x
 | yрқ‘Ұ\displaystyle y
 | =рқ‘ӘвҖӢрқ‘ҪвҖӢx~absentрқ‘Әрқ‘Ҫ~рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle=\bm{C}\bm{V}\tilde{x}
 | 


After multiplying the right side SSM by рқ‘Ҫрқ‘Ҫ\bm{V}, the two SSMs become identical with x=рқ‘ҪвҖӢx~рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҫ~рқ‘Ҙx=\bm{V}\tilde{x}.
Therefore these compute the exact same operator uвҶҰymaps-toрқ‘ўрқ‘Ұu\mapsto y, but with a change of basis by рқ‘Ҫрқ‘Ҫ\bm{V} in the state xрқ‘Ҙx.
вҲҺ


LemmaВ 3.1 motivates putting рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} into a canonical form by conjugation222Note that although we ultimately require рқ‘ЁВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё\bm{\overline{A}}, conjugation commutes with discretization so we refer to рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A}., which is ideally more structured and allows faster computation.
For example, if рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} were diagonal, the resulting computations become much more tractable.
In particular, the desired рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} (equation (4)) would be a Vandermonde product which theoretically only needs OвҖӢ((N+L)вҖӢlog2вҒЎ(N+L))рқ‘Ӯ
рқ‘Ғрқҗҝsuperscript2
рқ‘ҒрқҗҝO((N+L)\log^{2}(N+L)) arithmetic operations [29].


Unfortunately, the naive application of diagonalization does not work due to numerical issues.
Werive the explicit diagonalization for the HiPPO matrix (2) and show it has entries exponentially large in the state size Nрқ‘ҒN, rendering the diagonalization numerically infeasible (e.g. рқ‘ӘвҖӢрқ‘Ҫрқ‘Әрқ‘Ҫ\bm{C}\bm{V} in LemmaВ 3.1 would not be computable).
We note that Gu etВ al. [18] proposed a different (unimplemented) algorithm to compute рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} faster than the naive algorithm.
In AppendixВ B, we prove that it is also numerically unstable for related reasons.


###### 
Lemma 3.2.


The HiPPO matrix рқҗҖрқҗҖ\bm{A} in equation (2) is diagonalized by the matrix рқҗ•iвҖӢj=(i+jiвҲ’j)subscriptрқҗ•рқ‘–рқ‘—binomial
рқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘–рқ‘—\bm{V}_{ij}=\binom{i+j}{i-j}.
In particular, рқҗ•3вҖӢi,i=(4вҖӢi2вҖӢi)вүҲ24вҖӢisubscriptрқҗ•
- 3рқ‘–рқ‘–binomial4рқ‘–2рқ‘–superscript24рқ‘–\bm{V}_{3i,i}=\binom{4i}{2i}\approx 2^{4i}.
Therefore рқҗ•рқҗ•\bm{V} has entries of magnitude up to 24вҖӢN/3superscript24рқ‘Ғ32^{4N/3}.


### 
3.2 The S4 Parameterization: Normal Plus Low-Rank


The previous discussion implies that we should only conjugate by well-conditioned matrices рқ‘Ҫрқ‘Ҫ\bm{V}.
The ideal scenario is when the matrix рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} is diagonalizable by a perfectly conditioned (i.e., unitary) matrix.
By the Spectral Theorem of linear algebra, this is exactly the class of normal matrices.
However, this class of matrices is restrictive; in particular, it does not contain the HiPPO matrix (2).


Algorithm 1 S4 Convolution Kernel (Sketch)


1:S4 parameters рқҡІ,рқ‘·,рқ‘ё,рқ‘©,рқ‘ӘвҲҲв„ӮN
- рқҡІрқ‘·рқ‘ёрқ‘©рқ‘Әsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғ\bm{\Lambda},\bm{P},\bm{Q},\bm{B},\bm{C}\in\mathbbm{C}^{N} and step size О”О”\Delta


2:SSM convolution kernel рқ‘ІВҜ=рқ’ҰLвҖӢ(рқ‘ЁВҜ,рқ‘©ВҜ,рқ‘ӘВҜ)bold-ВҜрқ‘Іsubscriptрқ’Ұрқҗҝbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёbold-ВҜрқ‘©bold-ВҜрқ‘Ә\bm{\overline{K}}=\mathcal{K}_{L}(\bm{\overline{A}},\bm{\overline{B}},\bm{\overline{C}}) for рқ‘Ё=рқҡІвҲ’рқ‘·вҖӢрқ‘ёвҲ—рқ‘ЁрқҡІрқ‘·superscriptрқ‘ё\bm{A}=\bm{\Lambda}-\bm{P}\bm{Q}^{*} (equation (5))


3:рқ‘Ә~вҶҗ(рқ‘°вҲ’рқ‘ЁВҜL)вҲ—вҖӢрқ‘ӘВҜвҶҗbold-~рқ‘Әsuperscriptрқ‘°superscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ёрқҗҝbold-ВҜрқ‘Ә\bm{\widetilde{C}}\leftarrow\left(\bm{I}-\bm{\overline{A}}^{L}\right)^{*}\bm{\overline{C}}
в–·в–·\triangleright Truncate SSM generating function (SSMGF) to length LрқҗҝL


4:[k00вҖӢ(Пү)k01вҖӢ(Пү)k10вҖӢ(Пү)k11вҖӢ(Пү)]вҶҗ[рқ‘Ә~вҖӢрқ‘ё]вҲ—вҖӢ(2О”вҖӢ1вҲ’Пү1+ПүвҲ’рқҡІ)вҲ’1вҖӢ[рқ‘©вҖӢрқ‘·]вҶҗmatrixsubscriptрқ‘ҳ00рқң”subscriptрқ‘ҳ01рқң”subscriptрқ‘ҳ10рқң”subscriptрқ‘ҳ11рқң”superscriptdelimited-[]bold-~рқ‘Әрқ‘ёsuperscript2О”1рқң”
1рқң”рқҡІ1delimited-[]рқ‘©рқ‘·\begin{bmatrix}k_{00}(\omega)&k_{01}(\omega)\\
k_{10}(\omega)&k_{11}(\omega)\end{bmatrix}\leftarrow\left[\bm{\widetilde{C}}\;\bm{Q}\right]^{*}\left(\frac{2}{\Delta}\frac{1-\omega}{1+\omega}-\bm{\Lambda}\right)^{-1}\left[\bm{B}\;\bm{P}\right]

в–·в–·\triangleright Black-box Cauchy kernel


5:рқ‘І^вҖӢ(Пү)вҶҗ21+ПүвҖӢ[k00вҖӢ(Пү)вҲ’k01вҖӢ(Пү)вҖӢ(1+k11вҖӢ(Пү))вҲ’1вҖӢk10вҖӢ(Пү)]вҶҗbold-^рқ‘Ірқң”2
1рқң”delimited-[]subscriptрқ‘ҳ00рқң”subscriptрқ‘ҳ01рқң”superscript
1subscriptрқ‘ҳ11рқң”1subscriptрқ‘ҳ10рқң”\bm{\hat{K}}(\omega)\leftarrow\frac{2}{1+\omega}\left[k_{00}(\omega)-k_{01}(\omega)(1+k_{11}(\omega))^{-1}k_{10}(\omega)\right]
в–·в–·\triangleright Woodbury Identity 


6:рқ‘І^={рқ‘І^вҖӢ(Пү):Пү=expвҒЎ(2вҖӢПҖвҖӢiвҖӢkL)}bold-^рқ‘Іconditional-setbold-^рқ‘Ірқң”рқң”2рқңӢрқ‘–рқ‘ҳрқҗҝ\bm{\hat{K}}=\{\bm{\hat{K}}(\omega):\omega=\exp(2\pi i\frac{k}{L})\}
в–·в–·\triangleright Evaluate SSMGF at all roots of unity ПүвҲҲО©Lрқң”subscriptО©рқҗҝ\omega\in\Omega_{L}


7:рқ‘ІВҜвҶҗрқ—Ӯрқ–Ҙрқ–Ҙрқ–івҖӢ(рқ‘І^)вҶҗbold-ВҜрқ‘Ірқ—Ӯрқ–Ҙрқ–Ҙрқ–іbold-^рқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}}\leftarrow\mathsf{iFFT}(\bm{\hat{K}})
в–·в–·\triangleright Inverse Fourier Transform


We make the observation that although the HiPPO matrix is not normal, it can be decomposed as the sum of a normal and low-rank matrix.
However, this is still not useful by itself:
unlike a diagonal matrix, powering up this sum (in (5)) is still slow and not easily optimized.
We overcome this bottleneck by simultaneously applying three new techniques.


- 
вҖў 


Instead of computing рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} directly,
we compute its spectrum by evaluating its truncated generating function вҲ‘j=0LвҲ’1рқ‘ІВҜjвҖӢО¶jsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—0рқҗҝ1subscriptbold-ВҜрқ‘Ірқ‘—superscriptрқңҒрқ‘—\sum_{j=0}^{L-1}\bm{\overline{K}}_{j}\zeta^{j} at the roots of unity О¶рқңҒ\zeta.
рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}} can then be found by applying an inverse FFT.


- 
вҖў 


This generating function is closely related to the matrix resolvent, and now involves a matrix inverse instead of power.
The low-rank term can now be corrected by applying the Woodbury identity which reduces (рқ‘Ё+рқ‘·вҖӢрқ‘ёвҲ—)вҲ’1superscript
рқ‘Ёрқ‘·superscriptрқ‘ё1(\bm{A}+\bm{P}\bm{Q}^{*})^{-1} in terms of рқ‘ЁвҲ’1superscriptрқ‘Ё1\bm{A}^{-1}, truly reducing to the diagonal case.


- 
вҖў 


Finally, we show that the diagonal matrix case is equivalent to the computation of a Cauchy kernel 1ПүjвҲ’О¶k1subscriptрқң”рқ‘—subscriptрқңҒрқ‘ҳ\frac{1}{\omega_{j}-\zeta_{k}}, a well-studied problem with stable near-linear algorithms [31, 30].


Our techniques apply to any matrix that can be decomposed as Normal Plus Low-Rank (NPLR).


###### 
Theorem 1.


All HiPPO matrices from [16] have a NPLR representation


 | 
 | рқ‘Ё=рқ‘ҪвҖӢрқҡІвҖӢрқ‘ҪвҲ—вҲ’рқ‘·вҖӢрқ‘ёвҠӨ=рқ‘ҪвҖӢ(рқҡІвҲ’(рқ‘ҪвҲ—вҖӢрқ‘·)вҖӢ(рқ‘ҪвҲ—вҖӢрқ‘ё)вҲ—)вҖӢрқ‘ҪвҲ—рқ‘Ёрқ‘ҪрқҡІsuperscriptрқ‘Ҫрқ‘·superscriptрқ‘ёtopрқ‘ҪрқҡІsuperscriptрқ‘Ҫрқ‘·superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ҫрқ‘ёsuperscriptрқ‘Ҫ\bm{A}=\bm{V}\bm{\Lambda}\bm{V}^{*}-\bm{P}\bm{Q}^{\top}=\bm{V}\left(\bm{\Lambda}-\left(\bm{V}^{*}\bm{P}\right)(\bm{V}^{*}\bm{Q})^{*}\right)\bm{V}^{*}
 | 
 | (6)


for unitary рқҗ•вҲҲв„ӮNГ—Nрқҗ•superscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ғ\bm{V}\in\mathbbm{C}^{N\times N}, diagonal рқҡІрқҡІ\bm{\Lambda}, and low-rank factorization рқҗҸ,рқҗҗвҲҲв„қNГ—r
- рқҗҸрқҗҗsuperscriptв„қрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹ\bm{P},\bm{Q}\in\mathbbm{R}^{N\times r}.
These matrices HiPPO- LegS, LegT, LagT all satisfy r=1рқ‘ҹ1r=1 or r=2рқ‘ҹ2r=2.
In particular, equation (2) is NPLR with r=1рқ‘ҹ1r=1.


### 
3.3 S4 Algorithms and Computational Complexity


By equation (6), note that NPLR matrices can be conjugated into diagonal plus low-rank (DPLR) form
(now over в„Ӯв„Ӯ\mathbbm{C} instead of в„қв„қ\mathbbm{R}).
TheoremsВ 2 andВ 3 describe the complexities of SSMs where рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} is in DPLR form.
S4 is optimal or near-optimal for both recurrent and convolutional representations.


###### 
Theorem 2 (S4 Recurrence).


Given any step size О”О”\Delta, computing one step of the recurrence (3) can be done in OвҖӢ(N)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒO(N) operations where Nрқ‘ҒN is the state size.


TheoremВ 2 follows from the fact that the inverse of a DPLR matrix is also DPLR (e.g. also by the Woodbury identity).
This implies that the discretized matrix рқ‘ЁВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё\bm{\overline{A}} is the product of two DPLR matrices and thus has OвҖӢ(N)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒO(N) matrix-vector multiplication.
SectionВ C.2 computes рқ‘ЁВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘Ё\bm{\overline{A}} in closed DPLR form.


###### 
Theorem 3 (S4 Convolution).


Given any step size О”О”\Delta, computing the SSM convolution filter рқҗҠВҜbold-ВҜрқҗҠ\bm{\overline{K}} can be reduced to 4 Cauchy multiplies,
requiring only
O~вҖӢ(N+L)~рқ‘Ӯ
рқ‘Ғрқҗҝ\widetilde{O}(N+L) operations and OвҖӢ(N+L)рқ‘Ӯ
рқ‘ҒрқҗҝO(N+L) space.


AppendixВ C, DefinitionВ 3 formally defines Cauchy matrices, which are related to rational interpolation problems.
Computing with Cauchy matrices is an extremely well-studied problem in numerical analysis,
with both fast arithmetic and numerical algorithms based on the famous Fast Multipole Method (FMM)
[29, 31, 30].
The computational complexities of these algorithms under various settings are described in AppendixВ C, PropositionВ 5.


We reiterate that TheoremВ 3 is our core technical contribution,
and its algorithm is the very motivation of the NPLR S4 parameterization.
This algorithm is formally sketched in AlgorithmВ 1.


### 
3.4 Architecture Details of the Deep S4 Layer


Concretely, an S4 layer is parameterized as follows.
First initialize a SSM with рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} set to the HiPPO matrix (2).
By LemmaВ 3.1 and TheoremВ 1,
this SSM is unitarily equivalent to some (рқҡІвҲ’рқ‘·вҖӢрқ‘ёвҲ—,рқ‘©,рқ‘Ә)рқҡІрқ‘·superscriptрқ‘ёрқ‘©рқ‘Ә(\bm{\Lambda}-\bm{P}\bm{Q}^{*},\bm{B},\bm{C}) for some diagonal рқҡІрқҡІ\bm{\Lambda} and vectors рқ‘·,рқ‘ё,рқ‘©,рқ‘ӘвҲҲв„ӮNГ—1
- рқ‘·рқ‘ёрқ‘©рқ‘Әsuperscriptв„Ӯрқ‘Ғ1\bm{P},\bm{Q},\bm{B},\bm{C}\in\mathbbm{C}^{N\times 1}.
These comprise S4вҖҷs 5вҖӢN5рқ‘Ғ5N trainable parameters.


The overall deep neural network (DNN) architecture of S4 is similar to prior work.
As defined above,
S4 defines a map from в„қLвҶ’в„қLвҶ’superscriptв„қрқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқҗҝ\mathbbm{R}^{L}\to\mathbbm{R}^{L}, i.e. a 1-D sequence map.
Typically, DNNs operate on feature maps of size Hрқҗ»H instead of 111.
S4 handles multiple features by simply defining Hрқҗ»H independent copies of itself, and then mixing the Hрқҗ»H features with a position-wise linear layer for a total of OвҖӢ(H2)+OвҖӢ(HвҖӢN)
рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқҗ»2рқ‘Ӯрқҗ»рқ‘ҒO(H^{2})+O(HN) parameters per layer.
Nonlinear activation functions are also inserted between these layers.
Overall, S4 defines a sequence-to-sequence map of shape (batch size, sequence length, hidden dimension), exactly the same as related sequence models such as Transformers, RNNs, and CNNs.


Table 1: 
Complexity of various sequence models in terms of sequence length (рқ‘ірқ‘і\bm{L}), batch size (рқ‘©рқ‘©\bm{B}), and hidden dimension (рқ‘Ҝрқ‘Ҝ\bm{H});
tildes denote log factors.
Metrics are parameter count, training computation, training space requirement, training parallelizability, and inference computation (for 1 sample and time-step).
For simplicity, the state size Nрқ‘ҒN of S4 is tied to Hрқҗ»H.
Bold denotes model is theoretically best for that metric.
Convolutions are efficient for training while recurrence is efficient for inference, while SSMs combine the strengths of both.

 | Yes


 | Inference
 | LвҖӢH2рқҗҝsuperscriptрқҗ»2LH^{2}
 | рқ‘Ҝрқҹҗsuperscriptрқ‘Ҝ2\bm{H^{2}}
 | L2вҖӢH+H2вҖӢL
superscriptрқҗҝ2рқҗ»superscriptрқҗ»2рқҗҝL^{2}H+H^{2}L
 | рқ‘Ҝрқҹҗsuperscriptрқ‘Ҝ2\bm{H^{2}}


Note that the core S4 module is a linear transformation, but the addition of non-linear transformations through the depth of the network makes the overall deep SSM non-linear.
This is analogous to a vanilla CNN, since convolutional layers are also linear.
The broadcasting across Hрқҗ»H hidden features described in this section is also analogous to depthwise-separable convolutions.
Thus, the overall deep S4 model is closely related to a depthwise-separable CNN but with global convolution kernels.


Finally, we note that follow-up work found that this version of S4 can sometimes suffer from numerical instabilities when the рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} matrix has eigenvalues on the right half-plane [14].
It introduced a slight change to the NPLR parameterization for S4 from рқҡІвҲ’рқ‘·вҖӢрқ‘ёвҲ—рқҡІрқ‘·superscriptрқ‘ё\bm{\Lambda}-\bm{P}\bm{Q}^{*} to рқҡІвҲ’рқ‘·вҖӢрқ‘·вҲ—рқҡІрқ‘·superscriptрқ‘·\bm{\Lambda}-\bm{P}\bm{P}^{*} that corrects this potential problem.


TableВ 1 compares the complexities of the most common deep sequence modeling mechanisms.


## 
4 Experiments


SectionВ 4.1 benchmarks S4 against the LSSL and efficient Transformer models.
SectionВ 4.2 validates S4 on LRDs: the LRA benchmark and raw speech classification.
SectionВ 4.3 investigates whether S4 can be used as a general sequence model to perform effectively and efficiently in a wide variety of settings including image classification, image and text generation, and time series forecasting.


### 
4.1 S4 Efficiency Benchmarks


We benchmark that S4 can be trained quickly and efficiently, both compared to the LSSL, as well as efficient Transformer variants designed for long-range sequence modeling.
As outlined in SectionВ 3, S4 is theoretically much more efficient than the LSSL, and Fig.В 3 confirms that the S4 is orders of magnitude more speed- and memory-efficient for practical layer sizes.
In fact, S4вҖҷs speed and memory use is competitive with the most efficient Transformer variants benchmarked by Tay etВ al. [40]вҖ”Linear TransformerВ [22] and PerformerВ [8]вҖ”in a parameter-matched setting (Fig.В 3, following the protocol of Tay etВ al. [40]).

### 
4.2 Learning Long Range Dependencies


As described in SectionsВ 2.2 andВ 3.1, S4 uses a principled approach to address LRDs based on the HiPPO theory of continuous-time memorization.
Our goal in this section is to validate that S4 achieves high performance on difficult tasks that require long-range reasoning.
We focus here on two problems:
(i) the Long-Range Arena, a well-known benchmark designed to test efficient sequence models on LRDs, and
(ii) a speech classification problem as a real-world test of LRDs.

(Left) Example from the Path-X task, which involves deducing if the markers are connected by a path (Top) Filters from the first layer (Bottom) Filters from the last layer.

Long Range Arena (LRA).
LRAВ [40] contains 666 tasks with lengths 1K-16K steps, encompassing modalities and objectives that require similarity, structural, and visuospatial reasoning.
TableВ 2 compares S4 against the 11 Transformer variants from Tay etВ al. [40] as well as follow-up work.
S4 substantially advances the SoTA, outperforming all baselines on all tasks and averaging 80.48%percent80.4880.48\% compared to less than 60%percent6060\% for every baseline.
Notably, S4 solves the Path-X task, an extremely challenging task
that involves reasoning about LRDs over sequences of length 128Г—128=1638412812816384128\times 128=16384.
All previous models have failed (i.e.В random guessing) due to memory or computation bottlenecks, or simply being unable to learn such long dependencies.


We analyze S4вҖҷs performance on Path-X by visualizing its learned representations,
in particular 1-D convolution kernels рқ‘ІВҜbold-ВҜрқ‘І\bm{\overline{K}}
which are the focus of our technical results in SectionВ 3.
Fig.В 4 shows that S4 learns a variety of filters that display spatially consistent structure
and demonstrate awareness of the 2-D nature of the data.
In particular, the lower layers learn simple kernels that extract features from just a few rows of local context while ignoring the rest of the image.
On the other hand, higher layers aggregate information globally across full columns of the image at varying spatial frequencies.
Filters in these higher layers span the entire context (163841638416384 pixels), confirming S4вҖҷs ability to learn LRDs.

Raw Speech Classification.
Speech is a typical real-world time series domain, involving signals sampled from an underlying physical process at high frequency.
We perform speech classification using the SC10 subset of the Speech Commands datasetВ [47] (see SectionВ D.5).
While most sequence models for speech rely on extensive preprocessing (e.g. to MFCC features), we classify raw speech (length-160001600016000) followingВ Romero etВ al. [35].
S4 achieves 98.3%percent98.398.3\% accuracy, higher than all baselines that use the 100Г—100\times shorter MFCC features, and validates that a powerful LRD model is able to extract more information from the raw data and outperform hand-crafted pre-processing.
Additionally, we include a baseline CNN specifically designed for raw speech, the discriminator from the WaveGAN modelВ [11],
which performs worse than S4 while having 90Г—90\times more parameters and incorporating many more
architectural heuristics (SectionВ D.2).


### 
4.3 S4 as a General Sequence Model


A key goal of sequence modeling research is to develop a single model that can be applied in many domains (e.g. images, audio, text, time-series) with a broad range of capabilities (e.g. efficient training, fast generation, handling irregularly sampled data).
As a fundamental scientific model, SSMs are a promising candidate that come with a range of capabilities, and S4вҖҷs strong results on LRD benchmarks spanning images, text, and speech are evidence of S4вҖҷs potential as a general sequence model.
In this section, we focus on understanding this question in more depth by highlighting key strengths of S4 in settings that usually require specialized models.
The tasks we focus on (generative modeling, image classification, time-series forecasting)
are considered as LRD tasks in the literature,
and serve as additional validation that S4 handles LRDs efficiently.


Large-scale generative modeling.
We investigate two well-studied image and text benchmarks to validate the scalability, flexibility, and efficiency of S4.
These tasks require much larger models than our previous tasks вҖ“ up to 250250250M parameters.


First, CIFAR density estimation is a popular benchmark for autoregressive models, where images are flattened into a sequence of 307230723072 RGB subpixels that are predicted one by one.
Fig.В 8 shows that with no 2D inductive bias, S4 is competitive with the best models designed for this task.


Second, WikiText-103 is an established benchmark for language modeling, an important task for large-scale sequence models where tokens are predicted sequentially based on past context.
Although RNNs were the model of choice for many years, Transformers are now the dominant model in such applications that contain data that is inherently discrete.
We show that alternative models to Transformers can still be competitive in these settings.
By simply taking a strong Transformer baseline [2] and replacing the self-attention layers,
S4 substantially closes the gap to Transformers (within 0.80.80.8 ppl), setting SoTA for attention-free models by over 222 ppl.


Fast autoregressive inference.
A prominent limitation of autoregressive models is inference speed (e.g. generation),
since they require a pass over the full context for every new sample.
Several methods have been specifically crafted to overcome this limitation such as the Linear Transformer, a hybrid Transformer/RNN that switches to a stateful, recurrent view at inference time for speed.


As a stateful model, SSMs automatically have this ability (Fig.В 1).
By switching to its recurrent representation (SectionВ 2.3),
S4 requires constant memory and computation per time step вҖ“ in contrast to standard autoregressive models which scale in the context length.
On both CIFAR-10 and WikiText-103,
we report the throughput of various models at generation time, with S4 around 60Г—60\times faster than a vanilla Transformer on both tasks (details in SectionВ D.3.3).


Sampling resolution change.
As a continuous-time model, S4 automatically adapts to data sampled at different rates,
a challenging setting for time series with a dedicated line of work [37, 10, 35].
Without re-training, S4 achieves 96.3%percent96.396.3\% accuracy at 0.5Г—0.5\times the frequency on Speech Commands 10 (Fig.В 6), simply by changing its internal step size О”О”\Delta (SectionВ 2.3).


Figure 7: 
(CIFAR-10 density estimation)
As a generic sequence model, S4 is competitive with previous autoregressive models (in bits per dim.) while incorporating no 2D inductive bias,
and has fast generation through its recurrence mode.

 | 0.624В В 0.645
 | 7.019В В 5.105
 | 1.545В В 1.006
 | 0.657В В 0.683
 | 1.370В В 0.982
 | 6.901В В 4.264


Learning with weaker inductive bias.
Beyond our results on speech (SectionВ 4.2), we further validate that S4 can be applied with minimal modifications
on two domains that typically require specialized domain-specific preprocessing and architectures.
First, we compare S4 to the InformerВ [50],
a new Transformer architecture that uses a complex encoder-decoder designed for time-series forecasting problems.
A simple application of S4 that treats forecasting as a masked sequence-to-sequence transformation (Fig.В 11)
outperforms the Informer and other baselines on 40/50405040/50 settings across 555 forecasting tasks.
Notably, S4 is better on the longest setting in each task, e.g.В reducing MSE by 37%percent3737\% when forecasting 303030 days of weather data
(TableВ 3).


Finally,
we evaluate S4 on pixel-level sequential image classification tasks (Fig.В 6),
popular benchmarks which were originally LRD tests for RNNsВ [1].
Beyond LRDs, these benchmarks point to a recent effort of the ML community to solve vision
problems with reduced domain knowledge,
in the spirit of models such as Vision Transformers [12] and MLP-Mixer [41] which involve patch-based models that without 2-D inductive bias.
Sequential CIFAR is a particularly challenging dataset where outside of SSMs, all sequence models have a gap of over 25%percent2525\% to a simple 2-D CNN.
By contrast, S4 is competitive with a larger ResNet18 (7.9M vs. 11.0M parameters), both with (93.16% vs. 95.62%) or without (91.12% vs. 89.46%) data augmentation.
Moreover, it is much more robust to other architectural choices (e.g.В 90.46% vs. 79.52% when swapping BatchNorm for LayerNorm).


### 
4.4 SSM Ablations: the Importance of HiPPO


A critical motivation of S4 was the use of the HiPPO matrices to initialize an SSM.
We consider several simplifications of S4 to ablate the importance of each of these components, including:


(i) how important is the HiPPO initialization?

(ii) how important is training the SSM on top of HiPPO?

(iii) are the benefits of S4 captured by the NPLR parameterization without HiPPO?


As a simple testbed, all experiments in this section were performed on the sequential CIFAR-10 task,
whicih we found transferred well to other settings.
Models were constrained to at most 100K trainable parameters and trained with a simple plateau learning rate scheduler and no regularization.


##### Unconstrained SSMs.


We first investigate generic SSMs with various initializations.
We consider a random Gaussian initialization (with variance scaled down until it did not NaN), and the HiPPO initialization.
We also consider a random diagonal Gaussian matrix as a potential structured method; parameterizing рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} as a diagonal matrix would allow substantial speedups without going through the complexity of S4вҖҷs NPLR parameterization.
We consider both freezing the рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} matrix and training it.


Fig.В 9 shows both training and validation curves, from which we can make several observations.
First, training the SSM improved all methods, particularly the randomly initialized ones.
For all methods, training the SSM led to improvements in both training and validation curves.


Second, a large generalization gap exists between the initializations.
In particular, note that when рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} is trained, all initializations are able to reach perfect training accuracy.
However, their validation accuracies are separated by over 15%percent1515\%.


Figure 9: CIFAR-10 classification with unconstrained, real-valued SSMs with various initializations. (Left) Train accuracy. (Right) Validation accuracy.


##### NPLR SSMs.


The previous experiment validates the importance of HiPPO in SSMs.
This was the main motivation of the NPLR algorithm in S4,
which utilizes structure of the HiPPO matrix (2) to make SSMs computationally feasible.
Fig.В 10(a) shows that random NPLR matrices still do not perform well,
which validates that S4вҖҷs effectiveness primarily comes from the HiPPO initialization, not the NPLR parameterization.


(a) 


(b) 


Figure 10: 
CIFAR-10 validation accuracy of SSMs with different initializations and parameterizations.
(Left) NPLR parameterization with random versus HiPPO initialization.
(Right) All methods considered in this section, including minor Dropout regularization. S4 achieves SotA accuracy on sequential CIFAR-10 with just 100K parameters.


Finally, Fig.В 10(b) considers the main ablations considered in this section (with trainable SSMs) and adds minor regularization.
With 0.1 Dropout, the same trends still hold, and the HiPPO initializationвҖ”in other words, the full S4 methodвҖ”achieves 84.27%percent84.2784.27\% test accuracy with just 100K parameters.


## 
5 Conclusion


We introduce S4, a sequence model that uses a new parameterization for the state space modelвҖҷs continuous-time, recurrent, and convolutional views to efficiently model LRDs in a principled manner.
Results across established benchmarks evaluating a diverse range of data modalities and model capabilities
suggest that S4 has the potential to be an effective general sequence modeling solution.

## 
Appendix A Discussion


##### Related Work.


Our work is most closely related to a line of work originally motivated by a particular biologically-inspired SSM, which led to mathematical models for addressing LRDs. Voelker [46], Voelker etВ al. [45] derived a non-trainable SSM motivated from approximating a neuromorphic spiking model, and Chilkuri and Eliasmith [7] showed that it could be sped up at train time with a convolutional view.
Gu etВ al. [16] extended this special case to a general continuous-time function approximation framework with several more special cases of рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} matrices designed for long-range dependencies.
However, instead of using a true SSM, all of these works fixed a choice of рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} and built RNNs around it.
Most recently, Gu etВ al. [18] used the full (1) explicitly as a deep SSM model, exploring new conceptual views of SSMs, as well as allowing рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} to be trained.
As mentioned in SectionВ 1, their method used a naive instantiation of SSMs that suffered from an additional factor of Nрқ‘ҒN in memory and N2superscriptрқ‘Ғ2N^{2} in computation.


Beyond this work, our technical contributions (SectionВ 3) on the S4 parameterization and algorithms are applicable to a broader family of SSMs including these investigated in prior works,
and our techniques for working with these models may be of independent interest.


##### Implementation.


The computational core of S4вҖҷs training algorithm is the Cauchy kernel discussed in SectionsВ 3.2, 3.3 andВ C.3.
As described in SectionВ C.3 PropositionВ 5,
there are many algorithms for it with differing computational complexities and sophistication.
Our current implementation of S4 actually uses the naive OвҖӢ(NвҖӢL)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҒрқҗҝO(NL) algorithm
which is easily parallelized on GPUs and has more easily accessible libraries allowing it to be implemented;
we leverage the pykeops library for memory-efficient kernel operations.
However, this library is a much more general library that may not be optimized for the Cauchy kernels used here,
and we believe that a dedicated CUDA implementation can be more efficient.
Additionally, as discussed in this work, there are asymptotically faster and numerically stable algorithms for the Cauchy kernel (PropositionВ 5).
However, these algorithms are currently not implemented for GPUs due to a lack of previous applications that require them.
We believe that more efficient implementations of these self-contained computational kernels are possible,
and that S4 (and SSMs at large) may have significant room for further improvements in efficiency.


##### Limitations and Future Directions.


In this work, we show that S4 can address a wide variety of data effectively.
However, it may not necessarily be the most suitable model for all types of data.
For example, Fig.В 8 still found a gap compared to Transformers for language modeling.
An interesting future direction is exploring combinations of S4 with other sequence models to complement their strengths.
We are excited about other directions, including continuing to explore the benefits of S4 on audio data (e.g. pre-training or generation settings),
and generalizing HiPPO and S4 to higher-dimensional data for image and video applications.


## 
Appendix B Numerical Instability of LSSL


This section proves the claims made in SectionВ 3.1 about prior work.
We first derive the explicit diagonalization of the HiPPO matrix, confirming its instability because of exponentially large entries.
We then discuss the proposed theoretically fast algorithm from [18] (Theorem 2) and show that it also involves exponentially large terms and thus cannot be implemented.


###

If k<jрқ‘ҳрқ‘—k<j, then for all iрқ‘–i inside the sum, either k<iрқ‘ҳрқ‘–k<i or i<jрқ‘–рқ‘—i<j.
In the first case рқ‘ЁkвҖӢi=0subscriptрқ‘Ёрқ‘ҳрқ‘–0\bm{A}_{ki}=0 and in the second case рқ’—i(j)=0subscriptsuperscriptрқ’—рқ‘—рқ‘–0\bm{v}^{(j)}_{i}=0,
so both sides of equation (7) are equal to 00.


It remains to show the case kвүҘjрқ‘ҳрқ‘—k\geq j, which proceeds by induction on kрқ‘ҳk.
Expanding equation (7) using the formula for рқ‘Ёрқ‘Ё\bm{A} yields


 | 
 | (рқ‘ЁвҖӢрқ’—)k(j)=вҲ‘iрқ‘ЁkвҖӢiвҖӢрқ’—i(j)=вҲ‘i=jkвҲ’1(вҲ’1)kвҲ’iвҖӢ(2вҖӢi+1)вҖӢ(i+j2вҖӢj)+(k+1)вҖӢ(k+j2вҖӢj).subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ёрқ’—рқ‘—рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ёрқ‘ҳрқ‘–subscriptsuperscriptрқ’—рқ‘—рқ‘–
