# The Rust Programming Language вҖ” The Slice Type

## The Slice Type

_Slices_ let you reference a contiguous sequence of elements in a
[collection](ch08-00-common-collections.md). A slice is a kind
of reference, so it does not have ownership.

HereвҖҷs a small programming problem: Write a function that takes a string of
words separated by spaces and returns the first word it finds in that string.
If the function doesnвҖҷt find a space in the string, the whole string must be
one word, so the entire string should be returned.

> Note: For the purposes of introducing slices, we are assuming ASCII only in
> this section; a more thorough discussion of UTF-8 handling is in the
> [вҖңStoring UTF-8 Encoded Text with StringsвҖқ][strings] section
> of Chapter 8.

LetвҖҷs work through how weвҖҷd write the signature of this function without using
slices, to understand the problem that slices will solve:

```rust,ignore
fn first_word(s: &String) -> ?
```

The `first_word` function has a parameter of type `&String`. We donвҖҷt need
ownership, so this is fine. (In idiomatic Rust, functions do not take ownership
of their arguments unless they need to, and the reasons for that will become
clear as we keep going.) But what should we return? We donвҖҷt really have a way
to talk about *part* of a string. However, we could return the index of the end
of the word, indicated by a space. LetвҖҷs try that, as shown in Listing 4-7.

<Listing number="4-7" file-name="src/main.rs" caption="The `first_word` function that returns a byte index value into the `String` parameter">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

Because we need to go through the `String` element by element and check whether
a value is a space, weвҖҷll convert our `String` to an array of bytes using the
`as_bytes` method.

```rust,ignore
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

Next, we create an iterator over the array of bytes using the `iter` method:

```rust,ignore
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

WeвҖҷll discuss iterators in more detail in [Chapter 13][ch13].
For now, know that `iter` is a method that returns each element in a collection
and that `enumerate` wraps the result of `iter` and returns each element as
part of a tuple instead. The first element of the tuple returned from
`enumerate` is the index, and the second element is a reference to the element.
This is a bit more convenient than calculating the index ourselves.

Because the `enumerate` method returns a tuple, we can use patterns to
destructure that tuple. WeвҖҷll be discussing patterns more in [Chapter
6][ch6]. In the `for` loop, we specify a pattern that has `i`
for the index in the tuple and `&item` for the single byte in the tuple.
Because we get a reference to the element from `.iter().enumerate()`, we use
`&` in the pattern.

Inside the `for` loop, we search for the byte that represents the space by
using the byte literal syntax. If we find a space, we return the position.
Otherwise, we return the length of the string by using `s.len()`.

```rust,ignore
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

We now have a way to find out the index of the end of the first word in the
string, but thereвҖҷs a problem. WeвҖҷre returning a `usize` on its own, but itвҖҷs
only a meaningful number in the context of the `&String`. In other words,
because itвҖҷs a separate value from the `String`, thereвҖҷs no guarantee that it
will still be valid in the future. Consider the program in Listing 4-8 that
uses the `first_word` function from Listing 4-7.

<Listing number="4-8" file-name="src/main.rs" caption="Storing the result from calling the `first_word` function and then changing the `String` contents">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

This program compiles without any errors and would also do so if we used `word`
after calling `s.clear()`. Because `word` isnвҖҷt connected to the state of `s`
at all, `word` still contains the value `5`. We could use that value `5` with
the variable `s` to try to extract the first word out, but this would be a bug
because the contents of `s` have changed since we saved `5` in `word`.

Having to worry about the index in `word` getting out of sync with the data in
`s` is tedious and error-prone! Managing these indices is even more brittle if
we write a `second_word` function. Its signature would have to look like this:

```rust,ignore
fn second_word(s: &String) -> (usize, usize) {
```

Now weвҖҷre tracking a starting _and_ an ending index, and we have even more
values that were calculated from data in a particular state but arenвҖҷt tied to
that state at all. We have three unrelated variables floating around that need
to be kept in sync.

Luckily, Rust has a solution to this problem: string slices.

### String Slices

A _string slice_ is a reference to a contiguous sequence of the elements of a
`String`, and it looks like this:

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

Rather than a reference to the entire `String`, `hello` is a reference to a
portion of the `String`, specified in the extra `[0..5]` bit. We create slices
using a range within square brackets by specifying
`[starting_index..ending_index]`, where _`starting_index`_ is the first
position in the slice and _`ending_index`_ is one more than the last position
in the slice. Internally, the slice data structure stores the starting position
and the length of the slice, which corresponds to _`ending_index`_ minus
_`starting_index`_. So, in the case of `let world = &s[6..11];`, `world` would
be a slice that contains a pointer to the byte at index 6 of `s` with a length
value of `5`.

Figure 4-7 shows this in a diagram.

<img alt="Three tables: a table representing the stack data of s, which points
to the byte at index 0 in a table of the string data &quot;hello world&quot; on
the heap. The third table represents the stack data of the slice world, which
has a length value of 5 and points to byte 6 of the heap data table."
src="img/trpl04-07.svg" class="center" style="width: 50%;" />

<span class="caption">Figure 4-7: A string slice referring to part of a
`String`</span>

With RustвҖҷs `..` range syntax, if you want to start at index 0, you can drop
the value before the two periods. In other words, these are equal:

```rust
let s = String::from("hello");

let slice = &s[0..2];
let slice = &s[..2];
```

By the same token, if your slice includes the last byte of the `String`, you
can drop the trailing number. That means these are equal:

```rust
let s = String::from("hello");

let len = s.len();

let slice = &s[3..len];
let slice = &s[3..];
```

You can also drop both values to take a slice of the entire string. So, these
are equal:

```rust
let s = String::from("hello");

let len = s.len();

let slice = &s[0..len];
let slice = &s[..];
```

> Note: String slice range indices must occur at valid UTF-8 character
> boundaries. If you attempt to create a string slice in the middle of a
> multibyte character, your program will exit with an error.

With all this information in mind, letвҖҷs rewrite `first_word` to return a
slice. The type that signifies вҖңstring sliceвҖқ is written as `&str`:

<Listing file-name="src/main.rs">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

We get the index for the end of the word the same way we did in Listing 4-7, by
looking for the first occurrence of a space. When we find a space, we return a
string slice using the start of the string and the index of the space as the
starting and ending indices.

Now when we call `first_word`, we get back a single value that is tied to the
underlying data. The value is made up of a reference to the starting point of
the slice and the number of elements in the slice.

Returning a slice would also work for a `second_word` function:

```rust,ignore
fn second_word(s: &String) -> &str {
```

We now have a straightforward API thatвҖҷs much harder to mess up because the
compiler will ensure that the references into the `String` remain valid.
Remember the bug in the program in Listing 4-8, when we got the index to the
end of the first word but then cleared the string so our index was invalid?
That code was logically incorrect but didnвҖҷt show any immediate errors. The
problems would show up later if we kept trying to use the first word index with
an emptied string. Slices make this bug impossible and let us know much sooner
that we have a problem with our code. Using the slice version of `first_word`
will throw a compile-time error:

<Listing file-name="src/main.rs">

```rust,ignore,does_not_compile
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

HereвҖҷs the compiler error:

```console
[ver ejemplo]
```

Recall from the borrowing rules that if we have an immutable reference to
something, we cannot also take a mutable reference. Because `clear` needs to
truncate the `String`, it needs to get a mutable reference. The `println!`
after the call to `clear` uses the reference in `word`, so the immutable
reference must still be active at that point. Rust disallows the mutable
reference in `clear` and the immutable reference in `word` from existing at the
same time, and compilation fails. Not only has Rust made our API easier to use,
but it has also eliminated an entire class of errors at compile time!



<a id="string-literals-are-slices"></a>

#### String Literals as Slices

Recall that we talked about string literals being stored inside the binary. Now
that we know about slices, we can properly understand string literals:

```rust
let s = "Hello, world!";
```

The type of `s` here is `&str`: ItвҖҷs a slice pointing to that specific point of
the binary. This is also why string literals are immutable; `&str` is an
immutable reference.

#### String Slices as Parameters

Knowing that you can take slices of literals and `String` values leads us to
one more improvement on `first_word`, and thatвҖҷs its signature:

```rust,ignore
fn first_word(s: &String) -> &str {
```

A more experienced Rustacean would write the signature shown in Listing 4-9
instead because it allows us to use the same function on both `&String` values
and `&str` values.

<Listing number="4-9" caption="Improving the `first_word` function by using a string slice for the type of the `s` parameter">

```rust,ignore
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

If we have a string slice, we can pass that directly. If we have a `String`, we
can pass a slice of the `String` or a reference to the `String`. This
flexibility takes advantage of deref coercions, a feature we will cover in
the [вҖңUsing Deref Coercions in Functions and MethodsвҖқ][deref-coercions]<!--
ignore --> section of Chapter 15.

Defining a function to take a string slice instead of a reference to a `String`
makes our API more general and useful without losing any functionality:

<Listing file-name="src/main.rs">

```rust
[ver cГіdigo en rustdoc]
```

</Listing>

### Other Slices

String slices, as you might imagine, are specific to strings. But thereвҖҷs a
more general slice type too. Consider this array:

```rust
let a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
```

Just as we might want to refer to part of a string, we might want to refer to
part of an array. WeвҖҷd do so like this:

```rust
let a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

let slice = &a[1..3];

assert_eq!(slice, &[2, 3]);
```

This slice has the type `&[i32]`. It works the same way as string slices do, by
storing a reference to the first element and a length. YouвҖҷll use this kind of
slice for all sorts of other collections. WeвҖҷll discuss these collections in
detail when we talk about vectors in Chapter 8.

## Summary

The concepts of ownership, borrowing, and slices ensure memory safety in Rust
programs at compile time. The Rust language gives you control over your memory
usage in the same way as other systems programming languages. But having the
owner of data automatically clean up that data when the owner goes out of scope
means you donвҖҷt have to write and debug extra code to get this control.

Ownership affects how lots of other parts of Rust work, so weвҖҷll talk about
these concepts further throughout the rest of the book. LetвҖҷs move on to
Chapter 5 and look at grouping pieces of data together in a `struct`.

[ch13]: ch13-02-iterators.html
[ch6]: ch06-02-match.html#patterns-that-bind-to-values
[strings]: ch08-02-strings.html#storing-utf-8-encoded-text-with-strings
[deref-coercions]: ch15-02-deref.html#using-deref-coercions-in-functions-and-methods

# Advances and Open Problems in Federated Learning вҖ” Kairouz et al. 2021
# ArXiv: 1910.06054
# Fuente: arXiv (acceso abierto)

## Abstract


We propose a new algorithm for adversarial multi-armed bandits with unrestricted delays. The algorithm is based on a novel hybrid regularizer applied in the Follow the Regularized Leader (FTRL) framework. It achieves рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+DвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\sqrt{D\log(k)}) regret guarantee, where kрқ‘ҳk is the number of arms, nрқ‘ӣn is the number of rounds, and Dрқҗ·D is the total delay. The result matches the lower bound within constants and requires no prior knowledge of nрқ‘ӣn or Dрқҗ·D. Additionally, we propose a refined tuning of the algorithm, which achieves рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+minSвҒЎ(|S|+DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҷ
рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\min_{S}(|S|+\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)})) regret guarantee, where Sрқ‘ҶS is a set of rounds excluded from delay counting, SВҜ=[n]вҲ–SВҜрқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҷ\bar{S}=[n]\setminus S are the counted rounds, and DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD_{\bar{S}} is the total delay in the counted rounds. If the delays are highly unbalanced, the latter regret guarantee can be significantly tighter than the former. The result requires no advance knowledge of the delays and resolves an open problem of Thune etВ al. (2019). The new FTRL algorithm and its refined tuning are anytime and require no doubling, which resolves another open problem of Thune etВ al. (2019).


## 
1 Introduction


Multi-armed bandits are a fundamental sequential decision making problem with an increasing number of industrial applications.
In the multi-armed bandit setting, a learner repeatedly chooses an action from a finite set of actions and immediately observes a loss for that specific action.
The action might be, for example, a choice of an advertisement layout out of a finite set of layouts.
The loss could be the response of a user to the layout, for example, a lack of a click on the advertisement.
In practice, it is often required to make decisions for new users before observing the feedback of all previous users,
either due to response latency or parallel interaction with multiple users.
This can be modeled by introducing a delay between the action and observation.


Table 1: Overview of state-of-the-art regret bounds for multi-armed bandits with delayed feedback. (*) requires oracle knowledge of the time horizon nрқ‘ӣn and the total delay Dрқҗ·D; the result appeared independently in two papers. (**) requires advance knowledge of the delays dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t} вҖңat action timeвҖқ tрқ‘Ўt.

рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқҗ·subscriptВҜрқ‘ҶрқӣҪ\mathcal{O}(\min_{\beta}|S_{\beta}|+\beta\log(k)+\beta^{-1}(kn+D_{\bar{S}_{\beta}}))
 | (**)
 | Thune etВ al. (2019)

 | 
 | This paper


We focus on the oblivious adversarial (a.k.a.В non-stochastic) bandit setting, meaning that the sequence of losses and the delays are fixed before the start of the game.
The setting was first studied by Cesa-Bianchi etВ al. (2016) under the assumption of uniform delays, which are all equal to dрқ‘‘d.
They proved a lower bound of О©вҖӢ(maxвҒЎ{kвҖӢn,dвҖӢnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)})О©рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\Omega(\max\{\sqrt{kn},\sqrt{dn\log(k)}\}) for dвүӨn/logвҒЎ(k)рқ‘‘рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳd\leq n/\log(k) (they do not report the logвҒЎ(k)рқ‘ҳ\log(k) term)
and an almost matching upper bound of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)+dвҖӢnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳрқ‘‘рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn\log(k)}+\sqrt{dn\log(k)}).
By translating individual delays into the total delay D=dвҖӢnрқҗ·рқ‘‘рқ‘ӣD=dn the lower bound for uniform delays is О©вҖӢ(maxвҒЎ{kвҖӢn,DвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)})О©рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\Omega(\max\{\sqrt{kn},\sqrt{D\log(k)}\}).
Thune etВ al. (2019) and Bistritz etВ al. (2019) independently derived an algorithm that can handle non-uniform delays and achieves an рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)+DвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn\log(k)}+\sqrt{D\log(k)}) regret bound under the assumption that nрқ‘ӣn and Dрқҗ·D are known in advance.
Thune etВ al. further provide a doubling scheme that achieves the same regret bound under the assumption that the delays dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t} are known вҖқat action timeвҖқ, i.e., at time tрқ‘Ўt, but nрқ‘ӣn and Dрқҗ·D are unknown, whereas
Bistritz etВ al. provide a doubling scheme that achieves an рқ’ӘвҖӢ(k2вҖӢnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)+DвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Ә
superscriptрқ‘ҳ2рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{k^{2}n\log(k)}+\sqrt{D\log(k)}) regret bound when nрқ‘ӣn and Dрқҗ·D are unknown and the delays dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t} are observed together with the observations, i.e., at time t+dt
рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўt+d_{t}.


Thune etВ al. further observe that if the delays are highly unbalanced it may be worth вҖңskippingвҖқ rounds with excessively large delays.
вҖңSkippingвҖқ means that the regret in the corresponding round is trivially bounded by 1 and the observation is ignored by the algorithm.
The skipping approach of Thune etВ al.В requires knowledge of the delays вҖңat action timeвҖқ.
Under the assumption that this information is available, Thune etВ al.В provide an algorithm that achieves рқ’ӘвҖӢ(minОІвҒЎ|SОІ|+ОІвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)+ОІвҲ’1вҖӢ(kвҖӢn+DSВҜОІ))рқ’Ә
subscriptрқӣҪsubscriptрқ‘ҶрқӣҪрқӣҪрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқӣҪ1
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқҗ·subscriptВҜрқ‘ҶрқӣҪ\mathcal{O}(\min_{\beta}|S_{\beta}|+\beta\log(k)+\beta^{-1}(kn+D_{\bar{S}_{\beta}})) regret guarantee, where ОІрқӣҪ\beta is the skipping threshold (the rounds with delays dtвүҘОІsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ЎрқӣҪd_{t}\geq\beta are skipped), SОІsubscriptрқ‘ҶрқӣҪS_{\beta} is the set of skipped rounds and |SОІ|subscriptрқ‘ҶрқӣҪ|S_{\beta}| is their number, SВҜОІ=[n]вҲ–SОІsubscriptВҜрқ‘ҶрқӣҪdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘ҶрқӣҪ\bar{S}_{\beta}=[n]\setminus S_{\beta} are the remaining rounds (where [n]={1,вҖҰ,n}delimited-[]рқ‘ӣ1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ[n]=\left\{1,\dots,n\right\}), and DSВҜОІ=вҲ‘tвҲҲSВҜОІdtsubscriptDsubscriptВҜрқ‘ҶрқӣҪsubscriptрқ‘ЎsubscriptВҜрқ‘ҶрқӣҪsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ў\operatorname{D}_{\bar{S}_{\beta}}=\sum_{t\in\bar{S}_{\beta}}d_{t} is their total delay.
Thune etВ al.В provide an example, where the first вҢҠkвҖӢn/logвҒЎ(k)вҢӢрқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\lfloor\sqrt{kn/\log(k)}\rfloor rounds have delays of order nрқ‘ӣn and the remaining rounds have zero delays.
By skipping the first rounds, the dependence of the regret bound on nрқ‘ӣn improves from order n3/4superscriptрқ‘ӣ34n^{3/4} to n1/2superscriptрқ‘ӣ12n^{1/2}.
The skipping procedure of Thune etВ al.В crucially depends on availability of delays вҖңat action timeвҖқ in order to make the skipping decision and the skipping threshold ОІрқӣҪ\beta is tuned by doubling.
Relaxation of the assumption on early availability of delays, as well as replacement of doubling with anytime strategies (i.e., algorithms without resets) were left as open questions.


We resolve both open questions and make the following contributions:


- 
1. 


We provide an anytime FTRL algorithm based on a novel hybrid regularizer. The regularizer combines 1212\frac{1}{2}-Tsallis entropy and negative entropy, each with its own learning rate. The algorithm requires no advance knowledge of the delays and
achieves a regret bound of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+DвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\sqrt{D\log(k)}), which matches the lower bound within constants.


- 
2. 


We provide a novel вҖңskippingвҖқ technique, which allows to вҖңignoreвҖқ rounds with excessively large delays with no advance knowledge of the delays. We put вҖңskippingвҖқ and вҖңignoreвҖқ in quotation marks, because the observations are still used by the algorithm and the вҖңskippedвҖқ rounds are only excluded from updates of the learning rate.
We prove an рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+minSвҒЎ(|S|+DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҷ
рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\min_{S}(|S|+\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)})) regret bound for the refined algorithm. The bound is slightly tighter than the refined regret bound of Thune etВ al. (2019), but most importantly it requires no advance knowledge of the delays.
111We note that the new skipping technique could also be combined with the doubling scheme of Thune etВ al. to eliminate the need in advanced knowledge of delays there as well. However, the anytime FTRL algorithm presented here is much more elegant than doubling.


In TableВ 1 we provide a comparison of state-of-the art bounds with our new results. Additional prior work in other online learning settings with delayed feedback includes the full information setting studied by Joulani etВ al. (2016), who derived a general reduction to a non-delayed problem.
To the best of our knowledge, no similar reduction under bandit feedback has been found yet. Another related setting are bandits with anonymous composite feedback, where the learner is not informed about the round from which the delayed observation is coming from, neither the identity of the action it corresponds to, and delayed observations from several rounds may be composed together with no possibility to separate them. This harder setting was studied by Cesa-Bianchi etВ al., who derived an рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢdmвҖӢaвҖӢxвҖӢnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Әрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ҡрқ‘Һрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kd_{max}n\log(k)}) regret bound, where dmвҖӢaвҖӢxsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ҡрқ‘Һрқ‘Ҙd_{max} is a known upper bound on the delays. We refer the reader to Thune etВ al. (2019) for further review of prior work in related settings.


The paper is structured in the following way:
SectionВ 2 provides a formal definition of the problem setting.
SectionВ 3 explains in detail our algorithm and two versions of learning rate tuning.
SectionВ 4 contains our main theorems, as well as an intuition behind the refined learning rate tuning.
SectionВ 5 presents a general analysis of FTRL for multi-armed bandits with delays and formally proves the theorems from the previous section.
Finally, SectionВ 6 provides a summary and directions for future work.


## 
2 Problem setting


Adversarial bandits with delay is a sequential game between a learner and an environment with kрқ‘ҳk fixed actions.
At time steps t=1,вҖҰ,nрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣt=1,\dots,n the learner picks actions AtвҲҲ[k]subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ўdelimited-[]рқ‘ҳA_{t}\in[k] and immediately suffers the loss в„“t,Atsubscriptв„“
- рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ў\ell_{t,A_{t}}, where (в„“t)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptв„“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(\ell_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} are vectors in [0,1]ksuperscript01рқ‘ҳ[0,1]^{k}.
Unlike in the regular bandit problem, the learner does not necessarily observe the loss в„“t,Atsubscriptв„“
- рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ў\ell_{t,A_{t}} at the end of round tрқ‘Ўt.
Instead, the environment chooses a sequence of delays (dt)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(d_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} and the player observes the tuples (s,в„“s,As)рқ‘ subscriptв„“
- рқ‘ subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ (s,\ell_{s,A_{s}}) for each sрқ‘ s such that s+ds=t
рқ‘ subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘Ўs+d_{s}=t at the end of round tрқ‘Ўt.
Without loss of generality, we assume that all outstanding tuples are observed at the end of the game, i.e., t+dtвүӨn
рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣt+d_{t}\leq n for all tрқ‘Ўt.
We focus on the oblivious adversarial setting (sometimes called вҖңnon-stochasticвҖқ), which means that both the sequence of losses (в„“t)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptв„“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(\ell_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} and the sequence of delays (dt)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(d_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} are chosen by the environment at the beginning of the game.
We use D=вҲ‘t=1ndtрқҗ·superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ЎD=\sum_{t=1}^{n}d_{t} to denote the total delay.
The learner has no prior knowledge of the quantities n,D
- рқ‘ӣрқҗ·n,D, or (dt)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(d_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n}.
The performance of the algorithm is measured by its expected regret


 | 
 | в„ңn:=рқ”јвҖӢ[вҲ‘t=1nв„“t,At]вҲ’miniвҲҲ[k]вҖӢвҲ‘t=1nв„“t,i.assignsubscriptв„ңрқ‘ӣрқ”јdelimited-[]superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptв„“
- рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘–delimited-[]рқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptв„“
- рқ‘Ўрқ‘–\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{n}:=\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=1}^{n}\ell_{t,A_{t}}\right]-\min_{i\in[k]}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\ell_{t,i}\,.
 | 


##### Some technical definitions


We use О”вҖӢ([k])={xвҲҲв„қ+k|вҖ–xвҖ–1=1}О”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳconditional-setрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscriptв„қ
рқ‘ҳsubscriptnormрқ‘Ҙ11\Delta([k])=\{x\in\mathbb{R}_{+}^{k}|\|x\|_{1}=1\} to denote the (kвҲ’1)рқ‘ҳ1(k-1)-simplex. For a set SвҠӮ[n]={1,вҖҰ,n}рқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣ1вҖҰрқ‘ӣS\subset[n]=\{1,\dots,n\}, we denote its complement by SВҜ=[n]вҲ–SВҜрқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҷ\bar{S}=[n]\setminus S. For a convex function Fрқҗ№F we use FвҲ—superscriptрқҗ№F^{*} to denote its convex conjugate (a.k.a.В Fenchel conjugate) and FВҜвҲ—superscriptВҜрқҗ№\overline{F}^{*} to denote the constrained convex conjugate. They are defined, respectively, by


 | 
 | FвҲ—вҖӢ(y)=maxxвҲҲв„қkвҒЎвҹЁx,yвҹ©вҲ’FвҖӢ(x),superscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұрқҗ№рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle F^{*}(y)=\max_{x\in\mathbb{R}^{k}}\langle x,y\rangle-F(x),
 | 


 | 
 | FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(y)=maxxвҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])вҒЎвҹЁx,yвҹ©вҲ’FвҖӢ(x).superscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘ҘО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұрқҗ№рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle\overline{F}^{*}(y)=\max_{x\in\Delta([k])}\langle x,y\rangle-F(x)\,.
 | 


## 
3 Algorithm


Our AlgorithmВ 1 is a standard Follow the Regularized Leader (FTRL) algorithm that works with importance weighted loss estimators of all observations available up to the current point in time.
The loss estimators are defined by


 | 
 | в„“^s=в„“s,Asxs,AsвҖӢрқҗһAs,subscript^в„“рқ‘ subscriptв„“
- рқ‘ subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ subscriptрқ‘Ҙ
- рқ‘ subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ subscriptрқҗһsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ \hat{\ell}_{s}=\frac{\ell_{s,A_{s}}}{x_{s,A_{s}}}\mathbf{e}_{A_{s}}\,,
 | 


where xs,Assubscriptрқ‘Ҙ

We call a sequence of learning rates (О·t)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(\eta_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} proper if it is non-increasing and can be defined using information available at the beginning of round tрқ‘Ўt.


### 
3.1 Intuition behind the regularizer


Hybrid regularizers have been successfully used in adaptive regret bounds for sparse bandits, online portfolio selection, adversarially robust semi-bandits, and adaptive first order bounds for multi-armed bandits (Bubeck etВ al., 2018; Luo etВ al., 2018; Zimmert etВ al., 2019; Pogodin and Lattimore, 2019).
They are useful for targeting multiple objectives.
In our case, the regret lower bound for bandits with fixed delay dрқ‘‘d is О©вҖӢ(maxвҒЎ{kвҖӢn,dвҖӢnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)})О©рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\Omega(\max\{\sqrt{kn},\sqrt{dn\log(k)}\}) (Cesa-Bianchi etВ al., 2016). The first part of the bound is the standard regret lower bound for multi-armed bandits with no delays, which is clearly also a lower bound for the problem with delays. The second part of the bound is achieved by grouping the game rounds into batches of size dрқ‘‘d and reducing the game to a full information game over n/dрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘n/d rounds with loss range [0,d]0рқ‘‘[0,d]. The second part is then a lower bound on the regret in the full information game.


Our regularizer uses the same decomposition of the problem.
We combine the optimal regularizer for the standard bandit problem with no delay, the 1212\frac{1}{2}-Tsallis Entropy, with the optimal regularizer for the full information problems, the negative entropy. We further tune the learning rate for the second part to the actual delay sequence (dt)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(d_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n}.


###

AlgorithmВ 1 FTRL for bandits with delay


##### Simple tuning


We define the key quantity, which is used for tuning the learning rate.


###### 
Definition 1.


The number of outstanding observations at round tрқ‘Ўt is defined by


 | 
 | рқ”Ўt=вҲ‘s=1tвҲ’1рқ•ҖвҖӢ{s+dsвүҘt},subscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ 1рқ‘Ў1рқ•Җ
рқ‘ subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘Ў\mathfrak{d}_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}\mathbb{I}\{s+d_{s}\geq t\},
 | 


where рқ•Җрқ•Җ\mathbb{I} is the indicator function.


рқ”Ўtsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\mathfrak{d}_{t} counts how many observations from rounds s<tрқ‘ рқ‘Ўs<t are still missing at the beginning of round tрқ‘Ўt.
Note that рқ”Ўtsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\mathfrak{d}_{t} is an observable quantity, unlike the delays dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t}.
Therefore, рқ”Ўtsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\mathfrak{d}_{t} can be used for online tuning of the learning rate.
The learning rate under the simple tuning is given by


 | 
 | рқ”Үt=вҲ‘s=1tрқ”Ўt,О·tвҲ’1=2вҖӢрқ”Үt/logвҒЎ(k).\mathfrak{D}_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathfrak{d}_{t}\qquad,\qquad\eta_{t}^{-1}=\sqrt{2\mathfrak{D}_{t}/\log(k)}\,.
 | 


The algorithm only uses the inverse of the learning rate. If рқ”Үt=0subscriptрқ”Үрқ‘Ў0\mathfrak{D}_{t}=0, then О·tвҲ’1=0superscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ў10\eta_{t}^{-1}=0 and the algorithm is well-defined, even though О·t=вҲһsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ў\eta_{t}=\infty.


##### Advanced tuning


In the advanced tuning, we maintain a running estimate рқ”Ү~tsubscript~рқ”Үрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t} of the cumulative delay DSВҜ=вҲ‘tвҲҲSВҜdtsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘ЎВҜрқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ЎD_{\bar{S}}=\sum_{t\in\bar{S}}d_{t} in the optimal subset of rounds SВҜВҜрқ‘Ҷ\bar{S}, whereas the regret in the remaining rounds S=[n]вҲ–SВҜрқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣВҜрқ‘ҶS=[n]\setminus\bar{S} is bounded by |S|рқ‘Ҷ|S| (the sense of optimality of SВҜВҜрқ‘Ҷ\bar{S} is explained in the next section).
In order to estimate DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD_{\bar{S}} we modify the quantity рқ”Ўtsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\mathfrak{d}_{t} by вҖңskippingвҖқ some outstanding observations.
To be precise, we keep indicator variables astвҲҲ{0,1}superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ рқ‘Ў01a_{s}^{t}\in\{0,1\}, where astsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ рқ‘Ўa_{s}^{t} indicates whether an outstanding observation from round sрқ‘ s should still be counted at round tрқ‘Ўt:


 | 
 | рқ”Ў~t=вҲ‘s=1tвҲ’1astвҖӢрқ•ҖвҖӢ{s+dsвүҘt}.subscript~рқ”Ўрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ 1рқ‘Ў1superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ рқ‘Ўрқ•Җ
рқ‘ subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{d}}_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}a_{s}^{t}\mathbb{I}\{s+d_{s}\geq t\}.
 | 


We define


 | 
 | рқ”Ү~t=вҲ‘s=1tрқ”Ў~t,О·tвҲ’1=рқ”Ү~t/logвҒЎ(k).\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t}\tilde{\mathfrak{d}}_{t}\qquad,\qquad\eta_{t}^{-1}=\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t}/\log(k)}\,.
 | 


The algorithm initially waits for all observations, but as soon as the waiting time exceeds a threshold the round is вҖңskippedвҖқ.
If for s<tрқ‘ рқ‘Ўs<t we experience a delay ds>рқ”Ү~t/logвҒЎ(k)subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ subscript~рқ”Үрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳd_{s}>\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t}/\log(k)}, we set (astвҖІ)tвҖІ>tsubscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ superscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІрқ‘Ў(a_{s}^{t^{\prime}})_{t^{\prime}>t} to 00.
The indicators are not changed retrospectively, which means that the initial waiting time still counts toward рқ”Ү~tsubscript~рқ”Үрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t}. The intuition behind advanced tuning is explained in SectionВ 4.3.

## 
4 Main results


In this section, we present regret upper bounds for AlgorithmВ 1 with simple tuning and advanced tuning.
The first result confirms the conjecture of Cesa-Bianchi etВ al. (2016) that an upper bound of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+DвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\sqrt{D\log(k)}) is achievable with a simple algorithm.
The second result shows that it is possible to obtain a refined bound of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+minSвҠӮ[n]вҒЎ(|S|+DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣ
рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\min_{S\subset[n]}(|S|+\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)})) by a more careful tuning of the learning rate.


### 
4.1 Adaptation to the total delay Dрқҗ·D


The following theorem provides a regret bound for AlgorithmВ 1 with simple tuning.


###### 
Theorem 1.


The regret of AlgorithmВ 1 with any non-increasing positive sequence of learning rates (О·t)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(\eta_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} satisfies


 | 
 | в„ңnвүӨ4вҖӢkвҖӢn+О·nвҲ’1вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)+вҲ‘t=1nО·tвҖӢрқ”Ўt.subscriptв„ңрқ‘ӣ
4рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘ӣ1рқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{n}\leq 4\sqrt{kn}+\eta_{n}^{-1}\log(k)+\sum_{t=1}^{n}\eta_{t}\mathfrak{d}_{t}\,.
 | 


In particular, the simple tuning О·tвҲ’1=2вҖӢрқ”Үt/logвҒЎ(k)=2вҖӢ(вҲ‘s=1tрқ”Ўs)/logвҒЎ(k)superscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ў12subscriptрқ”Үрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ 1рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘ рқ‘ҳ\eta_{t}^{-1}=\sqrt{2\mathfrak{D}_{t}/\log(k)}=\sqrt{2(\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathfrak{d}_{s})/\log(k)} is proper and leads to a regret bound of


 | 
 | в„ңnвүӨ4вҖӢkвҖӢn+8вҖӢDвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).subscriptв„ңрқ‘ӣ
4рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ8рқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{n}\leq 4\sqrt{kn}+\sqrt{8D\log(k)}\,.
 | 


###### Proof.


The first statement is a special case of TheoremВ 3, which is presented in SectionВ 5.
For the second statement we use a standard summation lemma, by which for a sequence of positive рқ”Ў1,вҖҰ,рқ”Ўn
- subscriptрқ”Ў1вҖҰsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘ӣ\mathfrak{d}_{1},\dots,\mathfrak{d}_{n} we have вҲ‘t=1n(рқ”Ўt/вҲ‘s=1tрқ”Ўs)вүӨ2вҖӢвҲ‘s=1tрқ”Ўtsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ 1рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘ 2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ 1рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\sum_{t=1}^{n}\left(\mathfrak{d}_{t}/\sqrt{\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathfrak{d}_{s}}\right)\leq 2\sqrt{\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathfrak{d}_{t}} (Seldin etВ al., 2014, Lemma 8) and the convention that if рқ”Ўt=0subscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў0\mathfrak{d}_{t}=0 then О·tвҖӢрқ”Ўt=0subscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў0\eta_{t}\mathfrak{d}_{t}=0 (so that zero terms naturally fall out of the summation). By substituting the definition of the learning rate in the second statement into the first statement and using the summation lemma we obtain


 | 
 | в„ңnвүӨ4вҖӢkвҖӢn+8вҖӢрқ”ҮnвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).subscriptв„ңрқ‘ӣ
4рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ8subscriptрқ”Үрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\mathfrak{R}_{n}\leq 4\sqrt{kn}+\sqrt{8\mathfrak{D}_{n}\log(k)}\,.
 | 


Finally, note that an observation from round tрқ‘Ўt with delay dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t} contributes 1 to each of рқ”Ўt,вҖҰ,рқ”Ўt+dt
- subscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘ЎвҖҰsubscriptрқ”Ў
рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ў\mathfrak{d}_{t},\dots,\mathfrak{d}_{t+d_{t}}, i.e., it contributes dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t} to the total sum of the number of outstanding observations вҲ‘t=1nрқ”Ўtsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\sum_{t=1}^{n}\mathfrak{d}_{t}.
Since we have assumed that t+dtвүӨn
рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣt+d_{t}\leq n for all tрқ‘Ўt, we have вҲ‘t=1nрқ”Ўt=вҲ‘t=1ndt=Dsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ”Ўрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқҗ·\sum_{t=1}^{n}\mathfrak{d}_{t}=\sum_{t=1}^{n}d_{t}=D.
вҲҺ


The main advantage of AlgorithmВ 1 and TheoremВ 1 compared to the work of Thune etВ al. (2019) is that the tuning requires neither the knowledge of Dрқҗ·D and nрқ‘ӣn, nor doubling.


### 
4.2 Refined bounds for unbalanced delays


Thune etВ al. (2019) observed that if the delays are highly unbalanced it may be worth skipping rounds with overly large delays rather than keeping them in the analysis. Let Sрқ‘ҶS denote the set of skipped rounds and |S|рқ‘Ҷ|S| their number. The regret in every skipped round is trivially bounded by 1 and, assuming we knew which rounds to skip, we could reduce the regret bound to рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+|S|+DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}\left(\sqrt{kn}+|S|+\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)}\right). As shown by Thune etВ al., this could potentially be much smaller than the regret bound in TheoremВ 1. For example, if the delay in the first ОёвҖӢ(kвҖӢn)рқңғрқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ\theta(\sqrt{kn}) rounds is of order nрқ‘ӣn and the delay in the remaining rounds is zero, then the regret bound in TheoremВ 1 is of order n3/4superscriptрқ‘ӣ34n^{3/4}, whereas the refined regret bound is of order n1/2superscriptрқ‘ӣ12n^{1/2} (ignoring the dependence on kрқ‘ҳk). The challenge faced by Thune etВ al. was that they had to know the delays in advance (more precisely, вҖңat action timeвҖқ) in order to tune the parameters of their algorithm and make the skipping decision. Since we have an anytime algorithm, we are able to obtain the refinement with no need in advance knowledge of the delay information. Strictly speaking, we even do not need to skip observations and we can obtain the refinement by using all observations and only adjusting the learning rate appropriately, although technically the вҖңno-skippingвҖқ solution yields the same regret bound as skipping.


The following theorem provides our adaptive bound.


###### 
Theorem 2.


AlgorithmВ 1 with advanced learning rate tuning provided in AlgorithmВ 2 satisfies


 | 
 | в„ңnsubscriptв„ңрқ‘ӣ\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{n}
 | вүӨ4вҖӢkвҖӢnabsent4рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣ\displaystyle\leq 4\sqrt{kn}
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | +10вҖӢmaxвҒЎ{minSвҠӮ[n]вҒЎ|S|+DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k),2вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).
10cases
subscriptрқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳotherwise2рқ‘ҳotherwise\displaystyle\quad+10\max\begin{cases}\min_{S\subset[n]}|S|+\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)},\\
2\log(k).\end{cases}\,
 | 


The proof is postponed to SectionВ 5


### 
4.3 Intuition behind the вҖңskippingвҖқ procedure


In order to give an intuition behind the refined algorithm we provide a simple back-of-the-envelope calculation. If we skip |S|рқ‘Ҷ|S| rounds and trivially bound their regret by 1 and apply TheoremВ 1 to the remaining rounds, then the regret bound is рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)+|S|)рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҷ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)}+|S|). Thus, the number of skipped rounds can be as large as DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)subscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)} without significantly impacting the bound. Obviously, we want to skip rounds with the largest delays, but how should we determine the skipping threshold Xрқ‘ӢX? If we want to achieve a significant reduction in the regret bound, the skipped delay DS=вҲ‘tвҲҲSdtвүҘXвҖӢ|S|subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ӣрқ‘ҶD_{S}=\sum_{t\in S}d_{t}\geq X|S| should be at least as large as the remaining delay DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD_{\bar{S}}, because D=DS+DSВҜрқҗ·
subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD=D_{S}+D_{\bar{S}} and our aim is to reduce the DвҖӢlogвҒЎkрқҗ·рқ‘ҳ\sqrt{D\log k} term. Thus, if we put a threshold at Xрқ‘ӢX and skip DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)subscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)} rounds we want to have XвҖӢDSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)вүҘDSВҜрқ‘Ӣsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶX\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)}\geq D_{\bar{S}}. Therefore, we aim at X=DSВҜ/logвҒЎ(k)рқ‘Ӣsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳX=\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}/\log(k)}. However, there are two challenges: (a) we do not know the delays dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t} in advance and, therefore, we do not know which rounds to skip, and (b) the threshold definition is recursive: Xрқ‘ӢX depends on DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD_{\bar{S}} and DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD_{\bar{S}} depends on Xрқ‘ӢX.


The strategy that we take in AlgorithmВ 2 is the following: we keep a running estimate рқ”Ү~tsubscript~рқ”Үрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t} of DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD_{\bar{S}}. For an observation from round sрқ‘ s we initially start waiting and count it in the number of outstanding observations рқ”Ў~tsubscript~рқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{d}}_{t} for the initial rounds. However, we constantly monitor the waiting time and if the observation has not arrived within рқ”Ү~t/logвҒЎ(k)subscript~рқ”Үрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t}/\log(k)} rounds we stop waiting. The initial rounds we have been waiting for still count for the estimate рқ”Ү~tsubscript~рқ”Үрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t}. Another quick back-of-the-envelope calculation shows that if рқ”Ү~tsubscript~рқ”Үрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{t} is indeed a good approximation of DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘ҶD_{\bar{S}}, then the extra delay from the initial waiting rounds is of order DSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)вҖӢDSВҜ/logвҒЎ(k)=DSВҜsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷ\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)}\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}/\log(k)}=D_{\bar{S}}, where the first term is a rough estimate of the number of rounds that we skip and the second term is a rough estimate of the initial waiting time for each of the observations. Thus, the initial waiting time has no significant impact on the final bound.


AlgorithmВ 2 follows this intuitive approach.
We use indicator variables (ast)(s,t)вҲҲ[n]2subscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ рқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Ўsuperscriptdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣ2(a_{s}^{t})_{(s,t)\in[n]^{2}} to keep track of which observations в„“s,Assubscriptв„“
- рқ‘ subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘ \ell_{s,A_{s}} we are still waiting for at round tрқ‘Ўt (expressed by ast=1superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ рқ‘Ў1a_{s}^{t}=1) and which not (expressed by ast=0superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ рқ‘Ў0a_{s}^{t}=0). We use рқ”Ў~tsubscript~рқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\tilde{\mathfrak{d}}_{t} to count the truncated number of outstanding observations, where those observations we are no longer waiting for at round tрқ‘Ўt are excluded from counting. We provide a detailed analysis in SectionВ 5.1, but before we get there we provide a refined version of TheoremВ 1, which allows us to use all observations and only use skipping in the tuning of the learning rate. (Though, as already mentioned, complete skipping of the observations would lead to the same regret bound as in TheoremВ 2.)


## 
5 Analysis of FTRL for bandits with delays


In this section we develop a novel analysis of FTRL-style algorithms, leading to TheoremВ 3, which generalizes the first part of TheoremВ 1.
The analysis is based on skipping, similar to the techniques used by Thune etВ al. (2019).
Afterward, we use the general regret bound to prove TheoremВ 2.
We introduce a straightforward generalisation of the outstanding delay with вҖңskippingвҖқ.


###### 
Definition 2.


For any set SвҠӮ[n]рқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣS\subset[n], define the outstanding delay on SВҜ=[n]вҲ–SВҜрқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҷ\bar{S}=[n]\setminus S as


 | 
 | рқ”ЎtSВҜ=вҲ‘s=1tвҲ’1рқ•ҖвҖӢ{s+dsвүҘtвҲ§sвҲҲSВҜ}.subscriptsuperscriptрқ”ЎВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ 1рқ‘Ў1рқ•Җ
рқ‘ subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘Ўрқ‘ ВҜрқ‘Ҷ\displaystyle\mathfrak{d}^{\bar{S}}_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}\mathbb{I}\{s+d_{s}\geq t\,\land\,s\in\bar{S}\}\,.
 | 


The cumulative outstanding delays on SВҜВҜрқ‘Ҷ\bar{S} are рқ”ҮtSВҜ=вҲ‘s=1tрқ”ЎsSВҜsubscriptsuperscriptрқ”ҮВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ 1рқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscriptрқ”ЎВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ \mathfrak{D}^{\bar{S}}_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathfrak{d}^{\bar{S}}_{s}.


Next we present a general regret bound which holds for any set Sрқ‘ҶS.

For any set Sрқ‘ҶS, the regret of AlgorithmВ 1 with non-increasing positive learning rates (О·t)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(\eta_{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} satisfies

##### Support lemmas for the proof of TheoremВ 3


The proofs for all the support lemmas are given in the supplementary material, SectionВ 7.3.
The first Lemma is a small modification of the classical result that bounds the Bregman divergence by the local norm of the regularizer.
We show that we can bound the local norm by the contribution of the Tsallis entropy.


###### 
Lemma 1.


For any tрқ‘Ўt it holds that


 | 
 | рқ”јвҖӢ[FВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢsвҲ’в„“^t)вҲ’FВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢs)+вҹЁxt,в„“^tвҹ©]вүӨkt.рқ”јdelimited-[]
superscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘Ўsubscript^в„“рқ‘ЎsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘Ў
- subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsubscript^в„“рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ў\displaystyle\mathbb{E}\left[\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t}-\hat{\ell}_{t})-\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t})+\langle x_{t},\hat{\ell}_{t}\rangle\right]\leq\frac{\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{t}}\,.
 | 


The second Lemma bounds the so-called вҖңpenaltyвҖқ term coming from the regularization penalty. It appears in almost identical form in the literature (Lattimore and SzepesvГЎri, 2019, Exercise 28.12).

Lemma 2.

 | вҲ‘tвҲҲSВҜ(FВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^tSВҜ)вҲ’FВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^t+1SВҜ))вҲ’L^n+1,iвҲ—вүӨ2вҖӢkвҖӢn+О·nвҲ’1вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).subscriptрқ‘ЎВҜрқ‘ҶsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ЎsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝВҜрқ‘Ҷ
рқ‘Ў1subscript^рқҗҝ
- 
рқ‘ӣ1superscriptрқ‘–
2рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘ӣ1рқ‘ҳ\sum_{t\in\bar{S}}\Bigg{(}\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{\bar{S}}_{t})-\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{\bar{S}}_{t+1})\Bigg{)}-\hat{L}_{n+1,i^{*}}\qquad\\
\leq 2\sqrt{kn}+\eta_{n}^{-1}\log(k)\,.
 | 


The third quantity does not show up in the regular analysis without delays.
We show that similarly to the Bregman divergence, it depends on the local norm of the regularizer.
However, it is beneficial to use the norm of the negative entropy instead of the Tsallis entropy.


###### 
Lemma 3.


For any tрқ‘Ўt it holds that


 | 
 | рқ”ј[FВҜtвҲ—\displaystyle\mathbb{E}\Bigg{[}\overline{F}_{t}^{*}
 | (вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢs)вҲ’FВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢsвҲ’в„“^t)subscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ЎsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘Ўsubscript^в„“рқ‘Ў\displaystyle(-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t})-\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t}-\hat{\ell}_{t})
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | вҲ’FВҜtвҲ—(вҲ’L^tSВҜ)+FВҜtвҲ—(вҲ’L^t+1SВҜ)]вүӨО·tрқ”ЎSВҜt.\displaystyle-\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{\bar{S}}_{t})+\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{\bar{S}}_{t+1})\Bigg{]}\leq\eta_{t}\mathfrak{d}^{\bar{S}}_{t}\,.
 | 


### 
5.1 Refined regret bound


With a suitable learning rate, TheoremВ 3 leads directly to the bound


 | 
 | в„ңnвүӨ4вҖӢkвҖӢn+|S|+2вҖӢвҲ‘tвҲҲSВҜdtвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).subscriptв„ңрқ‘ӣ
4рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ҷ2subscriptрқ‘ЎВҜрқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{n}\leq 4\sqrt{kn}+|S|+2\sqrt{\sum_{t\in\bar{S}}d_{t}\log(k)}\,.
 | 


Below, in the proof of TheoremВ 2, we show that the learning rate in AlgorithmВ 2 brings us within a multiplicative constant factor of the minimum of the above bound, 4вҖӢkвҖӢn+minSвҒЎ(|S|+2вҖӢDSВҜвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k))
4рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҷ
рқ‘Ҷ2subscriptрқҗ·ВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ4\sqrt{kn}+\min_{S}(|S|+2\sqrt{D_{\bar{S}}\log(k)}).


From now on, let Sрқ‘ҶS be the set


 | 
 | S={tвҲҲ[n]|atn=0},рқ‘Ҷconditional-setрқ‘Ўdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣ0S=\{t\in[n]\,|\,a_{t}^{n}=0\}\,,
 | 


which is the set of rounds вҖңskippedвҖқ by AlgorithmВ 2. Since (ast)t=1,вҖҰ,nsubscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ў
- 1вҖҰрқ‘ӣ(a_{s}^{t})_{t=1,\dots,n} is non-increasing, we have for any tвҲҲSВҜрқ‘ЎВҜрқ‘Ҷt\in\bar{S}:
рқ”ЎtSВҜвүӨрқ”Ў~tsubscriptsuperscriptрқ”ЎВҜрқ‘Ҷрқ‘Ўsubscript~рқ”Ўрқ‘Ў\mathfrak{d}^{\bar{S}}_{t}\leq\tilde{\mathfrak{d}}_{t}.
Furthermore, the following lemma bounds the magnitude of |S|рқ‘Ҷ|S|:


###### 
Lemma 4.


For any sequence of delays dtsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ўd_{t}, AlgorithmВ 2 satisfies


 | 
 | |S|=вҲ‘t=1nрқ•ҖвҖӢ{atn=0}вүӨ2вҖӢрқ”Ү~nвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).рқ‘Ҷsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ӣрқ•Җsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣ02subscript~рқ”Үрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\displaystyle|S|=\sum_{t=1}^{n}\mathbb{I}\{a_{t}^{n}=0\}\leq 2\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{n}\log(k)}\,.
 | 


The proof is provided in the supplementary material, SectionВ 7.1.


Finally we have all the prerequisites to prove TheoremВ 2.


###### Proof of TheoremВ 2.


Using TheoremВ 3 and LemmaВ 4, we have


 | 
 | в„ңnsubscriptв„ңрқ‘ӣ\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{n}

 | 


The function is concave in rрқ‘ҹr so the minimum is achieved at one of the endpoints of the interval, which happens to be
r=12вҖӢрқ”Ү~nвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)рқ‘ҹ12subscript~рқ”Үрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳr=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{n}\log(k)} for which the function equals 12вҖӢрқ”Ү~nвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)12subscript~рқ”Үрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{n}\log(k)}.
Hence, we have shown


 | 
 | рқ”Ү~nвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)вүӨ2вҖӢminRвҠӮ[n]вҒЎ(|R|+вҲ‘sвҲҲRВҜdsвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)),subscript~рқ”Үрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ2subscriptрқ‘…delimited-[]рқ‘ӣ
рқ‘…subscriptрқ‘ ВҜрқ‘…subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{n}\log(k)}\leq 2\min_{R\subset[n]}\left(|R|+\sqrt{\sum_{s\in\bar{R}}d_{s}\log(k)}\right)\,,
 | 


which concludes the proof.
вҲҺ


## 
6 Discussion


We confirmed an open conjecture from Cesa-Bianchi etВ al. (2016) by presenting a simple FTRL algorithm for adversarial bandits with arbitrary delays and proving regret upper bound that matches the lower bound within constants.
Furthermore, we proposed a refined tuning of the learning rate that achieves even tighter regret bound for highly unbalanced delays.
We strictly improve on the state-of-the-art bounds and present the first anytime result requiring no doubling, skipping, or advance information about the delays.


If the delays are all 00, then our algorithm reduces to the Tsallis-INF algorithm of Zimmert and Seldin (2019), which has been proven to be simultaneously optimal in both the stochastic and the adversarial setting.
We conjecture that the algorithm presented in this paper is capable of obtaining logarithmic regret in the stochastic setting, but leave the analysis for future work.


Another open question is the tightness of our adaptive bound рқ’ӘвҖӢ(kвҖӢn+minSвҠӮ[n]вҒЎ(|S|+DSвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)))рқ’Ә
рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҷdelimited-[]рқ‘ӣ
рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқҗ·рқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҳ\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kn}+\min_{S\subset[n]}(|S|+\sqrt{D_{S}\log(k)})).
We conjecture that for a fixed set of delays {d1,вҖҰ,dn}subscriptрқ‘‘1вҖҰsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ӣ\{d_{1},\dots,d_{n}\} which the adversary is allowed to permute without changing the magnitudes,
the upper bound is actually tight.

### 
7.1 Auxiliary lemmas for AlgorithmВ 2


###### 
Lemma 5.


AlgorithmВ 2 will not deactivate more than 1 point at a time.


By deactivating we mean setting atn=0superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ӣ0a_{t}^{n}=0.


###### Proof.


We prove the lemma by contradiction.
Assume that s1,s2
- subscriptрқ‘ 1subscriptрқ‘ 2s_{1},s_{2} are both deactivated at time tрқ‘Ўt.
W.l.o.g.В let s2вүӨs1вҲ’1subscriptрқ‘ 2subscriptрқ‘ 11s_{2}\leq s_{1}-1.
Deactivation of s1subscriptрқ‘ 1s_{1} at time tрқ‘Ўt means tвҲ’s1вүҘрқ”Үt/logвҒЎ(k)вүҘрқ”ҮtвҲ’1/logвҒЎ(k)рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘ 1subscriptрқ”Үрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ”Үрқ‘Ў1рқ‘ҳt-s_{1}\geq\sqrt{\mathcal{\mathfrak{D}}_{t}/\log(k)}\geq\sqrt{\mathcal{\mathfrak{D}}_{t-1}/\log(k)}.
At the same time we assumed tвҲ’1вҲ’s2вүҘtвҲ’s1рқ‘Ў1subscriptрқ‘ 2рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘ 1t-1-s_{2}\geq t-s_{1}, which means that s2subscriptрқ‘ 2s_{2} would have been deactivated at round tвҲ’1рқ‘Ў1t-1 or earlier.
вҲҺ


###### Proof of LemmaВ 4.


Recall that d~t=вҲ‘s=t+1t+dtatssubscript~рқ‘‘рқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘

Let (t1,вҖҰ,t|S|)subscriptрқ‘Ў1вҖҰsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҷ(t_{1},\dots,t_{|S|}) be an indexing of Sрқ‘ҶS. By LemmaВ 5 we deactivate at most one
atmnsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡрқ‘ӣa_{t_{m}}^{n} per round. Thus, we have that


 | 
 | d~tm>рқ”Ү~tm+dtm/logвҒЎ(k)вүҘвҲ‘i=1md~ti/logвҒЎ(k)=d~m+вҲ‘i=1mвҲ’1d~tilogвҒЎ(k).subscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡsubscript~рқ”Ү
subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡsubscriptрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҡsubscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–рқ‘ҳ

By solving the quadratic inequality in dtmsubscriptрқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡd_{t_{m}} we obtain


 | 
 | d~tm>1+1+4вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)вҖӢвҲ‘i=1mвҲ’1d~ti2вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).subscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡ
1
14рқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҡ1subscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–2рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\tilde{d}_{t_{m}}>\frac{1+\sqrt{1+4\log(k)\sum_{i=1}^{m-1}\tilde{d}_{t_{i}}}}{2\log(k)}\,.
 | 


Now we prove by induction that d~tm>m2вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)subscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡрқ‘ҡ2рқ‘ҳ\tilde{d}_{t_{m}}>\frac{m}{2\log(k)}.
The induction base holds since d~t1=1subscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ў11\tilde{d}_{t_{1}}=1.
For the inductive step we have


 | 
 | d~tm>1+1+4вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)вҖӢвҲ‘i=1mвҲ’1d~ti2вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)>1+1+mвҖӢ(mвҲ’1)2вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)>m2вҖӢlogвҒЎ(k).subscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡ
1
14рқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҡ1subscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘–2рқ‘ҳ
1
1рқ‘ҡрқ‘ҡ12рқ‘ҳрқ‘ҡ2рқ‘ҳ\displaystyle\tilde{d}_{t_{m}}>\frac{1+\sqrt{1+4\log(k)\sum_{i=1}^{m-1}\tilde{d}_{t_{i}}}}{2\log(k)}>\frac{1+\sqrt{1+m(m-1)}}{2\log(k)}>\frac{m}{2\log(k)}\,.
 | 


Finally, we have


 | 
 | рқ”Ү~nвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)вүҘвҲ‘m=1|S|d~tmвҖӢlogвҒЎ(k)>|S|вҖӢ(|S|+1)4>12вҖӢ|S|.subscript~рқ”Үрқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡ1рқ‘Ҷsubscript~рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҡрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҷ
рқ‘Ҷ1412рқ‘Ҷ\displaystyle\sqrt{\tilde{\mathfrak{D}}_{n}\log(k)}\geq\sqrt{\sum_{m=1}^{|S|}\tilde{d}_{t_{m}}\log(k)}>\sqrt{\frac{|S|(|S|+1)}{4}}>\frac{1}{2}|S|\,.
 | 


вҲҺ


### 
7.2 Standard properties of FTRL analysis


First we list some standard properties of FTRL that we use in the proofs of the remaining lemmas. We recall that ftвҖӢ(x)=вҲ’2вҖӢtвҖӢx+О·tвҲ’1вҖӢxвҖӢlogвҒЎ(x)subscriptрқ‘“рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ
2рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҘsuperscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ў1рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙf_{t}(x)=-2\sqrt{t}\sqrt{x}+\eta_{t}^{-1}x\log(x).


###### 
Fact 1.


ftвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x):в„қ+вҶ’в„қ+:subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘“вҖІвҖІрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҘвҶ’subscriptв„қ
subscriptв„қ
f^{\prime\prime}_{t}(x):\mathbb{R}_{+}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}_{+} are monotonically decreasing functions and fвҲ—tвҖІ:в„қвҶ’в„қ+:superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІвҶ’в„қsubscriptв„қ
{f^{*}}_{t}^{\prime}:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}_{+} are convex and monotonically increasing.


###### Proof.


By definition ftвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x)=12вҖӢtвҖӢxвҲ’3/2+О·tвҲ’1вҖӢxвҲ’1subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘“вҖІвҖІрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙ
12рқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ32superscriptsubscriptрқңӮрқ‘Ў1superscriptрқ‘Ҙ1f^{\prime\prime}_{t}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{t}x^{-3/2}+\eta_{t}^{-1}x^{-1}, which concludes the first statement.
Since ftsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘Ўf_{t} are Legendre functions, we have ftвҲ—вҖІвҖІвҖӢ(y)=ftвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(ftвҲ—вҖІвҖӢ(y))вҲ’1>0superscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІвҖІрқ‘Ұsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІвҖІsuperscriptsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІрқ‘Ұ10{f_{t}^{*}}^{\prime\prime}(y)=f_{t}^{\prime\prime}({f_{t}^{*}}^{\prime}(y))^{-1}>0.
Therefore the function is monotonically increasing.
Since both ftвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x)вҲ’1superscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1f_{t}^{\prime\prime}(x)^{-1}, as well as ftвҲ—вҖІвҖӢ(y)superscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІрқ‘Ұ{f_{t}^{*}}^{\prime}(y) are increasing, the composition is as well and ftвҲ—вҖІвҖІвҖІ>0superscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІвҖІвҖІ0{f_{t}^{*}}^{\prime\prime\prime}>0.
вҲҺ


###### 
Fact 2.


For any convex Fрқҗ№F, for LвҲҲв„қkрқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ҳL\in\mathbb{R}^{k} and cвҲҲв„қрқ‘җв„қc\in\mathbb{R}:


 | 
 | FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(L+cвҖӢрқҹҸk)=FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(L)+c.superscriptВҜрқҗ№
рқҗҝрқ‘җsubscript1рқ‘ҳ
superscriptВҜрқҗ№рқҗҝрқ‘җ\displaystyle\overline{F}^{*}(L+c\mathbf{1}_{k})=\overline{F}^{*}(L)+c\,.
 | 


###### Proof.


By definition FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(L+cвҖӢрқҹҸk)=maxxвҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])вҒЎвҹЁx,L+cвҖӢрқҹҸkвҹ©вҲ’FвҖӢ(x)=maxxвҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])вҒЎвҹЁx,Lвҹ©вҲ’FвҖӢ(x)+c=FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(L)+csuperscriptВҜрқҗ№
рқҗҝрқ‘җsubscript1рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘ҘО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙ
рқҗҝрқ‘җsubscript1рқ‘ҳрқҗ№рқ‘Ҙ
subscriptрқ‘ҘО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝрқҗ№рқ‘Ҙрқ‘җ
superscriptВҜрқҗ№рқҗҝрқ‘җ\overline{F}^{*}(L+c\mathbf{1}_{k})=\max_{x\in\Delta([k])}\langle x,L+c\mathbf{1}_{k}\rangle-F(x)=\max_{x\in\Delta([k])}\langle x,L\rangle-F(x)+c=\overline{F}^{*}(L)+c.
вҲҺ


###### 
Fact 3.


For any xtsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўx_{t} there exists cвҲҲв„қрқ‘җв„қc\in\mathbb{R}, such that:


 | 
 | xt=вҲҮFВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢs)=вҲҮFtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢs+cвҖӢрқҹҸk)=вҲҮFtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲҮFtвҖӢ(xt)).subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ЎвҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘ЎвҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ў
subscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘Ўрқ‘җsubscript1рқ‘ҳвҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘ЎвҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў\displaystyle x_{t}=\nabla\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t})=\nabla F_{t}^{*}(-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t}+c\mathbf{1}_{k})=\nabla F_{t}^{*}(\nabla F_{t}(x_{t}))\,.
 | 


###### Proof.


By the KKT conditions, there exists cвҲҲв„қрқ‘җв„қc\in\mathbb{R}, such that xt=argвҖӢmaxxвҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])вҒЎвҹЁx,вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢsвҹ©+FtвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
subscriptargmaxрқ‘ҘО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙx_{t}=\operatornamewithlimits{arg\,max}_{x\in\Delta([k])}\langle x,-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t}\rangle+F_{t}(x) satisfies вҲҮFtвҖӢ(xt)=вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢs+cвҖӢрқҹҸkвҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ў
subscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝрқ‘ңрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘Ўрқ‘җsubscript1рқ‘ҳ\nabla F_{t}(x_{t})=-\hat{L}^{obs}_{t}+c\mathbf{1}_{k}. The rest follows by the standard property вҲҮF=(вҲҮFвҲ—)вҲ’1вҲҮрқҗ№superscriptвҲҮsuperscriptрқҗ№1\nabla F=(\nabla F^{*})^{-1} of Legendre Fрқҗ№F.
вҲҺ


###### 
Fact 4.


For any Legendre function Fрқҗ№F and LвҲҲв„қkрқҗҝsuperscriptв„қрқ‘ҳL\in\mathbb{R}^{k} it holds that


 | 
 | FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(L)вүӨFвҲ—вҖӢ(L)superscriptВҜрқҗ№рқҗҝsuperscriptрқҗ№рқҗҝ\displaystyle\overline{F}^{*}(L)\leq F^{*}(L)\,
 | 


with equality iff there exists xвҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])рқ‘ҘО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳx\in\Delta([k]), such that L=вҲҮFвҖӢ(x)рқҗҝвҲҮрқҗ№рқ‘ҘL=\nabla F(x).


###### Proof.


The first statement follows from the definition, since for any AвҠӮBрқҗҙрқҗөA\subset B: maxxвҲҲAвҒЎfвҖӢ(x)вүӨmaxxвҲҲBвҒЎfвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗҙрқ‘“рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗөрқ‘“рқ‘Ҙ\max_{x\in A}f(x)\leq\max_{x\in B}f(x).
The second part follows because equality means that argвҖӢmaxxвҒЎвҹЁx,Lвҹ©вҲ’FвҖӢ(x)=вҲҮFвҲ—вҖӢ(L)вҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])subscriptargmaxрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝрқҗ№рқ‘ҘвҲҮsuperscriptрқҗ№рқҗҝО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳ\operatornamewithlimits{arg\,max}_{x}\langle x,L\rangle-F(x)=\nabla F^{*}(L)\in\Delta([k]), which is equivalent to the statement.
вҲҺ


###### 
Fact 5.


For any xвҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])рқ‘ҘО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳx\in\Delta([k]), LвүҘ0рқҗҝ0L\geq 0 and iвҲҲ[k]рқ‘–delimited-[]рқ‘ҳi\in[k]:


 | 
 | вҲҮFВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲҮFtвҖӢ(x)вҲ’L)iвүҘвҲҮFtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲҮFtвҖӢ(x)вҲ’L)i.вҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘ЎsubscriptвҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝрқ‘–вҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘ЎsubscriptвҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝрқ‘–\displaystyle\nabla\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(\nabla F_{t}(x)-L)_{i}\geq\nabla F_{t}^{*}(\nabla F_{t}(x)-L)_{i}\,.
 | 


###### Proof.


By FactВ 3, there exists cвҲҲв„қ:вҲҮFВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲҮFtвҖӢ(x)вҲ’L)=вҲҮFtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲҮFtвҖӢ(x)вҲ’L+cвҖӢрқҹҸk):рқ‘җв„қвҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘ЎвҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҘрқҗҝвҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ў
вҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝрқ‘җsubscript1рқ‘ҳc\in\mathbb{R}:\,\nabla\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(\nabla F_{t}(x)-L)=\nabla F_{t}^{*}(\nabla F_{t}(x)-L+c\mathbf{1}_{k}).
The statement is equivalent to cрқ‘җc being non-negative, since fвҲ—вҖІsuperscriptsuperscriptрқ‘“вҖІ{f^{*}}^{\prime} are monotonically increasing.
If c<0рқ‘җ0c<0, then


 | 
 | 1=вҲ‘i=1k(вҲҮFВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲҮFtвҖӢ(x)вҲ’L))i=вҲ‘i=1k(вҲҮFtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲҮFtвҖӢ(x)вҲ’L+cвҖӢрқҹҸk))i=вҲ‘i=1kftвҲ—вҖІвҖӢ(ftвҖІвҖӢ(xi)вҲ’Li+c)<вҲ‘i=1kftвҲ—вҖІвҖӢ(ftвҖІвҖӢ(xi))=1,1superscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳsubscriptвҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқҗ№рқ‘ЎвҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳsubscriptвҲҮsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ў
вҲҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҙрқҗҝрқ‘җsubscript1рқ‘ҳрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІ
superscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–рқ‘җsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘–1рқ‘ҳsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘“рқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–1\displaystyle 1=\sum_{i=1}^{k}(\nabla\overline{F}_{t}^{*}(\nabla F_{t}(x)-L))_{i}=\sum_{i=1}^{k}(\nabla F_{t}^{*}(\nabla F_{t}(x)-L+c\mathbf{1}_{k}))_{i}=\sum_{i=1}^{k}{f_{t}^{*}}^{\prime}(f_{t}^{\prime}(x_{i})-L_{i}+c)<\sum_{i=1}^{k}{f_{t}^{*}}^{\prime}(f_{t}^{\prime}(x_{i}))=1\,,
 | 


which is a contradiction and completes the proof.
вҲҺ


###### 
Fact 6.


Let DFвҖӢ(x,y)=FвҖӢ(x)вҲ’FвҖӢ(y)вҲ’вҹЁxвҲ’y,вҲҮFвҖӢ(y)вҹ©subscriptрқҗ·рқҗ№рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұрқҗ№рқ‘Ҙрқҗ№рқ‘Ұ
- рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰвҲҮрқҗ№рқ‘ҰD_{F}(x,y)=F(x)-F(y)-\langle x-y,\nabla F(y)\rangle be the Bregman divergence of a function Fрқҗ№F.
For any Legendre function fрқ‘“f with monotonically decreasing second derivative, xвҲҲdomвҒЎ(f)рқ‘Ҙdomрқ‘“x\in\operatorname{dom}(f), and в„“вүҘ0в„“0\ell\geq 0, such that fвҖІвҖӢ(x)вҲ’в„“вҲҲdomвҒЎ(fвҲ—)superscriptрқ‘“вҖІрқ‘Ҙв„“domsuperscriptрқ‘“f^{\prime}(x)-\ell\in\operatorname{dom}(f^{*}):


 | 
 | DfвҲ—вҖӢ(fвҖІвҖӢ(x)вҲ’в„“,fвҖІвҖӢ(x))вүӨв„“22вҖӢfвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x).subscriptрқҗ·superscriptрқ‘“superscriptрқ‘“вҖІрқ‘Ҙв„“superscriptрқ‘“вҖІрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptв„“22superscriptрқ‘“вҖІвҖІрқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle D_{f^{*}}(f^{\prime}(x)-\ell,f^{\prime}(x))\leq\frac{\ell^{2}}{2f^{\prime\prime}(x)}\,.
 | 


###### Proof.


By TaylorвҖҷs theorem, there exists x~вҲҲ[fвҲ—вҖІвҖӢ(fвҖІвҖӢ(x)вҲ’в„“),x]~рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsuperscriptрқ‘“вҖІsuperscriptрқ‘“вҖІрқ‘Ҙв„“рқ‘Ҙ\tilde{x}\in[{f^{*}}^{\prime}(f^{\prime}(x)-\ell),x], such that
DfвҲ—вҖӢ(fвҖІвҖӢ(x)вҲ’в„“,fвҖІвҖӢ(x))=в„“22вҖӢfвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x~)subscriptрқҗ·superscriptрқ‘“superscriptрқ‘“вҖІрқ‘Ҙв„“superscriptрқ‘“вҖІрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptв„“22superscriptрқ‘“вҖІвҖІ~рқ‘ҘD_{f^{*}}(f^{\prime}(x)-\ell,f^{\prime}(x))=\frac{\ell^{2}}{2f^{\prime\prime}(\tilde{x})}. x~~рқ‘Ҙ\tilde{x} is smaller than xрқ‘Ҙx, since fвҲ—вҖІsuperscriptsuperscriptрқ‘“вҖІ{f^{*}}^{\prime} is monotonically increasing. Finally, using the fact that the second derivative is decreasing allows to bound fвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x~)вҲ’1вүӨfвҖІвҖІвҖӢ(x)вҲ’1superscriptрқ‘“вҖІвҖІsuperscript~рқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘“вҖІвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ1f^{\prime\prime}(\tilde{x})^{-1}\leq f^{\prime\prime}(x)^{-1}.
вҲҺ


###### 
Fact 7.


For any convex Fрқҗ№F, L2вүҘL1subscriptрқҗҝ2subscriptрқҗҝ1L_{2}\geq L_{1} (coordinate wise), it holds


 | 
 | FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L1)вүҘFВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L2).superscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ1superscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ2\displaystyle\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{1})\geq\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{2})\,.
 | 


###### Proof.


 | 
 | FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L2)superscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ2\displaystyle\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{2})
 | =вҹЁвҲҮFВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L2),вҲ’L2вҹ©+FвҖӢ(вҲҮFВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L2))absent

- вҲҮsuperscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ2subscriptрқҗҝ2рқҗ№вҲҮsuperscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ2\displaystyle=\langle\nabla\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{2}),-L_{2}\rangle+F(\nabla\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{2}))
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | вүӨвҹЁвҲҮFВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L2),вҲ’L1вҹ©+FвҖӢ(вҲҮFВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L2))absent

- вҲҮsuperscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ2subscriptрқҗҝ1рқҗ№вҲҮsuperscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ2\displaystyle\leq\langle\nabla\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{2}),-L_{1}\rangle+F(\nabla\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{2}))
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | вүӨmaxxвҲҲО”вҖӢ([k])вҒЎвҹЁx,вҲ’L1вҹ©+FвҖӢ(x)absent
subscriptрқ‘ҘО”delimited-[]рқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқҗҝ1рқҗ№рқ‘Ҙ\displaystyle\leq\max_{x\in\Delta([k])}\langle x,-L_{1}\rangle+F(x)
 | 


 | 
 | 
 | =FВҜвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L1).absentsuperscriptВҜрқҗ№subscriptрқҗҝ1\displaystyle=\overline{F}^{*}(-L_{1})\,.
 | 


вҲҺ


### 
7.3 Proofs of the Main Lemmas


###### Proof of LemmaВ 1.


 | 
 | FВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢsвҲ’в„“^t)вҲ’FВҜtвҲ—вҖӢ(вҲ’L^toвҖӢbвҖӢs)+вҹЁxt,в„“^tвҹ©

###### Proof of LemmaВ 3.

 | 
 | L^t+1SВҜ=L^tSВҜ+в„“^t+вҲ‘sвҲҲS:s+ds=tв„“^sвүҘL^tSВҜ+в„“^t.subscriptsuperscript^рқҗҝВҜрқ‘Ҷ

 |
